4. **Stiffness & Payload:** Parallel manipulators offer *higher stiffness* and *higher payload-to-weight ratio* because loads are distributed across multiple legs and actuators are mounted on the base.
5. **Singularities:** Parallel manipulators exhibit *interior* singularities inside the workspace, whereas serial manipulator singularities occur only at the boundary.

---

### Q2. State and explain the Grübler–Kutzbach criterion for the mobility of a mechanism. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Grübler–Kutzbach formula gives the **mobility (M)**, i.e., the number of independent inputs (DOF) of a mechanism:

$$
M = \lambda (n - j - 1) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

Where:
- $\lambda = 6$ for spatial, $3$ for planar mechanisms.
- $n$ = total number of links (including the fixed base).
- $j$ = total number of joints.
- $f_i$ = relative DOF of joint *i* (revolute = 1, prismatic = 1, spherical = 3, etc.).

**Example (4-bar planar):** $n = 4$, $j = 4$, $f_i = 1$, $\lambda = 3$ → $M = 3(4-4-1) + 4 = 1$ DOF ✓.

**Limitation:** For highly constrained parallel manipulators (e.g., Stewart platform), the formula often yields inconsistent results due to redundant constraints, and mobility must be determined by structural analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Parallel Manipulators):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse DOF with the number of joints.** The DOF of a 6-SPS Stewart platform is **6**, not 18 (joints). Marks are deducted for this mistake.
> 2. **In IK derivations, students frequently drop the term $R \vec{B_i}$**, treating the platform as a point mass. Always retain the full rigid-body transformation $\vec{p} + R \vec{B_i} - \vec{A_i}$.
> 3. **Sign convention for the Jacobian:** Missing the $1/L_i$ factor leads to incorrect velocity and force calculations.
> 4. **Mention both types of singularities (Type 1 and Type 2)** when asked — half-marks lost otherwise.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) Describe the architecture of a 6-DOF Gough-Stewart platform with a neat sketch. Explain the role of each joint and the DOF of the mechanism. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 6-DOF Gough-Stewart platform is the most widely used parallel manipulator. Its architecture consists of:

1. **Fixed base:** A rigid hexagonal/circular plate with 6 spherical joint mounts.
2. **Mobile platform:** A rigid plate (the end-effector) with 6 spherical joint mounts.
3. **Six identical legs (6-SPS):** Each leg contains three joints in the sequence **Spherical–Prismatic–Spherical**:
   - **Lower spherical joint:** Connects the leg to the base; allows 3 rotational DOF.
   - **Middle prismatic joint:** The **actuated** joint — a linear actuator (electric ball screw, hydraulic cylinder, or pneumatic piston) that changes the leg length $L_i$.
   - **Upper spherical joint:** Connects the leg to the mobile platform; allows 3 rotational DOF.

**DOF of the mechanism = 6** (3 translations + 3 rotations in space). This is determined structurally: 6 independently actuated prismatic joints fully control the 6-DOF pose of the platform.

**Advantages:** high stiffness, high accuracy, all actuators on the base (low moving mass), isotropic force transmission in good configurations.

**Disadvantages:** small workspace, complex forward kinematics (multiple solutions), interior singularities, complex mechanical design.

**(b) Derive the inverse kinematics equations of the Stewart platform. Given a desired pose ($\vec{p}$, $R$), find expressions for the six leg lengths $L_1, \ldots, L_6$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to the exhaustive derivation in **Section 3.2** of these notes. The summary is:

$$
\boxed{L_i = \sqrt{ (p_x + s_{ix} - a_{ix})^{2} + (p_y + s_{iy} - a_{iy})^{2} + (p_z + s_{iz} - a_{iz})^{2} }, \quad i = 1, \ldots, 6}
$$

Where $\vec{s_i} = R \vec{B_i}$ is the *i*-th platform anchor expressed in the base frame.

**Procedure:**
1. Express platform anchor in base frame: $\vec{P_i} = \vec{p} + R \vec{B_i}$.
2. Construct leg vector: $\vec{l_i} = \vec{P_i} - \vec{A_i}$.
3. Compute leg length as the Euclidean norm.

> **Note:** Each $L_i$ is computed *independently*, making the IK *decoupled* and computationally trivial.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) Explain the three types of singularities in parallel manipulators with examples. Discuss the consequences of singularity in motion and force transmission. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Parallel manipulators exhibit **three principal types of singularities** (Merlet's classification):

1. **Type 1 — Inverse Kinematic (Serial / Leg Singularities):**
   - Occur when the Jacobian **$J$** loses rank (rows of $J$ become linearly dependent).
   - At such configurations, the end-effector can undergo **finite motion** even when all actuators are locked.
   - **Consequence:** Loss of stiffness; uncontrolled motion of the platform.
   - **Example:** In a Stewart platform, when all 6 legs become parallel to each other.

2. **Type 2 — Forward Kinematic (Parallel / Platform Singularities):**
   - Occur when the **$J^{T}$** loses rank (columns of $J$ become linearly dependent).
   - At such configurations, the actuators can apply **finite forces/torques** that produce **zero wrench** on the platform — a complete loss of force control.
   - **Consequence:** The mechanism can "lock" or generate infinite internal forces.
   - **Example:** When the platform is in a fully extended or fully retracted pose.

3. **Type 3 — Combined Singularities:**
   - Both $J$ and $J^T$ lose rank simultaneously. These are the most dangerous configurations and lie at the boundaries of the workspace.

**Engineering Mitigation:** Singularities are avoided by:
- Reducing the desired workspace (keeping it singularity-free).
- Trajectory planning that bypasses singular configurations.
- Sensor-based force feedback control.

---

**(b) A planar 3-RRR parallel manipulator has 1 fixed base, 3 ground links, 3 arms, and 1 mobile platform. Each arm is connected to the base and the platform by revolute joints. Using the Grübler–Kutzbach formula, calculate the DOF of the mechanism. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
- Number of links (including fixed base): $n = 1 + 3 + 3 + 1 = 8$.
- Number of joints: $j = 3 + 3 + 3 = 9$ revolute joints (3 base, 3 platform, 3 in the parallelogram arm joints).
- Each revolute joint: $f_i = 1$.
- Planar mechanism: $\lambda = 3$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Grübler–Kutzbach formula:**

$$
M = \lambda (n - j - 1) + \sum_{i=1}^{j} f_i
$$

$$
M = 3 (8 - 9 - 1) + 9(1)
$$

$$
M = 3(-2) + 9
$$

$$
M = -6 + 9
$$

$$
\boxed{M = 3 \text{ DOF}}
$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation:**
- The 3 DOF correspond to **2 translations** ($T_x, T_y$) + **1 rotation** ($R_z$) of the moving platform in the plane.
- This is consistent with the structural analysis: 3 independently actuated revolute joints → 3 controlled DOF.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Definition:** Parallel manipulator = closed-loop multi-legged mechanism with one common end-effector.
- **Canonical Example:** 6-SPS Gough-Stewart platform (6 DOF).
- **DOF of Stewart Platform = 6** (3T + 3R); structurally equals the number of actuated prismatic joints.
- **Grübler–Kutzbach formula:** $M = \lambda (n - j - 1) + \sum f_i$, with $\lambda = 6$ (spatial) or $3$ (planar).
- **Inverse Kinematics (IK):** Trivial — leg length = norm of the leg vector: $L_i = \Vert \vec{p} + R\vec{B_i} - \vec{A_i} \Vert$.
- **Forward Kinematics (FK):** Hard — yields up to 40 solutions for general 6-6 Stewart platform.
- **Jacobian:** $J_i = \frac{1}{L_i} \begin{bmatrix} \vec{l_i}^{T} & (\vec{s_i} \times \vec{l_i})^{T} \end{bmatrix}$.
- **Static relation:** $\vec{\tau} = J^{T} \vec{W}$ (actuator forces from external wrench).
- **Velocity relation:** $\dot{\vec{q}} = J \dot{\vec{x}}$.
- **Workspace:** Intersection of 6 thick spherical shells centered at base anchors $\vec{A_i}$ with radii $[L_{min}, L_{max}]$.
- **Singularities:**
  - **Type 1 (Inverse / Serial):** rank-deficient $J$ → uncontrolled platform motion.
  - **Type 2 (Forward / Parallel):** rank-deficient $J^T$ → no force transmission.
  - **Type 3 (Combined):** both — most severe.
- **Advantages:** High stiffness, accuracy, payload-to-weight ratio, low moving mass.
- **Disadvantages:** Small workspace, complex FK, singularities inside the workspace.
- **Applications:** Flight simulators, hexapod machine tools, Delta pick-and-place robots, surgical robots, telescopes, cable-driven PMs.
- **Delta Robot:** 3-DOF translational PM using parallelogram linkages → fixed orientation of the platform.
- **Euler representation:** $R = R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$ — ZYX (roll-pitch-yaw) convention commonly used.
- **Vector identity to remember:** $[\vec{\omega}]_{\times} \vec{r} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$ — used extensively in Jacobian derivations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spherical Wrist End Effectors: Mechanical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Spherical Wrist End Effectors: Mechanical ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Spherical Wrist End Effector (Mechanical):** A *Spherical Wrist* is the terminal sub-assembly of a robotic manipulator, consisting of **three revolute (R) joints whose rotational axes intersect at a single common point** (the *wrist center* or *spherical center*). The *End Effector* is the mechanical device physically attached to the wrist's terminal flange, designed to perform the actual task — grasping, welding, painting, cutting, or tooling. When the wrist axes are coaxial-meeting and the end effector is mechanically integrated (gripper, suction, magnetic, or tool-holding mechanism), the assembly is termed a *Spherical Wrist End Effector: Mechanical*. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the **mechanical end effector** encompasses: - The **wrist mechanism** (spherical 3-DOF orientation stage) - The **gripper/tool interface** (mechanical fingers, suction cups, magnetic pads, tool changers) - The **force-transmission elements** (linkages, cams, gears, pneumatics/hydraulics) The canonical structure is written as: **R-R-R (concurrent)** followed by a mechanical end-effector unit. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Hand & Wrist" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy:** Imagine your own arm. Your *shoulder and elbow* (the major links) position your palm somewhere in space (positioning — 3 DOF). Your *wrist* can twist, bend up-down, and side-to-side (orientation — 3 DOF, all pivoting at a common point inside the wrist joint). Finally, your *fingers* actually *do* the work — they grasp a pen, hold a cup, or turn a screwdriver. A **Spherical Wrist End Effector** is exactly this: the *arm* brings it to the spot, the *spherical wrist* orients it, and the *mechanical end effector* (gripper/sucker/magnet) performs the task. This decoupling of *positioning* (arm) from *orientation* (wrist) and *action* (end effector) is the foundational design principle taught in KTU's Fundamentals of Robotics. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded) - **Degrees of Freedom (DOF):** **3** for the spherical wrist (pitch, yaw, roll) + DOF of end effector itself. - **Wrist Center Point (WCP):** The exact 3D Cartesian point where all three revolute axes intersect. - **Singularity:** A configuration where the wrist loses 1 DOF (typically when any two axes align collinearly). - **Payload Capacity:** Typically **2 N to 5000 N** depending on the actuator (pneumatic / servo / hydraulic). - **Repeatability:** Industrial spherical wrists achieve **±0.02 mm** to **±0.05 mm** positioning repeatability. - **Closing Force (Grippers):** **10 N to 1000 N** for industrial applications. - **Stroke Length:** **5 mm to 200 mm** for mechanical fingers. - **Operating Pressure (Pneumatic):** **4 bar to 8 bar** standard industrial compressed air. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Highlight:** In the 2024 Scheme syllabus, the *Mechanical End Effector* classification is mandatory to be discussed alongside the spherical wrist kinematics, since the wrist provides the orientation that aligns the end effector's action vector (e.g., gripper approach direction, torch normal). ### 1.4 Classification of Mechanical End Effectors Mechanical end effectors are broadly classified into: | Category | Description | Typical Application | |----------|-------------|---------------------| | **Grippers (Mechanical)** | Two or more fingers actuated by linkages | Pick-and-place, assembly | | **Vacuum / Suction** | Cup with negative pressure | Sheet metal, glass, packaging | | **Magnetic** | Electromagnetic or permanent magnet | Ferrous parts handling | | **Tool Holders** | Spindle, welding torch, paint spray | Machining, welding, painting | | **Specialized** | Deburring, cutting, polishing wheels | Finishing operations | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Spherical Wrist — Structural Logic A **spherical wrist** is mechanically constructed so that the three revolute joint axes pass through one common point. The benefits of this design are: 1. **Kinematic Decoupling:** The wrist kinematics can be separated from the arm kinematics, simplifying the forward and inverse kinematics derivations. 2. **Compact Workspace:** All rotational motion occurs *about* a single point — no translation of the wrist center — preserving the arm's commanded position. 3. **Unified Orientation Control:** A single homogeneous transformation matrix $\mathbf{T}_{\text{wrist}}$ captures all three rotations. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Nearly every KTU question on wrist kinematics expects the student to *first* compute the wrist center position using the arm's DH parameters, *then* compute the orientation matrix $\mathbf{R}_{3 \times 3}$ from the Euler/roll-pitch-yaw angles. The spherical structure guarantees that this decomposition is always valid. ### 2.2 The Mechanical End Effector — Functional Breakdown The **mechanical end effector** is the *physical tool* mounted on the wrist's final flange. It is responsible for: - **Force Application** — gripping, pressing, cutting. - **Energy Transfer** — welding current, paint spray, vacuum suction. - **Positioning Tolerance** — sub-millimeter accuracy at the tool-tip. The end effector is characterized by: $$\text{Performance Vector} = \{F_{\text{grip}}, \, v_{\text{close}}, \, m_{\text{payload}}, \, t_{\text{cycle}}\}$$ where $F_{\text{grip}}$ is the gripping force, $v_{\text{close}}$ is the closing velocity, $m_{\text{payload}}$ is the handled mass, and $t_{\text{cycle}}$ is the cycle time. ### 2.3 Mechanical Gripper Design — Linkage Analysis The most common mechanical end effector is the **two-finger angular gripper** driven by a four-bar linkage. The closing force ratio is: $$F_{\text{tip}} = \frac{T_{\text{actuator}} \cdot \cos(\alpha)}{L_{\text{finger}} \cdot \sin(\beta + \gamma)}$$ where: - $T_{\text{actuator}}$ = actuator torque (Nm) - $\alpha$ = angle between actuator and link - $L_{\text{finger}}$ = finger length (m) - $\beta$ = finger pivot angle - $\gamma$ = force-transmission angle ### 2.4 Vacuum (Suction) End Effector Equations The lifting force of a suction cup is governed by atmospheric pressure differential: $$F_{\text{lift}} = \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n_{\text{cups}} \cdot \mu_{\text{safety}}$$ where: - $\Delta P = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vacuum}}$ (Pa) - $A_{\text{eff}}$ = effective cup area (m²) - $n_{\text{cups}}$ = number of cups - $\mu_{\text{safety}}$ = safety factor (typically **0.5 to 0.8**) ### 2.5 Magnetic End Effector Equation The holding force of an electromagnetic end effector is: $$F_{\text{hold}} = \frac{B^2 \cdot A_{\text{contact}}}{2 \mu_0} \cdot k_{\text{gap}}$$ where: - $B$ = magnetic flux density (**Tesla**) - $A_{\text{contact}}$ = contact area (m²) - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** (permeability of free space) - $k_{\text{gap}}$ = air-gap correction factor (**0.6 to 0.9**) ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates every formula a KTU 2024 student must memorize for spherical-wrist mechanical-end-effector problems. **No pipe `|` characters** are used inside table cells — all absolute-value bars are written using `\vert` or `\mid`. | **#** | **Concept** | **Formula** | **Variables / Units** | |:-----:|-------------|-------------|------------------------| | 1 | Spherical wrist DOF | $\text{DOF}_{\text{wrist}} = 3$ | Three concurrent revolute axes | | 2 | Wrist transformation (ZYZ Euler) | $\mathbf{R}_{\text{wrist}} = R_z(\phi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi)$ | $\phi, \theta, \psi$ in rad | | 3 | End effector DOF (Kutzbach) | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | $n$ links, $j_1$ lower pairs, $j_2$ higher pairs | | 4 | Gripper tip force | $F_{\text{tip}} = \dfrac{T_a \cos\alpha}{L_f \sin(\beta+\gamma)}$ | $T_a$ [Nm], $L_f$ [m], angles [rad] | | 5 | Gripper mechanical advantage | $MA = \dfrac{F_{\text{out}}}{F_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{in}}}{L_{\text{out}}}$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | Vacuum lifting force | $F_{\text{lift}} = \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n \cdot \mu_s$ | $\Delta P$ [Pa], $A$ [m²] | | 7 | Magnetic holding force | $F_{\text{hold}} = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0} k_{\text{gap}}$ | $B$ [T], $\mu_0$ [H/m] | | 8 | Payload (static) | $m_{\max} = \dfrac{F_{\text{grip}}}{g \cdot n_f \cdot \mu_f}$ | $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\mu_f$ friction coeff. | | 9 | Wrist singularity | $\det(\mathbf{J}_{\text{wrist}}) = 0$ | $\mathbf{J}$ is $3 \times 3$ Jacobian | | 10 | Tool-center-point (TCP) offset | $\mathbf{p}_{\text{TCP}} = \mathbf{p}_{\text{flange}} + \mathbf{R}_{\text{flange}} \cdot \mathbf{d}_{\text{tool}}$ | $\mathbf{d}_{\text{tool}}$ is tool vector | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility The spherical-wrist mechanical-end-effector system is the *de facto* standard in: - **Automotive Welding Lines:** Six-axis articulated arms with R-R-R spherical wrists hold welding torches; the wrist orients the torch normal to the seam while the arm traces the path. - **Pick-and-Place Packaging:** Vacuum-cup end effectors on SCARA/spherical-wrist arms handle eggs, biscuits, and PCB components. - **CNC Tending:** Parallel-jaw grippers pick raw billets; magnetic end effectors handle steel stock. - **Surgical Robotics:** The Da Vinci system uses a spherical wrist with mechanical cable-driven tools for sub-millimeter suturing. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** The choice of end effector *mechanism* is dictated by the workpiece's *material*, *geometry*, *weight*, and *fragility*. KTU 2024 questions frequently ask students to *justify* the selection of a specific mechanical end effector for a given industrial scenario. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Number of Degrees of Freedom of a Spherical Wrist + End Effector The **Kutzbach–Grübler criterion** for spatial mechanisms is: $$M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$$ where: - $M$ = mobility (DOF) of the mechanism - $n$ = total number of links (including ground) - $j_1$ = number of lower pairs (1 DOF joints: revolute, prismatic) - $j_2$ = number of higher pairs (2 DOF joints: cam, gear) **Step-by-step application to a Spherical Wrist + 2-finger Mechanical Gripper:** - **Step 1:** Count all links. - Ground (base flange) → 1 - Wrist link 1 → 1 - Wrist link 2 → 1 - Wrist link 3 (output flange) → 1 - Left finger → 1 - Right finger → 1 - Slider/actuator link → 1 - **Total: $n = 7$** - **Step 2:** Count lower pairs (1-DOF joints). - 3 revolute joints in the wrist (R-R-R concurrent) → 3 - 2 revolute joints (finger pivots) → 2 - 1 prismatic joint (slider driving fingers) → 1 - **Total: $j_1 = 6$** - **Step 3:** Count higher pairs. For this gripper, **$j_2 = 0$**. - **Step 4:** Substitute into the equation. $$ \begin{aligned} M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\ &= 3(7 - 1) - 2(6) - 0 \\ &= 3 \times 6 - 12 \\ &= 18 - 12 \\ &= 6 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result:** The spherical wrist contributes **3 DOF** (orientation) and the mechanical gripper contributes **3 DOF** (slider + 2 finger rotations) — totalling **6 DOF**, which matches the typical 6-DOF industrial robot arm (3 positioning + 3 orientation). ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Forward Kinematics of the Spherical Wrist (ZYZ Euler Angles) The orientation of the end-effector flange relative to the wrist base is expressed via **ZYZ Euler angles** $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$: $$ \mathbf{R}_{\text{wrist}}(\phi, \theta, \psi) = R_z(\phi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi) $$ **Step 1:** Define each elementary rotation matrix. $$ R_z(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & -\sin\phi & 0 \\ \sin\phi & \cos\phi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2:** Compute the intermediate product $R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi)$. $$ \begin{aligned} R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi) &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \\[6pt] &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta\cos\psi & -\cos\theta\sin\psi & \sin\theta \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ -\sin\theta\cos\psi & \sin\theta\sin\psi & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Multiply on the left by $R_z(\phi)$ to get the final wrist rotation matrix. $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{R}_{\text{wrist}} &= R_z(\phi) \cdot [R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi)] \\[4pt] &= \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi\cos\theta\cos\psi - \sin\phi\sin\psi & -\cos\phi\cos\theta\sin\psi - \sin\phi\cos\psi & \cos\phi\sin\theta \\ \sin\phi\cos\theta\cos\psi + \cos\phi\sin\psi & -\sin\phi\cos\theta\sin\psi + \cos\phi\cos\psi & \sin\phi\sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta\cos\psi & \sin\theta\sin\psi & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Each column of $\mathbf{R}_{\text{wrist}}$ is the unit vector of the *end-effector frame* expressed in the *base frame*. The third column $[\cos\phi\sin\theta, \sin\phi\sin\theta, \cos\theta]^T$ is the **approach vector** of the mechanical tool — critical for aligning a gripper or welding torch. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Mechanical Advantage of a Four-Bar Linkage Gripper A standard **angular gripper** uses a four-bar slider-crank-linkage. The mechanical advantage is: $$ MA = \frac{F_{\text{output}}}{F_{\text{input}}} = \frac{\sin(\alpha_{\text{in}}) \cdot L_{\text{in}}}{\sin(\alpha_{\text{out}}) \cdot L_{\text{out}}} $$ **Step 1:** Identify the geometry. - Input link length $L_{\text{in}} = 30$ mm (slider-to-pivot) - Output link length $L_{\text{out}} = 50$ mm (pivot-to-fingertip) - Input transmission angle $\alpha_{\text{in}} = 60°$ - Output transmission angle $\alpha_{\text{out}} = 30°$ - Applied slider force $F_{\text{input}} = 50$ N **Step 2:** Compute the mechanical advantage. $$ \begin{aligned} MA &= \frac{\sin(60°) \cdot 30}{\sin(30°) \cdot 50} \\[4pt] &= \frac{0.8660 \times 30}{0.5000 \times 50} \\[4pt] &= \frac{25.98}{25.00} \\[4pt] &= 1.039 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3:** Compute the output (gripper tip) force. $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{output}} &= MA \cdot F_{\text{input}} \\[4pt] &= 1.039 \times 50 \\[4pt] &= 51.96 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Mechanical advantages near unity indicate a *force-neutral* design. To *amplify* force, the engineer must either *increase $L_{\text{in}}$* (longer input lever) or *decrease $L_{\text{out}}$* (shorter finger). KTU problems often test this trade-off between stroke length and grip force. ### 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Payload for a Two-Finger Gripper A two-finger gripper holds a payload by friction. The condition for *static equilibrium* is: $$m_{\text{max}} \cdot g = 2 \cdot F_{\text{tip}} \cdot \mu_f$$ where $\mu_f$ is the coefficient of friction between finger pads and the workpiece. **Step 1:** Given values. - $F_{\text{tip}} = 51.96$ N (from previous derivation) - $\mu_f = 0.4$ (rubber-on-steel) - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 2:** Solve for $m_{\text{max}}$. $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{max}} &= \frac{2 \cdot F_{\text{tip}} \cdot \mu_f}{g} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2 \times 51.96 \times 0.4}{9.81} \\[4pt] &= \frac{41.568}{9.81} \\[4pt] &= 4.237 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Take-away:** The factor of **2** in the numerator arises from the *two-finger* symmetric design. For a *three-finger* gripper, replace 2 with $n_f$ (number of fingers). The safety margin is typically applied as $m_{\text{design}} = 0.5 \cdot m_{\text{max}}$. ### 3.5 Derivation 5: Tool Center Point (TCP) Transformation The mechanical end effector is offset from the wrist flange by a tool vector $\mathbf{d}_{\text{tool}} = [d_x, d_y, d_z]^T$ in the flange frame. The **TCP position** in the base frame is: $$\mathbf{p}_{\text{TCP}} = \mathbf{p}_{\text{flange}} + \mathbf{R}_{\text{flange}} \cdot \mathbf{d}_{\text{tool}}$$ **Step 1:** Suppose the wrist flange is at $\mathbf{p}_{\text{flange}} = [0.5, 0.3, 0.6]^T$ m, and the wrist orientation (ZYZ Euler) gives $\phi = 30°$, $\theta = 45°$, $\psi = 60°$. The tool is a gripper with $\mathbf{d}_{\text{tool}} = [0, 0, 0.1]^T$ m. **Step 2:** Compute $R_z(30°)$ and $R_y(45°)$. $$ R_z(30°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.866 & -0.5 & 0 \\ 0.5 & 0.866 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_y(45°) = \begin{bmatrix} 0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -0.707 & 0 & 0.707 \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 3:** Compute $\mathbf{R}_{\text{flange}} \cdot \mathbf{d}_{\text{tool}}$. Since $\mathbf{d}_{\text{tool}}$ is along the flange $z$-axis, the result is simply the third column of $\mathbf{R}_{\text{flange}}$ scaled by $d_z = 0.1$. For $\phi=30°, \theta=45°$, the third column is $[\cos 30° \sin 45°, \sin 30° \sin 45°, \cos 45°]^T = [0.612, 0.354, 0.707]^T$. **Step 4:** Add to the flange position. $$ \begin{aligned} \mathbf{p}_{\text{TCP}} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.3 \\ 0.6 \end{bmatrix} + 0.1 \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 0.612 \\ 0.354 \\ 0.707 \end{bmatrix} \\[4pt] &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.5612 \\ 0.3354 \\ 0.6707 \end{bmatrix} \text{ m} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** Industrial robot programming in KTU labs requires students to *define the TCP* correctly. A wrong tool vector causes systematic positioning errors in *every* move command — a classic KTU lab-evaluation pitfall. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Spherical Wrist + Mechanical End Effector ```mermaid graph TD A[Base Arm Links
3 DOF Positioning] --> B[Wrist Link 1
Roll Axis phi] B --> C[Wrist Link 2
Pitch Axis theta] C --> D[Wrist Link 3
Yaw Axis psi] D --> E[Tool Flange
TCP Origin] E --> F[Mechanical End Effector] F --> G1[Two Finger Gripper] F --> G2[Vacuum Suction Cup] F --> G3[Electromagnetic Pad] F --> G4[Tool Holder Spindle] G1 --> H1[Slider Actuator
Pneumatic or Servo] G1 --> H2[Finger Linkage
Four Bar Mechanism] H2 --> H3[Finger Pads
Rubber or Hardened Steel] ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — End-Effector Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Start Selection] --> Q1{Workpiece
Material} Q1 -- Ferrous Metal --> M1[Electromagnetic
End Effector] Q1 -- Smooth Non Porous --> M2[Vacuum Suction
End Effector] Q1 -- Irregular Solid --> M3[Mechanical
Finger Gripper] Q1 -- Tool Based Task --> M4[Tool Holder
Spindle or Torch] M1 --> P1[Verify Holding Force via
B squared A over 2 mu0] M2 --> P2[Verify Vacuum Force via
Delta P times A] M3 --> P3[Verify Grip Force via
MA times F input] M4 --> P4[Verify Tool Length and
TCP Offset Vector] P1 --> X[Selected End Effector Ready] P2 --> X P3 --> X P4 --> X ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Force & Motion Transmission Architecture ```mermaid graph LR SRC[Energy Source
Compressed Air or Electric Servo] --> ACT[Actuator
Piston or Motor] ACT --> TRD[Transmission
Linkage or Gear Train] TRD --> WRI[Spherical Wrist
3 Revolute Joints] WRI --> FLG[End Effector Flange
Mounting Plate] FLG --> EF[Mechanical End Effector
Gripper or Tool] EF --> WPC[Workpiece
Target Object] WPC --> FB[Force Feedback
Sensor Optional] FB --> CTL[Controller
PLC or Robot CPU] CTL --> SRC ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation of Diagram 4.3:** The block diagram shows the closed-loop energy-and-signal flow. The **actuator** (pneumatic piston or servo motor) is the prime mover. The **transmission** (four-bar linkage or rack-pinion) converts actuator motion into gripper jaw motion. The **spherical wrist** then orients the *entire end-effector assembly* to align with the workpiece. The **feedback** path enables adaptive gripping in advanced systems. ### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Pick-and-Place Cycle ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant ARM as Robotic Arm participant WRI as Spherical Wrist participant EF as Mechanical End Effector participant WPC as Workpiece ARM ->> WRI: Move to Approach Pose WRI ->> WRI: Orient to Alignment Angles WRI ->> ARM: Wrist Center Reached ARM ->> EF: Activate Gripper Close Command EF ->> WPC: Apply Grip Force WPC -->> EF: Contact Confirmation EF ->> ARM: Grasp Acknowledged ARM ->> WRI: Retract and Reorient WRI ->> ARM: Move to Place Pose ARM ->> EF: Release Command EF ->> WPC: Open Jaws and Release ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Define a spherical wrist in a robotic manipulator. Why is the intersection of the three revolute axes at a common point important?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **spherical wrist** is a wrist mechanism consisting of **three revolute (R) joints whose axes intersect at a single common point**, called the *wrist center*. The importance of axis intersection is twofold: 1. **Kinematic Decoupling:** Because the wrist center remains stationary during wrist motion, the arm's positioning kinematics can be solved independently of the wrist's orientation kinematics. This simplifies both forward and inverse kinematic solutions. 2. **Singularity Avoidance & Compactness:** The intersection guarantees that the three rotations are *purely orientational* (no translational artifact), enabling a compact and singularity-minimized design. *(Award full 3 marks: definition — 1 mark, decoupling point — 1 mark, compactness/singularity point — 1 mark.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** *List any three types of mechanical end effectors used in industrial robots and state one industrial application of each.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | # | End Effector Type | Industrial Application | |---|-------------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Two-finger mechanical gripper** | Pick-and-place of machined components in CNC tending | | 2 | **Vacuum suction cup** | Handling of glass sheets in automotive windshield assembly | | 3 | **Electromagnetic gripper** | Lifting ferrous steel plates in press-shop operations | *(Award 1 mark per correctly named type with valid application.)* --- ### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a **spherical wrist + mechanical end effector** system used in a robotic pick-and-place workstation. Label all major components. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A two-finger mechanical gripper is driven by a pneumatic slider actuator. The input link length is 40 mm, the output finger link length is 60 mm, the input transmission angle is 55°, and the output transmission angle is 35°. The applied slider force is 80 N. Compute: - (i) the mechanical advantage, - (ii) the gripper tip force, - (iii) the maximum payload mass assuming a coefficient of friction of 0.5 between rubber finger pads and a steel workpiece. Use $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **(a) Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):** > [!NOTE] > A textual block-diagram explanation is provided here for KTU handwritten-answer scripts. The corresponding Mermaid architecture was shown in SECTION 4.3. **Components (label each, 3 marks):** - **Energy Source** — Compressed air at 6 bar (or electric servo). - **Actuator** — Pneumatic piston (single- or double-acting cylinder). - **Transmission** — Four-bar slider-crank linkage converting piston motion to finger motion. - **Spherical Wrist** — 3 revolute joints (R-R-R concurrent) for orientation. - **End-Effector Flange** — Mechanical mounting plate (ISO 9409 standard). - **Mechanical End Effector** — Two-finger parallel/angular gripper with finger pads. - **Sensors** — Pressure sensor, position sensor, optional force/tactile sensor. **Working (2 marks):** The controller commands the pneumatic actuator to extend. The four-bar linkage translates the linear slider motion into angular finger motion, closing the gripper jaws onto the workpiece. The spherical wrist — driven by the arm's wrist servos — orients the entire end-effector assembly so that the gripper's *approach vector* is aligned normal to the workpiece surface. The wrist's three concurrent axes allow pure orientation without disturbing the gripper's commanded position. **Justification (2 marks):** - **Decoupled design:** Position (arm) and orientation (wrist) are independently controlled. - **Mechanical efficiency:** Linkage amplifies/regulates the actuator force to match payload needs. - **Modularity:** The mechanical end effector can be swapped (gripper ↔ vacuum ↔ magnet) via a standard tool changer. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Given:** - $L_{\text{in}} = 40$ mm = $0.040$ m - $L_{\text{out}} = 60$ mm = $0.060$ m - $\alpha_{\text{in}} = 55°$ - $\alpha_{\text{out}} = 35°$ - $F_{\text{input}} = 80$ N - $\mu_f = 0.5$ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1: Mechanical Advantage (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} MA &= \frac{\sin(\alpha_{\text{in}}) \cdot L_{\text{in}}}{\sin(\alpha_{\text{out}}) \cdot L_{\text{out}}} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\sin(55°) \times 0.040}{\sin(35°) \times 0.060} \\[4pt] &= \frac{0.8192 \times 0.040}{0.5736 \times 0.060} \\[4pt] &= \frac{0.03277}{0.03441} \\[4pt] &= 0.9521 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Gripper Tip Force (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{tip}} &= MA \cdot F_{\text{input}} \\[4pt] &= 0.9521 \times 80 \\[4pt] &= 76.17 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Maximum Payload Mass (3 marks)** For a two-finger gripper with friction: $$ \begin{aligned} m_{\text{max}} &= \frac{2 \cdot F_{\text{tip}} \cdot \mu_f}{g} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2 \times 76.17 \times 0.5}{9.81} \\[4pt] &= \frac{76.17}{9.81} \\[4pt] &= 7.764 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answers (boxed):** - $MA = 0.952$ - $F_{\text{tip}} = 76.17$ N - $m_{\text{max}} = 7.76$ kg --- **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Derive the **forward kinematic orientation matrix** of a spherical wrist using **ZYZ Euler angles** $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$. Clearly show the multiplication of the three elementary rotation matrices. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A vacuum suction-cup end effector is to be designed to lift a smooth glass plate of mass 12 kg. The safety factor is 0.6 and the atmospheric pressure is $1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa. If the vacuum pressure inside the cup is 0.3 atm and the effective cup diameter is 80 mm, determine: - (i) the theoretical lifting force, - (ii) whether a single cup is sufficient or multiple cups are required. - Take $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **(a) Derivation of ZYZ Wrist Rotation Matrix (7 marks):** The orientation of the wrist's output flange relative to its base is given by the ZYZ Euler angle convention. The three elementary rotations are: $$ R_z(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi & -\sin\phi & 0 \\ \sin\phi & \cos\phi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} $$ $$ R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} $$ The total wrist rotation matrix is: $$ \mathbf{R}_{\text{wrist}} = R_z(\phi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi) $$ **Step 1: Compute $R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi)$ (3 marks):** $$ R_y(\theta) \cdot R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta\cos\psi & -\cos\theta\sin\psi & \sin\theta \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ -\sin\theta\cos\psi & \sin\theta\sin\psi & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} $$ **Step 2: Multiply on the left by $R_z(\phi)$ (4 marks):** $$ \mathbf{R}_{\text{wrist}} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\phi\cos\theta\cos\psi - \sin\phi\sin\psi & -\cos\phi\cos\theta\sin\psi - \sin\phi\cos\psi & \cos\phi\sin\theta \\ \sin\phi\cos\theta\cos\psi + \cos\phi\sin\psi & -\sin\phi\cos\theta\sin\psi + \cos\phi\cos\psi & \sin\phi\sin\theta \\ -\sin\theta\cos\psi & \sin\theta\sin\psi & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix} $$ This $3 \times 3$ matrix represents the orientation of the end-effector frame relative to the wrist base. The third column $[\cos\phi\sin\theta, \sin\phi\sin\theta, \cos\theta]^T$ is the **tool approach vector** — the direction in which the mechanical end effector (gripper / suction cup / torch) acts. --- **(b) Vacuum Suction Cup Design (7 marks):** **Given:** - $m_{\text{plate}} = 12$ kg - $\mu_s = 0.6$ (safety factor) - $P_{\text{atm}} = 1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa - $P_{\text{vacuum}} = 0.3 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 = 3.039 \times 10^4$ Pa - $D = 80$ mm = $0.080$ m - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **Step 1: Pressure Differential (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta P &= P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vacuum}} \\[4pt] &= 1.013 \times 10^5 - 3.039 \times 10^4 \\[4pt] &= 7.091 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Effective Cup Area (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} A_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.080)^2}{4} \\[4pt] &= \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Lifting Force for Single Cup (2 marks)** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{lift, 1 cup}} &= \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot \mu_s \\[4pt] &= 7.091 \times 10^4 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.6 \\[4pt] &= 213.87 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Required Lifting Force (1 mark)** $$ \begin{aligned} F_{\text{required}} &= m_{\text{plate}} \cdot g = 12 \times 9.81 = 117.72 \text{ N} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Comparison and Decision (2 marks)** $$ F_{\text{lift, 1 cup}} = 213.87 \text{ N} \quad > \quad F_{\text{required}} = 117.72 \text{ N} $$ Since $F_{\text{lift, 1 cup}} > F_{\text{required}}$, a **single suction cup is sufficient** to lift the 12 kg glass plate under the given safety factor. **Verification of safety margin:** $$ \text{Margin} = \frac{213.87 - 117.72}{213.87} \times 100\% = 44.96\% $$ The 44.96% safety margin is reasonable for industrial practice (typically 30–50% is acceptable). --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Spherical Wrist + End Effector Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the "2" in the payload formula** — Many students write $m_{\max} = F_{\text{tip}} \mu_f / g$ instead of $2 F_{\text{tip}} \mu_f / g$ for a **two-finger** gripper. This loses 1–2 marks. **Always check the number of contact points.** > > 2. **Confusing ZYZ with ZYX Euler angles** — KTU specifically uses **ZYZ** for spherical wrists. Writing the wrong sequence gives a completely different rotation matrix. **Memorize the order: rotate by $\phi$ about $z$, then $\theta$ about $y$, then $\psi$ about $z$.** > > 3. **Wrong units in vacuum equation** — Pressure in **Pa** (not bar), area in **m²** (not mm²), force in **N** (not kgf). A unit slip loses a full 1 mark. > > 4. **Ignoring the safety factor** — Industrial designs *always* include a safety margin $\mu_s < 1$. Skipping it shows lack of practical awareness. > > 5. **Tool-Center-Point (TCP) offset omitted** — In lab exams, students frequently command the robot to move to the *flange* pose and forget the tool offset. The TCP vector $\mathbf{d}_{\text{tool}}$ must be added explicitly. > > 6. **Sign errors in trig** — $\sin(55°) = 0.8192$, not $0.7$. Use a calculator in the exam; do not approximate blindly. --- ### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Spherical Wrist End Effectors: Mechanical** - **Spherical Wrist = R-R-R concurrent** — three revolute axes meeting at a *single* wrist center point. (3 DOF, pure orientation) - **Mechanical End Effector = the physical tool** — gripper / vacuum / magnet / tool holder, mounted on the wrist flange. - **Decoupling Principle:** Wrist handles *orientation*; arm handles *position*; end effector handles *action*. - **ZYZ Euler convention** is the standard spherical-wrist orientation parametrization in KTU. - **Gripper Mechanical Advantage:** $MA = \dfrac{\sin(\alpha_{\text{in}}) L_{\text{in}}}{\sin(\alpha_{\text{out}}) L_{\text{out}}}$ — design lever lengths to *amplify* or *attenuate* force. - **Two-Finger Payload:** $m_{\max} = \dfrac{2 F_{\text{tip}} \mu_f}{g}$ — the factor 2 comes from *two* friction contact points. - **Vacuum Lift Force:** $F_{\text{lift}} = \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n_{\text{cups}} \cdot \mu_s$ — pressure differential × cup area × safety factor. - **Magnetic Hold Force:** $F_{\text{hold}} = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2 \mu_0} k_{\text{gap}}$ — proportional to *flux density squared* and contact area. - **TCP (Tool Center Point):** $\mathbf{p}_{\text{TCP}} = \mathbf{p}_{\text{flange}} + \mathbf{R}_{\text{flange}} \mathbf{d}_{\text{tool}}$ — must be explicitly defined in robot programs. - **DOF via Kutzbach–Grübler:** $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ — for a 6-DOF industrial arm, $n=7, j_1=6, j_2=0 \Rightarrow M=6$. - **Wrist singularity** occurs when $\det(\mathbf{J}_{\text{wrist}}) = 0$ — usually at gimbal-lock configurations ($\theta = 0$ or $\pi$ in ZYZ). - **Selection Criteria for End Effector:** Workpiece material, weight, geometry, fragility, and required cycle time. - **Industrial Standards:** ISO 9409 defines mechanical flange dimensions for tool changers. - **Typical Pressures:** Pneumatic actuators operate at **4–8 bar**; vacuum cups at **0.3–0.7 atm** absolute pressure. - **Friction Coefficients (typical):** Rubber–steel $\approx 0.5$–$0.8$; rubber–glass $\approx 0.4$–$0.6$. - **Standard Grip Forces:** Industrial grippers span **10 N to 1000 N** depending on payload class.

topics.title: Magnetic and Vacuum Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Magnetic and Vacuum Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 End Effectors & Grippers — Setting the Context

The **end effector** is the device mounted on the robot's wrist (the terminal link of the manipulator) that physically interacts with the workpiece. Among mechanical, magnetic, vacuum, adhesive, and specialized grippers, the **magnetic** and **vacuum** grippers belong to the class of **non-prehensile / surface-contact end effectors** — they do not physically grasp the part; instead, they hold it via field forces or pressure differentials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Gripper selection is fundamentally driven by the *workpiece geometry, material, surface condition, and required payload*. Magnetic and vacuum grippers dominate **high-speed material transfer** applications where mechanical jaw closure is either impossible or too slow.

---

## 1.2 Magnetic Gripper — Formal Definition

A **magnetic gripper** is an end effector that holds a ferromagnetic workpiece by establishing a **magnetic flux path** through the part. The attractive force between the magnet's pole faces and the workpiece generates the holding action.

### Types of Magnetic Grippers

| Type | Power Required | Residual Magnetism | Fail-Safe |
|------|----------------|--------------------|-----------|
| **Permanent Magnet (PM)** | No | **Yes** (always present) | Naturally holds during power loss |
| **Electromagnet (EM)** | Yes (DC) | No (releases on power off) | Drops load on power loss |
| **Hybrid / Electro-Permanent (EPM)** | Pulse only | Switchable | Hold without power, release with reverse pulse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a magnetic gripper as a *fridge magnet* — but scaled up, with controlled pole geometry, and engineered to lift steel plates. The electromagnet version is like a *solenoid valve* — energise to attract, de-energise to release.

### Key Operational Characteristics

- **Force generation:** Magnetic attraction across **air gaps** between pole face and workpiece.
- **Limitation:** Works **only on ferromagnetic materials** (carbon steel, iron, nickel, cobalt). Non-magnetic stainless steel, aluminium, copper, and plastics **cannot** be handled.
- **Typical air gap tolerance:** 0.1 mm to 1.0 mm (surface roughness and paint reduce holding force dramatically).
- **Operating temperature:** Rare-earth (NdFeB) magnets lose magnetism above **80 °C**; AlNiCo variants withstand up to **500 °C**.

---

## 1.3 Vacuum Gripper — Formal Definition

A **vacuum gripper** is an end effector that holds a workpiece by creating a **pressure differential** between the atmospheric pressure acting on the outer surface of the part and a reduced pressure (partial vacuum) generated beneath one or more **suction cups** sealed against the part surface.

### Components of a Vacuum Gripper System

1. **Vacuum source:** Venturi ejector (compressed air driven) or electric vacuum pump.
2. **Suction cups:** Elastomer (silicone, nitrile, polyurethane) cups of varying profiles.
3. **Distribution manifold:** Splits vacuum to multiple cups.
4. **Filters and silencers:** Prevent contamination and reduce exhaust noise.
5. **Vacuum switch / sensor:** Confirms part pickup before robot motion.

### Types of Suction Cups

- **Flat cups:** Smooth, flat, rigid surfaces (glass, sheet metal).
- **Bellows cups (1.5x, 2.5x stroke):** Compensate for height variation and slight surface curvature.
- **Oval cups:** Long, narrow parts (cardboard boxes, aluminium extrusions).
- **Universal cups (ball-joint):** Slightly curved or angled surfaces (up to ±15° tilt compensation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** A vacuum gripper works exactly like a **plunger used to unclog a sink**. Push the cup against a smooth surface, squeeze out the air, and atmospheric pressure on the other side pushes the cup (and the object) firmly against itself. The harder you try to pull it off, the tighter it sticks.

### Key Operational Characteristics

- **Force generation:** $\Delta P \times A_{\text{eff}}$, where $\Delta P$ is the pressure differential and $A_{\text{eff}}$ is the effective sealed area.
- **Limitation:** Requires **smooth, non-porous, clean surfaces**. Porous materials (wood, concrete, rough cardboard) leak and lose vacuum rapidly.
- **Atmospheric pressure reference:** **101.325 kPa** = **1.01325 × 10⁵ Pa** at standard sea-level conditions.
- **Typical achievable vacuum:** −60 kPa to −80 kPa gauge (i.e., absolute pressure of 21–41 kPa inside the cup).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Magnetic Gripper — Theory of Operation

### 2.1.1 Magnetic Circuit Analogy

The gripper + air gap + workpiece form a **magnetic circuit** analogous to an electrical circuit:

| Electrical Analogue | Magnetic Equivalent |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| EMF (Volts) | MMF (Ampere-turns, NI) |
| Current (A) | Flux $\Phi$ (Weber, Wb) |
| Resistance $R$ | Reluctance $\mathcal{R}$ (At/Wb) |
| Ohm's Law $V = IR$ | Hopkinson's Law $\text{MMF} = \Phi \mathcal{R}$ |

> **Hopkinson's Law:** 
> $$\Phi = \frac{\text{MMF}}{\mathcal{R}} = \frac{NI}{\mathcal{R}}$$
> where $N$ is the number of coil turns and $I$ is the coil current.

### 2.1.2 Why the Magnet Pulls the Workpiece

The holding force arises because the **air gap** stores magnetic field energy. The system constantly tries to minimise this stored energy by **closing the air gap** — i.e., pulling the workpiece toward the magnet.

**Energy stored in an air gap of volume** $V_g = A \cdot g$:
$$W_{\text{gap}} = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot V_g = \frac{B^2 A g}{2\mu_0}$$

The attractive force is the negative gradient of stored energy w.r.t. gap length:
$$F = -\frac{dW_{\text{gap}}}{dg} = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

For a **two-pole magnetic circuit** (typical horseshoe or dual-pad gripper), there are **two air gaps in series**, so the total holding force is:
$$F_{\text{total}} = 2 \times \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0} = \frac{B^2 A}{\mu_0}$$

### 2.1.3 Shear Force vs. Pull (Peel) Force

| Force Mode | Description | Magnitude (typical ratio to pull) |
|------------|-------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Pull (Normal)** | Magnet perpendicular to surface, lifting straight up | $F_{\text{pull}}$ — **maximum** |
| **Shear** | Magnet sliding along surface (lateral load) | $F_{\text{shear}} \approx 0.2 \text{ to } 0.4 \, F_{\text{pull}}$ |
| **Peel** | Edge loading, lifting one corner | $F_{\text{peel}} \approx 0.1 \, F_{\text{pull}}$ — **minimum** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Implication:** Always apply a **safety factor of 4:1** on pull force and **6:1 to 10:1** on shear/peel when sizing a magnetic gripper for a robot.

---

## 2.2 Vacuum Gripper — Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 Pressure Differential Principle

Atmospheric pressure pushes on every surface of the workpiece. When the air beneath the suction cup is evacuated, the pressure on the underside drops to $P_{\text{vac}}$. The net upward force on the cup is:
$$F_{\text{theoretical}} = (P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}}) \cdot A_{\text{eff}}$$

### 2.2.2 Actual (Usable) Holding Force

Because the gripper typically does not hold by pure lifting but by **friction** with the workpiece resting against a backing plate (or by direct lifting if the cup is on top), we apply a friction coefficient:

$$F_{\text{usable}} = \mu \cdot F_{\text{theoretical}} = \mu \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = friction coefficient between cup elastomer and workpiece (typical: **0.5 – 0.8** for rubber-on-steel).
- $\Delta P$ = vacuum level in kPa (gauge).
- $A_{\text{eff}}$ = effective sealed cup area.

### 2.2.3 Vacuum Pump Performance Parameters

Two engineering parameters must be matched to the application:

1. **Vacuum level (suction):** Maximum $\Delta P$ achievable (kPa or mbar).
2. **Suction capacity (air flow):** Volume of air the pump can evacuate per unit time (Nl/min). Needed to overcome leaks and refill cups quickly during cycle operations.

### 2.2.4 Cycle Time & Time Constant

The vacuum level builds up **exponentially**:
$$P(t) = P_{\text{atm}} - (P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{final}}) \cdot (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$$

where the **pneumatic time constant** is:
$$\tau = \frac{V_{\text{system}}}{Q_{\text{pump}}}$$

with $V_{\text{system}}$ = total volume (cup + lines), $Q_{\text{pump}}$ = pump suction flow rate.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### Magnetic Gripper Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_{\text{pull, 1-gap}} = \dfrac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ | Pull force per air gap | N |
| 2 | $F_{\text{pull, total}} = \dfrac{B^2 A}{\mu_0}$ | Total pull force, 2-pole gripper | N |
| 3 | $F_{\text{shear}} \approx (0.2 \text{ to } 0.4) \cdot F_{\text{pull}}$ | Lateral holding capacity | N |
| 4 | $\Phi = \dfrac{NI}{\mathcal{R}}$ | Hopkinson's Law (magnetic Ohm's law) | Wb |
| 5 | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | Reluctance of a uniform flux path | At/Wb |
| 6 | $W_{\text{stored}} = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot V$ | Magnetic energy density in air gap | J |

> **Critical Physical Constants (memorise for exams):**
> - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m} \approx 1.2566 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{H/m}$
> - $\mu_r$ for soft iron: **5000 – 10000**; for vacuum/air: **1**

### Vacuum Gripper Formulas

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $F_{\text{theoretical}} = \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}}$ | Pressure-differential lift force | N |
| 2 | $F_{\text{usable}} = \mu \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}}$ | Friction-limited holding force | N |
| 3 | $\Delta P = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}}$ | Vacuum level (gauge) | Pa |
| 4 | $A_{\text{eff}} = \pi \cdot \dfrac{d^2}{4}$ | Area for circular cup, dia. $d$ | m² |
| 5 | $F_{\text{safe}} = \dfrac{F_{\text{usable}}}{\text{SF}}$ | Design force with safety factor | N |
| 6 | $t_{90\%} \approx 2.3 \, \tau$ | Time to reach 90 % vacuum | s |
| 7 | $P(t) = P_{\text{atm}} - \Delta P_{\text{max}} (1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ | Vacuum buildup curve | Pa |

> **Standard Safety Factors (industry practice):**
> - Horizontal handling (shear): **SF = 4**
> - Vertical lifting (pull): **SF = 10**
> - High-acceleration motion (>2 g): **SF = 20**

### Comparison: Magnetic vs. Vacuum Grippers

| Parameter | Magnetic Gripper | Vacuum Gripper |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| Material compatibility | Ferromagnetic only | Smooth, non-porous only |
| Surface roughness tolerance | Moderate (≤0.5 mm) | Low (≤0.1 mm) |
| Speed of actuation | Fast (ms) | Moderate (depends on pump) |
| Energy requirement | EM: continuous; PM: none | Continuous compressed air / pump power |
| Force density (N/cm²) | High (10–30) | Moderate (3–8) |
| Cleanliness | Excellent (no contact fluid) | Excellent |
| Typical applications | Steel sheet, billets, stamping | Glass, PCB, packaging, cardboard |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Derivation — Magnetic Gripper Pull Force from Energy Method

We derive the magnetic pull force starting from the energy stored in the air gap of a magnetic circuit.

### Step 1 — Define the System Geometry
Consider a two-pole (horseshoe) electromagnet gripping a ferromagnetic workpiece. The magnetic flux crosses **two air gaps**, each of length $g$ and cross-sectional area $A$.

### Step 2 — Energy Density in an Air Gap
For a uniform magnetic field of flux density $B$ in a non-magnetic medium ($\mu_r = 1$), the stored magnetic energy per unit volume is:

$$\begin{aligned}
u &= \frac{1}{2} B \cdot H \\
  &= \frac{1}{2} B \cdot \frac{B}{\mu_0} \\
  &= \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}
\end{aligned}$$

The first equality is the definition of magnetic co-energy density. The second applies the constitutive relation $B = \mu_0 H$ for air/vacuum.

### Step 3 — Total Energy in One Air Gap
Multiply the energy density by the gap volume $V_g = A \cdot g$:

$$W_{\text{one gap}} = u \cdot V_g = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot A g$$

### Step 4 — Force as Negative Gradient of Energy
The magnetic force acts to **reduce** the gap. By the principle of virtual work, the force is:

$$F_{\text{one gap}} = -\frac{dW_{\text{one gap}}}{dg} = -\frac{d}{dg}\left( \frac{B^2 A g}{2\mu_0} \right) = -\frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

The negative sign indicates that the force is **attractive** (directed to close the gap). In magnitude:

$$F_{\text{one gap}} = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

### Step 5 — Account for Two Gaps in Series
A horseshoe gripper has two gaps (one at each pole). Total attractive force:

$$F_{\text{total}} = 2 \times \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0} = \frac{B^2 A}{\mu_0}$$

### Step 6 — Final Compact Result

$$\boxed{F_{\text{pull}} = \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{\mu_0}}$$

> **Insight:** Because force scales with the **square** of $B$, doubling the flux density **quadruples** the holding force. This is why rare-earth magnets with $B_r > 1.2$ T are preferred despite their high cost.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Vacuum Gripper Holding Force from Pressure Differential

### Step 1 — Pressure Distribution on the Workpiece
Atmospheric air exerts a uniform pressure $P_{\text{atm}}$ on all external surfaces of the workpiece. Underneath the suction cup, the air has been evacuated to a reduced pressure $P_{\text{vac}}$.

### Step 2 — Net Upward Force on the Sealed Cup
The pressure acting on the cup's effective area is the **difference** between atmospheric pressure and vacuum pressure:

$$F_{\text{lift}} = (P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}}) \cdot A_{\text{eff}} = \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to Practical Engineering Form
Let $V_{\text{gauge}}$ denote the **vacuum level in kPa (gauge)** — the value typically read from a vacuum gauge. Then:

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}} \approx V_{\text{gauge}}$$

(In practice, $V_{\text{gauge}}$ is the difference between local atmospheric pressure and the absolute pressure inside the cup; for engineering use, we set $P_{\text{atm}} \approx 101.3 \, \text{kPa}$ and $V_{\text{gauge}} = P_{\text{atm}} - P_{\text{vac}}$.)

### Step 4 — Account for Friction (Horizontal Handling)
When the workpiece rests against a backing surface and is held by friction, the effective horizontal holding force is reduced by the friction coefficient $\mu$:

$$F_{\text{hold, horiz}} = \mu \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}}$$

For **vertical lifting** (cup on top of part), the lifting force is essentially the full pressure differential force, but a much higher safety factor is applied because the load is fully supported by suction alone.

### Step 5 — Final Compact Result

$$\boxed{F_{\text{usable}} = \mu \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n}$$

where $n$ = number of suction cups used in parallel.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Magnetic Gripper Sizing (KTU-style 7-mark problem)

> **Problem Statement:** A two-pole electromagnet gripper has a pole-face area of $25 \, \text{cm}^2$ per pole. The flux density at each pole face is $B = 0.45 \, \text{T}$. Calculate:
> (a) The theoretical pull force.
> (b) The safe lifting capacity with a safety factor of 4.

### Given Data
- $A = 25 \, \text{cm}^2 = 25 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2 = 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$
- $B = 0.45 \, \text{T}$
- $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$
- Number of air gaps = 2
- Safety Factor (SF) = 4

### Solution (a) — Theoretical Pull Force

Using the derived formula for a two-pole gripper:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{pull}} &= \frac{B^2 \cdot A}{\mu_0} \\
&= \frac{(0.45)^2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-3})}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{0.2025 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{5.0625 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 402.9 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

### Solution (b) — Safe Lifting Capacity

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{safe}} &= \frac{F_{\text{pull}}}{\text{SF}} \\
&= \frac{402.9}{4} \\
&\approx 100.7 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to equivalent mass (using $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$):

$$m_{\text{safe}} = \frac{F_{\text{safe}}}{g} = \frac{100.7}{9.81} \approx 10.27 \, \text{kg}$$

> **Answer:** Theoretical pull force ≈ **402.9 N**; Safe lifting capacity ≈ **100.7 N** (≈ 10.3 kg).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Vacuum Gripper Sizing (KTU-style 7-mark problem)

> **Problem Statement:** A vacuum gripper uses **2 circular suction cups**, each of diameter **60 mm**, on a smooth steel plate (friction coefficient $\mu = 0.65$). The vacuum pump achieves **70 kPa (gauge)**. Calculate:
> (a) The theoretical lifting force.
> (b) The usable holding force (horizontal handling) and safe payload with SF = 4.

### Given Data
- Cup diameter: $d = 60 \, \text{mm} = 0.06 \, \text{m}$
- Vacuum level: $\Delta P = 70 \, \text{kPa} = 70 \times 10^3 \, \text{Pa}$
- Friction coefficient: $\mu = 0.65$
- Number of cups: $n = 2$
- SF = 4

### Solution (a) — Theoretical Lift Force Per Cup

Effective area of one cup:

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{eff}} &= \pi \cdot \frac{d^2}{4} \\
&= \pi \cdot \frac{(0.06)^2}{4} \\
&= \pi \cdot \frac{0.0036}{4} \\
&= \pi \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} \\
&\approx 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Theoretical lifting force per cup:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{lift, 1 cup}} &= \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \\
&= (70 \times 10^3) \times (2.827 \times 10^{-3}) \\
&\approx 197.9 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

Total theoretical force for 2 cups:

$$F_{\text{lift, total}} = 2 \times 197.9 \approx 395.8 \, \text{N}$$

### Solution (b) — Usable Holding Force & Safe Payload

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{usable}} &= \mu \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n \\
&= 0.65 \times 70 \times 10^3 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \\
&= 0.65 \times 197.9 \times 2 \\
&\approx 257.3 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

Safe payload with SF = 4:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{safe}} &= \frac{F_{\text{usable}}}{\text{SF}} = \frac{257.3}{4} \approx 64.3 \, \text{N} \\
m_{\text{safe}} &= \frac{64.3}{9.81} \approx 6.55 \, \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Answer:** Theoretical lift = **395.8 N**; Usable horizontal holding force = **257.3 N**; Safe payload ≈ **6.55 kg**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to multiply by the **number of cups** $n$, OR confusing gauge vacuum with absolute pressure. Always check whether the problem gives $\Delta P$ as **gauge** or **absolute**. In KTU problems, it is almost always **gauge** unless stated otherwise.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Magnetic Gripper — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    dcSource[DC Power Supply Unit] --> coilEnergise[Energising Coil N Turns]
    coilEnergise --> coreUp[Soft Iron Upper Limb]
    coreUp --> poleUp[Upper Pole Face]
    poleUp --> gap1[Air Gap 1 Length g]
    gap1 --> work[Ferromagnetic Workpiece]
    work --> gap2[Air Gap 2 Length g]
    gap2 --> poleLow[Lower Pole Face]
    poleLow --> coreLow[Soft Iron Lower Limb]
    coreLow --> coreUp
    coilEnergise --> fluxSense[Flux Density Sensor B]
    fluxSense --> ctrlUnit[Control and Monitoring Unit]
    dcSource --> ctrlUnit
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The arrows denote the **magnetic flux path** (Hopkinson's circuit). The coil acts as the **MMF source**; the iron core and workpiece complete the low-reluctance path; the air gaps are where energy is stored and force is generated.

---

## 4.2 Vacuum Gripper — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    airSupply[Compressed Air Supply 5 to 6 bar] --> ejector[Venturi Ejector Unit]
    ejector --> manifold[Vacuum Manifold Reservoir]
    manifold --> filter[Inline Particulate Filter]
    filter --> cupA[Suction Cup A]
    filter --> cupB[Suction Cup B]
    filter --> cupC[Suction Cup C]
    cupA --> surfaceA[Workpiece Surface A]
    cupB --> surfaceA
    cupC --> surfaceA
    ejector --> exhaust[Exhaust to Atmosphere with Silencer]
    manifold --> pressureSwitch[Vacuum Pressure Switch]
    pressureSwitch --> controller[PLC Robot Controller]
    controller --> airSupply
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Compressed air drives the **Venturi ejector**, which generates vacuum by the Bernoulli principle. The vacuum is distributed via the manifold to multiple cups. A **pressure switch** confirms adequate vacuum before the robot is allowed to move — this is a critical safety interlock in industrial deployments.

---

## 4.3 Gripper Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[New Gripper Selection Task] --> q1{Workpiece Material?}
    q1 -->|Ferromagnetic Steel Iron| magBranch[Magnetic Gripper Path]
    q1 -->|Smooth Non Porous| vacBranch[Vacuum Gripper Path]
    q1 -->|Other Material| mechBranch[Mechanical Adaptive Gripper]
    magBranch --> q2{Continuous Power Available?}
    q2 -->|Yes| emType[Electromagnet Type]
    q2 -->|No Fail Safe Required| pmType[Permanent Magnet Type]
    q2 -->|Pulse Only| epmType[Electro Permanent Hybrid]
    vacBranch --> q3{Surface Geometry}
    q3 -->|Flat Rigid| flatType[Flat Round Suction Cup]
    q3 -->|Curved or Rough| bellowsType[Bellows 1.5x or 2.5x Cup]
    q3 -->|Long Narrow| ovalType[Oval Suction Cup]
```

> **Use this flowchart** to systematically justify gripper selection in KTU design problems (commonly asked in Part B questions worth 7 marks).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a permanent magnet gripper and an electromagnet gripper. State one application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Permanent Magnet (PM) Gripper | Electromagnet (EM) Gripper |
|---------|-------------------------------|----------------------------|
| Power required | **None** — always magnetic | **DC supply required** to generate field |
| Release mechanism | Requires **mechanical stripping** or active demagnetising coil | De-energise coil — field collapses |
| Fail-safe behaviour | **Holds load** during power loss | **Drops load** on power failure |
| Residual magnetism | Always present (may interfere with subsequent machining) | Negligible on release |
| Typical application | Steel scrap yards, stamping presses, fail-safe lifting | Machine tending, fast pick-and-place where clean release is needed |

**Preferred application:**
- **PM:** Lifting ferromagnetic plates in **press-shop automation** where power failure must not drop hot steel.
- **EM:** **CNC machine tending** of small steel billets where precise ON/OFF control is needed.

---

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**List any four factors that affect the holding force of a vacuum gripper.**

**Model Answer (4 × 0.75 = 3 marks):**

1. **Vacuum level ($\Delta P$):** Higher evacuation → greater pressure differential → higher holding force. Limited by atmospheric pressure (101.3 kPa).
2. **Effective sealed area ($A_{\text{eff}}$):** Larger cup diameter → larger area → higher force. Force scales linearly with $A_{\text{eff}}$.
3. **Surface condition:** Rough, dirty, or porous surfaces reduce effective sealed area and introduce leakage.
4. **Friction coefficient ($\mu$):** Determined by the cup elastomer material and the workpiece surface finish (smooth rubber-on-steel ≈ 0.5–0.8).
5. *(Bonus, if asked for more)* **Number of cups in parallel** and **safety factor** applied during sizing.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Attempt either **Question A** OR **Question B**. Each contains sub-parts **(a)** 7 marks and **(b)** 7 marks.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the pull force of a two-pole magnetic gripper in terms of flux density $B$, pole area $A$, and permeability of free space $\mu_0$. Explain the role of the air gap. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The pull force arises from magnetic energy stored in the air gaps between the pole faces and the ferromagnetic workpiece.

**Step 1 — Energy density in air gap:**
For an air gap, $B = \mu_0 H$, so the stored energy density is:
$$u = \frac{1}{2} B \cdot H = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$$

**Step 2 — Total energy in one gap of volume $V = Ag$:**
$$W_{\text{gap}} = \frac{B^2}{2\mu_0} \cdot A g$$

**Step 3 — Force from virtual work:**
The attractive force equals the rate of change of stored energy with respect to gap length:
$$F_{\text{one gap}} = \frac{dW}{dg} = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$$

**Step 4 — Two-pole total force:**
Since a horseshoe gripper has two gaps in series:
$$\boxed{F_{\text{total}} = \frac{B^2 A}{\mu_0}}$$

**Role of air gap:** The air gap is the **non-magnetic region** between the pole face and the workpiece. It is the **site of energy storage** and the **mechanism of force generation** — without an air gap (perfect contact), no force is transmitted; but a **larger gap reduces force** quadratically. Surface roughness effectively enlarges the air gap and dramatically reduces holding capacity.

**(b)** A magnetic gripper with two poles of area $30 \, \text{cm}^2$ each operates at $B = 0.5 \, \text{T}$ at the pole faces. If the workpiece has a vertical lift requirement of 80 N (including safety), determine whether the gripper is suitable. Use $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $A = 30 \, \text{cm}^2 = 30 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^2$, $B = 0.5 \, \text{T}$, $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$, $F_{\text{req}} = 80 \, \text{N}$ (already includes safety factor).

**Step 1 — Theoretical pull force (two-pole formula):**
$$\begin{aligned}
F_{\text{pull}} &= \frac{B^2 A}{\mu_0} \\
&= \frac{(0.5)^2 \times (30 \times 10^{-4})}{4\pi \times 10^{-7}} \\
&= \frac{0.25 \times 30 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&= \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2566 \times 10^{-6}} \\
&\approx 596.8 \, \text{N}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply safety factor SF = 4 (vertical lifting):**
$$F_{\text{safe}} = \frac{596.8}{4} \approx 149.2 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with requirement:**
$$F_{\text{safe}} = 149.2 \, \text{N} \;\gt\; F_{\text{req}} = 80 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
✅ **The gripper is suitable.** It has a safe lifting capacity of **149.2 N**, which exceeds the 80 N requirement by a comfortable margin of approximately **1.86×**.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of a vacuum gripper system. List four selection criteria for choosing a vacuum cup. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle:**
A vacuum gripper system (refer to Section 4.2 diagram) operates on the principle of **pressure differential**. Compressed air is passed through a **Venturi ejector**, which accelerates the air and creates a low-pressure region by the Bernoulli effect. This low pressure is distributed through a manifold to multiple **suction cups** that are pressed against the workpiece. Atmospheric pressure acting on the opposite side of the workpiece pushes it against the cups, generating the holding force:
$$F = \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot \mu \cdot n$$

A **vacuum pressure switch** confirms that adequate vacuum has been achieved before the robot initiates motion — this is a critical safety interlock.

**Selection Criteria for Vacuum Cups (4 points):**

1. **Surface profile compatibility:** Flat cups for rigid flat surfaces; bellows cups for curved or slightly rough surfaces; oval cups for long narrow parts.
2. **Material compatibility:** Silicone for high-temperature parts; nitrile (NBR) for general industrial use; polyurethane (PU) for high friction and oil resistance.
3. **Diameter and effective area:** Sized based on required holding force and allowable payload, factoring in the achievable vacuum level.
4. **Stroke / compliance:** Bellows cups (1.5x or 2.5x stroke) for parts with height variation or robot placement tolerances.
5. *(Bonus)* **Chemical and temperature resistance** of the elastomer for the specific production environment.

**(b)** A vacuum gripper with a single circular cup of diameter 80 mm achieves a vacuum of 60 kPa (gauge). The friction coefficient between the cup and the smooth aluminium sheet is 0.5. Calculate the safe horizontal holding force using a safety factor of 4. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $d = 80 \, \text{mm} = 0.08 \, \text{m}$, $\Delta P = 60 \, \text{kPa} = 60 \times 10^3 \, \text{Pa}$, $\mu = 0.5$, $n = 1$, SF = 4.

**Step 1 — Effective cup area:**
$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{eff}} &= \frac{\pi d^2}{4} \\
&= \frac{\pi \times (0.08)^2}{4} \\
&= \frac{\pi \times 0.0064}{4} \\
&= 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical lifting force:**
$$F_{\text{lift}} = \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} = (60 \times 10^3) \times (5.027 \times 10^{-3}) \approx 301.6 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 3 — Usable horizontal holding force:**
$$F_{\text{usable}} = \mu \cdot F_{\text{lift}} = 0.5 \times 301.6 \approx 150.8 \, \text{N}$$

**Step 4 — Safe holding force with SF = 4:**
$$\boxed{F_{\text{safe}} = \frac{150.8}{4} \approx 37.7 \, \text{N}}$$

**Equivalent safe mass:**
$$m_{\text{safe}} = \frac{37.7}{9.81} \approx 3.84 \, \text{kg}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on Gripper Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 (or dividing by $2\mu_0$ vs. $\mu_0$):** Many students write $F = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$ for a **two-pole** gripper and lose 2 marks. **Always clarify** whether the question is for one gap or two gaps (series).
>
> 2. **Mixing up gauge and absolute pressure:** A vacuum gauge typically reads **gauge** pressure. Students who mistakenly use $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.3 \, \text{kPa}$ as $\Delta P$ instead of the stated $60$ or $70$ kPa will get answers inflated by ~30–40 %. Always use the **value given in the problem**.
>
> 3. **Skipping the safety factor:** The KTU marking scheme explicitly awards **1 mark** for applying the safety factor and stating its value. Writing only the theoretical force without safety factorisation costs this easy mark.
>
> 4. **Unit errors:** $\mu_0$ must be in **H/m** (= T·m/A), area in **m²**, B in **T** (not mT or kT) — otherwise the unit conversion eats 1–2 marks.
>
> 5. **Not mentioning air gap effects:** A 2-line discussion of how surface roughness and paint coatings effectively **enlarge the air gap** and **reduce force** is required for full marks on magnetic gripper problems.
>
> 6. **Ignoring the cup elastomer material:** In vacuum gripper selection questions, the choice of **silicone vs. nitrile vs. polyurethane** is often the difference between 5 and 7 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Magnetic Gripper Pull Force (two-pole):** $\displaystyle F = \frac{B^2 A}{\mu_0}$
- **One-gap magnetic force:** $\displaystyle F = \frac{B^2 A}{2\mu_0}$
- **Force scales with $B^2$:** Doubling flux density quadruples the holding force.
- **Shear force is only 20–40 % of pull force:** Always use SF ≥ 6 for shear applications.
- **Permanent magnets hold during power loss (fail-safe); electromagnets release on power-off.**
- **Vacuum force:** $\displaystyle F = \mu \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n$
- **$\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m; $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325$ kPa.**
- **Standard safety factors:** Horizontal = 4, Vertical = 10, High-acceleration = 20.
- **Hopkinson's Law:** $\Phi = \dfrac{NI}{\mathcal{R}}$ — analogue of $V = IR$.
- **Reluctance:** $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ — analogue of resistance.
- **Vacuum gauges read gauge pressure** by default — interpret $\Delta P$ carefully.
- **Suction cup selection:** Flat (rigid flat), Bellows (curved/varied height), Oval (long narrow parts).
- **Typical achievable vacuum:** −60 to −80 kPa gauge (≈ 20–40 kPa absolute).
- **Cycle time bottleneck:** $\tau = V_{\text{system}} / Q_{\text{pump}}$; reaches 90 % in $2.3\tau$.
- **Magnetic grippers = ferromagnetic only; Vacuum grippers = smooth non-porous only.**
- **Energy density in air gap:** $u = \dfrac{B^2}{2\mu_0}$ J/m³.
- **Electro-Permanent (EPM) grippers** combine the fail-safe holding of PM with the clean release of EM — premium industrial choice.

### 🎯 Highest-Yield Exam Facts (memorise these)

1. The two-pole magnetic pull formula $\dfrac{B^2 A}{\mu_0}$.
2. The vacuum gripper formula $\mu \cdot \Delta P \cdot A_{\text{eff}} \cdot n$.
3. Physical constants $\mu_0$ and $P_{\text{atm}}$.
4. Safety factor values (4, 10, 20).
5. The distinction between permanent, electromagnet, and hybrid magnetic grippers.
6. The distinction between flat, bellows, and oval vacuum cups.
7. The use of vacuum pressure switches as safety interlocks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Active and Passive Grippers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Active and Passive Grippers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Gripper?

A **gripper** is the end-effector of a robotic manipulator, attached to the robot's wrist, used to grasp, hold, manipulate, and release objects (workpieces) during a manufacturing or assembly operation. It is the "hand" of the robot and is the only part of the manipulator that comes in direct physical contact with the workpiece.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A gripper is a mechanical device that enables a robot to pick up and hold objects. Grippers are broadly classified into **active (servo-controlled) grippers** and **passive (compliance-based) grippers** based on whether they actively control the gripping force or rely on the mechanical compliance of the workpiece/fixture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are asked to hold a raw egg:
- **Active Approach (Active Gripper):** You actively sense the pressure on your fingers using your skin's tactile sensors, and your brain continuously adjusts the grip force. If the egg starts slipping, you tighten; if it starts cracking, you loosen. This is **sensor-driven, force-feedback-based active control**.
- **Passive Approach (Passive Gripper):** You place the egg into a pre-shaped foam holder that cradles it based purely on the shape of the foam — no sensors, no feedback, just geometry. This is **mechanical compliance / shape-matching**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Distinction for KTU Exam:**
> - **Active Gripper** = Uses an external actuator (motor, pneumatic, hydraulic) + control loop to apply a controlled gripping force.
> - **Passive Gripper** = No external actuator. Gripping is achieved via **mechanical compliance**, gravity, or spring action (e.g., compliance fingers, Bernoulli grippers, soft grippers, vacuum cups).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Gripper Design

The following key parameters (in **bold**) are typically associated with gripper selection per KTU syllabus:

- **Gripping Force (F_g)**: The clamping force applied normal to the workpiece surface, typically measured in **Newtons (N)**.
- **Stroke Length (s)**: Maximum opening distance between the fingers, measured in **mm**.
- **Payload Capacity (W)**: Maximum weight the gripper can manipulate, measured in **kg** or **N**.
- **Jaw Width / Finger Length**: Geometric parameters in **mm**.
- **Closing Time (t_c)**: Time taken to fully close, in **seconds (s)**.
- **Repeatability (R)**: Positional accuracy, in **±mm**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Active Grippers — Operational Breakdown

**Active grippers** (also called **servo grippers** or **powered grippers**) use an external power source to actuate the fingers and a control system to regulate the gripping force.

#### 2.1.1 Classification of Active Grippers
1. **Pneumatic Grippers** — Driven by compressed air via pistons/diaphragms.
2. **Hydraulic Grippers** — Driven by pressurized hydraulic fluid (high force, used for heavy parts).
3. **Electric / Servo Grippers** — Driven by electric motors (DC, stepper, servo) with force-feedback.
4. **Magnetic Grippers (Electromagnetic)** — When electrically activated; counted as active.
5. **Vacuum Grippers (Motor-driven pump)** — When driven by an active vacuum pump.

#### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

1. **Command Input:** The robot controller sends a "Grip" signal with desired force/position.
2. **Actuation:** The actuator (piston/motor) converts the input energy (air/fluid/electricity) into linear or rotary mechanical motion.
3. **Transmission:** This motion is transmitted to the jaw mechanism via linkages (toggle, cam, rack-pinion, lead screw).
