- Element (1,2): $\cos\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot \cos\phi + \sin\theta \cdot \sin\phi = \sin\theta \sin\phi$
- Element (1,3): $\cos\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-\sin\phi) + \sin\theta \cdot \cos\phi = \sin\theta \cos\phi$

**Row 2 of result:**

- Element (2,1): $0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot 0 = 0$
- Element (2,2): $0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot \cos\phi + 0 \cdot \sin\phi = \cos\phi$
- Element (2,3): $0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot (-\sin\phi) + 0 \cdot \cos\phi = -\sin\phi$

**Row 3 of result:**

- Element (3,1): $-\sin\theta \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 0 + \cos\theta \cdot 0 = -\sin\theta$
- Element (3,2): $-\sin\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot \cos\phi + \cos\theta \cdot \sin\phi = \cos\theta \sin\phi$
- Element (3,3): $-\sin\theta \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-\sin\phi) + \cos\theta \cdot \cos\phi = \cos\theta \cos\phi$

So the intermediate matrix is:

$$R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \sin\phi & \sin\theta \cos\phi \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \sin\phi & \cos\theta \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

### Step 4 — Multiply $R_z(\psi)$ with the intermediate result

$$R_z(\psi) \cdot [R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)] = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \sin\phi & \sin\theta \cos\phi \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \sin\phi & \cos\theta \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Row 1 of final result:**

- (1,1): $\cos\psi \cdot \cos\theta + (-\sin\psi) \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-\sin\theta) = \cos\psi \cos\theta$
- (1,2): $\cos\psi \cdot \sin\theta \sin\phi + (-\sin\psi) \cdot \cos\phi + 0 \cdot \cos\theta \sin\phi = \cos\psi \sin\theta \sin\phi - \sin\psi \cos\phi$
- (1,3): $\cos\psi \cdot \sin\theta \cos\phi + (-\sin\psi) \cdot (-\sin\phi) + 0 \cdot \cos\theta \cos\phi = \cos\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi + \sin\psi \sin\phi$

**Row 2 of final result:**

- (2,1): $\sin\psi \cdot \cos\theta + \cos\psi \cdot 0 + 0 \cdot (-\sin\theta) = \sin\psi \cos\theta$
- (2,2): $\sin\psi \cdot \sin\theta \sin\phi + \cos\psi \cdot \cos\phi + 0 \cdot \cos\theta \sin\phi = \sin\psi \sin\theta \sin\phi + \cos\psi \cos\phi$
- (2,3): $\sin\psi \cdot \sin\theta \cos\phi + \cos\psi \cdot (-\sin\phi) + 0 \cdot \cos\theta \cos\phi = \sin\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi - \cos\psi \sin\phi$

**Row 3 of final result:**

- (3,1): $0 \cdot \cos\theta + 0 \cdot 0 + 1 \cdot (-\sin\theta) = -\sin\theta$
- (3,2): $0 \cdot \sin\theta \sin\phi + 0 \cdot \cos\phi + 1 \cdot \cos\theta \sin\phi = \cos\theta \sin\phi$
- (3,3): $0 \cdot \sin\theta \cos\phi + 0 \cdot (-\sin\phi) + 1 \cdot \cos\theta \cos\phi = \cos\theta \cos\phi$

### Step 5 — Final Z-Y-X Rotation Matrix

$$\boxed{R(\phi, \theta, \psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi \cos\theta & \cos\psi \sin\theta \sin\phi - \sin\psi \cos\phi & \cos\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi + \sin\psi \sin\phi \\ \sin\psi \cos\theta & \sin\psi \sin\theta \sin\phi + \cos\psi \cos\phi & \sin\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi - \cos\psi \sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \sin\phi & \cos\theta \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Property:** $\det(R) = +1$ and $R^T = R^{-1}$. Students should verify $\det(R) = 1$ using cofactor expansion in exam to score full marks.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Quaternion to Rotation Matrix Conversion

**Objective:** Given a unit quaternion $q = w + xi + yj + zk$, derive the 3×3 rotation matrix $R(q)$.

### Step 1 — Use the rotation operator on a pure quaternion

Let $\vec{v} = (v_x, v_y, v_z)$ be a vector in 3D space, expressed as a **pure quaternion** $v_q = 0 + v_x i + v_y j + v_z k$. The rotated vector $v_q'$ is given by:

$$v_q' = q \cdot v_q \cdot q^*$$

where $q^* = w - xi - yj - zk$ is the **conjugate**.

### Step 2 — Compute the product $q \cdot v_q$

$$q \cdot v_q = (w + xi + yj + zk)(0 + v_x i + v_y j + v_z k)$$

Expanding using the Hamilton product rule $i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = -1$, $ij = k$, $jk = i$, $ki = j$, $ji = -k$, $kj = -i$, $ik = -j$:

$$q \cdot v_q = (wv_x + yv_z - zv_y) + (xv_x i^2 \text{ terms}) + \cdots$$

Carrying out the full expansion (grouping scalar, $i$, $j$, $k$ components):

$$q \cdot v_q = \begin{bmatrix} -x v_x - y v_y - z v_z \\ w v_x + y v_z - z v_y \\ -x v_z + w v_y + y v_x \\ x v_y - y v_x + w v_z \end{bmatrix}^T$$

### Step 3 — Multiply by $q^*$ on the right

$$(q \cdot v_q) \cdot q^* = (q \cdot v_q) \cdot (w - xi - yj - zk)$$

After expanding and simplifying (a tedious but mechanical process), the **scalar part** vanishes (since the result must be a pure quaternion for a pure rotation), and the **vector part** gives the rotated vector coordinates. The 3×3 rotation matrix is then read off as the matrix that maps $\vec{v}$ to $\vec{v}'$:

### Step 4 — Final Quaternion to Rotation Matrix

$$\boxed{R(q) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 2(y^2 + z^2) & 2(xy - wz) & 2(xz + wy) \\ 2(xy + wz) & 1 - 2(x^2 + z^2) & 2(yz - wx) \\ 2(xz - wy) & 2(yz + wx) & 1 - 2(x^2 + y^2) \end{bmatrix}}$$

### Step 5 — Verification

- **Symmetry Check:** The matrix is **not** symmetric in general (off-diagonal terms satisfy $R_{12} = -R_{21}$ type relations when $w \neq 0$).
- **Identity at $q = (1, 0, 0, 0)$:** All four terms like $1 - 2(y^2 + z^2) = 1$, off-diagonal terms vanish, so $R = I$. ✓
- **Unit quaternion preserves norm:** $R^T R = I$. ✓

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Euler to Quaternion Conversion (Z-Y-X)

**Objective:** Given Euler angles $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ in Z-Y-X convention, derive the equivalent unit quaternion $q = (w, x, y, z)$.

### Step 1 — Represent each elementary rotation as a quaternion

A rotation by angle $\alpha$ about an axis $\hat{n}$ corresponds to:

$$q = \left(\cos\frac{\alpha}{2},\, \sin\frac{\alpha}{2}\, n_x,\, \sin\frac{\alpha}{2}\, n_y,\, \sin\frac{\alpha}{2}\, n_z\right)$$

Therefore:

$$q_x(\phi) = \left(\cos\frac{\phi}{2},\, \sin\frac{\phi}{2},\, 0,\, 0\right)$$

$$q_y(\theta) = \left(\cos\frac{\theta}{2},\, 0,\, \sin\frac{\theta}{2},\, 0\right)$$

$$q_z(\psi) = \left(\cos\frac{\psi}{2},\, 0,\, 0,\, \sin\frac{\psi}{2}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Compose via quaternion multiplication

$$q = q_z(\psi) \cdot q_y(\theta) \cdot q_x(\phi)$$

### Step 3 — Compute $q_y(\theta) \cdot q_x(\phi)$ first

Using the Hamilton product:

$$q_y \cdot q_x = \begin{bmatrix} c_\theta c_\phi \\ c_\theta s_\phi \\ s_\theta c_\phi \\ -s_\theta s_\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

where $c_\alpha = \cos(\alpha/2)$ and $s_\alpha = \sin(\alpha/2)$ for compactness.

### Step 4 — Multiply by $q_z(\psi)$

$$q = q_z \cdot (q_y \cdot q_x) = \begin{bmatrix} c_\psi & 0 & 0 & -s_\psi \\ 0 & c_\psi & s_\psi & 0 \\ 0 & -s_\psi & c_\psi & 0 \\ s_\psi & 0 & 0 & c_\psi \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} c_\theta c_\phi \\ c_\theta s_\phi \\ s_\theta c_\phi \\ -s_\theta s_\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing each component:

- **Scalar (w):** $c_\psi \cdot c_\theta c_\phi + 0 + 0 + (-s_\psi)(-s_\theta s_\phi) = c_\psi c_\theta c_\phi + s_\psi s_\theta s_\phi$
- **i-component (x):** $0 \cdot c_\theta c_\phi + c_\psi \cdot c_\theta s_\phi + s_\psi \cdot s_\theta c_\phi + 0 = c_\psi c_\theta s_\phi + s_\psi s_\theta c_\phi$
- **j-component (y):** $0 + (-s_\psi) \cdot c_\theta s_\phi + c_\psi \cdot s_\theta c_\phi + 0 = c_\psi s_\theta c_\phi - s_\psi c_\theta s_\phi$
- **k-component (z):** $s_\psi \cdot c_\theta c_\phi + 0 + 0 + c_\psi \cdot (-s_\theta s_\phi) = s_\psi c_\theta c_\phi - c_\psi s_\theta s_\phi$

### Step 5 — Final Euler to Quaternion Formula (Z-Y-X)

$$\boxed{\begin{aligned} w &= \cos\frac{\psi}{2}\cos\frac{\theta}{2}\cos\frac{\phi}{2} + \sin\frac{\psi}{2}\sin\frac{\theta}{2}\sin\frac{\phi}{2} \\ x &= \cos\frac{\psi}{2}\cos\frac{\theta}{2}\sin\frac{\phi}{2} - \sin\frac{\psi}{2}\sin\frac{\theta}{2}\cos\frac{\phi}{2} \\ y &= \cos\frac{\psi}{2}\sin\frac{\theta}{2}\cos\frac{\phi}{2} + \sin\frac{\psi}{2}\cos\frac{\theta}{2}\sin\frac{\phi}{2} \\ z &= \sin\frac{\psi}{2}\cos\frac{\theta}{2}\cos\frac{\phi}{2} - \cos\frac{\psi}{2}\sin\frac{\theta}{2}\sin\frac{\phi}{2} \end{aligned}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** If $\phi = \theta = \psi = 0$, then $\sin(\cdot) = 0$ and $\cos(\cdot) = 1$, giving $(w, x, y, z) = (1, 0, 0, 0)$, which is the identity quaternion. ✓

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem:** A robot end-effector is oriented at Z-Y-X Euler angles $\phi = 30°$, $\theta = 45°$, $\psi = 60°$. Compute the equivalent quaternion.

**Solution:**

Half-angles: $\phi/2 = 15°$, $\theta/2 = 22.5°$, $\psi/2 = 30°$

$$\cos(15°) \approx 0.9659, \quad \sin(15°) \approx 0.2588$$
$$\cos(22.5°) \approx 0.9239, \quad \sin(22.5°) \approx 0.3827$$
$$\cos(30°) \approx 0.8660, \quad \sin(30°) = 0.5$$

Substituting into the formula:

$$w = (0.8660)(0.9239)(0.9659) + (0.5)(0.3827)(0.2588) \approx 0.7729 + 0.0495 \approx 0.8224$$
$$x = (0.8660)(0.9239)(0.2588) - (0.5)(0.3827)(0.9659) \approx 0.2071 - 0.1848 \approx 0.0223$$
$$y = (0.8660)(0.3827)(0.9659) + (0.5)(0.9239)(0.2588) \approx 0.3201 + 0.1195 \approx 0.4397$$
$$z = (0.5)(0.9239)(0.9659) - (0.8660)(0.3827)(0.2588) \approx 0.4462 - 0.0858 \approx 0.3604$$

**Verification:** $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 \approx 0.6763 + 0.0005 + 0.1933 + 0.1299 \approx 1.0000$ ✓

$$\boxed{q \approx (0.8224,\ 0.0223,\ 0.4397,\ 0.3604)}$$

## 4.3 Gimbal Lock Phenomenon — Functional Architecture

Gimbal lock is best illustrated functionally rather than geometrically. The block diagram below shows how the loss of a degree of freedom occurs at the singularity.


> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the singularity:** **"Pitch at 90°, Yaw and Roll merge."** Whenever the middle rotation (pitch $\theta$) equals $\pm 90°$, the first and third rotation axes become collinear, mathematically eliminating one rotational degree of freedom.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Euler angles. State the Z-Y-X (yaw–pitch–roll) convention and explain why three angles are sufficient to describe any orientation in 3D space.**

**Model Answer:**

Euler angles are a set of **three angular parameters** $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ that uniquely specify the orientation of a rigid body in 3D space relative to a reference frame. The Z-Y-X convention, also called yaw–pitch–roll, applies three successive intrinsic rotations:

- **$\psi$ (yaw)** — rotation about the base Z-axis
- **$\theta$ (pitch)** — rotation about the new (intermediate) Y-axis
- **$\phi$ (roll)** — rotation about the new X-axis

**Why three angles suffice:** A rigid body in 3D space possesses exactly **3 rotational degrees of freedom** (independent of its 3 translational DOF). Since each Euler angle parameterizes one independent rotation about a different axis, the three angles form a minimal, complete parameterization of orientation. The orientation matrix is recovered as $R = R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: What is a unit quaternion? How is it used to represent a rotation in 3D space?**

**Model Answer:**

A **unit quaternion** is a four-dimensional hypercomplex number $q = w + xi + yj + zk$ satisfying the constraint $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$. It represents a 3D rotation as an **axis-angle pair**: the unit vector $\hat{n} = (n_x, n_y, n_z)$ defines the rotation axis, and the scalar $w = \cos(\alpha/2)$ encodes the half-angle, while $(x, y, z) = \sin(\alpha/2) \cdot \hat{n}$.

Given a vector $\vec{v}$, its rotation by $q$ is computed using the **sandwich product** $v' = q \cdot v_q \cdot q^*$, where $v_q$ is the pure quaternion form of $\vec{v}$ and $q^* = w - xi - yj - zk$ is the conjugate. This operation is **singularity-free** and computationally efficient, making it the preferred representation in real-time robotic control systems.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: (a) Derive the rotation matrix for the Z-Y-X Euler angle convention. Clearly show all intermediate steps. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the phenomenon of gimbal lock with a suitable example. How does it limit the use of Euler angles in robotic manipulators? (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Define the three elementary rotation matrices:**

$$R_x(\phi) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\phi & \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_y(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & 0 & \sin\theta \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ -\sin\theta & 0 & \cos\theta \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_z(\psi) = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi & -\sin\psi & 0 \\ \sin\psi & \cos\psi & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Intrinsic Z-Y-X composition:**

$$R = R_z(\psi) \cdot R_y(\theta) \cdot R_x(\phi)$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$ (detailed row-by-row expansion shown in §3.1):**

$$R_y R_x = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & \sin\theta \sin\phi & \sin\theta \cos\phi \\ 0 & \cos\phi & -\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta \sin\phi & \cos\theta \cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $R_z(\psi)$ on the left:**

Row 1, Col 1: $\cos\psi \cos\theta$
Row 1, Col 2: $\cos\psi \sin\theta \sin\phi - \sin\psi \cos\phi$
Row 1, Col 3: $\cos\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi + \sin\psi \sin\phi$

Row 2, Col 1: $\sin\psi \cos\theta$
Row 2, Col 2: $\sin\psi \sin\theta \sin\phi + \cos\psi \cos\phi$
Row 2, Col 3: $\sin\psi \sin\theta \cos\phi - \cos\psi \sin\phi$

Row 3, Col 1: $-\sin\theta$
Row 3, Col 2: $\cos\theta \sin\phi$
Row 3, Col 3: $\cos\theta \cos\phi$

**Final Result:**

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\psi\cos\theta & \cos\psi\sin\theta\sin\phi - \sin\psi\cos\phi & \cos\psi\sin\theta\cos\phi + \sin\psi\sin\phi \\ \sin\psi\cos\theta & \sin\psi\sin\theta\sin\phi + \cos\psi\cos\phi & \sin\psi\sin\theta\cos\phi - \cos\psi\sin\phi \\ -\sin\theta & \cos\theta\sin\phi & \cos\theta\cos\phi \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Verification:**

$$\det(R) = \cos\psi \cos\theta [\sin\psi\sin\theta\sin\phi\cos\phi + \cos\psi\cos^2\phi + \cos\psi\cos^2\theta\cos^2\phi] - \cdots = +1 \text{ (after simplification)}$$

This confirms the matrix is a **proper rotation** (det = +1) and orthogonal ($R^T R = I$).

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Definition of Gimbal Lock:** Gimbal lock is a **singular configuration** of the Euler angle representation that occurs when the middle rotation angle (pitch, $\theta$) equals $\pm 90°$. In this configuration, two of the three rotation axes become aligned, and the system effectively loses one rotational degree of freedom.

**Mathematical Explanation:**

Substituting $\theta = 90°$ into the Z-Y-X rotation matrix (from part a), we get $\cos\theta = 0$ and $\sin\theta = 1$. The matrix reduces to:

$$R = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \cos\psi\sin\phi - \sin\psi\cos\phi & \cos\psi\cos\phi + \sin\psi\sin\phi \\ 0 & \sin\psi\sin\phi + \cos\psi\cos\phi & \sin\psi\cos\phi - \cos\psi\sin\phi \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & \sin(\phi - \psi) & \cos(\phi - \psi) \\ 0 & \cos(\phi - \psi) & -\sin(\phi - \psi) \\ -1 & 0 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Notice that the orientation now depends **only on the difference $(\phi - \psi)$**, not on $\phi$ and $\psi$ independently. **Two distinct Euler angle triplets can produce the same orientation** — for example, $(\phi, 90°, \psi)$ and $(\phi + 10°, 90°, \psi + 10°)$ yield identical matrices.

**Numerical Example:**

Consider a robot arm reaching to grasp an object directly overhead. As the gripper's pitch approaches 90°, the wrist cannot rotate independently to align the gripper's roll with the desired orientation — yaw and roll motions become **coupled**, making fine manipulation impossible.

**Impact on Robotic Manipulators:**

- **Trajectory planning becomes ill-conditioned** near the singularity, causing large joint velocities.
- **Inverse kinematics algorithms fail** to produce unique solutions.
- **Control loops become unstable** due to numerical sensitivity.
- **Real-world example:** Apollo 13 (1970) — gimbal lock caused the spacecraft's guidance computer to lose attitude information.

**Remedy:** Switch to **quaternion representation** (singularity-free) or use a different Euler convention (e.g., Z-X-Z) depending on the workspace, or re-plan the trajectory to avoid the singular configuration.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a) Define a quaternion. Derive the conversion formula from a unit quaternion $q = (w, x, y, z)$ to the equivalent 3×3 rotation matrix. (7 marks)**

**(b) A robot end-effector is oriented at Z-Y-X Euler angles $\phi = 30°$, $\theta = 45°$, $\psi = 60°$. Convert these angles into a unit quaternion and verify the result. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Definition:** A quaternion is a four-dimensional hypercomplex number $q = w + xi + yj + zk$ that generalizes complex numbers to 4D, with basis elements satisfying Hamilton's rules $i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1$. For representing rotations, the quaternion must be a **unit quaternion** satisfying $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$.

**Rotation operation:** Given a 3D vector $\vec{v} = (v_x, v_y, v_z)$ as a pure quaternion $v_q = 0 + v_x i + v_y j + v_z k$, the rotated vector is:

$$v_q' = q \cdot v_q \cdot q^*$$

where $q^* = w - xi - yj - zk$ is the conjugate.

**Derivation of R(q):**

**Step 1 — Compute $q \cdot v_q$ using Hamilton product:**

$$q \cdot v_q = (w + xi + yj + zk)(v_x i + v_y j + v_z k)$$

Expanding the 16 terms (using $i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = -1$ and $ij = k$, $jk = i$, $ki = j$):

$$q \cdot v_q = \begin{bmatrix} \text{Scalar: } -xv_x - yv_y - zv_z \\ i: wv_x + yv_z - zv_y \\ j: wv_y - xv_z + zv_x \\ k: wv_z + xv_y - yv_x \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by $q^*$ on the right:**

$$v_q' = (q \cdot v_q) \cdot (w - xi - yj - zk)$$

After full expansion and simplification (the scalar part cancels to zero, confirming that the result is a pure quaternion representing a pure rotation), the components of $\vec{v}' = (v_x', v_y', v_z')$ are obtained as:

**Step 3 — Final Quaternion to Rotation Matrix:**

$$\boxed{R(q) = \begin{bmatrix} 1 - 2(y^2 + z^2) & 2(xy - wz) & 2(xz + wy) \\ 2(xy + wz) & 1 - 2(x^2 + z^2) & 2(yz - wx) \\ 2(xz - wy) & 2(yz + wx) & 1 - 2(x^2 + y^2) \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Verification:**

- For $q = (1, 0, 0, 0)$: $R = I$ (identity, no rotation). ✓
- $\det(R) = 1$ for all unit quaternions. ✓
- $R^T R = I$ (orthogonal matrix). ✓

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Given:** $\phi = 30°$, $\theta = 45°$, $\psi = 60°$ (Z-Y-X convention).

**Step 1 — Compute half-angles:**

$$\frac{\phi}{2} = 15°, \quad \frac{\theta}{2} = 22.5°, \quad \frac{\psi}{2} = 30°$$

**Step 2 — Compute the trigonometric values:**

$$\cos(15°) = 0.9659, \quad \sin(15°) = 0.2588$$
$$\cos(22.5°) = 0.9239, \quad \sin(22.5°) = 0.3827$$
$$\cos(30°) = 0.8660, \quad \sin(30°) = 0.5000$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Euler-to-Quaternion formula (from §3.3):**

**Computing w (scalar):**
$$w = (0.8660)(0.9239)(0.9659) + (0.5000)(0.3827)(0.2588)$$
$$w = 0.7729 + 0.0495 = 0.8224$$

**Computing x (i-component):**
$$x = (0.8660)(0.9239)(0.2588) - (0.5000)(0.3827)(0.9659)$$
$$x = 0.2071 - 0.1848 = 0.0223$$

**Computing y (j-component):**
$$y = (0.8660)(0.3827)(0.9659) + (0.5000)(0.9239)(0.2588)$$
$$y = 0.3201 + 0.1195 = 0.4396$$

**Computing z (k-component):**
$$z = (0.5000)(0.9239)(0.9659) - (0.8660)(0.3827)(0.2588)$$
$$z = 0.4462 - 0.0858 = 0.3604$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{q = (0.8224,\ 0.0223,\ 0.4396,\ 0.3604)}$$

**Step 4 — Verification (unit norm check):**

$$w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 0.8224^2 + 0.0223^2 + 0.4396^2 + 0.3604^2$$
$$= 0.6763 + 0.0005 + 0.1932 + 0.1299 = 0.9999 \approx 1.000 \checkmark$$

The small deviation from 1.0000 is due to rounding of trigonometric values to 4 decimal places; the exact mathematical expression satisfies unit norm identically.

**Step 5 — Sanity check (using the inverse formula):** To convert back, $\phi = \arctan\left(\frac{2(wx + yz)}{1 - 2(x^2 + y^2)}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{2(0.0183 + 0.1584)}{1 - 2(0.0005 + 0.1932)}\right) = \arctan\left(\frac{0.3534}{0.6126}\right) = 30°$ ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Order of matrix multiplication** in Part (a) derivations — writing $R_x R_y R_z$ instead of $R_z R_y R_x$ loses full marks. Always re-derive the order carefully.
> 2. **Sign errors in trigonometric terms** are extremely common in the $R(1,2)$, $R(2,3)$, $R(3,2)$ positions. Double-check signs by verifying $R(\phi=0, \theta=0, \psi=0) = I$.
> 3. **Quaternion normalization** is mandatory after computation. Forgetting the unit-norm check costs 1–2 marks in Part (b) of conversion problems.
> 4. **Half-angle formulas** must use $\alpha/2$, not $\alpha$. Writing $q = (\cos\alpha, \sin\alpha \hat{n})$ is a frequent mistake that yields incorrect orientation matrices.
> 5. **In gimbal lock problems**, students often forget to show the **reduced matrix** at $\theta = 90°$ explicitly. Always substitute the singular value to demonstrate the loss of DOF.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Euler Angles and Quaternions**

### Euler Angles — Key Points
- Euler angles are **3 angular parameters** $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ that fully describe 3D orientation.
- **Z-Y-X (yaw–pitch–roll)** is the standard intrinsic convention in robotics: $R = R_z(\psi) R_y(\theta) R_x(\phi)$.
- The full Z-Y-X rotation matrix has **9 trigonometric terms** — memorize the structure (third row is simplest: $[-\sin\theta, \cos\theta\sin\phi, \cos\theta\cos\phi]$).
- **Gimbal lock** occurs at $\theta = \pm 90°$ (cos θ = 0), where yaw and roll become indistinguishable.
- Always verify $\det(R) = +1$ and $R^T R = I$ for any derived rotation matrix.

### Quaternions — Key Points
- A unit quaternion is $q = w + xi + yj + zk$ with $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$.
- **Axis-angle to quaternion:** $w = \cos(\alpha/2)$, $(x, y, z) = \sin(\alpha/2) \cdot \hat{n}$.
- **Hamilton multiplication** is **non-commutative**: $q_1 q_2 \neq q_2 q_1$.
- **Quaternion to rotation matrix** uses the formula with $1 - 2(y^2 + z^2)$ on the diagonal pattern.
- **Quaternion conjugate** $q^* = w - xi - yj - zk$ equals $q^{-1}$ for unit quaternions.
- **Euler to quaternion** formula for Z-Y-X involves products of half-angle sines and cosines — memorize the sign patterns.

### Comparative & Application Points
- Quaternions are **singularity-free**; Euler angles suffer from gimbal lock.
- Quaternions are **compact** (4 floats) and **interpolable** (SLERP is well-defined).
- In **real-time control loops** (drones, spacecraft), quaternions are preferred.
- In **human-machine interfaces** (teach pendants), Euler angles are preferred (readability).
- For the KTU exam, the most common **14-mark question** asks to: derive the Z-Y-X rotation matrix AND explain gimbal lock with example.
- The most common **7-mark sub-question** is: convert Euler angles to a quaternion and verify the result.

### Numerical & Memory Tips
- Half-angle values for **30°, 45°, 60°, 90°** should be memorized: $\cos(15°) = 0.9659$, $\sin(22.5°) = 0.3827$, $\cos(30°) = 0.8660$, $\sin(30°) = 0.5000$.
- The full Z-Y-X matrix can be **partially verified** by setting two angles to zero and checking the matrix reduces to a known single-axis rotation.
- For quaternion results, **always verify $w^2 + x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = 1$** — this is your safety net for partial marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of a Robot (KTU 2024 Canonical Form)

The formal, board-exam accepted definition of a robot as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus (OERAT611) for **Fundamentals of Robotics** is derived from the international standards set by the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8373:2021)** and the **Robotic Industries Association (RIA)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standard):**
> *"A robot is an automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which may be either fixed in place or mobile, for use in industrial automation applications."*
> — Adapted from **ISO 8373** & **RIA** (This is the definition KTU examiners expect.)

### Breaking Down the Canonical Definition

| Key Phrase | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| Automatically Controlled | Operates without continuous human intervention; uses a controller (CPU/MCU/PLC). |
| Reprogrammable | Task logic can be altered via software, not hardware rewiring. |
| Multipurpose Manipulator | Capable of performing diverse tasks (welding, pick-and-place, painting). |
| Three or More Axes | Possesses **≥ 3 Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**. |
| Fixed or Mobile | Includes robotic arms (fixed) and AGVs/AMRs (mobile). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently ask students to define a robot by listing these **5 characteristics**. Memorize the keyword chain: *Automatic → Reprogrammable → Multipurpose → Manipulator → ≥3 Axes*.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **human arm** lifting a cup from a table.

- The **shoulder, elbow, and wrist** mimic the **joints** of a robot.
- The **upper arm and forearm** mimic the **links** (rigid segments).
- The **hand gripping the cup** mimics the **end effector**.
- The **brain sending signals** mimics the **controller**.
- The **muscles providing force** mimic the **actuators**.

A robot is essentially a **mechanical, reprogrammable, mathematical analog of the human arm**, designed to perform tasks that are **dangerous, repetitive, or precise** for humans.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Reprogrammable" is Critical:** A CNC milling machine is automatic but NOT reprogrammable in the robotic sense for varied geometry — it follows G-code for a specific part. A robot can be re-tasked to weld, paint, or assemble **without physical modification**.

---

## 1.3 Fundamental Component Definitions (Module 2 Vocabulary Bank)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to precisely define the following building-block terms. These form the **backbone of all Module 2 questions**.

### 1.3.1 Manipulator
> The **manipulator** is the mechanical arm structure of the robot, composed of a series of **rigid links** connected by **joints**, whose relative motion allows the **end effector** to be positioned and oriented in the workspace.

### 1.3.2 End Effector
> The **end effector** is the special-purpose tool or gripper attached to the **wrist** (or final flange) of the manipulator, designed to perform the actual task (e.g., welding torch, gripper, suction cup, spray nozzle).

### 1.3.3 Actuator
> An **actuator** is the energy-conversion device that provides the mechanical driving force (torque or force) to move the manipulator links. Common types include **electric (servo/Stepper) motors, hydraulic cylinders, and pneumatic cylinders**.

### 1.3.4 Sensor
> A **sensor** is a transduction device that converts a physical quantity (position, velocity, force, vision) into an electrical signal for the controller. Examples: **encoders, resolvers, tachometers, force-torque sensors, vision cameras**.

### 1.3.5 Controller
> The **controller** is the computational brain of the robot — a microcontroller, PLC, or industrial PC that executes the programmed trajectory and closes the feedback loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "M-A-E-S-C"** (Order of Power Flow):
> **C**ontroller → **A**ctuator → (drives) → **M**anipulator → (carries) → **E**nd **E**ffector → **S**ensor → (feeds back to) **C**ontroller.

---

## 1.4 Definition of Degrees of Freedom (DOF)

> The **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a robot is defined as the **number of independent parameters (coordinates) required to completely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of the manipulator or end effector in 3D space.**

### Intuitive Analogy
A **point in a plane** has **2 DOF** (x, y).
A **point in 3D space** has **3 DOF** (x, y, z).
A **rigid body in 3D space** has **6 DOF**: 3 for **position** (x, y, z) and 3 for **orientation** (roll, pitch, yaw).

A robot needs to:
- **Position** its end effector at a point → requires 3 translations.
- **Orient** its end effector at that point → requires 3 rotations.
- **Total for full freedom** = **6 DOF** (called a *6-axis* or *6-DOF robot*).

---

## 1.5 Definition of Joints

> A **joint** is a kinematic pair between two consecutive links that permits relative motion between them. Each joint provides exactly **1 DOF**.

### The Two Primary Joint Types (KTU Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1. Revolute Joint (R):** Allows **rotational motion** about a fixed axis. Symbolized by **R**. Examples: Shoulder, elbow, wrist joints.
>
> **2. Prismatic Joint (P):** Allows **linear (sliding) motion** along an axis. Symbolized by **P**. Examples: Telescopic arms, linear slides.

A third class, the **spherical joint** (ball-and-socket), provides 3 rotational DOF at once but is **rarely used as a discrete unit** in industrial robots.

---

## 1.6 Definition of Links

> A **link** is a **rigid structural member** of the manipulator that connects two consecutive joints. Links maintain a **fixed geometric relationship** (length, twist angle, offset) between the joint axes they connect.

### Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) Context
The **length** of a link ($a_i$) and the **twist angle** ($\alpha_i$) are two of the four D-H parameters used to mathematically define the kinematic structure of a robot link.

---

## 1.7 Definition of Workspace

> The **workspace** (or **work envelope**) of a robot is the **total set of all points in 3D space that can be reached by the end effector (specifically, the tool center point - TCP)**, given all possible joint motions within their physical limits.

### Two Sub-Definitions

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Reachable Workspace** | All points the TCP can reach with **at least one orientation**. |
| **Dexterous Workspace** | All points the TCP can reach with **all possible orientations** (always a subset of reachable). |

> [!NOTE]
> For a 6-DOF robot, dexterous = reachable (mostly). For robots with fewer than 6 DOF, dexterous workspace is empty or near-empty.

---

## 1.8 Definition of Robot Specifications (KTU Common Terms)

| Specification | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Payload** | Maximum weight the robot can carry at the end effector while maintaining rated performance, usually expressed in **kg** or **N**. |
| **Reach** | Maximum distance from the robot base to the TCP, expressed in **mm** or **m**. |
| **Repeatability** | The **closeness of agreement** between repeated movements to the same commanded position under identical conditions (typically ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm for industrial arms). |
| **Accuracy** | The **closeness** between the **commanded position** and the **actual attained position**. |
| **Resolution** | The **smallest incremental motion** the controller can command, limited by encoder bit-resolution and mechanical backlash. |
| **Speed** | Maximum TCP velocity or individual joint angular velocity (deg/s or m/s). |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Structural Anatomy of an Industrial Robot (Logical Decomposition)

An industrial robot (as per ISO 8373 and the KTU OERAT611 syllabus) is structured into four interacting subsystems. KTU frequently frames 14-mark questions around explaining this anatomy.

### Layer 1: Mechanical Subsystem (The Body)
- **Manipulator arm** — the kinematic chain of links + joints.
- **End effector** — the tool/gripper.
- **Base** — the mounting platform.

### Layer 2: Actuation Subsystem (The Muscles)
- **Electric motors** (servo/Stepper) — most common in modern industrial arms.
- **Hydraulic actuators** — for high-payload robots (>100 kg).
- **Pneumatic actuators** — for simple pick-and-place, low-cost applications.

### Layer 3: Sensory Subsystem (The Nerves)
- **Internal sensors** — encoders, resolvers, tachometers (proprioception).
- **External sensors** — vision, force/torque, proximity (exteroception).

### Layer 4: Control Subsystem (The Brain)
- Hardware: PLC / Industrial PC / Microcontroller.
- Software: Trajectory planner, inverse kinematics solver, PID controller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This layered decomposition is what allows modular design — you can swap a hydraulic actuator for an electric one without redesigning the control software, provided the interface (torque/speed envelope) matches.

---

## 2.2 Degrees of Freedom — The Rigorous Counting Argument

### Why DOF Matters
Every independent task the robot performs consumes 1 DOF. Standard tasks consume:
- **Reach a point** → 3 DOF (x, y, z translation).
- **Reach a point + orient tool** → 6 DOF (3 translation + 3 rotation).
- **Avoid an obstacle during motion** → adds constraints, but no new DOF needed; rather requires **redundant DOF**.

### Grubler's Formula (For Planar/Dexterous DOF Calculation)

For a planar mechanism with $n$ links and $j$ joints (each joint providing 1 DOF, with $f_i = 1$):

$$
\text{DOF} = 3(n - 1) - 2j_1 - j_2
$$

where:
- $j_1$ = number of full joints (1 DOF each, 2 constraints).
- $j_2$ = number of half joints (2 DOF each, 1 constraint).

> For a serial-chain robot arm with $n$ links and $n$ revolute joints:
> $$\text{DOF} = n$$
> This is because each revolute joint contributes exactly 1 DOF, and the base link is fixed (ground).

---

## 2.3 Joint Classification — Expanded Reference

| Joint Type | Symbol | Motion | DOF Provided | Real Robot Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Revolute** | R | Rotation about axis | 1 | Shoulder of PUMA 560 |
| **Prismatic** | P | Linear translation along axis | 1 | Linear slide in Cartesian gantry |
| **Cylindrical** | C | R + P combined (1 joint housing) | 2 | Older cylindrical-coordinate robots |
| **Spherical** | S | 2R + 1R combined | 3 | Ball-joint wrist (rare as standalone) |
| **Screw/Helical** | H | Rotation + coupled translation | 1 | Lead screw in actuators |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Definition Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD FOR EXAMS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This Table — These Are Board-Exam Favorites.**

| # | Term | Mathematical / Formal Definition | Units | Constraint / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Robot (ISO 8373) | Auto-controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, ≥3 axes | — | 5-keyword chain required |
| 2 | Degree of Freedom (DOF) | Number of independent coordinates to specify configuration | Dimensionless | Rigid body in 3D → max 6 |
| 3 | Workspace Volume | $V = \int_{q_1^{min}}^{q_1^{max}} \cdots \int_{q_n^{min}}^{q_n^{max}} \vert J(q) \vert \, dq_1 \cdots dq_n$ | m³ | $J$ = Jacobian |
| 4 | Payload | $P = m_{\text{tool}} \cdot g + m_{\text{part}} \cdot g$ | N or kg | Must include gripper mass |
| 5 | Repeatability | $\text{Repeatability} = \pm 3\sigma_x$ (statistical) or ± worst-case deviation | mm | Better metric for production |
| 6 | Accuracy | $\text{Accuracy} = \vert x_{\text{commanded}} - x_{\text{actual}} \vert$ | mm | Worst-case deviation |
| 7 | Resolution | $\Delta x = \dfrac{\text{Total travel}}{\text{Encoder bits}}$ | mm/bit or mm | Limited by mechanics too |
| 8 | D-H Parameters | $a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$ | mm / rad | 4 params per joint |
| 9 | Joint DOF (Grubler) | $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | — | For planar mechanisms |
| 10 | 6-DOF Criterion | 3 translation + 3 rotation | — | Industry standard for full flexibility |

> **Boundary Condition for DOF:**
> If DOF $< 3$: robot cannot position its TCP at an arbitrary 3D point.
> If DOF $= 3$: position only (no orientation control).
> If DOF $= 6$: full position + orientation control (industry benchmark).
> If DOF $> 6$: redundant manipulator (used for obstacle avoidance).

---

## 2.5 Why These Definitions Matter in Real Engineering

| Definition | Real-World Engineering Use |
|---|---|
| DOF | Selecting the right robot for a welding task (6-DOF) vs. a palletizing task (4-DOF). |
| Workspace | Factory floor planning — ensuring the robot's reach covers all station points. |
| Payload | Selecting between a 6 kg SCARA and a 500 kg heavy-payload arm for an automotive body shop. |
| Repeatability | A 6-axis robot with ±0.05 mm repeatability is required for PCB assembly, not ±0.5 mm. |
| End Effector | Tool-changer design — quick-change grippers vs. fixed torches. |
| Controller | Integration with PLCs, SCADA, and Industry 4.0 digital twins. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Insight:** The FANUC M-2000iA (2.3-ton payload) and the KUKA KR 1000 Titan (1000 kg payload) are extreme examples of how payload, reach, and DOF definitions govern real product selection in automotive and foundry industries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: DOF of a Standard 6-Axis Articulated Robot Arm

**Problem Context:** The most exam-asked derivation in Module 2 is computing the DOF of a serial-chain manipulator using **Grubler's Formula** (for spatial, single-loop mechanisms).

### Extended Grubler Formula (Spatial Case)

For a spatial mechanism with $n$ links (including ground) and $j$ joints:

$$
\text{DOF} = 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i)
$$

where $f_i$ is the DOF provided by joint $i$.

### Step-by-Step Application to a 6R Robot Arm

**Step 1 — Count the links.**
The robot has 1 base + 6 movable links (upper arm, forearm, 3 wrist links, and end-effector flange).
$$n = 1 \, (\text{base}) + 6 \, (\text{moving links}) = 7$$

**Step 2 — Count the joints.**
There are 6 revolute joints connecting the 6 moving links to either the base or to each other.
$$j = 6$$

**Step 3 — Identify the DOF of each joint.**
Each revolute joint provides $f_i = 1$ DOF.

**Step 4 — Substitute into Grubler's formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i) \\
&= 6(7 - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{6}(6 - 1) \\
&= 6(6) - 6(5) \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification.**
A 6R articulated robot has 6 DOF. ✅ This matches the industry-standard benchmark for full position + orientation control.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: DOF of a 3-DOF Cartesian (Gantry) Robot

### Setup
A Cartesian robot has 3 prismatic joints aligned with X, Y, Z axes.

### Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1:** $n = 4$ (base + 3 moving links).
**Step 2:** $j = 3$ (all prismatic).
**Step 3:** Each prismatic joint gives $f_i = 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{3}(6 - f_i) \\
&= 6(4 - 1) - 3(6 - 1) \\
&= 6(3) - 3(5) \\
&= 18 - 15 \\
&= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A Cartesian robot can **position** its TCP at any (x, y, z) but cannot **orient** the tool. It needs an additional 3-DOF wrist for full 6-DOF flexibility (becoming a 6-DOF gantry used in CNC and 3D printing).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Robot Types vs. DOF vs. Joint Configurations

| Robot Type | Joint Notation | DOF | Workspace Shape | Typical Application | Industry Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (Gantry) | PPP | 3 | Rectangular box | CNC, 3D printing, pick-place | CNC routers |
| Cylindrical | RPP | 3 | Hollow cylinder | Assembly, machine loading | Older Cincinnati Milacron T3 |
| Spherical (Polar) | RRP | 3 | Hollow sphere | Welding, die casting | Unimate 2000 series |
| SCARA | RRP (vertical) | 4 (in some) | Flat cylinder | PCB assembly, packaging | Epson SCARA |
| Articulated (6R) | RRR-RRR | 6 | Roughly spherical | Welding, painting, assembly | KUKA, FANUC, ABB |
| Cylindrical Coordinate | RPP | 3 | Cylinder | Heavy load handling | Versatran |

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: Workspace Reach Calculation

### Problem
A 2-DOF planar robot has two links of lengths $L_1 = 5$ units and $L_2 = 3$ units. Compute the maximum and minimum reachable distances from the base.

### Forward Kinematics Setup

The TCP position $(x, y)$ in Cartesian coordinates is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta_1, \theta_2$ are the joint angles.

### Step 1 — Maximum Reach
Maximum reach occurs when both links are aligned in the same direction:
$$R_{\max} = L_1 + L_2 = 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ units}$$

### Step 2 — Minimum Reach
Minimum reach occurs when the links fold back on themselves:
$$R_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert = \vert 5 - 3 \vert = 2 \text{ units}$$

### Step 3 — Workspace Area

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{workspace}} &= \pi(R_{\max}^2 - R_{\min}^2) \\
&= \pi(8^2 - 2^2) \\
&= \pi(64 - 4) \\
&= 60\pi \approx 188.5 \text{ square units}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Engineering Takeaway
A planar 2-DOF arm traces a **circular ring (annulus)** of inner radius 2 and outer radius 8. Adding a third revolute joint out of the plane creates a **3D toroidal** workspace — the basis of SCARA and articulated robot designs.

---

## 3.5 D-H Parameter Definition (Foundational for Forward Kinematics)

While D-H parameters themselves are a separate derivation, the **definition** expected by KTU is:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Denavit-Hartenberg (D-H) parameters** are **four parameters** ($a_i, \alpha_i, d_i, \theta_i$) assigned to each link of a serial manipulator to mathematically describe its **relative position and orientation** between consecutive joint axes:
> - $a_i$ = link length (mm)
> - $\alpha_i$ = link twist (rad)
> - $d_i$ = link offset (mm)
> - $\theta_i$ = joint angle (rad)
>
> They form the **4×4 homogeneous transformation matrix** $A_i$ that maps link $i-1$ to link $i$.

The transformation matrix (definition only, derivation in next module):

$$
A_i = \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta_i & -\sin\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & \sin\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \cos\theta_i \\ \sin\theta_i & \cos\theta_i \cos\alpha_i & -\cos\theta_i \sin\alpha_i & a_i \sin\theta_i \\ 0 & \sin\alpha_i & \cos\alpha_i & d_i \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

> **Note:** The exact numerical derivation of the forward kinematics using this matrix is covered in Module 3 of OERAT611. Here, KTU only requires the **definition** of each parameter.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Block Diagram — Robot Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ROBOT SYSTEM] --> B[CONTROL SUBSYSTEM]
    A --> C[ACTUATION SUBSYSTEM]
    A --> D[MECHANICAL SUBSYSTEM]
    A --> E[SENSORY SUBSYSTEM]

    B --> B1[Trajectory Planner]
    B --> B2[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    B --> B3[PID Controller]
    B --> B4[PLC / Industrial PC]

    C --> C1[Electric Servo Motors]
    C --> C2[Hydraulic Cylinders]
    C --> C3[Pneumatic Cylinders]

    D --> D1[Base Link]
    D --> D2[Manipulator Links]
    D --> D3[Joints R and P]
    D --> D4[End Effector]

    E --> E1[Encoders Internal]
    E --> E2[Force Torque Sensors]
    E --> E3[Vision Cameras]
    E --> E4[Proximity Sensors]

    B1 --> B3
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E1
    E1 --> B3
    E2 --> B3
    E3 --> B3
    E4 --> B3
```

## 4.2 Robot Joint Types — Structural Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    J[ROBOT JOINTS] --> J1[Revolute R]
    J --> J2[Prismatic P]
    J --> J3[Cylindrical C]
    J --> J4[Spherical S]
    J --> J5[Helical H]

    J1 --> J1a[Rotational motion]
    J1 --> J1b[1 DOF]
    J1 --> J1c[Example: Shoulder]

    J2 --> J2a[Linear sliding]
    J2 --> J2b[1 DOF]
    J2 --> J2c[Example: Gantry slide]

    J3 --> J3a[R plus P combined]
    J3 --> J3b[2 DOF]
    J3 --> J3c[Older industrial arms]

    J4 --> J4a[3R combined]
    J4 --> J4b[3 DOF]
    J4 --> J4c[Rare in serial chains]

    J5 --> J5a[Screw thread]
    J5 --> J5b[1 DOF]
    J5 --> J5c[Lead screw actuators]
```

## 4.3 Robot Anatomy — Link-Joint-End Effector Block Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BASE[Base Link L0] -->|Joint J1| L1[Link L1]
    L1 -->|Joint J2| L2[Link L2]
    L2 -->|Joint J3| L3[Link L3]
    L3 -->|Joint J4| L4[Link L4 Wrist 1]
    L4 -->|Joint J5| L5[Link L5 Wrist 2]
    L5 -->|Joint J6| L6[Link L6 Wrist 3]
    L6 --> EE[End Effector Tool]

    BASE -.->|Fixed to ground| G((Ground))
    EE -.->|Operates on| T((Task Object))
```

## 4.4 DOF Concept Diagram — Rigid Body in 3D Space

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DOF[Degrees of Freedom DOF] --> P[Position 3]
    DOF --> O[Orientation 3]
    DOF --> TOT[Total 6 DOF for Full Manipulation]

    P --> P1[Translation along X]
    P --> P2[Translation along Y]
    P --> P3[Translation along Z]

    O --> O1[Roll rotation about X]
    O --> O2[Pitch rotation about Y]
    O --> O3[Yaw rotation about Z]

    TOT --> APP[Application: 6 axis robot is industry standard]
```

## 4.5 Workspace Concept — Reachable vs. Dexterous

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WS[WORKSPACE] --> RW[Reachable Workspace]
    WS --> DW[Dexterous Workspace]

    RW --> RW1[All points reachable with at least one orientation]
    RW --> RW2[Outer boundary of robot reach]
    RW --> RW3[Larger region]

    DW --> DW1[All points reachable with all orientations]
    DW --> DW2[Subset of reachable workspace]
    DW --> DW3[Smaller inner region]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
Define a robot as per ISO 8373. List the five essential characteristics.

**Model Answer:**

A robot, as per ISO 8373, is defined as:
*"An automatically controlled, reprogrammable, multipurpose manipulator, programmable in three or more axes, which may be either fixed in place or mobile, for use in industrial automation applications."*

The **five essential characteristics** are:
1. **Automatically controlled** — operates without continuous human intervention.
2. **Reprogrammable** — task logic is alterable through software.
3. **Multipurpose** — capable of performing varied tasks.
4. **Manipulator** — physically interacts with the environment.
5. **Three or more axes** — possesses ≥ 3 DOF.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
Define the following terms: (a) End Effector, (b) Manipulator, (c) Payload.

**Model Answer:**

(a) **End Effector:** The special-purpose tool or device attached to the wrist/flange of the robot manipulator that performs the actual task. Examples include grippers, welding torches, suction cups, and spray nozzles.

(b) **Manipulator:** The mechanical arm structure of the robot consisting of a series of rigid links connected by joints, which positions and orients the end effector in the workspace.

(c) **Payload:** The maximum weight (including the end effector mass and the workpiece mass) that the robot can carry while maintaining its rated performance, typically expressed in kilograms (kg) or Newtons (N).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔵 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `(Choose either A or B)`

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Define the Degree of Freedom (DOF) of a robot. With a neat sketch, explain the six degrees of freedom of a rigid body in 3D space. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 6R articulated robot arm has 7 links (including base) and 6 revolute joints. Using Grubler's formula, calculate its DOF. List the joint notations used to describe this robot. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Definition of DOF + 6-DOF Sketch

**Definition:**
The Degree of Freedom (DOF) of a robot is the **number of independent parameters (coordinates) required to completely specify the configuration** of the manipulator or end effector in 3D space.

**Six DOF of a Rigid Body in 3D Space:**
A rigid body in 3D space requires **6 independent parameters** — 3 for **position** and 3 for **orientation**.

**The 3 Translational DOF (Position):**
1. Translation along **X-axis** — $T_x$
2. Translation along **Y-axis** — $T_y$
3. Translation along **Z-axis** — $T_z$

**The 3 Rotational DOF (Orientation):**
4. **Roll** — rotation about X-axis
5. **Pitch** — rotation about Y-axis
6. **Yaw** — rotation about Z-axis

**Diagram Description:**
Imagine a rectangular box floating in 3D space. To place it at any desired location, you specify $(x, y, z)$ — its **center position**. To orient it correctly, you specify the rotations (roll, pitch, yaw) about its center. Together, these 6 parameters uniquely define the box's pose (position + orientation) in space.

> **Conclusion:** A fully flexible robot needs **6 DOF** (3 + 3) to position **and** orient its end effector arbitrarily in 3D space.

---

#### Part (b) — DOF Calculation Using Grubler's Formula

**Grubler's Formula for Spatial Mechanisms:**

$$
\text{DOF} = 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i)
$$

**Given:**
- $n = 7$ links (including base).
- $j = 6$ revolute joints.
- $f_i = 1$ for each revolute joint.

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DOF} &= 6(n - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{j}(6 - f_i) \\
&= 6(7 - 1) - \sum_{i=1}^{6}(6 - 1) \\
&= 6(6) - 6 \times 5 \\
&= 36 - 30 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Joint Notation of a 6R Articulated Robot:**
The robot is described using the notation **RRR-RRR**, where:
- The first three revolute joints (RRR) form the **shoulder, elbow, and forearm** (positioning the wrist).
- The last three revolute joints (RRR) form the **spherical wrist** (orienting the end effector).

**Engineering Interpretation:** A 6 DOF articulated robot can reach any point in its workspace with any orientation — this is why such robots are the **industry benchmark** for welding, painting, and complex assembly tasks.

---

### 🟢 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]`**

**(a)** Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Revolute Joint, (ii) Prismatic Joint, (iii) Spherical Joint, (iv) End Effector. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Define (i) Workspace, (ii) Repeatability, (iii) Resolution of a robot. A 2-DOF planar robot has two links of lengths $L_1 = 4$ units and $L_2 = 2$ units. Compute the maximum reach, minimum reach, and the workspace area. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Joint Definitions + End Effector

**(i) Revolute Joint (R):** A kinematic pair that allows **rotational motion** about a fixed axis. Each revolute joint provides **1 DOF**.
*Example:* The shoulder or elbow joint of an articulated robot arm (e.g., KUKA KR 16).

**(ii) Prismatic Joint (P):** A kinematic pair that allows **linear (sliding) translation** along a fixed axis. Each prismatic joint provides **1 DOF**.
*Example:* The linear slide of a Cartesian (gantry) robot used in CNC machines and 3D printers.

**(iii) Spherical Joint (S):** A kinematic pair that allows **3 independent rotational motions** (like a ball-and-socket joint), providing **3 DOF** simultaneously.
*Example:* The combined 3-axis wrist unit of an industrial robot, often implemented as three intersecting revolute joints at a common point.

**(iv) End Effector:** The task-specific tool mounted on the robot's wrist flange that performs the actual operation.
*Example:* A two-finger pneumatic gripper for pick-and-place, or a MIG welding torch for arc welding.

---

#### Part (b) — Definitions + 2-DOF Workspace Calculation

**Definitions:**

**(i) Workspace (Work Envelope):** The total set of all points in 3D space that can be reached by the tool center point (TCP) of the robot's end effector, considering the physical limits of all joints.

**(ii) Repeatability:** The closeness of agreement among repeated movements of the robot to the same commanded position, under identical conditions of load, speed, and path. Usually expressed as **±3σ** deviation in **mm**.

**(iii) Resolution:** The **smallest incremental motion** that the robot's controller can command and the mechanical system can execute, determined by encoder resolution and mechanical backlash.

**Workspace Calculation:**

**Step 1 — Maximum Reach:**
The maximum reach occurs when both links are aligned in the same direction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\max} &= L_1 + L_2 \\
&= 4 + 2 \\
&= 6 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Minimum Reach:**
The minimum reach occurs when the links are folded back on themselves.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\min} &= \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \\
&= \vert 4 - 2 \vert \\
&= 2 \text{ units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Workspace Area:**
The 2-DOF planar workspace is an **annulus** (ring-shaped region).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{workspace}} &= \pi(R_{\max}^2 - R_{\min}^2) \\
&= \pi(6^2 - 2^2) \\
&= \pi(36 - 4) \\
&= 32\pi \\
&\approx 100.53 \text{ square units}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Summary:**
- $R_{\max} = 6$ units
- $R_{\min} = 2$ units
- $A_{\text{workspace}} = 32\pi \approx 100.53$ square units

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Lose Marks on "Definition" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Robot Definition Truncation:** Writing only "A robot is an automatic machine" — KTU expects the **full ISO 8373 chain** with all **5 keywords** (automatic, reprogrammable, multipurpose, manipulator, ≥3 axes).
>
> 2. **DOF Confusion:** Mixing up **DOF** with **number of joints**. A 6R arm has **6 joints and 6 DOF**, but a spherical joint counts as 1 joint with **3 DOF**.
>
> 3. **Grubler Formula Mistake:** Forgetting to subtract the **base link count correctly** ($n$ includes the ground link). Wrong: $n = 6$. Right: $n = 7$ for a 6R arm.
>
> 4. **Repeatability vs. Accuracy:** A common trap. **Repeatability** is the spread of repeated attempts. **Accuracy** is the deviation from the commanded point. They are **NOT the same**.
>
> 5. **Workspace Units:** KTU expects workspace in **length units (m, mm)** for reach and **area/volume units** (m², m³) for full workspace. Don't write workspace in "joint radians".
>
> 6. **Joint Notation Order:** In joint notation (e.g., RRP), the order must follow the **kinematic chain from base to end effector**, not random order.
>
> 7. **End Effector vs. Manipulator:** Students often conflate these. The **manipulator is the arm; the end effector is the tool attached to it.** A welding torch is NOT the manipulator.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save This List — Last-Minute KTU Revision for Module 2 Definitions:**

### Core Definitions to Memorize Verbatim
- [ ] **Robot (ISO 8373):** Automatic, reprogrammable, multipurpose, manipulator, ≥3 axes.
- [ ] **DOF:** Independent coordinates to specify configuration.
- [ ] **Workspace:** Total reachable points of TCP.
- [ ] **End Effector:** Tool attached to wrist; performs the task.
- [ ] **Manipulator:** Arm structure of links + joints.
- [ ] **Actuator:** Provides torque/force (electric, hydraulic, pneumatic).
- [ ] **Sensor:** Converts physical quantity → electrical signal.
- [ ] **Controller:** Computational brain (PLC/PC/MCU).

### Joint Types
- [ ] **Revolute (R):** Rotation → 1 DOF.
- [ ] **Prismatic (P):** Linear slide → 1 DOF.
- [ ] **Spherical (S):** 3 rotations → 3 DOF.
- [ ] **Cylindrical (C):** R + P → 2 DOF.

### DOF Counting Rules
- [ ] 3 translations + 3 rotations = **6 DOF for full freedom**.
- [ ] 6R robot → **6 DOF** (Grubler verified).
- [ ] 3P Cartesian → **3 DOF** (position only).
- [ ] DOF < 3: cannot reach arbitrary point.
- [ ] DOF > 6: redundant manipulator.

### Robot Specifications to Remember
- [ ] **Payload:** in kg (include gripper mass).
- [ ] **Reach:** in mm/m.
- [ ] **Repeatability:** ±3σ in mm.
- [ ] **Accuracy:** deviation from commanded position.
- [ ] **Resolution:** smallest command increment.

### Key Formulas (Board Exam Favorites)
- [ ] $R_{\max} = L_1 + L_2$ (fully extended).
- [ ] $R_{\min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ (folded back).
- [ ] $A_{\text{workspace}} = \pi(R_{\max}^2 - R_{\min}^2)$.
- [ ] $\text{DOF} = 6(n-1) - \sum(6 - f_i)$ — Grubler (spatial).
- [ ] $\text{DOF} = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ — Grubler (planar).

### Mnemonics for the Day of Exam
- [ ] **"M-A-E-S-C"** — Power flow order.
- [ ] **"R-Rotates, P-Pushes"** — Joint types.
- [ ] **"Auto-Re-Multi-Manip-Axes"** — 5 keyword robot definition chain.

> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 scheme Module 2 questions on "Definition" typically appear as 3-mark short-answer questions (definition-based) or as 7-mark sub-parts within 14-mark Module 2 questions covering anatomy, DOF, and workspace. **Memorize the verbatim ISO 8373 definition** — it appears in almost every Module 2 paper.

topics.title: Kinematic Chains
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Kinematic Chains

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Chain:** A kinematic chain is an assemblage of rigid bodies (called **links**) connected by means of **kinematic pairs** (joints) in such a way that each link is constrained to move relative to its neighbours in a prescribed manner. In robotics, a kinematic chain provides the mechanical skeleton of a manipulator, transmitting motion and forces from the **base** (ground) through successive links to the **end-effector**.

Every robotic manipulator is essentially a controllable kinematic chain. The discipline that studies the geometry of this motion — without considering the forces/moments that cause it — is called **robot kinematics**.

The two principal subdivisions of robot kinematics are:

| Sub-division | What it Studies | Typical Inputs | Typical Outputs |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Forward (Direct) Kinematics** | Joint variables $\rightarrow$ End-effector pose | Joint angles $\theta_i$, link lengths $a_i$ | Cartesian position $(x,y,z)$ and orientation $(\phi,\theta,\psi)$ |
| **Inverse Kinematics** | End-effector pose $\rightarrow$ Joint variables | Desired pose of end-effector | Required joint displacements $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** A complete study of kinematic chains requires mastery of (a) link/joint taxonomy, (b) mobility / degree-of-freedom analysis, (c) the distinction between serial (open) and parallel (closed) chains, and (d) DH-style representation of chains.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Your Own Arm is a Kinematic Chain"

Imagine you are seated at a desk and reaching for a coffee mug:

- Your **shoulder** is the first revolute joint (it lets your upper arm rotate).
- Your **elbow** is the second revolute joint.
- Your **wrist** is a compound of two/three smaller joints.
- Your **fingertips** are the *end-effector*.

Each bone in your arm is a **link**, and each articulation is a **joint**. Together they form a **serial kinematic chain** starting at the shoulder (fixed to the torso) and ending at the fingers. The chain has a definite **number of independent ways it can be moved** — that number is its **Degree of Freedom (DOF)**, also called **mobility** $M$.

If you now press your palm flat on the table and try to move your hand, your finger motion is now constrained by the contact with the table — the chain is no longer purely serial; a **loop closure** has been introduced. This is exactly analogous to a robot whose last link touches a workpiece or fixture.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** A kinematic chain is to a robot what a skeleton is to a human — the geometry that defines *how* things can move, before we worry about *how strongly* they move.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Definition | Robotic Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Link** | A rigid body in the chain | Upper arm, forearm |
| **Joint / Kinematic Pair** | A constraint between two adjacent links | Revolute, Prismatic |
| **Base Link (Frame 0)** | The link treated as fixed / ground | Pedestal of an industrial arm |
| **End-Effector (Frame $n$)** | The free, terminal link of the chain | Gripper, welding torch |
| **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** $M$ | The number of independent variables needed to fully specify the configuration of the chain | $M = 6$ for a general 6-axis arm |
| **Mobility** | Synonymous with DOF in mechanism theory | $M = 1$ for a 4-bar planar mechanism |
| **Open Chain (Serial)** | No loop; a unique path from base to end-effector | PUMA, Stanford, KUKA arms |
| **Closed Chain (Parallel)** | One or more kinematic loops present | Stewart platform, Delta robot |
| **Redundancy** | DOF $M >$ DOF required by the task | 7-DOF arm reaching around an obstacle |
| **Singularity** | A configuration where DOF is instantaneously lost | Fully extended 2R planar arm |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Kinematic Chain — Step-by-Step Logic

A kinematic chain is constructed by the systematic assembly of three primitives: **links**, **joints**, and **frames of reference**. The logic proceeds as follows:

1. **Identify the Ground (Base) Link.** A single link is designated as the fixed reference. All subsequent kinematic analysis is performed *relative to this link*. In KTU problems this is *Link 0* with attached frame $\{0\}$.

2. **Enumerate the Moving Links.** Each movable rigid body in the manipulator is numbered. For an $n$-joint robot, there are $n$ moving links (Link 1, Link 2, …, Link $n$). The end-effector is the last link.

3. **Classify Every Joint as a Kinematic Pair.** Each joint constrains the relative motion between its two adjacent links. By Reuleaux/Kutzbach convention, joints are classified by the number of degrees of freedom they **remove** between two otherwise free rigid bodies (6 DOF in 3D space, 3 DOF in a plane).

4. **Apply the Mobility Criterion.** Substitute the counted values of $n$ and the joint DOFs into the **Kutzbach (Grübler) mobility formula** to obtain $M$.

5. **Decide the Topological Class.** If a unique, acyclic path connects the base to the end-effector, the chain is **open (serial)**. If at least one closed loop exists, it is a **closed (parallel) chain**.

6. **Attach Coordinate Frames** (typically Denavit–Hartenberg convention) for downstream forward/inverse kinematics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** The DOF count $M$ is *the* single most important kinematic descriptor of a chain. A general positioning-and-orienting task in 3D space requires **$M = 6$**. Anything less is *deficient* (e.g., SCARA with $M=4$ cannot orient in 3D); anything more is *kinematically redundant*.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Kinematic Pairs (Joints)

| Class | Joint Type | Symbol | DOF Removed | DOF Allowed | Robotic Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Lower Pair (1 DOF)** | Revolute (R) | ⬤ | 5 | 1 rotation | Shoulder, elbow, wrist |
| **Lower Pair (1 DOF)** | Prismatic (P) | ⬛ | 5 | 1 translation | Slider, telescoping |
| **Lower Pair (1 DOF)** | Helical (H) | ⚙ | 5 | 1 screw | Lead screw, ball-screw |
| **Higher Pair (2 DOF)** | Cam, Gear teeth | — | 4 | 2 | Rare in robot arms |
| **Higher Pair (3 DOF)** | Sphere-on-sphere | ⚪ | 3 | 3 | Ball-joint (passive wrists) |
| **Universal (U)** | 2 consecutive R about intersecting axes | ⨯ | 4 | 2 | Wrist, hip |

> **DOF-Removed vs DOF-Allowed:** Note that 6 (or 3 for planar) total DOF is split into "removed" (the joint *constrains*) and "allowed" (the joint *permits*). A *lower pair* has line/area contact and removes 5 DOF in 3D; a *higher pair* has point contact and removes 4 DOF.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the *high-yield* formulas that KTU examiners test most frequently. Memorise them with units and conditions.

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning of Each Symbol | Units / Domain | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ | Kutzbach planar mobility | $n$ = links, $j_1$ = 1-DOF joints, $j_2$ = 2-DOF joints | Planar mechanisms |
| 2 | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5$ | Kutzbach spatial mobility | $n$ = links, $j_k$ = $k$-DOF joints | Spatial mechanisms |
| 3 | $M = \sum_{i=1}^{n} f_i - 6L$ | Equivalent spatial form using constraint loops | $f_i$ = joint freedoms, $L$ = independent loops | General |
| 4 | $\sum f_i = 6n - 5$ | Static determinacy (no extra constraints) | — | Grashof check |
| 5 | $M = n$ | Condition for a serial (open) chain | $n$ moving links | Trivial identity |
| 6 | $\tau = 1 + L - n + \sum (f_i - 1)$ | Mobility via independent loops (alt. form) | $L$ = loops | Equivalent to Kutzbach |
| 7 | $T_{0}^{n} = A_{1} A_{2} \cdots A_{n}$ | Forward-kinematics composition | $A_i \in SE(3)$ | DH / screw theory |
| 8 | $\vert \mathbf{x} \vert = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$ | Cartesian norm | Position vector | All kinematics |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Some students compute $M = 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2$ with $n$ equal to the *number of joints*. Always remember: $n$ = **number of links (including the fixed ground)** in the Kutzbach formula. If a problem states "a 4-bar mechanism", then $n = 4$.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The kinematic chain concept is the *lingua franca* of the following domains:

- **Industrial Robotics** — Designing 6-axis articulated arms (e.g., KUKA KR, ABB IRB) whose chain provides full spatial dexterity.
- **Parallel Manipulators** — Flight simulators and high-precision milling machines built on the **Stewart platform** (6-DOF closed chain).
- **Biomechanics & Prosthetics** — Modelling the human skeleton as a multi-loop chain for gait analysis and prosthetic design.
- **Vehicle Suspension** — Multi-link suspensions are planar closed chains whose mobility governs wheel motion.
- **Cable-Driven Robots** — Suspended cameras, rescue cranes — analysed using the same closed-chain mobility rules.
- **CNC Machines & Parallel Kinematic Machines (PKM)** — Tricept, Hexapods; the high stiffness of closed chains enables precision machining.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kutzbach Criterion (Planar Mobility)

Consider a planar mechanism with $n$ rigid links moving in the $xy$-plane. In the absence of joints, each link would be a *free* rigid body in the plane, with **3 degrees of freedom** (two translations $x, y$ and one rotation $\phi$). For $n$ links, the *unconstrained* mobility is therefore $3n$.

A joint *removes* DOF. By Reuleaux's classification, a **lower pair** (1-DOF joint) in the plane removes $3 - 1 = 2$ DOF; a **higher pair** (2-DOF joint) removes $3 - 2 = 1$ DOF.

However, one link must be designated as the **fixed ground**, which removes 3 DOF entirely. So the *net* mobility of the system is:

$$M = \underbrace{3n}_{\text{unconstrained DOF}} - \underbrace{2 j_1}_{\text{removed by 1-DOF joints}} - \underbrace{1 \cdot j_2}_{\text{removed by 2-DOF joints}} - \underbrace{3}_{\text{ground is fixed}}$$

Regrouping the constants:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3n - 2j_1 - j_2 - 3 \\
  &= 3n - 3 - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2
\end{aligned}$$

This is the celebrated **Kutzbach mobility formula for planar mechanisms**. Each subtraction term has a clear physical meaning: the $-3$ corresponds to the immobilised ground, each $-2$ is the "lock-down" imposed by a 1-DOF joint, and each $-1$ is the partial constraint of a 2-DOF joint.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Spatial Kutzbach Criterion

A free rigid body in three-dimensional space possesses **6 DOF** (3 translations + 3 rotations). Repeating the same accounting argument with $n$ spatial links, the unconstrained DOF is $6n$, and subtracting the immobilisation of the ground ($6$) and the constraints imposed by joints of varying freedom yields:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 6n - \sum_{k=1}^{5} (6 - k)\, j_k \;-\; 6 \\
  &= 6n - 6 - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - 1 \cdot j_5 \\
  &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5
\end{aligned}$$

where $j_k$ is the number of joints that *retain* $k$ DOF (and hence remove $6 - k$ DOF).

**Special cases that are heavily tested in KTU examinations:**

| Special Case | Substituted Formula | Common Robotic Instance |
|---|---|---|
| All joints are revolute (R) or prismatic (P), 1-DOF | $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1$ | 6R arm, 3R planar arm |
| Mix of R and P joints (only 1-DOF) | $M = 6(n-1) - 5 j_1$ | SCARA: $M = 6(4-1) - 5(4) = -2$?? — see next row |
| Including the *common* form with no higher pairs | $M = 6(n - 1) - 5 \cdot \text{No.\ of 1-DOF joints}$ | A 6R arm: $M = 6(6-1) - 5(6) = 0$?? — **correct, IF ground counted** |

> **Pitfall:** A 6-DOF serial robot has $n = 7$ links (ground + 6 moving) and $j_1 = 6$ joints, giving $M = 6(7-1) - 5(6) = 6$. ✔ Always include the ground.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Planar 3R Manipulator (Open Chain)

A 3-revolute planar arm has three moving links and a ground, joined by three revolute joints.

- $n = 4$ (ground + 3 moving links)
- $j_1 = 3$ (three 1-DOF revolute joints)
- $j_2 = 0$

Apply planar Kutzbach:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(n-1) - 2j_1 - j_2 \\
  &= 3(4-1) - 2(3) - 0 \\
  &= 9 - 6 \\
  &= 3
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The end-effector has 3 DOF (two translations in plane + one orientation angle). This matches the intuition that we can position a 2D point and orient a single line — three independent commands.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 2 — 6R Spatial Articulated Arm (PUMA-class)

- $n = 7$ (ground + 6 moving links)
- $j_1 = 6$ revolute joints, $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$

Apply spatial Kutzbach:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 - 4j_2 - 3j_3 - 2j_4 - j_5 \\
  &= 6(7-1) - 5(6) - 0 - 0 - 0 - 0 \\
  &= 36 - 30 \\
  &= 6
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** $M = 6$ is the gold standard — full spatial dexterity (3 position + 3 orientation).

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 3 — SCARA (Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm)

SCARA has the joint sequence **R-R-P-R** (two horizontal revolute joints, one vertical prismatic, one final revolute).

- $n = 5$ (ground + 4 moving links)
- $j_1 = 4$ (all joints 1-DOF)
- $j_2 = j_3 = j_4 = j_5 = 0$

Apply spatial Kutzbach:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 6(n-1) - 5j_1 \\
  &= 6(5-1) - 5(4) \\
  &= 24 - 20 \\
  &= 4
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** $M = 4$ DOF. SCARA is intentionally *deficient* (cannot tilt the end-effector in 3D), which is ideal for vertical-axis pick-and-place in PCB assembly.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 4 — 4-Bar Planar Mechanism

Classic four-bar linkage with four links and four 1-DOF revolute joints.

- $n = 4$, $j_1 = 4$, $j_2 = 0$

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 3(4-1) - 2(4) \\
  &= 9 - 8 \\
  &= 1
\end{aligned}$$

Exactly **1 DOF** — drive one link and the entire mechanism moves deterministically. ✔

---

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — DOF of Common Industrial Robot Kinematic Chains

| Robot / Mechanism | Joint Sequence | $n$ (links) | $j_1$ | $M$ (Computed) | Functional Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cartesian (gantry) | P-P-P | 4 | 3 | 3 | Translation only; pick-and-place |
| Cylindrical | R-P-P | 4 | 3 | 3 | Reach in a cylindrical work envelope |
| Spherical (Polar) | R-R-P | 4 | 3 | 3 | Reach in a spherical envelope |
| SCARA | R-R-P-R | 5 | 4 | 4 | Planar positioning + vertical insertion |
| Articulated (PUMA) | R-R-R-R-R-R | 7 | 6 | 6 | Full 6-DOF dexterity |
| Stanford Arm | R-R-P-R-R-R | 7 | 6 | 6 | RRP spherical wrist |
| Planar 3R | R-R-R | 4 | 3 | 3 | Educational benchmark |
| 4-bar planar | R-R-R-R | 4 | 4 | 1 | Classic 1-DOF linkage |
| Slider-crank | R-R-P | 4 | 3 | 1 | Engine, pump mechanism |
| Stewart Platform | 6 × (P universal) | 14 | 18 | 6 | Closed-chain 6-DOF parallel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity check the table:** Each spatial serial chain obeys $M = 6(n-1) - 5j_1$ and matches intuition. A 7-DOF "redundant" manipulator (e.g., KUKA LBR iiwa) would have $n = 8$, $j_1 = 7$, $M = 7$ — perfect for obstacle avoidance.

---

### 3.8 Loop-Closure Equation (Conceptual)

For a closed chain, the position and orientation of the end-effector reached by *any* path from base must agree. Formally, with $L$ independent loops:

$$\prod_{i \in \text{path 1}} A_i \;=\; \prod_{j \in \text{path 2}} A_j$$

The number of scalar equations these produce equals the number of constraints imposed by the loop, and they form the *kinematic constraints* used to solve the dependent joint variables in parallel manipulators.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Taxonomy of Kinematic Chains — Mermaid Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Kinematic Chain]:::root --> B[Open Serial Chain]
    A --> C[Closed Parallel Chain]
    A --> D[Hybrid Mixed Chain]
    B --> B1[Cartesian PPP]
    B --> B2[Cylindrical RPP]
    B --> B3[Spherical RRP]
    B --> B4[Articulated 6R]
    B --> B5[SCARA RRPR]
    C --> C1[Planar 4 Bar]
    C --> C2[Stewart Platform]
    C --> C3[Delta Robot]
    C --> C4[Tricept PKM]
    D --> D1[Hybrid LAMBDA Arm]
    D --> D2[Trunk Manipulator]

    classDef root fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef root,sub fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,color:#000
```

---

### 4.2 Open vs Closed Chain — Topology Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPEN[Open Serial Chain]
        G0[Base / Ground]:::fixed --> L1[Link 1 j1] --> L2[Link 2 j2] --> L3[Link 3 j3] --> LE[End Effector]:::ee
    end
    classDef fixed fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef ee fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#000

    subgraph CLOSED[Closed Parallel Chain]
        G1[Moving Platform]:::ee
        G0c[Fixed Base]:::fixed
        G0c --- LA1[Leg 1] --- G1
        G0c --- LA2[Leg 2] --- G1
        G0c --- LA3[Leg 3] --- G1
        G0c --- LA4[Leg 4] --- G1
    end
```

---

### 4.3 Kutzbach Counting Workflow — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: Sketch the mechanism] --> S2[Is it planar or spatial?]
    S2 -->|Planar| P1[Use M equals 3 of n minus 1 minus 2 j1 minus j2]
    S2 -->|Spatial| P2[Use M equals 6 of n minus 1 minus sum k jk]
    P1 --> P3[Count n includes ground]
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4[Count j1 1 DOF joints]
    P4 --> P5[Count j2 2 DOF joints]
    P5 --> P6[Substitute into formula]
    P6 --> P7[Validate against intuition]
    P7 --> S8[Identify topological class: Open or Closed]
    S8 --> END[Result: Mobility M of the chain]
```

---

### 4.4 Functional Architecture of a 6-DOF Serial Kinematic Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Controller Joint 1] --> M1[Actuator 1] --> J1[Revolute Joint 1] --> LK1[Link 1 Upper Arm] --> J2[Revolute Joint 2 Shoulder] --> LK2[Link 2 Forearm] --> J3[Revolute Joint 3 Elbow] --> LK3[Link 3 Wrist Link 1] --> J4[Revolute Joint 4] --> LK4[Link 4 Wrist Link 2] --> J5[Revolute Joint 5] --> LK5[Link 5 Wrist Link 3] --> J6[Revolute Joint 6] --> LK6[Link 6 End Effector Flange] --> TOOL[Tool / Gripper]:::ee
    SENS[Encoders at each joint] -.-> A0
    classDef ee fill:#86efac,stroke:#166534,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading aid:** Each rectangle is a *link*; each labelled circle/box (J1, J2, …) is a *kinematic pair*. Encoders (dashed feedback line) measure joint angles — the *forward kinematics* equation $\mathbf{T}_{0}^{6}$ maps these to the end-effector pose.

---

### 4.5 Schematic Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Constraint Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INP[Desired End Effector Pose X Y Z phi theta psi] --> INV[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    INV --> QV[Dependent Joint Vector q]
    QV --> FWD[Forward Kinematics Evaluation]
    FWD --> RES[Residual Loop Closure Error]
    RES -->|non zero| ITR[Newton Raphson Iteration]
    ITR --> FWD
    RES -->|below tolerance| OUT[Verified Configuration]
```

---
