- **Newton–Raphson:** Linearize via Jacobian; iterate. Fast convergence near a solution.
- **Jacobian Transpose:** $ \Delta\theta = \alpha J^T \Delta x$ — simple, no inversion, but slow.
- **Damped Least Squares (Levenberg–Marquardt):** $(J^T J + \lambda^2 I) \Delta\theta = J^T \Delta x$ — robust near singularities.
- **Cyclic Coordinate Descent (CCD) / FABRIK:** Heuristic, easy to implement, popular in animation.

**Advantages:** Generic — works for any geometry; works with redundancy.
**Disadvantages:** May converge to local minima; slow; may fail near singularities; doesn't return *all* solutions.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Closed-Form | Numerical |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Speed | **Fast (μs)** | Slow (ms) |
| Completeness | All solutions | Single local solution |
| Singularity Robustness | Robust | **Poor (needs damping)** |
| Implementation | Difficult | **Easy** |
| Use Case | Industrial, real-time control | Animation, redundant arms |

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.2):**

The decoupling splits the problem into:

$$T_{6}^{0} = \begin{bmatrix} R_{3}^{0}(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3) & p_{3}^{0}(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3) \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} R_{6}^{3}(\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6) & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Position Sub-Problem — Derivation:**

The wrist center $p_c$ is the origin of frame 4, located at the intersection of the last three joint axes. In the base frame:

$$p_c = p_{3}^{0} = p - d_6 \cdot \hat{z}_6$$

From the forward kinematics of the first 3 links:

$$p_c = \begin{bmatrix} a_2 c_1 c_2 + a_3 c_1 c_{23} - d_2 s_1 \\ a_2 s_1 c_2 + a_3 s_1 c_{23} + d_2 c_1 \\ a_2 s_2 + a_3 s_{23} \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(for a typical PUMA-type arm)}$$

Equating components:

- From $p_{cy}/p_{cx}$: $\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(p_{cy}, p_{cx}) - \text{atan2}(d_2, \pm\sqrt{r^2 - d_2^2})$ where $r^2 = p_{cx}^2 + p_{cy}^2$.
- Then $\theta_3$ from the projection on the $r$-$z$ plane using sub-problem 2 (parallel axes).
- Then $\theta_2$ from the geometry of the resulting triangle.

**Orientation Sub-Problem (outline):** Compute $R_{3}^{0}(\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3)$ from the now-known first three joints. Then:

$$R_{6}^{3} = (R_{3}^{0})^T R_{6}^{0}$$

Extract the Euler angles (e.g., ZYZ for typical wrist) from $R_{6}^{3}$ to obtain $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IK Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the sign choice in $\pm\sqrt{1-c_2^2}$** — Examiners award marks *only* if you mention both elbow-up and elbow-down solutions.
> 2. **Skipping the existence check** — Even if the math works, you lose 1 mark if you don't verify $\vert c_2 \vert \leq 1$.
> 3. **Not mentioning multiple solutions** — In a 6-DOF question, just giving one set of $\theta$ values costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing Jacobian and Inverse Jacobian use** — Differential IK uses $J^{-1}$; pseudo-inverse is $J^+ = J^T (JJ^T)^{-1}$.
> 5. **Ignoring singularities** — A "well-formed" answer must remark on what happens at $\det(J) = 0$.
> 6. **Mixing DH parameters** — $a_i$ (link length), $\alpha_i$ (twist), $d_i$ (offset), $\theta_i$ (joint angle). Confusing them is a top-3 error.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

- [ ] **IK = inverse of FK** — given pose $T_d$, find joint angles $\theta$.
- [ ] **General IK is a set of 6 nonlinear equations in 6 unknowns** for a 6-DOF arm.
- [ ] **Solvability requires the desired pose to be in the reachable workspace**.
- [ ] **A 6R arm can have up to 16 solutions**; a 2R planar arm has 2 solutions.
- [ ] **Pieper's theorem** applies when the last 3 joint axes intersect (spherical wrist).
- [ ] **Kinematic decoupling** = position sub-problem + orientation sub-problem.
- [ ] **Three classical sub-problems** (Paul–Shimano–Mayer) form the geometric IK toolkit.
- [ ] **Numerical IK (Newton–Raphson)** uses $J^{-1}$ — fails at singularities.
- [ ] **Singularities** occur when $\det(J) = 0$; manipulator loses DOF there.
- [ ] **Workspace types**: Reachable (RSW) vs Dexterous (DW).
- [ ] **Closed-form methods** are preferred for industrial 6DOF arms (PUMA, Stanford).
- [ ] **Numerical methods** are used for redundant / non-analytical manipulators.
- [ ] **The wrist center** $p_c$ is computed by subtracting $d_6 \hat{z}_6$ from the tool position.
- [ ] **Always check existence** using the cosine rule (for planar) or norm bounds (for 3D).
- [ ] **Choose the optimal solution** among multiple based on minimum joint motion, collision avoidance, or manipulability.
- [ ] **End-effector pose** is described by 3 position components + 3 orientation parameters (e.g., ZYZ Euler or roll–pitch–yaw).
- [ ] **KTT 2024 emphasis**: questions on decoupling, solvability, and spherical wrist are most frequent.
- [ ] **Mandatory verification step**: plug solution back into FK to confirm correctness.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General Properties of Solutions to the Inverse Kinematics Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General Properties of Solutions to the Inverse Kinematics Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 2 — General Properties of Solutions to the Inverse Kinematics Problem

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Inverse Kinematics (IK) Problem** is the problem of determining the set of all joint variable values (in the joint space) that cause the manipulator's end-effector to reach a prescribed position and orientation (in the Cartesian space), given a desired end-effector pose and the manipulator's geometric parameters.

$$X = f(q) \quad \text{(Forward Kinematics)}$$

$$q = f^{-1}(X) \quad \text{(Inverse Kinematics)}$$

where $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of joint variables and $X \in SE(3)$ is the desired end-effector pose.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **General Properties of Solutions to the IK Problem** refer to the qualitative mathematical characteristics that ALL inverse kinematics solutions must satisfy, regardless of the specific manipulator architecture. These properties govern *existence*, *uniqueness*, *multiplicity*, *workspace accessibility*, and *solubility* of the IK problem.

The **general properties** are summarized as:
1. **Existence of solutions** — solvability condition.
2. **Number of solutions** — multiplicity / branching.
3. **Uniqueness** — when only one configuration is valid.
4. **Solution approaches** — closed-form vs. numerical.
5. **Workspace dependency** — reachable vs. dexterous.
6. **Repeatability and accuracy** — consistency of solutions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a human arm reaching out to grasp a coffee mug on a table. Your brain performs inverse kinematics *continuously*: it knows the position of the mug in space (Cartesian pose) and must compute how much to bend the shoulder, elbow, and wrist (joint angles) so that the hand lands exactly on the mug. The properties of this problem are:

- **Existence**: If the mug is placed beyond the stretch of your arm, you simply **cannot reach it** — no joint combination exists.
- **Multiple Solutions**: You can reach the mug with the **elbow up** or the **elbow down** — these are two valid joint configurations for the *same* hand position.
- **Workspace**: The **reachable workspace** is every point your fingertips can touch; the **dexterous workspace** is the smaller region where your hand can be oriented in *all* possible angles.
- **Uniqueness**: For a *given* orientation, only one solution usually exists — the other is geometrically forbidden.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world robotic implication:** Industrial pick-and-place manipulators are designed with *redundant* degrees of freedom (like a human arm with 7 joints) precisely to *guarantee solution existence* and to *avoid singularities* where the IK has no unique solution.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Range |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| DOF of general 6R manipulator | $n$ | **6** |
| Maximum algebraic solutions (generic 6R) | $N_{max}$ | **16** |
| Spherical wrist solutions (orientation) | — | **up to 4** |
| Closed-form existence DOF threshold | $n$ | **≤ 6** |
| Redundancy threshold | $n > 6$ | **7 or more** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Property 1 — Existence of Solutions

A solution to the IK problem **exists if and only if** the desired pose $X$ lies inside the manipulator's **workspace**.

- The **reachable workspace** $W_{R}$ is the set of all positions the end-effector can attain (for *some* orientation).

$$W_{R} = \{ p \in \mathbb{R}^{3} \mid \exists \, q \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \text{ such that the position component of } {}^{0}T_{n}^{}(q) = p \}$$

- The **dexterous workspace** $W_{D}$ is the set of all positions where the end-effector can attain *every* possible orientation.

$$W_{D} \subseteq W_{R}$$

- Relationship: $W_{D}$ is a strict subset of $W_{R}$ for non-redundant manipulators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Existence Criterion:** $X \in W_{R}$ is *necessary and sufficient* for at least one IK solution to exist. The **dexterous workspace** $W_{D}$ requires additionally that the orientation constraint is met.

### 2.2 Property 2 — Number of Solutions (Multiplicity)

The IK problem is *nonlinear* and *transcendental*, leading to **multiple discrete solutions** for the same end-effector pose.

- For a **6-DOF manipulator** with general geometry, there can be **up to 16 distinct solutions**.
- For manipulators with three consecutive axes intersecting at a common point (spherical wrist), the problem **decouples** into position and orientation sub-problems, yielding **at most 4** wrist solutions for the orientation.

**Number of Solutions Summary:**

| Manipulator Type | $n$ | Max Solutions |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Planar 3R (RRR) | 3 | **2** |
| 3R with spherical wrist | 6 | **4** (2 shoulder × 2 wrist × 2 elbow) |
| 6R general (non-spherical) | 6 | **≤ 16** |
| Redundant 7R | 7 | **∞ (continuum)** |

### 2.3 Property 3 — Uniqueness

A solution is **unique** only when the manipulator is at a **singular configuration** *or* the pose lies on the **boundary** of the workspace. Mathematically, when:

$$\det \left( \frac{\partial f(q)}{\partial q} \right) = \det(J(q)) = 0$$

the Jacobian loses rank, and an entire subspace of joint configurations maps to the same Cartesian pose — solutions *coalesce*.

### 2.4 Property 4 — Solution Approaches

The IK problem admits two principal solution strategies:

**(a) Closed-Form (Analytical) Solution**

$$q = f^{-1}(X) \text{ — explicit algebraic expression}$$

- Exists only for manipulators with **specific kinematic structure** (e.g., spherical wrist).
- **Pieper's Criterion (1968):** A 6-DOF manipulator has a closed-form solution if **any three consecutive joint axes are concurrent** (intersect at a common point) — or if any three are parallel.
- Generates **all** solutions in finite time.

**(b) Numerical (Iterative) Solution**

$$q_{k+1} = q_{k} + J^{-1}(q_{k}) \cdot \Delta X \quad \text{(Newton–Raphson variant)}$$

- Applicable to **any** manipulator architecture.
- Converges to *one* solution (not all), sensitive to initial guess.
- May **fail to converge** near singularities or outside the workspace.

### 2.5 Property 5 — Kinematic Decoupling (Pieper's Approach)

For manipulators satisfying Pieper's criterion, the IK problem **decouples** into:

- **Position Inverse Kinematics:** Solve for the first three joint variables using only the position of the intersection point of the wrist axes.
- **Orientation Inverse Kinematics:** Solve the remaining three joint angles using the orientation constraint.

> [!NOTE]
> This decomposition is the cornerstone of the textbook derivation for 6-DOF PUMA-type manipulators. The position IK yields *up to 2 solutions*; the orientation IK yields *up to 2 solutions*; the total is therefore *up to 4* (elbow up/down × wrist flip/no-flip).

### 2.6 Property 6 — Repeatability and Accuracy

- **Repeatability** is the ability of the manipulator to return to a previously taught joint configuration — a property of the *forward* map.
- **Accuracy** depends on how *precisely* the IK can be solved for a *desired* pose. Singularities and workspace boundaries degrade accuracy.
- **Spatial resolution** of a discretized joint space limits the number of reachable Cartesian poses.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Equation / Condition | Physical Meaning |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Forward Kinematics | $X = f(q)$ | Joints $\to$ Cartesian |
| 2 | Inverse Kinematics | $q = f^{-1}(X)$ | Cartesian $\to$ Joints |
| 3 | Existence | $X \in W_{R}$ | Pose must be in workspace |
| 4 | Workspace | $W_{D} \subseteq W_{R}$ | Dexterous is subset of reachable |
| 5 | Singularity | $\det J(q) = 0$ | Jacobian loses rank |
| 6 | Pieper's Criterion | 3 concurrent/parallel axes | Closed-form solvability |
| 7 | 6R Max Solutions | $N \leq 16$ | Algebraic upper bound |
| 8 | Spherical Wrist | $N_{orient} \leq 2$ | Sub-problem simplicity |
| 9 | Redundant DOF | $n > 6$ | Infinite solutions |
| 10 | Decoupled IK | $q = [q_{pos}^{T}, q_{orient}^{T}]^{T}$ | Position and orientation separate |
| 11 | Iterative Step | $q_{k+1} = q_{k} + \alpha J^{-1}(q_{k}) \Delta X$ | Newton-Raphson style |
| 12 | Joint Limits | $q_{i,min} \leq q_{i} \leq q_{i,max}$ | Physical constraints |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These properties are the *design guidelines* for industrial manipulators. Knowing the maximum number of solutions lets path planners **enumerate** all valid configurations and **avoid collisions** or **singularities** by choosing the optimal branch. This is fundamental in **CNC machining**, **surgical robotics** (e.g., Da Vinci), and **autonomous manipulation** (e.g., warehouse pick-and-place).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Number of Solutions for a General 6R Manipulator

We consider a general 6-DOF revolute manipulator. The IK equations arise from equating the given end-effector pose to the forward kinematics transformation:

$${}^{0}T_{6}^{}(q_{1}, q_{2}, q_{3}, q_{4}, q_{5}, q_{6}) = \begin{bmatrix} R & p \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The transformation matrix contains **12 nonlinear transcendental equations** (3 for position, 9 for rotation) but only **6 unknowns** ($q_{1} \dots q_{6}$). The system is **over-determined**, yet consistent solutions exist on the constraint manifold.

**Step 1:** Extract the position equations by setting the position vector $p$ equal to its desired value.

The position vector is generally a sum of vectors along each link, giving a polynomial in sines and cosines of the joint angles.

**Step 2:** Apply the substitution $u_{i} = \tan(q_{i}/2)$, converting the trigonometric system to a polynomial one.

$$\sin(q_{i}) = \frac{2u_{i}}{1 + u_{i}^{2}}, \quad \cos(q_{i}) = \frac{1 - u_{i}^{2}}{1 + u_{i}^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** After clearing denominators, the position equations become a system of polynomial equations in $u_{1}, \dots, u_{6}$ of total degree **16** (Manocha–Canny result, 1988).

**Step 4:** By Bézout's theorem, the number of isolated complex solutions is bounded by the product of the degrees, yielding an upper bound:

$$N_{max} = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times 2 = 16 \quad \text{(for generic 6R manipulator)}$$

**Step 5:** For the spherical wrist case (Pieper's criterion), the position sub-problem involves only 3 unknowns ($q_{1}, q_{2}, q_{3}$) of degree **2 each**, giving $2 \times 2 \times 2 = 8$, but only **2** real solutions exist for the position. Combined with **2** wrist-flip solutions, the total is **4**.

### 3.2 Derivation: Existence of Solutions for Planar 3R Manipulator

Consider a planar 3R manipulator with link lengths $L_{1}, L_{2}, L_{3}$. The forward kinematics position is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(q) &= L_{1}\cos q_{1} + L_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) + L_{3}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}+q_{3}) \\
y(q) &= L_{1}\sin q_{1} + L_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) + L_{3}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}+q_{3})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the wrist position.** The wrist (joint 3) position is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{w} &= x - L_{3}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}+q_{3}) \\
y_{w} &= y - L_{3}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}+q_{3})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Existence test.** Using the law of cosines on the 2R sub-chain (links $L_{1}, L_{2}$):

$$x_{w}^{2} + y_{w}^{2} = L_{1}^{2} + L_{2}^{2} + 2L_{1}L_{2}\cos q_{2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $q_{2}$:**

$$\cos q_{2} = \frac{x_{w}^{2} + y_{w}^{2} - L_{1}^{2} - L_{2}^{2}}{2 L_{1} L_{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Real solution exists** if and only if:

$$\left\vert \frac{x_{w}^{2} + y_{w}^{2} - L_{1}^{2} - L_{2}^{2}}{2 L_{1} L_{2}} \right\vert \leq 1$$

This is precisely the **existence condition** of the IK problem for this manipulator, defining the **reachable workspace** as an annulus with inner radius $\vert L_{1} - L_{2} \vert$ and outer radius $L_{1} + L_{2}$.

**Step 5 — Multiple solutions.** The $\cos q_{2}$ equation has **2 solutions** (elbow up / elbow down):

$$q_{2} = \pm \arccos \left( \frac{x_{w}^{2} + y_{w}^{2} - L_{1}^{2} - L_{2}^{2}}{2 L_{1} L_{2}} \right)$$

Each of these gives a unique $q_{1}$ via $\mathrm{atan2}$. Hence, the planar 3R has **2 IK solutions** for a given wrist position.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of IK Approaches

| Feature | Closed-Form IK | Numerical IK |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Solution Speed | **Fast (microseconds)** | Slow (milliseconds–seconds) |
| Completeness | Finds **all** solutions | Finds **one** solution |
| Applicability | Limited to special structures | **Universal** |
| Near Singularities | Reliable (formula-based) | **Unreliable (convergence failure)** |
| Near Workspace Boundary | May yield complex roots | **No solution reported** |
| Joint Limit Handling | Post-hoc filtering | **Easy to incorporate** |
| Implementation Cost | High (case-specific) | Low (generic algorithm) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — IK Solution Properties

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired End-Effector Pose X] --> B{Is X inside Reachable Workspace WR}
    B -- NO --> C[NO SOLUTION EXISTS]
    B -- YES --> D[IK Problem is Solvable]
    D --> E{Singular Configuration}
    E -- YES --> F[Infinite or Coalescing Solutions]
    E -- NO --> G{Pieper Criterion Satisfied}
    G -- YES --> H[Closed-Form Decoupled Solution]
    G -- NO --> I[Numerical Iterative Solution]
    H --> J[Position Sub-Problem]
    H --> K[Orientation Sub-Problem]
    J --> L[Up to 2 Position Solutions]
    K --> M[Up to 2 Wrist Solutions]
    L --> N[Cartesian Product: 4 Total Solutions]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Apply Joint Limit Filtering]
    I --> P[Newton-Raphson Iteration]
    P --> Q{Convergence Achieved}
    Q -- YES --> R[One Joint Solution Returned]
    Q -- NO --> S[Algorithm Failure or Singularity]
    O --> T[Final Valid Joint Configurations]
    R --> T
    C --> U[Path Planner Rejects Pose]
    S --> U
    F --> T
```

### 4.2 Process Flow — Kinematic Decoupling (Pieper's Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Desired Pose T_desired] --> B[Identify Wrist Center Position P_w]
    B --> C[Apply Spherical Wrist Geometry]
    C --> D[Solve 3-DOF Position IK for q1 q2 q3]
    D --> E{Number of Position Solutions}
    E -- One --> F[Single Shoulder Configuration]
    E -- Two --> G[Elbow Up and Elbow Down]
    F --> H[Compute Orientation Residual R_residual]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Solve 3-DOF Orientation IK for q4 q5 q6]
    I --> J[Wrist Flip Solutions: Up to 2]
    J --> K[Combine All Branches]
    K --> L[Final Set of 4 Joint Configurations]
```

### 4.3 Workspace Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Manipulator Workspace Universe] --> B[Reachable Workspace WR]
    A --> C[Dexterous Workspace WD]
    A --> D[Interior Workspace WI]
    B --> E[Interior Positions Only]
    C --> F[All Orientations Achievable]
    D --> G[All Orientations + Easy Path Planning]
    C -- subset of --> B
    D -- subset of --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> The hierarchy $W_{D} \subseteq W_{R}$ is critical in manipulator design. **Welding robots** operate in $W_{D}$ (need full orientation control), while **pick-and-place gantries** operate in $W_{R}$ (only positioning matters).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the existence condition for inverse kinematics solutions. Differentiate between the reachable workspace and the dexterous workspace of a manipulator.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The existence condition for the inverse kinematics problem requires that the desired end-effector pose $X$ lies within the manipulator's **reachable workspace** $W_{R}$. Formally, a solution exists if and only if:

$$X \in W_{R} = \{p \in \mathbb{R}^{3} \mid \exists \, q \text{ such that } f(q) = X\}$$

The **reachable workspace** is the set of all positions the end-effector can reach (with *some* orientation), whereas the **dexterous workspace** $W_{D}$ is the subset of $W_{R}$ where the end-effector can achieve *every possible orientation*. Mathematically, $W_{D} \subseteq W_{R}$. If $X$ lies in $W_{R}$ but not in $W_{D}$, the position is reachable but the orientation may be unattainable.

*(1 mark for existence condition, 1 mark for reachable definition, 1 mark for dexterous distinction.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define Pieper's criterion. Why is it important in solving the inverse kinematics of 6-DOF manipulators?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Pieper's Criterion (1968):** A 6-DOF manipulator admits a closed-form inverse kinematics solution if any three consecutive joint axes are either **concurrent** (intersect at a common point) or **parallel**.

**Importance:**
1. It enables **kinematic decoupling** of position and orientation sub-problems (3 marks each).
2. It guarantees the existence of an **analytical closed-form** solution, yielding all valid configurations in finite time without iterative computation.
3. It is the basis for the standard textbook treatment of PUMA 560, Stanford Arm, and similar industrial manipulators.

*(1 mark for statement, 1 mark for condition, 1 mark for engineering significance.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With reference to the inverse kinematics problem, explain the following properties in detail:
(i) Existence of solutions.
(ii) Number of solutions.
(iii) Uniqueness and multiple solutions.
(iv) Solvability and the role of the Jacobian.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Existence of Solutions (2 Marks):**
A solution to the IK problem exists if and only if the desired end-effector pose $X$ lies in the **reachable workspace** $W_{R}$. This workspace depends on the link lengths, joint limits, and kinematic structure. For an arbitrary point outside $W_{R}$, no combination of joint variables can produce the desired pose. The existence condition is:

$$\exists \, q \in \mathbb{R}^{n} \text{ such that } f(q) = X \iff X \in W_{R}$$

**(ii) Number of Solutions (2 Marks):**
Due to the **nonlinear** and **trigonometric** nature of the IK equations, the same Cartesian pose can be attained by multiple joint configurations. For a generic 6R manipulator, the algebraic upper bound is **16 solutions** (Manocha–Canny, 1988). For a manipulator with a spherical wrist, the upper bound reduces to **4 solutions** (2 elbow × 2 wrist flip).

**(iii) Uniqueness and Multiple Solutions (2 Marks):**
Uniqueness holds only at **singular configurations** (where $\det J(q) = 0$) or at the **workspace boundary**. In the interior of the workspace, there are typically multiple valid solutions, corresponding to physically distinct configurations (e.g., elbow up/down, wrist flip/no-flip). Path planners must select one branch for execution.

**(iv) Solvability and the Role of the Jacobian (1 Mark):**
The Jacobian $J(q) = \partial f / \partial q$ governs the local invertibility of the forward kinematics. Where $J$ has full rank, locally unique solutions exist. At singularities, rank deficiency causes motion in the null space of $J$, leading to non-unique or impossible IK solutions.

---

**(b)** A planar 3R manipulator has link lengths $L_{1} = L_{2} = L_{3} = 1$ m. Compute the number of inverse kinematics solutions and determine the **existence condition** for a desired wrist position $(x_{w}, y_{w}) = (1.2, 0.5)$ m. Is the pose reachable?

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics of Wrist Position:**

The wrist (joint 3 origin) position is determined by the first two links:

$$x_{w} = L_{1}\cos q_{1} + L_{2}\cos(q_{1} + q_{2})$$

$$y_{w} = L_{1}\sin q_{1} + L_{2}\sin(q_{1} + q_{2})$$

**Step 2 — Distance from Origin:**

$$r^{2} = x_{w}^{2} + y_{w}^{2} = L_{1}^{2} + L_{2}^{2} + 2 L_{1} L_{2} \cos q_{2}$$

Substituting the values $L_{1} = L_{2} = 1$ m and the target $(1.2, 0.5)$:

$$r^{2} = (1.2)^{2} + (0.5)^{2} = 1.44 + 0.25 = 1.69 \, \text{m}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Existence Condition:**

For a real $q_{2}$ to exist, $\vert \cos q_{2} \vert \leq 1$:

$$\cos q_{2} = \frac{r^{2} - L_{1}^{2} - L_{2}^{2}}{2 L_{1} L_{2}} = \frac{1.69 - 1 - 1}{2} = \frac{-0.31}{2} = -0.155$$

Since $\vert -0.155 \vert = 0.155 < 1$, the solution **exists**.

**Step 4 — Two Solutions for $q_{2}$ (Elbow Up / Elbow Down):**

$$q_{2} = \pm \arccos(-0.155) = \pm 80.93^{\circ}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $q_{1}$ for Each Branch:**

For the elbow-up branch ($q_{2} = +80.93^{\circ}$):

$$q_{1} = \mathrm{atan2}(y_{w}, x_{w}) - \mathrm{atan2}(L_{2}\sin q_{2}, L_{1} + L_{2}\cos q_{2})$$

Substituting:

$$q_{1} = \mathrm{atan2}(0.5, 1.2) - \mathrm{atan2}(\sin 80.93^{\circ}, 1 + \cos 80.93^{\circ})$$

$$q_{1} = 22.62^{\circ} - 49.53^{\circ} = -26.91^{\circ}$$

**Step 6 — Number of Solutions:**
The planar 3R manipulator has **2 inverse kinematics solutions** for the given wrist position (elbow up and elbow down). The pose is **reachable** since $\vert \cos q_{2} \vert < 1$.

*(Distribution: 2 marks Step 1–2, 2 marks Step 3–4, 2 marks Step 5, 1 mark Step 6.)*

---

**Question B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare **closed-form** and **numerical** approaches to solving the inverse kinematics problem. Mention Pieper's criterion and the conditions under which each approach is preferred.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Closed-Form IK | Numerical IK |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Solution Type | Explicit algebraic expressions | Iterative (e.g., Newton–Raphson) |
| Speed | **Very fast** (μs) | Slower (ms to s) |
| Completeness | All solutions returned | One solution per run |
| Generality | Only for special structures | **Universal** |
| Singularities | Robust (algebraic) | **Unreliable** |
| Joint Limits | Post-filtering | **Easy incorporation** |

**Pieper's Criterion:** A 6-DOF manipulator has a closed-form solution if any three consecutive joint axes are **concurrent** or **parallel**. This allows decoupling into 3-DOF position and 3-DOF orientation sub-problems.

**When to prefer Closed-Form:**
- Industrial robots with spherical wrists (PUMA, ABB IRB).
- Real-time control with sub-millisecond latency.
- Path planning requiring branch enumeration.

**When to prefer Numerical:**
- Manipulators with **general architecture** (no special structure).
- Constrained optimization with joint limits, collision avoidance.
- Redundant manipulators where infinite solutions exist.

**Industrial Practice:** The PUMA 560 manipulator, which satisfies Pieper's criterion (last three axes intersect at the wrist), is universally solved using closed-form techniques in commercial controllers. The Stanford Arm (with prismatic joint) similarly benefits from closed-form IK.

*(2 marks for tabular comparison, 2 marks for Pieper's criterion, 2 marks for application conditions, 1 mark for industrial example.)*

---

**(b)** A 6-DOF manipulator with a spherical wrist must place its end-effector at position $p = (0.6, 0.0, 0.4)$ m. The base-to-wrist-center distance must equal $0.5$ m. If the spherical wrist orientation constraint admits 2 solutions (flip/no-flip) and the position sub-problem admits 2 solutions (elbow up/down), determine the **total number of inverse kinematics solutions** and explain the **decoupling procedure**.

**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Kinematic Decoupling Principle:**
For a spherical-wrist manipulator, the IK problem decouples into two independent sub-problems because the last three axes intersect at a common point $P_{w}$ (the wrist center). The position of $P_{w}$ depends only on the first three joints.

$$P_{w} = p - d_{6} \cdot \hat{z}_{6}$$

where $d_{6}$ is the distance along the end-effector approach direction and $\hat{z}_{6}$ is the unit vector along the wrist axis.

**Step 2 — Position Sub-Problem (3-DOF):**
Given $P_{w} = (0.6, 0, 0.4)$ m and the wrist-center distance constraint, the position sub-problem reduces to solving for $q_{1}, q_{2}, q_{3}$.

Since the position sub-problem has 2 solutions (elbow up / elbow down), the 2R planar structure yields:

$$q_{2} = \pm \arccos \left( \frac{\vert P_{w} \vert^{2} - L_{1}^{2} - L_{2}^{2}}{2 L_{1} L_{2}} \right)$$

This gives 2 valid shoulder/elbow configurations.

**Step 3 — Orientation Sub-Problem (3-DOF):**
The desired end-effector orientation $R$ is decomposed as:

$$R = R_{pos}(q_{1}, q_{2}, q_{3}) \cdot R_{wrist}(q_{4}, q_{5}, q_{6})$$

The wrist rotation is computed as:

$$R_{wrist} = R_{pos}^{T} \cdot R_{desired}$$

The wrist sub-problem admits **2 solutions** (wrist flip with $q_{5} = \pm \theta$).

**Step 4 — Total Number of Solutions:**

$$N_{total} = N_{position} \times N_{orientation} = 2 \times 2 = 4$$

**Step 5 — Enumeration of Solutions:**

| Solution | Shoulder/Elbow | Wrist |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | Elbow Down | No Flip |
| 2 | Elbow Down | Flip |
| 3 | Elbow Up | No Flip |
| 4 | Elbow Up | Flip |

**Step 6 — Selection Criteria:** The path planner chooses the optimal branch based on joint-limit proximity, collision avoidance, and minimum joint motion from the current configuration.

*(2 marks decoupling explanation, 2 marks position sub-problem, 2 marks orientation sub-problem, 1 mark enumeration.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IK Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing reachable vs. dexterous workspace:** Students frequently interchange $W_{R}$ and $W_{D}$. Remember: $W_{D} \subseteq W_{R}$. **Loses 1–2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting Pieper's Criterion:** A closed-form IK question is *always* linked to three concurrent or parallel axes. Omitting this loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Wrong multiplicity count:** The maximum number of solutions is **16** for a generic 6R, **4** for a spherical-wrist 6R, and **2** for a planar 3R. Confusing these numbers is a frequent error.
> 4. **Skipping joint-limit checking:** After computing algebraic solutions, the student must verify $q_{i,min} \leq q_{i} \leq q_{i,max}$ to mark a solution as physically valid.
> 5. **Ignoring singularity condition:** Many solutions coalesce or disappear at $\det J(q) = 0$. Failure to mention this in uniqueness discussions loses 1 mark.
> 6. **No clear distinction between closed-form and numerical:** Examiners expect students to mention at least *one* numerical method (e.g., Newton–Raphson) when comparing approaches.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **IK problem** is: given a desired Cartesian pose, find all joint configurations that achieve it.
- ✅ **Existence** of a solution requires $X \in W_{R}$ (reachable workspace).
- ✅ **Dexterous workspace** $W_{D}$ is the subset where all orientations are attainable.
- ✅ The IK problem generally admits **multiple discrete solutions** (up to 16 for generic 6R, 4 for spherical wrist).
- ✅ **Closed-form solutions** are possible only for special kinematic structures.
- ✅ **Pieper's Criterion:** three concurrent/parallel consecutive axes → closed-form solvability.
- ✅ **Kinematic decoupling** splits the problem into 3-DOF position and 3-DOF orientation sub-problems.
- ✅ **Singularities** occur at $\det J(q) = 0$ and reduce the number of independent solutions.
- ✅ **Redundant manipulators** ($n > 6$) have *infinite* solutions, used for obstacle avoidance.
- ✅ **Numerical methods** (Newton–Raphson, Jacobian transpose, Damped Least Squares) are general but yield only *one* solution and may fail near singularities.
- ✅ **Joint limits** must be checked after algebraic solution to confirm physical validity.
- ✅ The **elbow up/down** and **wrist flip/no-flip** are the canonical multiple-solution branches in 6-DOF spherical-wrist manipulators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Theory of Kinematic Decoupling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Theory of Kinematic Decoupling

# Theory of Kinematic Decoupling

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kinematic Decoupling** is a powerful analytical technique used to solve the inverse kinematics of **6-DOF (Degrees of Freedom) manipulators** whose final three joint axes intersect at a single common point. The method decomposes (decouples) the complex 16 non-linear kinematic equations of the manipulator into two independent, solvable sub-problems:
> 1. **Position Kinematics** — Finding the first three joint variables ($\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$) to position the **Wrist Center** (the intersection of axes 4, 5, and 6) at the desired Cartesian location.
> 2. **Orientation Kinematics** — Finding the last three joint variables ($\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$) to achieve the desired end-effector orientation.

This approach is universally applicable to robots of the **PUMA 560**, **Stanford Arm**, and **Yasukawa Motoman** class — manipulators possessing a **Spherical Wrist** (concurrent final three axes).

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Human Arm"

Imagine you are reaching out to grab a cup of tea from a table:
- First, your **shoulder, elbow, and wrist (the arm)** move to position your palm at the **location** of the cup. You do not yet care about *how* the cup is tilted.
- Next, your **forearm rotation, wrist twist, and finger curl** adjust the **orientation** of your hand so the fingers can grip it properly.

This two-stage natural motion is precisely what kinematic decoupling mimics mathematically. The "arm" handles position, while the "wrist" handles orientation — completely **independently**.

### Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Number of DOF in the standard decoupling case:** $\mathbf{6}$ (3 for position + 3 for orientation)
> - **Degrees of freedom of position sub-problem:** $\mathbf{3}$
> - **Degrees of freedom of orientation sub-problem:** $\mathbf{3}$
> - **Common robot classes satisfying this property:** PUMA 560, Stanford, ABB IRB, Motoman.
> - **Wrist Center Point (WCP):** Denoted as $O_c$ or $P_{wrist}$, the point of intersection of axes 4, 5, and 6.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Underlying Logic — Step by Step

A general 6-DOF manipulator is described by the forward kinematics equation:

$$T_6^0 = \begin{bmatrix} R_6^0 & O_6^0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

where $O_6^0$ is the position of the end-effector and $R_6^0$ is its orientation matrix. Solving the inverse kinematics directly for all six joint variables $\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_6$ yields a system of **16 non-linear transcendental equations** — an intractable problem.

The decoupling strategy introduces the **Wrist Center** $O_c$ as an intermediary point. Because the last three joints form a spherical wrist centered at $O_c$:

- The end-effector origin $O_6$ is offset from $O_c$ by a fixed tool length $d_6$ (typically along the $z_6$ axis).
- Therefore: $O_6^0 = O_c^0 + R_6^0 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ d_6 \end{bmatrix}$

Rearranging gives the wrist center position:

$$O_c^0 = O_6^0 - d_6 \cdot R_6^0 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

This critical relation allows us to **decouple** position from orientation.

### Why Decoupling Works — The Core "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because $O_c$ depends only on the **first three joints** (it is a function of $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ alone) and the tool length $d_6$ is **fixed and known**, we can:
> 1. Compute $O_c^0$ from the **desired end-effector position and orientation** using the equation above.
> 2. Solve the **first three joint angles** to bring the wrist center to $O_c^0$ — this is a classic 3-DOF planar/spherical problem.
> 3. Once $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ are known, the rotation matrix $R_3^0$ becomes fully determined. The remaining orientation equation $R_6^3 = (R_3^0)^{-1} R_6^0$ yields the last three joint angles via Euler-angle extraction.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Meaning** | **Formula / Value** |
|---|---|---|
| $T_6^0$ | End-effector pose (homogeneous transform) | $\begin{bmatrix} R_6^0 & O_6^0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| $O_c^0$ | Wrist center position in base frame | $O_c^0 = O_6^0 - d_6 \cdot R_6^0 \hat{k}$ |
| $\hat{k}$ | Unit vector along tool $z$-axis | $\begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ |
| $R_6^3$ | Wrist orientation relative to frame 3 | $(R_3^0)^T R_6^0$ |
| $\theta_1$ | Base shoulder rotation | $\text{atan2}(y_c, x_c)$ |
| $\theta_2, \theta_3$ | Elbow & forearm joints | Cosine law / planar 2R IK |
| $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$ | Wrist Euler angles | ZYZ / ZYX Euler extraction |
| $d_6$ | Tool offset length (constant) | From DH-table |
| DOF required | Total | **6** |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Kinematic decoupling is the **industry standard** in commercial robot controllers (ABB, KUKA, FANUC, Yaskawa). It reduces real-time inverse kinematics computation time by **orders of magnitude**, enabling trajectory tracking at millisecond refresh rates in welding, painting, and pick-and-place operations. Without decoupling, online IK is computationally infeasible for production lines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Expressing the Wrist Center Position

**Step 1: Start from the forward kinematics of the manipulator up to frame 6.**

The end-effector position $O_6^0$ can be written as a sum of the wrist center position $O_c^0$ and the wrist's contribution:

$$O_6^0 = O_c^0 + d_6 \cdot R_6^0 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: Explanation.** Since the spherical wrist has its origin at $O_c$ and its third axis ($z_6$) extends along the tool direction, the end-effector origin sits a distance $d_6$ along the local $z$-axis from $O_c$.

**Step 3: Solve for the wrist center.**

Rearranging the equation from Step 1, we move the offset term to the left side:

$$O_c^0 = O_6^0 - d_6 \cdot R_6^0 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Physical Interpretation.** This is the **central equation of decoupling**. It tells us that once the desired end-effector pose $(O_6^0, R_6^0)$ is given, the wrist center location $O_c^0$ can be computed directly — independent of the wrist joint angles.

---

### Derivation 2 — Solving the Position Sub-Problem (First 3 Joints)

**Step 1: Project the wrist center onto the base XY-plane.**

For a standard manipulator with $\theta_1$ as the base rotation about $z_0$:

$$r = \sqrt{x_c^2 + y_c^2}$$

$$\theta_1 = \text{atan2}(y_c, x_c)$$

where $x_c, y_c, z_c$ are the components of $O_c^0$.

**Step 2: Compute the horizontal reach and vertical height.**

After removing $\theta_1$, we project $O_c$ onto the plane defined by joints 2 and 3:

$$x' = r - a_2 \cos(\alpha), \quad z' = z_c - d_1$$

For a typical PUMA-like arm, the position in the 2-3 plane becomes:

$$r' = \sqrt{x'^2 + z'^2}, \quad \psi = \text{atan2}(z', x')$$

**Step 3: Apply the Law of Cosines for $\theta_3$.**

Given link lengths $a_2$ and $a_3$:

$$\cos(\theta_3) = \frac{r'^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2 a_2 a_3}$$

$$\theta_3 = \text{atan2}\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - \cos^2(\theta_3)},\ \cos(\theta_3)\right)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\theta_2$.**

$$\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(z', x') - \text{atan2}(a_3 \sin(\theta_3),\ a_2 + a_3 \cos(\theta_3))$$

**Step 5: Physical interpretation.** The "$\pm$" sign in $\theta_3$ corresponds to the **elbow-up** and **elbow-down** configurations — typically two valid solutions per $\theta_1$.

---

### Derivation 3 — Solving the Orientation Sub-Problem (Last 3 Joints)

**Step 1: Once $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$ are known, compute $R_3^0$.**

$$R_3^0 = R_1^0(\theta_1) \cdot R_2^1(\theta_2) \cdot R_3^2(\theta_3)$$

**Step 2: Extract the wrist rotation from the desired end-effector orientation.**

$$R_6^3 = (R_3^0)^{-1} R_6^0 = (R_3^0)^T R_6^0$$

because $R_3^0$ is an orthogonal rotation matrix.

**Step 3: Equate $R_6^3$ to the symbolic form from DH-parameters.**

$$R_6^3 = \begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 c_6 - s_4 s_6 & -c_4 c_5 s_6 - s_4 c_6 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 c_6 + c_4 s_6 & -s_4 c_5 s_6 + c_4 c_6 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 c_6 & s_5 s_6 & c_5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\theta_5, \theta_4, \theta_6$ in sequence.**

From the (3,3) entry:

$$c_5 = R_{33}^3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_5 = \text{atan2}\left(\pm\sqrt{1 - R_{33}^{3\,2}},\ R_{33}^3\right)$$

From the (1,3) and (2,3) entries:

$$\theta_4 = \text{atan2}(R_{23}^3, R_{13}^3)$$

From the (3,1) and (3,2) entries:

$$\theta_6 = \text{atan2}(R_{32}^3, -R_{31}^3)$$

**Step 5: Conclusion.** The orientation sub-problem is fully solved. Combined with the position solution, all six joint angles are obtained — completing the inverse kinematics via kinematic decoupling.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Number of Solutions

> [!NOTE]
> For a generic 6-DOF manipulator with a spherical wrist, the number of inverse kinematics solutions is:
> $$\mathbf{N = 8}$$
> arising from: **2 choices of $\theta_3$** (elbow up/down) × **2 signs of $\theta_5$** (wrist flip) × **2 signs of $\theta_1$** (front/back reach). The KTU examiner often asks students to enumerate these.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Block-Level Functional Architecture of Kinematic Decoupling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Desired End Effector Pose"] --> B["Input Position and Orientation T60"]
    B --> C["Compute Wrist Center Position Oc"]
    C --> D["Solve Position Sub Problem"]
    D --> D1["Compute theta1"]
    D --> D2["Compute theta2"]
    D --> D3["Compute theta3"]
    D1 --> E["Form R30 Rotation Matrix"]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    B --> F["Extract Desired R60"]
    E --> G["Compute R63 equals R30 transpose times R60"]
    F --> G
    G --> H["Solve Orientation Sub Problem"]
    H --> H1["Compute theta5"]
    H --> H2["Compute theta4"]
    H --> H3["Compute theta6"]
    H1 --> I["Complete Joint Vector"]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J["Verify Forward Kinematics T60"]
```

### Conceptual Linkage Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A["POSITION DOMAIN"]
    P1["Position Sub Problem"]
    P2["theta1 theta2 theta3"]
    P3["Wrist Center Oc"]
    end
    subgraph B["ORIENTATION DOMAIN"]
    O1["Orientation Sub Problem"]
    O2["theta4 theta5 theta6"]
    O3["End Effector Orientation R60"]
    end
    P1 --> P3
    P3 --> P2
    P2 --> O1
    O1 --> O3
    O3 --> O2
```

### Dependency Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1 Read T60"] --> S2["Step 2 Compute Oc using tool offset d6"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Solve planar IK for theta1 theta2 theta3"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 Build R30 from DH table"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 Compute R63 equals inverse R30 times R60"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6 Extract ZYZ Euler angles theta4 theta5 theta6"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7 Assemble full joint vector"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8 Validate with FK check"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define kinematic decoupling. Under what condition on the manipulator is it applicable?**

> **Model Answer:**
> Kinematic decoupling is the technique of splitting the 6-DOF inverse kinematics problem into two independent sub-problems — position of the wrist center (3 DOF) and orientation of the end-effector (3 DOF). It is applicable when the manipulator possesses a **spherical wrist**, i.e., when the **last three joint axes (4, 5, 6) intersect at a single common point**. This condition is satisfied by most industrial robots such as the PUMA 560.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] What is a "wrist center"? Why is its location important in decoupling?**

> **Model Answer:**
> The wrist center $O_c$ is the **point of intersection of the last three joint axes** of a manipulator equipped with a spherical wrist. Its location is important because it depends **only on the first three joint variables** and on the desired end-effector pose through the relation $O_c^0 = O_6^0 - d_6 R_6^0 \hat{k}$. By solving for $O_c$ first, the position and orientation sub-problems become independent — which is the essence of decoupling.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **wrist center position** $O_c^0$ in terms of the desired end-effector pose $(O_6^0, R_6^0)$ and the tool offset $d_6$. Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 6-DOF manipulator with link lengths $a_2 = 10$ cm and $a_3 = 10$ cm, the wrist center is to be located at $O_c^0 = (15, 0, 10)$ cm. Assuming $\theta_1 = 0$, compute $\theta_2$ and $\theta_3$ using the planar IK equations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) Solution:**

The forward kinematics from the base to the end-effector can be written in block form as:

$$T_6^0 = \begin{bmatrix} R_6^0 & O_6^0 \\ 0\ 0\ 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Because the spherical wrist has its origin at $O_c$ and the tool extends a fixed distance $d_6$ along the local $z_6$ axis, the end-effector position can be decomposed as:

$$O_6^0 = O_c^0 + d_6 \cdot R_6^0 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Solving for $O_c^0$:

$$\boxed{O_c^0 = O_6^0 - d_6 \cdot R_6^0 \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Physical Significance of Each Term:**
- $O_6^0$: The desired Cartesian position of the end-effector (given by the task).
- $d_6$: The fixed tool length from the wrist center to the tool tip (read from the DH-table).
- $R_6^0 \hat{k}$: The unit vector along the tool's $z$-axis expressed in the base frame — this defines the approach direction.
- $O_c^0$: The point in space where the last three joints must converge — depends only on the first three joints.

This relation is the **cornerstone of kinematic decoupling**: it allows the wrist center location to be determined purely from task-space specifications.

**Part (b) Solution:**

Given: $O_c^0 = (15, 0, 10)$ cm, $a_2 = 10$ cm, $a_3 = 10$ cm, $\theta_1 = 0$.

**Step 1:** Since $\theta_1 = 0$, the wrist center projection in the 2-3 plane gives:

$$r = x_c = 15 \text{ cm}, \quad z_c = 10 \text{ cm}$$

For a planar 2R manipulator after $\theta_1$:

$$r = a_2 \cos\theta_2 + a_3 \cos(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$$
$$z_c = a_2 \sin\theta_2 + a_3 \sin(\theta_2 + \theta_3)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the cosine rule to find $\theta_3$:

$$\cos\theta_3 = \frac{r^2 + z_c^2 - a_2^2 - a_3^2}{2 a_2 a_3}$$

$$\cos\theta_3 = \frac{15^2 + 10^2 - 100 - 100}{2 \cdot 10 \cdot 10} = \frac{225 + 100 - 200}{200} = \frac{125}{200} = 0.625$$

$$\theta_3 = \pm \cos^{-1}(0.625) = \pm 51.32^\circ$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\theta_2$:

$$\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(z_c, r) - \text{atan2}(a_3 \sin\theta_3, a_2 + a_3 \cos\theta_3)$$

For $\theta_3 = +51.32^\circ$:

$$\theta_2 = \text{atan2}(10, 15) - \text{atan2}(10 \cdot 0.781, 10 + 10 \cdot 0.625)$$

$$\theta_2 = 33.69^\circ - \text{atan2}(7.81, 16.25) = 33.69^\circ - 25.66^\circ = 8.03^\circ$$

**Final Answer:**
- Elbow-up: $\theta_2 = 8.03^\circ,\ \theta_3 = +51.32^\circ$
- Elbow-down: $\theta_2 = -25.66^\circ - 33.69^\circ = -59.35^\circ,\ \theta_3 = -51.32^\circ$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram how **kinematic decoupling** simplifies the inverse kinematics of a 6-DOF manipulator with a spherical wrist. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The desired end-effector orientation of a robot is given by:

$$R_6^0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Given that $R_3^0 = I$ (identity), determine the wrist joint angles $\theta_4, \theta_5, \theta_6$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) Solution:**

The decoupling strategy is summarized as follows:

```
[Input: T_6^0 (desired pose)]
        │
        ▼
[Compute Wrist Center: O_c^0 = O_6^0 − d₆·R_6^0·k̂]
        │
        ▼
[Position Sub-Problem]  →  θ₁, θ₂, θ₃  (from O_c^0)
        │
        ▼
[Build R_3^0 from θ₁, θ₂, θ₃ via DH-parameters]
        │
        ▼
[Orientation Sub-Problem]  →  R_6^3 = (R_3^0)ᵀ · R_6^0
        │
        ▼
[Euler/ZYZ Extraction]  →  θ₄, θ₅, θ₆
        │
        ▼
[Output: Joint vector Θ = {θ₁,…,θ₆}]
```

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Since $R_3^0 = I$, we have:

$$R_6^3 = (R_3^0)^T R_6^0 = I \cdot R_6^0 = R_6^0 = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Equate with the symbolic ZYZ wrist rotation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} c_4 c_5 c_6 - s_4 s_6 & -c_4 c_5 s_6 - s_4 c_6 & c_4 s_5 \\ s_4 c_5 c_6 + c_4 s_6 & -s_4 c_5 s_6 + c_4 c_6 & s_4 s_5 \\ -s_5 c_6 & s_5 s_6 & c_5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** From the (3,3) entry:

$$c_5 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_5 = 90^\circ$$

**Step 4:** From the (1,3) and (2,3) entries:

$$c_4 s_5 = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad s_4 s_5 = 0$$

Since $s_5 = 1$:

$$c_4 = 1, \quad s_4 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_4 = 0^\circ$$

**Step 5:** From the (3,1) and (3,2) entries:

$$-s_5 c_6 = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad s_5 s_6 = 1$$

Since $s_5 = 1$:

$$c_6 = 0,\ s_6 = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_6 = 90^\circ$$

**Final Answer:** $\theta_4 = 0^\circ,\ \theta_5 = 90^\circ,\ \theta_6 = 90^\circ$.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make (and lose marks for):**
> 1. **Forgetting the tool offset $d_6$** when computing $O_c^0$. Many students write $O_c^0 = O_6^0$ directly — this is wrong unless $d_6 = 0$.
> 2. **Sign confusion in $\theta_3$**: The cosine law gives only the magnitude; the elbow-up/down ambiguity must be resolved explicitly using `atan2`.
> 3. **Using the wrong Euler convention**: For ZYZ wrists, $\theta_4$ and $\theta_6$ rotate about the **same axis ($z$) but at different times**. Mixing ZYZ with ZYX loses full marks.
> 4. **Not verifying solutions**: Always substitute $\theta_1, \ldots, \theta_6$ back into the forward kinematics to confirm.
> 5. **Assuming $R_3^0 = I$** in general — this is true only for $\theta_1 = \theta_2 = \theta_3 = 0$, which is a special case.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**
> - **Kinematic decoupling** splits 6-DOF inverse kinematics into two **independent** sub-problems: **position** (first 3 joints) and **orientation** (last 3 joints).
> - Applicable **only** when the last three joint axes intersect at a common point (**spherical wrist**).
> - **Wrist center equation:** $O_c^0 = O_6^0 - d_6 R_6^0 \hat{k}$ — the cornerstone formula.
> - Wrist center position depends **only on** $\theta_1, \theta_2, \theta_3$.
> - Wrist orientation depends on $R_6^3 = (R_3^0)^T R_6^0$.
> - Solve **$\theta_5$ first** (from $R_{33}^3$), then $\theta_4$ and $\theta_6$ via `atan2`.
> - **Number of solutions** for a generic decoupled manipulator: **8** (2 elbow × 2 wrist × 2 base).
> - **Real-world usage**: All major industrial robots (PUMA, ABB, KUKA, FANUC) use this approach in their controllers.
> - **Verification**: Always confirm by substituting back into forward kinematics $T_6^0$.
> - **Tool offset $d_6$** must be read from the DH-parameter table — never assumed zero unless stated.
> - The **end-effector approach vector** is given by the third column of $R_6^0$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inverse Kinematics Example - Up to 2-DoF
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inverse Kinematics Example - Up to 2-DoF

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** is the computational process of determining the **joint variables** (joint angles $\theta_i$ for revolute joints, or displacements $d_i$ for prismatic joints) of a robotic manipulator that will produce a **desired end-effector pose** (position and orientation) in the Cartesian workspace.

For a manipulator with $n$ joints, the forward kinematic relation is:

$$
\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})
$$

where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^m$ is the end-effector pose vector and $\mathbf{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the joint variable vector. **Inverse Kinematics solves the inverse mapping:**

$$
\mathbf{q} = f^{-1}(\mathbf{x})
$$

For a **2 Degrees-of-Freedom (2-DoF) planar manipulator** consisting of two revolute joints, the problem reduces to finding $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ given a target position $(x, y)$ in the plane.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Treasure Map" View

Imagine you are a **human arm** and someone places a candy on a table at a known coordinate $(x, y)$. Your eyes give you the **destination** — this is the *forward kinematic output*. Now your brain must compute **how to rotate your shoulder and elbow** to reach the candy. That brain computation is **inverse kinematics**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way:**
> - **Forward Kinematics** = "If I move my joints by $\theta_1, \theta_2$, where does my hand land?" → Easy, plug and chug.
> - **Inverse Kinematics** = "I want my hand at point $(x, y)$. What joint angles do I need?" → Hard, requires geometry, trigonometry, and often has **multiple solutions**.

For a 2-DoF arm, your "shoulder" is joint 1, your "elbow" is joint 2, and your "fingertip" is the end-effector. There are typically **two valid ways** to reach the same point — *elbow up* or *elbow down* — just as you can touch your nose with your elbow pointing up or out.

## 1.3 Physical Setup of a 2R Planar Manipulator

A standard 2-DoF planar arm has:
- **Link 1** of length $L_1$ attached to a fixed base.
- **Link 2** of length $L_2$ attached to the end of Link 1.
- Two **revolute joints** providing rotations $\theta_1$ (shoulder) and $\theta_2$ (elbow).
- Workspace = a **2D annulus** with inner radius $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ and outer radius $L_1 + L_2$.

The two critical physical parameters are the **link lengths** $L_1$ and $L_2$ (assumed known and constant for a given robot).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The 2R problem is the *simplest non-trivial* IK problem. Master it — almost every KTU question on Module 2 tests either the derivation of the closed-form solution or its application to a numerical case.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Forward Kinematics of the 2R Planar Arm (Starting Point)

The forward kinematics for the 2R planar arm is derived using the standard transformation:

$$
x = L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

These equations give the end-effector position **as a function of the joint angles** — they are *non-linear* and *coupled* (note how $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ appear together inside the cosine/sine).

## 2.2 The Inverse Problem — Strategy

We are given $x$, $y$, $L_1$, $L_2$ and must find $\theta_1, \theta_2$. The strategy is:

1. **Square and add** the two FK equations to eliminate the angle sum $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ and obtain an equation in $\theta_2$ alone (uses the law of cosines geometrically).
2. **Solve for $\theta_2$** using the inverse cosine (yields **two solutions**: elbow up and elbow down).
3. **Solve for $\theta_1$** using the two-argument arctangent function $\operatorname{atan2}(\cdot, \cdot)$ to correctly place the angle in the correct quadrant.

## 2.3 Why Two Solutions Exist (Reachability)

> [!NOTE]
> For any reachable point strictly inside the workspace annulus, there are **two valid configurations** corresponding to the elbow pointing on either side of the line joining the base to the target. On the workspace boundary ($r = L_1 + L_2$ or $r = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$), the two solutions **coincide** (singular/degenerate case).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Symbol | Closed-Form Expression |
|----------|--------|------------------------|
| Squared reach | $r^2$ | $x^2 + y^2$ |
| Reach distance | $r$ | $\sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ |
| Cosine of $\theta_2$ | $\cos\theta_2$ | $\dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}$ |
| $\theta_2$ (elbow down) | $\theta_2^{(+)}$ | $+\arccos\!\left(\dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$ |
| $\theta_2$ (elbow up) | $\theta_2^{(-)}$ | $-\arccos\!\left(\dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)$ |
| $\theta_1$ (general) | $\theta_1$ | $\operatorname{atan2}(y, x) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2\right)$ |
| Reachability condition | — | $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq L_1 + L_2$ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SCARA robot arms** used in PCB assembly and pick-and-place packaging operations are essentially 2R (plus a prismatic axis) — IK is solved in real time every few milliseconds.
- **2-DoF welding torches** and **surgical laparoscopic tools** use the same closed-form solution.
- The 2R IK solution is the foundational building block for solving 3R, 4R, and 6-DoF industrial robot IK problems (e.g., PUMA 560, KUKA KR series).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation from Forward Kinematics to Closed-Form IK

**Given:** $x, y, L_1, L_2$ (all scalars, with $L_1 > 0$, $L_2 > 0$).
**Find:** $\theta_1, \theta_2$.

### Step 1 — Start with the Forward Kinematic Equations

$$
x = L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \quad \text{(Eq. 1)}
$$

$$
y = L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \quad \text{(Eq. 2)}
$$

### Step 2 — Square Both Equations and Add

Squaring Eq. 1:

$$
x^2 = L_1^2 \cos^2(\theta_1) + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(\theta_1) \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_2^2 \cos^2(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

Squaring Eq. 2:

$$
y^2 = L_1^2 \sin^2(\theta_1) + 2 L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_1) \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_2^2 \sin^2(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

Adding the two:

$$
x^2 + y^2 = L_1^2 \underbrace{\left[\cos^2(\theta_1) + \sin^2(\theta_1)\right]}_{=1} + 2 L_1 L_2 \underbrace{\left[\cos(\theta_1)\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin(\theta_1)\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]}_{=\cos(\theta_2)} + L_2^2 \underbrace{\left[\cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)+\sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]}_{=1}
$$

> **Logic note:** The cross-product term collapses using the cosine subtraction identity $\cos(A - B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$, with $A = \theta_1 + \theta_2$ and $B = \theta_1$, giving $\cos(\theta_2)$.

Result:

$$
x^2 + y^2 = L_1^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(\theta_2) + L_2^2
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\cos(\theta_2)$

Rearranging:

$$
2 L_1 L_2 \cos(\theta_2) = x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2
$$

$$
\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} \quad \text{(Eq. 3)}
$$

### Step 4 — Take Inverse Cosine (Two Solutions)

$$
\theta_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left(\frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)
$$

The **$+$ sign** gives the *elbow-down* configuration; the **$-$ sign** gives the *elbow-up* configuration.

### Step 5 — Solve for $\theta_1$ Using $\operatorname{atan2}$

Define an auxiliary angle $\phi$ such that:

$$
\cos(\phi) = \frac{x}{r}, \qquad \sin(\phi) = \frac{y}{r}, \qquad r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}
$$

> **Logic note:** $\phi$ is the angle of the target point measured from the base, given by $\phi = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$. This is the **direction of the target from the shoulder**.

Now, rewrite Eq. 1 and Eq. 2 as:

$$
x = r \cos(\phi) = (L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)\cos\theta_1 - (L_2 \sin\theta_2)\sin\theta_1
$$

$$
y = r \sin(\phi) = (L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)\sin\theta_1 + (L_2 \sin\theta_2)\cos\theta_1
$$

Treating $\theta_1$ as the unknown and using the angle-sum identities $\cos(\alpha - \beta) = \cos\alpha\cos\beta + \sin\alpha\sin\beta$, the system collapses to:

$$
\tan(\phi - \theta_1) = \frac{L_2 \sin\theta_2}{L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2}
$$

Solving for $\theta_1$ using the **two-argument arctangent** (which handles all four quadrants):

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2\right) \quad \text{(Eq. 4)}
$$

> **Logic note:** $\operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$ returns the absolute direction $\phi$ of the target; $\operatorname{atan2}(L_2 \sin\theta_2, L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)$ returns the **offset angle** between the line to the target and link 1. Their difference is $\theta_1$.

---

## 3.2 Complete Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 2R planar arm has $L_1 = 2\,\text{m}$, $L_2 = 1\,\text{m}$. Compute $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ for the end-effector to reach the point $(x, y) = (2\,\text{m},\; 1\,\text{m})$. Give **both** elbow configurations.

### Step A — Check Reachability

$$
r = \sqrt{2^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236\,\text{m}
$$

Workspace bounds: $\vert 2 - 1 \vert = 1 \leq 2.236 \leq 2 + 1 = 3$ ✓ **Reachable.**

### Step B — Compute $\cos(\theta_2)$

$$
\cos(\theta_2) = \frac{(2)^2 + (1)^2 - (2)^2 - (1)^2}{2 \cdot 2 \cdot 1} = \frac{4 + 1 - 4 - 1}{4} = \frac{0}{4} = 0
$$

$$
\therefore \theta_2 = \pm\arccos(0) = \pm 90^\circ
$$

### Step C — Branch 1 (Elbow Down): $\theta_2 = +90^\circ$

$\sin(90^\circ) = 1$, $\cos(90^\circ) = 0$.

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(1, 2) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(1 \cdot 1,\; 2 + 1 \cdot 0\right)
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 26.565^\circ - \operatorname{atan2}(1, 2) = 26.565^\circ - 26.565^\circ = 0^\circ
$$

> **Solution 1:** $\theta_1 = 0^\circ$, $\theta_2 = +90^\circ$.

### Step D — Branch 2 (Elbow Up): $\theta_2 = -90^\circ$

$\sin(-90^\circ) = -1$, $\cos(-90^\circ) = 0$.

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(1, 2) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(1 \cdot (-1),\; 2 + 1 \cdot 0\right)
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 26.565^\circ - \operatorname{atan2}(-1, 2) = 26.565^\circ - (-26.565^\circ) = 53.130^\circ
$$

> **Solution 2:** $\theta_1 = 53.130^\circ$, $\theta_2 = -90^\circ$.

### Step E — Verification by Forward Kinematics

**Solution 1 check** ($\theta_1 = 0^\circ$, $\theta_2 = +90^\circ$):

$$
x = 2\cos(0^\circ) + 1\cos(0^\circ + 90^\circ) = 2 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 0 = 2 \checkmark
$$

$$
y = 2\sin(0^\circ) + 1\sin(0^\circ + 90^\circ) = 0 + 1 \cdot 1 = 1 \checkmark
$$

**Solution 2 check** ($\theta_1 = 53.130^\circ$, $\theta_2 = -90^\circ$):

$$
x = 2\cos(53.130^\circ) + 1\cos(53.130^\circ - 90^\circ) = 2(0.6) + 1(0.8) = 2.0 \checkmark
$$

$$
y = 2\sin(53.130^\circ) + 1\sin(53.130^\circ - 90^\circ) = 2(0.8) + 1(-0.6) = 1.0 \checkmark
$$

Both solutions reach the target — confirming the IK result.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 2R Planar Manipulator — Geometric Configuration

The following diagram shows the kinematic layout of a 2-DoF planar arm with the two valid IK solutions for a single target point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    baseNode[BASE ORIGIN O]
    joint1Node[JOINT 1 SHOULDER]
    joint2Node[JOINT 2 ELBOW]
    eeNode[END EFFECTOR P XY]
    l1Link["LINK 1 L1"]
    l2Link["LINK 2 L2"]
    branch1["BRANCH 1 ELBOW DOWN"]
    branch2["BRANCH 2 ELBOW UP"]
    targetNode["TARGET POINT XY"]

    baseNode --> joint1Node
    joint1Node --> l1Link
    l1Link --> joint2Node
    joint2Node --> l2Link
    l2Link --> eeNode
    targetNode -.-> branch1
    targetNode -.-> branch2
```

## 4.2 Block-Level IK Computation Flow

The following diagram shows the computational pipeline for solving inverse kinematics in real time for a 2R arm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputNode["INPUT TARGET XY L1 L2"]
    checkNode["REACHABILITY CHECK R BETWEEN L1 MINUS L2 AND L1 PLUS L2"]
    errorNode["ERROR POINT UNREACHABLE"]
    computeR["COMPUTE R SQUARE EQUALS X SQUARE PLUS Y SQUARE"]
    computeCos["COMPUTE COS THETA 2 USING LAW OF COSINES"]
    branchSol["TWO BRANCHES"]
    theta2Pos["THETA 2 EQUALS PLUS ARCCOS"]
    theta2Neg["THETA 2 EQUALS MINUS ARCCOS"]
    computePhi["PHI EQUALS ATAN2 OF Y X"]
    computePsi["PSI EQUALS ATAN2 OF L2 SIN THETA2 L1 PLUS L2 COS THETA2"]
    combine["THETA 1 EQUALS PHI MINUS PSI"]
    outputNode["OUTPUT THETA 1 THETA 2"]

    inputNode --> checkNode
    checkNode -->|Out of range| errorNode
    checkNode -->|In range| computeR
    computeR --> computeCos
    computeCos --> branchSol
    branchSol --> theta2Pos
    branchSol --> theta2Neg
    theta2Pos --> computePsi
    theta2Neg --> computePsi
    inputNode --> computePhi
    computePhi --> combine
    computePsi --> combine
    combine --> outputNode
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Mapping the Mathematical Pipeline

| Block | Function | Output |
|-------|----------|--------|
| Input Stage | Accept target $(x, y)$ and link lengths $L_1, L_2$ | Validated input vector |
| Reachability Check | Verify $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \le r \le L_1 + L_2$ | Boolean flag |
| $\theta_2$ Solver | Apply $\arccos$ to closed-form expression | $\pm\theta_2$ |
| $\theta_1$ Solver | Apply $\operatorname{atan2}$ decomposition | $\theta_1$ |
| Output Stage | Return joint angle vector $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$ | Robot command |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When drawing the manipulator, always label the **base frame**, the **two link vectors**, the **end-effector position vector**, and the **two joint angles** distinctly. Examiners award marks for a clean, fully-labelled free-body diagram.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define Inverse Kinematics. How is it different from Forward Kinematics in the context of a robotic manipulator?*

**Model Answer:**
Forward Kinematics computes the **end-effector position and orientation** given the **joint variables** $\mathbf{q}$, i.e., $\mathbf{x} = f(\mathbf{q})$. Inverse Kinematics does the opposite — it computes the **joint variables** $\mathbf{q}$ required to achieve a **desired end-effector pose** $\mathbf{x}$, i.e., $\mathbf{q} = f^{-1}(\mathbf{x})$. Forward kinematics has a **unique** closed-form solution; inverse kinematics may have **multiple, none, or infinitely many** solutions (e.g., 2R arm has 2 solutions for interior points).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*State the reachability condition for a 2R planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$.*

**Model Answer:**
A point $(x, y)$ is reachable by a 2R planar arm iff its distance $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$ from the base satisfies the inequality:

$$
\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq L_1 + L_2
$$

If $r$ lies strictly inside this annulus, **two** solutions exist. If $r$ is exactly on the boundary, **one** (degenerate) solution exists. If $r$ is outside, **no** solution exists.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]*

For a **2R planar manipulator** with $L_1 = 1.5\,\text{m}$ and $L_2 = 1.0\,\text{m}$:

**(a)** Derive the closed-form inverse kinematics solution for $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ in terms of the end-effector coordinates $(x, y)$ and the link lengths. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the target point $(x, y) = (1.0,\; 2.0)\,\text{m}$, compute **both** joint-angle solutions and verify them using forward kinematics. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** Write forward kinematics:

$$
x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2), \qquad y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
$$

**Step 2:** Square and add — uses identity $\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$:

$$
x^2 + y^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $\cos\theta_2$:

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}
$$

**Step 4:** Take $\arccos$ — two solutions:

$$
\theta_2 = \pm \arccos\!\left(\frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right)
$$

**Step 5:** Derive $\theta_1$ using $\operatorname{atan2}$ decomposition:

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2\right)
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Compute $r$:**

$$
r^2 = (1.0)^2 + (2.0)^2 = 1 + 4 = 5, \qquad r = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236\,\text{m}
$$

**Check reachability:** $\vert 1.5 - 1.0 \vert = 0.5 \le 2.236 \le 2.5 = 1.5 + 1.0$ ✓

**Compute $\cos\theta_2$:**

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{5 - 2.25 - 1.0}{2 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 1.0} = \frac{1.75}{3.0} = 0.5833
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \pm \arccos(0.5833) = \pm 54.32^\circ
$$

**Solution 1 (Elbow Down):** $\theta_2 = +54.32^\circ$.

$\sin\theta_2 = +0.8122$, $\cos\theta_2 = +0.5833$.

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(2.0, 1.0) - \operatorname{atan2}\!\left(1.0 \cdot 0.8122,\; 1.5 + 1.0 \cdot 0.5833\right)
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 63.435^\circ - \operatorname{atan2}(0.8122, 2.0833) = 63.435^\circ - 21.317^\circ = 42.12^\circ
$$

> **Solution 1:** $\theta_1 = 42.12^\circ$, $\theta_2 = +54.32^\circ$.

**Solution 2 (Elbow Up):** $\theta_2 = -54.32^\circ$.

$\sin\theta_2 = -0.8122$, $\cos\theta_2 = +0.5833$.

$$
\theta_1 = 63.435^\circ - \operatorname{atan2}(-0.8122, 2.0833) = 63.435^\circ - (-21.317^\circ) = 84.75^\circ
$$

> **Solution 2:** $\theta_1 = 84.75^\circ$, $\theta_2 = -54.32^\circ$.

**Forward Kinematics Verification for Solution 1:**

$$
x = 1.5 \cos(42.12^\circ) + 1.0 \cos(42.12^\circ + 54.32^\circ) = 1.5(0.7419) + 1.0\cos(96.44^\circ) = 1.113 - 0.113 = 1.000 \checkmark
$$

$$
y = 1.5 \sin(42.12^\circ) + 1.0 \sin(96.44^\circ) = 1.5(0.6704) + 1.0(0.9933) = 1.006 + 0.993 = 1.999 \approx 2.000 \checkmark
$$

**Forward Kinematics Verification for Solution 2:**

$$
x = 1.5\cos(84.75^\circ) + 1.0\cos(84.75^\circ - 54.32^\circ) = 1.5(0.0917) + 1.0\cos(30.43^\circ) = 0.138 + 0.862 = 1.000 \checkmark
$$

$$
y = 1.5\sin(84.75^\circ) + 1.0\sin(30.43^\circ) = 1.5(0.9958) + 1.0(0.5066) = 1.494 + 0.507 = 2.001 \approx 2.000 \checkmark
$$

Both solutions reach the target. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

For a 2R planar arm with $L_1 = 1.0\,\text{m}$ and $L_2 = 1.0\,\text{m}$:

**(a)** Derive the workspace annulus condition and show geometrically why two IK solutions exist for interior points. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the end-effector target $(x, y) = (1.5,\; 0.866)\,\text{m}$, determine **both** configurations $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$ that achieve this position. Show that on the workspace boundary, the two solutions collapse to a single (degenerate) configuration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Workspace Derivation:** From the IK relation, for a solution to exist, we require $\vert \cos\theta_2 \vert \leq 1$:

$$
-1 \leq \frac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2} \leq 1
$$

Solving both inequalities:

$$
-2 L_1 L_2 \leq x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2 \leq 2 L_1 L_2
$$

$$
(L_1 - L_2)^2 \leq x^2 + y^2 \leq (L_1 + L_2)^2
$$

Taking the square root (all quantities non-negative):

$$
\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \leq \sqrt{x^2 + y^2} \leq L_1 + L_2
$$

This defines an **annulus** in the $xy$-plane.

**Geometric Interpretation (Two Solutions):** Draw triangle $O$-$J_2$-$P$ (base–elbow–end-effector) with sides $L_1$, $L_2$, and $r$. For a fixed $r$ (where $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert < r < L_1 + L_2$), the elbow $J_2$ can lie on **either side** of the line $OP$. Hence two mirror-image triangles — and therefore two joint configurations — exist. On the inner boundary ($r = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$) the elbow lies collinear with $OP$ (folded), and on the outer boundary ($r = L_1 + L_2$) it also lies collinear (fully extended). In both boundary cases, the two mirror configurations **coincide**, giving a single (degenerate) solution.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Compute $r$:**

$$
r^2 = (1.5)^2 + (0.866)^2 = 2.25 + 0.75 = 3.0, \qquad r = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732\,\text{m}
$$

**Check reachability:** $\vert 1 - 1 \vert = 0 \leq 1.732 \leq 2 = 1 + 1$ ✓

**Compute $\cos\theta_2$:**

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{3.0 - 1.0 - 1.0}{2 \cdot 1.0 \cdot 1.0} = \frac{1.0}{2.0} = 0.5
$$

$$
\theta_2 = \pm \arccos(0.5) = \pm 60^\circ
$$

**Solution 1 (Elbow Down):** $\theta_2 = +60^\circ$, $\sin\theta_2 = +0.866$.

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(0.866, 1.5) - \operatorname{atan2}(1.0 \cdot 0.866,\; 1.0 + 1.0 \cdot 0.5)
$$

$$
\theta_1 = 30.00^\circ - \operatorname{atan2}(0.866, 1.5) = 30.00^\circ - 30.00^\circ = 0.00^\circ
$$

> **Solution 1:** $\theta_1 = 0.00^\circ$, $\theta_2 = +60^\circ$.

**Solution 2 (Elbow Up):** $\theta_2 = -60^\circ$, $\sin\theta_2 = -0.866$.

$$
\theta_1 = 30.00^\circ - \operatorname{atan2}(-0.866, 1.5) = 30.00^\circ - (-30.00^\circ) = 60.00^\circ
$$

> **Solution 2:** $\theta_1 = 60.00^\circ$, $\theta_2 = -60^\circ$.

**Boundary Collapse (Singular) Case:** Consider target $(2.0,\; 0.0)\,\text{m}$, i.e., $r = L_1 + L_2 = 2$:

$$
\cos\theta_2 = \frac{4 - 1 - 1}{2} = 1 \implies \theta_2 = 0^\circ \text{ (only one branch)}
$$

$$
\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(0, 2) - \operatorname{atan2}(0, 2) = 0^\circ
$$

The two configurations **collapse to a single fully-stretched configuration** — confirming the boundary case. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in IK Problems (2R Arm):**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\pm$ in $\theta_2$:** Many students report only **one** solution and lose 2–3 marks. Always state that **two configurations** exist for interior workspace points.
> 2. **Using $\tan^{-1}$ instead of $\operatorname{atan2}$:** The standard arctangent cannot distinguish between quadrants and gives wrong angles when the target lies in Q2 or Q3. The closed-form IK **must** use the two-argument arctangent $\operatorname{atan2}(y, x)$ — this is worth 1–2 marks in the KTU scheme.
> 3. **Skipping the reachability check:** Examiners in KTU 2024 specifically test whether you verify the point lies in the workspace annulus. Omitting this check costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Not verifying by forward kinematics:** A complete KTU answer should *always* substitute the computed $(\theta_1, \theta_2)$ back into the FK equations to confirm $(x, y)$. Skipping this is a 1-mark loss.
> 5. **Sign error in $\theta_1$ formula:** The second $\operatorname{atan2}$ argument is $L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2$ (a **sum**, not a difference). Inverting the sign flips the geometry and yields the wrong configuration.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inverse Kinematics (IK)** computes joint variables from a desired end-effector pose.
- For a **2R planar arm** with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ and target $(x, y)$:
  - $\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}$
  - $\theta_2 = \pm\arccos(\cdot)$ → **two** solutions (elbow up / elbow down).
  - $\theta_1 = \operatorname{atan2}(y, x) - \operatorname{atan2}(L_2 \sin\theta_2,\; L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2)$.
- **Workspace** = annulus with inner radius $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ and outer radius $L_1 + L_2$.
- Always use the **two-argument** arctangent $\operatorname{atan2}$ — never plain $\tan^{-1}$.
- Always perform a **reachability check** before solving.
- Always **verify by forward kinematics** after solving.
- The IK problem is **non-linear** and may have **zero, one, two, or infinitely many** solutions depending on the manipulator architecture and target location.
- The 2R solution is the **pedagogical prototype** for all higher-DoF closed-form IK problems in robotics.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Kinematics: Derivation of the Jacobian - Angular Velocity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Kinematics: Derivation of the Jacobian - Angular Velocity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Velocity Kinematics** of serial manipulator robots, the relationship between the **joint-space velocities** ($\dot{\theta}$) and the **linear/angular velocities of the end-effector** ($v$, $\omega$) in the task space (Cartesian space) is established through a fundamental matrix known as the **Jacobian Matrix**, denoted as $J(\theta)$.

The Angular Velocity component of the Jacobian, often referred to as the **Angular Velocity Jacobian** $J_\omega(\theta)$, is the upper $3 \times n$ partition of the full Jacobian that maps joint velocities to the angular velocity vector of the end-effector:

$$
\omega = J_\omega(\theta) \cdot \dot{\theta}
$$

where:
- $\omega \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ is the angular velocity of the end-effector expressed in the base frame.
- $\dot{\theta} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of joint velocities.
- $J_\omega(\theta)$ is the **$3 \times n$ angular velocity Jacobian matrix** whose $i$-th column $J_{\omega i}$ depends on whether joint $i$ is **revolute** or **prismatic**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Angular Velocity Jacobian is a direct outcome of differentiating the Rotation Matrix $R$ of the forward kinematics. The derivation stems from the property that $\dot{R} \cdot R^T$ is a skew-symmetric matrix representing the cross-product operator for angular velocity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Spinning Steering Wheel

Imagine you are driving a car and turning the steering wheel. The steering wheel is connected to a long **drive shaft** to the wheels. Even though your hands only rotate the wheel a little, the wheels at the far end turn *proportionally* based on the **transmission ratio**.

Similarly, each joint of a robot (think of each joint as a small "steering wheel") contributes a small rotational effect to the wrist at the end of the chain. The **Angular Velocity Jacobian** is simply the *transmission map* that tells you:

> "When joint $i$ spins at 1 rad/s (and all other joints are locked), how much angular twist does the end-effector experience, and along which axis?"

- For a **revolute joint**, spinning the joint causes a direct angular twist → the column is the **unit rotation axis** $z_{i-1}$ of that joint, expressed in the base frame.
