> **Examiner's Pitfall Note:** Many students will write "the Jacobian is singular so it cannot be inverted, hence no solution" — but the **correct** and more **valuable** answer is to *quantitatively demonstrate the singularity* by showing that the first row of $\mathbf{J}(0)$ is zero, which means the system of equations $\mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{v}$ has **no solution** for the desired $\mathbf{v}$. This shows the examiner you understand the *physical* reason, not just the algebraic failure.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Combining Linear and Angular Jacobians**

- ✅ The **Geometric Jacobian** is a **6 × n** matrix for spatial (3D) manipulators, with the **top 3 rows = $\mathbf{J}_v$** (linear) and **bottom 3 rows = $\mathbf{J}_\omega$** (angular).
- ✅ The *i*-th column of $\mathbf{J}$ corresponds to **joint *i*** and is constructed by the **joint-type rules**:
  - **Revolute (R):** $\mathbf{J}_{v,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1} \times (\mathbf{p}_{EE} - \mathbf{p}_{i-1})$; $\mathbf{J}_{\omega,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$.
  - **Prismatic (P):** $\mathbf{J}_{v,i} = \mathbf{z}_{i-1}$; $\mathbf{J}_{\omega,i} = \mathbf{0}_{3\times 1}$.
- ✅ $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ and $\mathbf{p}_{i-1}$ are extracted from the **DH frame** $\{i-1\}$ — the **third column** of $_ {i-1}^0 R$ gives $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$, and the **fourth column** of $_ {i-1}^0 T$ gives $\mathbf{p}_{i-1}$.
- ✅ The **spatial velocity** is defined as $\mathbf{v} = [\mathbf{v}_l^T, \boldsymbol{\omega}^T]^T \in \mathbb{R}^6$, with the **mapping** $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- ✅ **Singularity** condition: $\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}) < 6$ (for full spatial motion) or $< \min(m,n)$ in general. Detect via $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0$.
- ✅ **Manipulability** (Yoshikawa): $\mu = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)}$ — higher $\mu$ means better dexterity, away from singularities.
- ✅ **Inverse velocity** (square robots): $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}\mathbf{v}$. **Redundant robots** ($n > 6$): use **pseudoinverse** $\mathbf{J}^{\dagger} = \mathbf{J}^T(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)^{-1}$.
- ✅ **Static force** relation: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$ — requires the **full 6×n Jacobian** (not just $\mathbf{J}_v$).
- ✅ For **planar** manipulators, the angular velocity has only a **z-component** (out-of-plane), so $\mathbf{J}_\omega$ is often a $1 \times n$ row in 2D problems — the *form* stays 6×n but many rows are zero.
- ✅ **Common exam trap:** Confusing the joint-axis frame index: column *i* uses $\mathbf{z}_{i-1}$ (not $\mathbf{z}_i$). Always start indexing from **0** for the base frame.
- ✅ **Common exam trap #2:** Forgetting to express $\mathbf{p}_{EE}$ in the **base frame** (frame 0). The Jacobian is **frame-dependent** — KTU problems always use the base frame unless explicitly told otherwise.
- ✅ The Geometric Jacobian is **configuration-dependent**, not constant. It must be re-evaluated at every $\mathbf{q}$ along a trajectory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Velocity Jacobian Examples Given Forward Kinematic Matrices - 2 Link Planar Manipulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Velocity Jacobian Examples Given Forward Kinematic Matrices - 2 Link Planar Manipulator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

The **Velocity Jacobian (J)** of a robotic manipulator is a matrix that maps the **joint-space velocity vector** ($\dot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$) to the **task-space (Cartesian) velocity vector** of the end-effector ($\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$). For an $n$-DOF manipulator, the relationship is:

$$\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

For a **2-Link Planar Manipulator** (RR configuration), the Jacobian is a $3 \times 2$ matrix — three task variables ($x, y, \phi$) for two revolute joints ($\theta_1, \theta_2$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Check:** The Jacobian used in robotics is the **Analytic Jacobian**, derived from the partial derivatives of the forward kinematic position/orientation functions. Do not confuse it with the Geometric Jacobian used in advanced courses (Spong et al., Chapter 4).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Steering Wheel of a Robot Arm"

Imagine driving a car with **two knobs** instead of a steering wheel and pedals:
- The first knob turns the front wheels (joint $\theta_1$).
- The second knob controls the extension of a telescopic arm (joint $\theta_2$).

When you twist these knobs, the car's tip (end-effector) moves on the road. The **Jacobian is the translation chart** that tells you: *“If you twist knob 1 at 10°/s and knob 2 at 5°/s, the tip will move 7.5 cm/s to the right and 2.1 cm/s upward, while rotating at 15°/s.”*

In short — the Jacobian is the **"gearbox ratio"** between joint motion and end-effector motion.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Variables for the 2-Link Planar Manipulator:**
> - **Link 1 length:** $L_1$ (m) — distance from base to elbow joint.
> - **Link 2 length:** $L_2$ (m) — distance from elbow to end-effector.
> - **Joint angles:** $\theta_1, \theta_2$ (radians) — measured from the base $x$-axis.
> - **Workspace area:** $\leq (L_1 + L_2)^2$ — circular annulus.
> - **Singularity threshold:** $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = 0$, occurs when $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\theta_2 = \pi$ (arm fully stretched or folded).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The derivation of the Velocity Jacobian from the Forward Kinematic matrix follows a strict 5-step algorithm:

1. **Establish the Base Frame and Link Frames** — assign frames $\{0\}$, $\{1\}$, $\{2\}$ at the base, elbow, and end-effector respectively.
2. **Construct the Homogeneous Transformation Matrices** $A_1$ and $A_2$ (or use DH parameters).
3. **Compute the Composite Forward Kinematic Matrix** $T_0^2 = A_1 A_2$.
4. **Extract the Position Vector** $\mathbf{p} = [x, y]^T$ and Orientation $\phi$ from $T_0^2$.
5. **Differentiate with Respect to Time** — apply the chain rule $\frac{\partial \mathbf{p}}{\partial \mathbf{q}} \dot{\mathbf{q}}$ to build the Jacobian.

### 2.1.1 Why Each Step Matters

- **Step 1** ensures coordinate consistency.
- **Step 2** encodes the geometry (lengths and twist angles).
- **Step 3** yields the global pose of the end-effector.
- **Step 4** isolates the variables that matter for the task space.
- **Step 5** is the heart of differential kinematics — it linearizes the non-linear geometric mapping at the current configuration $\mathbf{q}$.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Equation** | **Description** | **Units** |
|:-:|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | $x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | End-effector X-coordinate | meters (m) |
| 2 | $y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ | End-effector Y-coordinate | meters (m) |
| 3 | $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$ | End-effector orientation angle | radians (rad) |
| 4 | $v_x = \dot{x} = -L_1\sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2)$ | Linear velocity along X | m/s |
| 5 | $v_y = \dot{y} = L_1\cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2)$ | Linear velocity along Y | m/s |
| 6 | $\omega = \dot\phi = \dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2$ | Angular velocity of end-effector | rad/s |
| 7 | $\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} J_{11} & J_{12} \\ J_{21} & J_{22} \\ J_{31} & J_{32} \end{bmatrix}$ | Velocity Jacobian (3×2) | mixed |
| 8 | $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Fundamental velocity mapping | — |
| 9 | $\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T) = L_1^2 L_2^2 \sin^2\theta_2$ | Manipulability measure (numerator) | m⁴ |
| 10 | Singularity: $\theta_2 = 0$ or $\theta_2 = \pi$ | Configuration where Jacobian loses rank | rad |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Symbol Warning:** In the KTU Formula Sheet above, $\vert$ inside math expressions has been replaced with absolute-value safe LaTeX (e.g., using $\det$ notation) to keep markdown table syntax intact.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Velocity Jacobian is the cornerstone of:
- **Resolved-rate motion control** — converting a desired Cartesian trajectory into joint commands.
- **Force control** — using the transpose $\mathbf{J}^T$ to map end-effector forces to joint torques.
- **Singularity avoidance algorithms** — used in surgical robots (e.g., Da Vinci), welding arms (KUKA, ABB), and pick-and-place SCARA systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the 2-Link Planar Jacobian

### Step 1 — Forward Kinematic Position Equations

Consider a planar 2R manipulator with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$ measured from the base $x$-axis. Using basic trigonometry, the end-effector position $(x, y)$ is:

$$x = L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$y = L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

**Logic:** The first term is the position of the elbow joint, and the second term is the contribution of link 2 with angle $\theta_1 + \theta_2$ relative to the base.

The end-effector orientation in the plane is:

$$\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$$

**Logic:** Both links contribute to the orientation since each revolute joint rotates the link plane.

### Step 2 — Time Differentiation of Position

Differentiating $x$ with respect to time using the chain rule $\frac{d}{dt}\cos(f) = -\sin(f) \cdot \dot{f}$:

$$v_x = \dot{x} = -L_1 \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot\theta_1 \;-\; L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2)$$

**Logic:** Two terms arise from the chain rule applied to compound argument $(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$.

Similarly, differentiating $y$ using $\frac{d}{dt}\sin(f) = \cos(f) \cdot \dot{f}$:

$$v_y = \dot{y} = L_1 \cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot\theta_1 \;+\; L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2)$$

**Logic:** The sign flips because the derivative of sine is cosine, which carries a positive coefficient.

The angular velocity of the end-effector is:

$$\omega = \dot{\phi} = \dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2$$

**Logic:** Direct differentiation of the orientation equation.

### Step 3 — Grouping by Joint Velocities $\dot\theta_1$ and $\dot\theta_2$

Rearranging $v_x$ by collecting $\dot\theta_1$ and $\dot\theta_2$ terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_x &= -L_1 \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot\theta_1 \;-\; L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cdot \dot\theta_1 \;-\; L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cdot \dot\theta_2 \\
v_x &= \underbrace{\big[-L_1 \sin\theta_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\big]}_{J_{11}} \cdot \dot\theta_1 \;+\; \underbrace{\big[-L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\big]}_{J_{12}} \cdot \dot\theta_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** The coefficients of $\dot\theta_1$ and $\dot\theta_2$ are the first-row entries of the Jacobian.

Similarly for $v_y$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_y &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot\theta_1 \;+\; L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cdot \dot\theta_1 \;+\; L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cdot \dot\theta_2 \\
v_y &= \underbrace{\big[L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\big]}_{J_{21}} \cdot \dot\theta_1 \;+\; \underbrace{\big[L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\big]}_{J_{22}} \cdot \dot\theta_2
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** The coefficients of $\dot\theta_1$ and $\dot\theta_2$ are the second-row entries of the Jacobian.

### Step 4 — Assembling the Final Velocity Jacobian

The angular velocity is already in the form:

$$\omega = (1) \cdot \dot\theta_1 + (1) \cdot \dot\theta_2$$

Stacking all three rows yields the **Velocity Jacobian**:

$$\mathbf{J}(\theta_1, \theta_2) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\[4pt] \;\;L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \;\;L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\[4pt] 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

The compact mapping:

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \;\;L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \;\;L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot\theta_1 \\ \dot\theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Logic:** This $3 \times 2$ matrix has rank 2 in non-singular configurations — three task variables over-constrained by two joints, a key reason 2-link planar manipulators cannot reach any arbitrary pose $(x, y, \phi)$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Numerical:** Given $L_1 = 1$ m, $L_2 = 0.5$ m, $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, $\dot\theta_1 = 1$ rad/s, $\dot\theta_2 = 0.5$ rad/s. Compute the end-effector velocity vector.

**Computation of intermediate angles:**

$$\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 30° + 45° = 75°$$

**Substituting numerical values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
v_x &= -1 \cdot \sin 30° - 0.5 \cdot \sin 75° \big) \cdot 1 \;+\; (-0.5 \cdot \sin 75°) \cdot 0.5 \\
v_x &= (-0.5000) - 0.5 \cdot 0.9659 \;+\; (-0.4829) \cdot 0.5 \\
v_x &= -0.5000 - 0.4830 - 0.2415 \\
v_x &= -1.2245 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
v_y &= (1 \cdot \cos 30° + 0.5 \cdot \cos 75°) \cdot 1 \;+\; (0.5 \cdot \cos 75°) \cdot 0.5 \\
v_y &= (0.8660 + 0.1294) + (0.0647) \\
v_y &= 1.0601 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\omega = \dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2 = 1 + 0.5 = 1.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Final Velocity Vector:**

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.2245 \\ \;\;1.0601 \\ 1.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Logic check:** The negative $v_x$ indicates the end-effector is moving leftward, while the positive $v_y$ shows upward motion, consistent with the chosen joint rotation directions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Kinematic Block Diagram of the 2-Link Planar Manipulator

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the conceptual data flow from joint space to Cartesian space via the Jacobian.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Joint Angles q = theta1 theta2"] --> B["Forward Kinematics FK"]
    A --> C["Velocity Jacobian J q"]
    D["Joint Velocities qdot = theta1dot theta2dot"] --> C
    B --> E["End Effector Pose x y phi"]
    C --> F["End Effector Velocity vx vy omega"]
    E --> G["Task Space Cartesian Frame"]
    F --> G
    H["Manipulability Index"] --> C
    I["Singularity Detector"] --> C
    I --> J{"theta2 equals 0 or pi"}
    J --> K["Singular Configuration"]
    J --> L["Non Singular Configuration"]
```

## 4.2 Physical Schematic of the 2-Link Planar Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BaseFrame["Base Frame frame 0"]
        O0["Origin frame 0 at base"]
    end
    subgraph Link1["Link 1 length L1"]
        J1["Revolute Joint 1 at angle theta1"]
        L1body["Rigid Link L1"]
    end
    subgraph ElbowFrame["Elbow Frame frame 1"]
        O1["Origin frame 1 at elbow"]
    end
    subgraph Link2["Link 2 length L2"]
        J2["Revolute Joint 2 at angle theta2"]
        L2body["Rigid Link L2"]
    end
    subgraph EEFrame["End Effector Frame frame 2"]
        O2["Origin frame 2 at end effector"]
        phi["Orientation phi equals theta1 plus theta2"]
    end
    O0 --> J1
    J1 --> L1body
    L1body --> O1
    O1 --> J2
    J2 --> L2body
    L2body --> O2
    O2 --> phi
```

## 4.3 Jacobian Derivation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1 Write Forward Kinematics x y phi"] --> S2["Step 2 Differentiate wrt time"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Apply Chain Rule"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4 Group Terms by qdot1 and qdot2"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5 Assemble 3x2 Jacobian Matrix J"]
    S5 --> S6["Output v equals J times qdot"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above comply with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE rules: all node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`), no reserved keywords are used as node names, and labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text without bold, italic, or HTML tags.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Velocity Jacobian of a robotic manipulator. What is its physical significance?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** The Velocity Jacobian $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the matrix of first-order partial derivatives of the end-effector's position and orientation variables with respect to the joint variables.
- **(1 mark)** It linearly maps joint-space velocities $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ to task-space velocities $\mathbf{v}$ via the relation $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- **(1 mark)** Physically, it quantifies how end-effector motion responds instantaneously to joint motion, and is used for resolved-rate motion control, singularity detection, and force transformation via its transpose.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**For a 2-link planar manipulator, state the condition for a singular configuration.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** A singular configuration occurs when the manipulator Jacobian loses full rank, i.e., $\text{rank}(\mathbf{J}) < 2$ for a 2-link arm.
- **(1 mark)** Mathematically, the manipulability measure becomes zero: $L_1^2 L_2^2 \sin^2\theta_2 = 0$, hence $\sin\theta_2 = 0$.
- **(1 mark)** Therefore, singularity occurs when $\theta_2 = 0°$ (arm fully stretched out) or $\theta_2 = 180°$ (arm fully folded back). At these configurations, the end-effector loses the ability to move in certain directions.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — June 2024] (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the forward kinematic position equations for a 2-link planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$ and joint angles $\theta_1, \theta_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the equations in part (a), derive the Velocity Jacobian matrix $\mathbf{J}$ in terms of $L_1, L_2, \theta_1, \theta_2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the link frames. Frame $\{0\}$ at the base, frame $\{1\}$ at the elbow, frame $\{2\}$ at the end-effector.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Write the position of the elbow joint (origin of frame 1) in base frame coordinates:

$$x_1 = L_1 \cos\theta_1, \qquad y_1 = L_1 \sin\theta_1$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** The end-effector position is the elbow position plus the contribution of link 2 oriented at angle $\theta_1 + \theta_2$:

$$x = x_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

$$y = y_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** The orientation of the end-effector is the sum of joint angles:

$$\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Differentiate $x$ and $y$ with respect to time using the chain rule. For the X-coordinate:

$$v_x = -L_1\sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2)$$

For the Y-coordinate:

$$v_y = L_1\cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2)$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Differentiate the orientation:

$$\omega = \dot\phi = \dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Group terms by $\dot\theta_1$ and $\dot\theta_2$:

$$v_x = [-L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]\dot\theta_1 + [-L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]\dot\theta_2$$

$$v_y = [L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)]\dot\theta_1 + [L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)]\dot\theta_2$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Assemble the Jacobian matrix and state the final equation:

$$\begin{bmatrix} v_x \\ v_y \\ \omega \end{bmatrix} = \underbrace{\begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ \;\;L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \;\;L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}_{\mathbf{J}(\theta_1,\theta_2)} \begin{bmatrix} \dot\theta_1 \\ \dot\theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** For a 2-link planar manipulator with $L_1 = 1$ m, $L_2 = 0.8$ m, $\theta_1 = 60°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$, compute the position $(x, y)$ and orientation $(\phi)$ of the end-effector. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the joint velocities are $\dot\theta_1 = 2$ rad/s and $\dot\theta_2 = -1$ rad/s, find the end-effector linear and angular velocities using the Velocity Jacobian. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the input values: $L_1 = 1$ m, $L_2 = 0.8$ m, $\theta_1 = 60°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$, so $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 90°$.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the X-coordinate:

$$x = 1 \cdot \cos 60° + 0.8 \cdot \cos 90° = 0.5 + 0 = 0.5 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the Y-coordinate:

$$y = 1 \cdot \sin 60° + 0.8 \cdot \sin 90° = 0.8660 + 0.8 = 1.6660 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the orientation:

$$\phi = 60° + 30° = 90° = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ rad}$$

**Final pose:** $(x, y, \phi) = (0.5 \text{ m}, 1.6660 \text{ m}, \pi/2 \text{ rad})$.

#### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Build the Jacobian numerically using the angles $60°$ and $90°$:

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -1\cdot\sin 60° - 0.8\cdot\sin 90° & -0.8\cdot\sin 90° \\ \;\;1\cdot\cos 60° + 0.8\cdot\cos 90° & \;\;0.8\cdot\cos 90° \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Substitute trigonometric values:

$$\mathbf{J} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.8660 - 0.8 & -0.8 \\ 0.5 + 0 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.6660 & -0.8 \\ 0.5 & 0 \\ 1 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Multiply by the joint velocity vector $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = [2, -1]^T$:

$$v_x = (-1.6660)(2) + (-0.8)(-1) = -3.3320 + 0.8 = -2.5320 \text{ m/s}$$

$$v_y = (0.5)(2) + (0)(-1) = 1.0 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute the angular velocity:

$$\omega = (1)(2) + (1)(-1) = 1.0 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Final velocity vector:** $\mathbf{v} = [-2.5320, 1.0, 1.0]^T$ (units: m/s, m/s, rad/s).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Sign Errors in Trig Derivatives:** The most common error is mixing up $\frac{d}{dt}\cos\theta = -\sin\theta \cdot \dot\theta$ with $\frac{d}{dt}\sin\theta = +\cos\theta \cdot \dot\theta$. A single sign flip in $J_{11}$ or $J_{21}$ will cost **2 marks** in the valuation key.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Compound Angle Rule:** When differentiating $\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$, you MUST apply the chain rule to BOTH $\theta_1$ AND $\theta_2$. Writing only $-\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \cdot \dot\theta_1$ is incomplete and forfeits **1–2 marks**.
>
> 3. **Confusing Jacobian Rows:** $J_{11}$ and $J_{12}$ belong to $v_x$ (the first row). Many students swap rows with columns, producing a $2 \times 3$ matrix — this is structurally wrong and loses **full marks** for the matrix assembly step.
>
> 4. **Units Mismatch:** Forgetting to specify units (m/s, rad/s) in the final answer results in a **0.5 mark deduction** in strict valuation.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Orientation Row:** Forgetting $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$ and the third row of the Jacobian (with 1's) loses **1 mark** — the Jacobian for planar manipulators is $3 \times 2$, not $2 \times 2$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Velocity Jacobian Definition:** $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — linear map from joint velocity to Cartesian velocity.
- **2-Link Planar Forward Kinematics:** $x = L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)$; $y = L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)$; $\phi = \theta_1 + \theta_2$.
- **Jacobian Size:** $3 \times 2$ — three task variables $(x, y, \phi)$ over two joints.
- **Jacobian Rows:** $J_{1*} = \partial x/\partial\theta$, $J_{2*} = \partial y/\partial\theta$, $J_{3*} = \partial \phi/\partial\theta$.
- **Singularities:** Occur at $\theta_2 = 0, \pi$ (arm fully extended or folded). Determined by $\sin\theta_2 = 0$.
- **Manipulability Index:** $w = \sqrt{\det(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{J}^T)} = \vert L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_2 \vert$ — measures distance from singularity.
- **Geometric Interpretation:** Column 1 of $\mathbf{J}$ is the linear velocity of the end-effector when only $\theta_1$ moves (with $\theta_2$ fixed); Column 2 is the velocity when only $\theta_2$ moves.
- **Inverse Velocity Kinematics:** When $\mathbf{J}$ is non-singular, $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{+}\mathbf{v}$ where $\mathbf{J}^{+} = (\mathbf{J}^T\mathbf{J})^{-1}\mathbf{J}^T$ is the Moore–Penrose pseudo-inverse.
- **Time Derivative Trick:** Always apply the chain rule to compound arguments like $(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$ carefully.
- **Practical Use:** Resolved-rate motion control, Cartesian trajectory tracking, singularity avoidance, and force-torque mapping via $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{J}^T \mathbf{F}$.
- **KTU 2024 Weightage:** Velocity Jacobian derivations typically carry **7–14 marks** in Part B; singularity questions appear in Part A for 2–3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Singularities - Definition and Importance of Identifying Singularities
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Singularities - Definition and Importance of Identifying Singularities

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of robotic manipulators, a **singularity** (also called a *kinematic singularity* or *degenerate configuration*) is a specific pose or configuration of the robot's end-effector in its task space at which the **Jacobian matrix (J) loses its full rank**. Mathematically, the manipulator is said to be in a singular configuration when:

$$ \det(J(\theta)) = 0 $$

At this configuration, the manipulator momentarily loses one or more of its **degrees of freedom (DOF)** in the Cartesian (task) space, meaning the end-effector cannot move or generate velocity along certain directions, even though the actuators are still capable of producing joint motions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Examiner Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> A singularity is a configuration of the manipulator at which the **Jacobian matrix is rank-deficient** (i.e., its determinant becomes zero), causing the loss of instantaneous mobility in one or more Cartesian directions and rendering the inverse Jacobian mathematically undefined.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to **push open a heavy door** by pressing your **outstretched arm** against it, with your elbow fully straightened.

- Your **shoulder joint** can rotate freely, but because your elbow is locked straight, your hand can only move along the **arc of your shoulder** — you cannot push the hand directly forward or backward in a straight line.
- From the door's perspective, your "robot arm" has lost its ability to move in one direction. It is, in a sense, "stuck."
- The same phenomenon happens in a robotic arm when it is fully extended or folded back on itself.

**Another everyday example:** Think of a **broomstick held vertically on your palm**. The broomstick is in an *unstable equilibrium* — a tiny disturbance causes it to fall, and in that exact vertical position, the broomstick can fall in *any* direction (or, conversely, cannot be controlled to stay still). This is conceptually similar to how a robot at singularity cannot be precisely controlled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Singularities are NOT defects in the robot — they are **inherent mathematical properties** of the kinematic chain. Every serial manipulator (with certain joint types) will have at least one or more singular configurations in its workspace. The engineer's job is to **identify, avoid, or manage** them.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in KTU Robotics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Manipulability Index | $w$ | $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^T)}$ — drops to **zero at singularity** |
| Condition Number | $\kappa(J)$ | $\kappa = \sigma_{max} / \sigma_{min}$ — **$\kappa \to \infty$ at singularity** |
| Smallest Singular Value | $\sigma_{min}$ | **Tends to 0** as singularity is approached |
| Joint Velocity Limit | $\dot{\theta}_{max}$ | Often **2 rad/s** for industrial joints |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall Trick for KTU Exams:** "**Determinant zero = DOF loss**." Whenever you see $\det(J) = 0$, immediately think "singularity → loss of motion in some direction."
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does a Singularity Occur? — The Underlying Logic

The relationship between joint-space velocity $\dot{\theta}$ and task-space (Cartesian) velocity $\dot{x}$ is given by the **fundamental velocity equation of robotics**:

$$ \dot{x} = J(\theta) \cdot \dot{\theta} $$

To find the joint velocities required to achieve a desired end-effector velocity (used in resolved-rate motion control, trajectory tracking, and inverse kinematics), we need the **inverse Jacobian**:

$$ \dot{\theta} = J^{-1}(\theta) \cdot \dot{x} $$

This inverse exists **only when the Jacobian is square and non-singular** (i.e., $\det(J) \neq 0$). When $\det(J) = 0$:

- $J^{-1}$ does **not exist** in the conventional sense.
- Small Cartesian velocities $\dot{x}$ may demand **infinite joint velocities** $\dot{\theta}$.
- The manipulator **physically cannot move** in certain Cartesian directions, no matter how the joints are driven.

---

## 2.2 Structured Logic Steps — The "Why" and "How"

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics:** Joint angles $\theta$ are mapped to end-effector pose $x$ via $x = f(\theta)$.

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. time:** This gives $\dot{x} = J(\theta)\dot{\theta}$, where $J = \frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta}$.

**Step 3 — Detect Singularity:** Check the rank of $J(\theta)$. If $\text{rank}(J) < \min(m, n)$ where $m$ is the task-space dimension and $n$ is the number of joints, the manipulator is in a singular configuration.

**Step 4 — Physical Consequence:** The null space of $J$ becomes non-trivial, meaning there exist non-zero joint velocities $\dot{\theta} \neq 0$ that produce **zero end-effector velocity** $\dot{x} = 0$. These are called **self-motions** or **internal motions** of the manipulator.

**Step 5 — Control Consequence:** A small desired $\dot{x}$ along the singular direction requires $\dot{\theta} \to \infty$, which is impossible for physical actuators (saturate at $\dot{\theta}_{max}$).

---

## 2.3 Classification of Singularities (KTU High-Yield)

Singularities are broadly classified into **three types** based on their location in the workspace and the joints involved:

| # | Type | Description | Common Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Boundary (Workspace-Edge) Singularities** | Occur when the manipulator is **fully stretched out** or **fully folded back** on itself. The end-effector lies on the boundary of the reachable workspace. | Outer/inner reach envelope of the arm |
| 2 | **Interior (In-Workspace) Singularities** | Occur **inside the workspace** due to alignment of two or more joint axes. Common in **elbow-type** 6-DOF arms. | Inside the reachable workspace |
| 3 | **Wrist Singularities** | Occur when **two wrist joint axes become collinear**, causing the wrist to lose one rotational DOF (alignment of joints 4 & 6, or 5 in line with 4 & 6). | Last three (wrist) joints of a spherical-wrist manipulator |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Question:** "Differentiate between boundary, interior, and wrist singularities with a 2-link planar arm example." — Practise this!

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J(\theta) = \frac{\partial f(\theta)}{\partial \theta}$ | Definition of geometric Jacobian | Velocity mapping: $\dot{x} = J\dot{\theta}$ |
| 2 | $\det(J) = 0$ | **Singularity condition** | Identify singular configurations |
| 3 | $\text{rank}(J) < n$ | Rank-deficient condition | Loss of DOF |
| 4 | $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^T)}$ | **Yoshikawa's Manipulability Index** | Measure of dexterity; $w = 0$ at singularity |
| 5 | $\sigma_{min}(J) \to 0$ | Smallest singular value | Proximity to singularity |
| 6 | $\kappa(J) = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{min}}$ | Condition number | Robustness of inverse mapping |
| 7 | $J^{-1} = \frac{\text{adj}(J)}{\det(J)}$ | Inverse via adjugate | Diverges as $\det(J) \to 0$ |
| 8 | $v = \omega \times r$ | Rigid body velocity relation | Used in Jacobian derivation |

> **Notation Used:** $\vert \cdot \vert$ (when needed for absolute value) is rendered as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking the markdown tables.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Identifying singularities is **not an academic exercise** — it is a **critical safety and performance requirement** in industry:

- **Path Planning:** Trajectory planners (e.g., ROS MoveIt) explicitly check for and avoid singular configurations to ensure smooth, executable paths.
- **Force Control:** At singularities, joint torques can also become indeterminate in certain directions, making force-sensitive tasks unsafe.
- **Surgery Robots (e.g., Da Vinci):** Surgeons must be warned of approaching singularities to prevent jerky or uncontrollable instrument motion.
- **Welding/Painting Robots:** Tool-path algorithms reroute paths around singularities to maintain constant tool velocity.
- **Collaborative Robots (Cobots):** Real-time singularity detection allows the controller to switch control modes (e.g., from Cartesian to joint-space) seamlessly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Truth:** A robot is not "broken" at a singularity — it is **mathematically constrained**. The controller simply needs a different strategy (e.g., damped least-squares inverse, or Jacobian pseudo-inverse with regularization) to handle it.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: 2-Link Planar Manipulator (RR Configuration)

To make the concept concrete, we derive the Jacobian for a **2-link planar manipulator** with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$, and identify its singular configuration.

### 3.1.1 Forward Kinematics

The end-effector position $(x, y)$ is given by:

$$ x = L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

$$ y = L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

**Logic:** This is a standard planar RR chain where the second link's angle is measured relative to the first link.

### 3.1.2 Compute the Jacobian

The Jacobian is the partial derivative of the position vector with respect to the joint angles:

$$ J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} & \dfrac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} \\[2mm] \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} & \dfrac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step-by-step partial differentiation:**

$$ \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_1} = -L_1 \sin(\theta_1) - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

$$ \frac{\partial x}{\partial \theta_2} = -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

$$ \frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_1} = L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

$$ \frac{\partial y}{\partial \theta_2} = L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

Therefore, the Jacobian matrix is:

$$ J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin(\theta_1) - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\[2mm] \;\;\;L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & \;\;\;L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix} $$

### 3.1.3 Compute the Determinant

The determinant of a 2×2 matrix $\begin{bmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{bmatrix}$ is $ad - bc$.

**Expansion:**

$$ \det(J) = \left[-L_1 \sin(\theta_1) - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] \cdot \left[L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] $$

$$ - \left[-L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] \cdot \left[L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\right] $$

**Simplify each term:**

$$ \text{Term 1} = -L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_1)\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) - L_2^2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

$$ \text{Term 2} = +L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cos(\theta_1) + L_2^2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

**Notice:** The $L_2^2$ terms cancel out:

$$ \text{Term 1 (} L_2^2 \text{ part)} = -L_2^2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

$$ \text{Term 2 (} L_2^2 \text{ part)} = +L_2^2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

These cancel, leaving:

$$ \det(J) = -L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_1)\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) + L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cos(\theta_1) $$

**Factor out** $L_1 L_2$:

$$ \det(J) = L_1 L_2 \left[ \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cos(\theta_1) - \sin(\theta_1)\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \right] $$

**Apply the trigonometric identity:** $\sin(A + B)\cos(A) - \sin(A)\cos(A + B) = \sin(B)$ (a form of $\sin(A+B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$ rearranged).

Setting $A = \theta_1$ and $B = \theta_2$:

$$ \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)\cos(\theta_1) - \sin(\theta_1)\cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) = \sin(\theta_2) $$

Therefore:

$$ \boxed{\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2)} $$

### 3.1.4 Identify the Singular Configuration

Setting the determinant to zero:

$$ L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2) = 0 $$

Since link lengths $L_1, L_2 > 0$ (physical assumption):

$$ \sin(\theta_2) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 0 \text{ or } \theta_2 = \pi \quad (\text{or } 0° \text{ or } 180°) $$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- $\theta_2 = 0$ (or $0°$): The arm is **fully stretched out** — end-effector is at the **outer boundary** of the workspace. This is a **boundary singularity**.
- $\theta_2 = \pi$ (or $180°$): The arm is **fully folded back** on itself — end-effector is at the **inner boundary**. This is also a **boundary singularity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** For a 2-DOF planar arm, ALL singularities are boundary singularities. Interior singularities require more degrees of freedom (typically 6-DOF arms with proper offset).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Singularity on Velocity Mapping

| Quantity | Near Singularity (e.g., $\theta_2 \to 0$) | At Singularity ($\theta_2 = 0$) |
|---|---|---|
| $\det(J)$ | $\to 0$ (very small) | $= 0$ exactly |
| $J^{-1}$ | Exists but entries are huge | **Undefined** (blows up) |
| Required $\dot{\theta}$ for given $\dot{x}$ | **Very large** | **Infinite** (theoretical) |
| Manipulability $w$ | $\to 0$ | $= 0$ |
| Condition number $\kappa$ | $\to \infty$ | $\to \infty$ |
| Physical motion along singular direction | Possible but with very high joint speeds | **Blocked** |

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this for the KTU exam:** The relationship "**small $\det(J) \Rightarrow$ large joint velocities**" is the most important practical consequence of singularity.

---

## 3.3 Damped Least-Squares (DLS) Inverse — A Practical Solution

To handle singularities, Nakamura & Hanafusa (1986) proposed the **Damped Least-Squares (DLS)** inverse of the Jacobian:

$$ J^{\dagger} = J^T (J J^T + \lambda^2 I)^{-1} $$

where $\lambda > 0$ is a **damping factor** (small positive scalar, typically $\lambda \approx 0.01$ to $0.1$).

**Effect:** As the manipulator approaches a singularity, instead of $J^{-1}$ blowing up, the DLS inverse gives a **bounded, smooth approximation** that trades off exact trajectory tracking for feasible joint velocities. This is widely used in modern robot controllers.

$$ \dot{\theta} = J^{\dagger} \dot{x} = J^T (J J^T + \lambda^2 I)^{-1} \dot{x} $$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Singularity Analysis Pipeline

The following block diagram illustrates the **functional flow** for detecting and handling singularities in a robotic manipulator controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current Joint Angles theta] --> B[Compute Forward Kinematics]
    B --> C[Compute Jacobian J theta]
    C --> D[Compute det J]
    D --> E{det J Approx 0?}
    E -- No --> F[Normal Mode: Use J inverse]
    E -- Yes --> G[Singularity Detected]
    G --> H[Select Robust Inverse Method]
    H --> H1[J transpose]
    H --> H2[Damped Least Squares]
    H --> H3[Singular Value Decomposition]
    H1 --> I[Generate Joint Velocities theta dot]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    F --> I
    I --> J[Send Commands to Actuators]
    G --> K[Issue Warning to Operator]
    K --> L[Path Planner Reroute]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Classification of Singularities

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[Kinematic Singularities] --> S1[Boundary Singularities]
    S --> S2[Interior Singularities]
    S --> S3[Wrist Singularities]
    S1 --> S1a[Arm Fully Stretched]
    S1 --> S1b[Arm Fully Folded]
    S2 --> S2a[Two Joint Axes Aligned]
    S2 --> S2b[Common in 6 DOF Arms]
    S3 --> S3a[Joints 4 and 6 Collinear]
    S3 --> S3b[Spherical Wrist Alignment]
    S1a --> X1[End effector at Workspace Edge]
    S1b --> X1
    S2a --> X2[Loss of DOF Inside Workspace]
    S3a --> X3[Loss of Wrist Rotation]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic — 2-Link Planar Arm at Singular Configuration

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **2-link planar RR arm** in its two singular configurations (fully extended and fully folded), as derived in Section 3.1.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Configuration A: Fully Stretched]
        O1((Base)) ---|L1| J1a((Shoulder))
        J1a ---|L2| E1((End Effector))
    end
    subgraph S2[Configuration B: Fully Folded]
        O2((Base)) ---|L1| J2b((Shoulder))
        J2b ---|L2| E2((End Effector))
        E2 -. Overlaps L1 .-> O2
    end
    subgraph S3[Configuration C: Normal Interior]
        O3((Base)) ---|L1| J3c((Shoulder))
        J3c ---|L2| E3((End Effector))
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **Configuration A**, the end-effector lies on the **outer boundary** of the reachable circular workspace (radius $L_1 + L_2$). In **Configuration B**, it lies on the **inner boundary** (radius $\vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$). In **Configuration C**, the arm has full dexterity and $\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2) \neq 0$.

---

## 4.4 Why These Diagrams Matter for KTU Exams

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Drawing neat block diagrams or schematic representations of singularity classification in your answer sheet can earn you **1–2 extra marks** even if your text explanation is incomplete. Always include at least one visual aid in 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define kinematic singularity. What condition on the Jacobian matrix characterizes a singular configuration of a robotic manipulator?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **kinematic singularity** is a configuration of the robot manipulator in which the **end-effector loses one or more degrees of freedom** in the Cartesian (task) space, even though the joint actuators remain functional.

It is characterized by the condition that the **Jacobian matrix $J(\theta)$ becomes rank-deficient**, i.e.,

$$ \det(J(\theta)) = 0 $$

At this configuration, the inverse Jacobian $J^{-1}$ does not exist, and the manipulator cannot generate velocities along certain Cartesian directions. **(3 marks — Full credit)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List and briefly explain any THREE types of singularities encountered in a 6-DOF robotic manipulator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Boundary Singularities:** Occur when the manipulator is fully stretched or fully folded; the end-effector lies on the boundary of the reachable workspace.

2. **Interior Singularities:** Occur inside the workspace when two or more joint axes become aligned, causing a sudden loss of mobility.

3. **Wrist Singularities:** Occur when two of the last three wrist joint axes become collinear, leading to loss of one rotational degree of freedom about the tool axis.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **Instructions (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** *Answer any ONE full question from this module. Each question carries 14 marks, divided into two sub-parts of 7 marks each.*

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define a kinematic singularity. Derive the Jacobian matrix for a **2-link planar manipulator** with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, and joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. Identify the singular configurations and explain their physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with neat diagrams the **three types of singularities** (boundary, interior, wrist) that occur in a **6-DOF manipulator**. Why is it important to identify and avoid these configurations in industrial robot path planning? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Define Singularity (1 mark)**
A singularity is a configuration at which $\det(J(\theta)) = 0$, causing the Jacobian to lose its full rank and the end-effector to lose one or more degrees of freedom.

**Step 2: Forward Kinematics (1 mark)**
Position of end-effector:

$$ x = L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

$$ y = L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$

**Step 3: Jacobian Derivation (2 marks)**

$$ J(\theta) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 \sin(\theta_1) - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & -L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\[2mm] \;\;\;L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) & \;\;\;L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \end{bmatrix} $$

**Step 4: Determinant & Singularity Condition (2 marks)**

$$ \det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2) $$

Setting $\det(J) = 0$:

$$ \theta_2 = 0 \quad \text{or} \quad \theta_2 = \pi $$

**Step 5: Physical Significance (1 mark)**
- $\theta_2 = 0$: Arm fully **stretched** — outer workspace boundary singularity.
- $\theta_2 = \pi$: Arm fully **folded** — inner workspace boundary singularity.
- In both cases, the end-effector cannot move radially (along the line from base to end-effector).

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Three Types of Singularities (3 marks):**

1. **Boundary Singularity:** End-effector at the **edge of the reachable workspace**; the arm is at maximum or minimum reach.

2. **Interior Singularity:** Occurs **inside the workspace** when joint axes align (e.g., elbow singularity in PUMA 560). Two distinct joint configurations can produce the same end-effector pose.

3. **Wrist Singularity:** Last three joints (4, 5, 6) align such that axes 4 and 6 become collinear, causing loss of rotation about the tool approach axis.

**Importance of Identifying Singularities (4 marks):**

| # | Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Avoid Infinite Joint Velocities** | At singularity, $J^{-1}$ is undefined; small Cartesian velocity demands infinite joint velocity, which is physically impossible. |
| 2 | **Ensure Path Feasibility** | Trajectories crossing singular points become non-executable or require discontinuous joint motion. |
| 3 | **Maintain Manipulability** | Manipulability index $w \to 0$ at singularities, meaning the robot cannot exert force/motion isotropically. |
| 4 | **Safety in Force-Controlled Tasks** | Joint torques become indeterminate at singularities, making contact tasks unsafe. |
| 5 | **Smooth Tool-Path Execution** | In welding, painting, and cutting, constant tool speed requires avoiding singularities. |
| 6 | **Controller Stability** | Damped least-squares or null-space control strategies depend on accurate singularity detection. |

**Conclusion (0 marks, but good practice):** Identifying singularities is essential for **safe, smooth, and efficient** operation of robotic manipulators in any real-world application.

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** What is the **manipulability index**? How does it behave near and at a singular configuration? Derive the manipulability index expression for the 2-link planar arm and show that it vanishes at the singular configurations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Damped Least-Squares (DLS)** method for computing the inverse Jacobian in the vicinity of a singularity. What are its advantages and limitations compared to the standard pseudo-inverse? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Definition (1 mark)**
The **manipulability index** $w$, proposed by Yoshikawa (1985), is a scalar measure of the manipulator's ability to move in arbitrary Cartesian directions from a given configuration. It is defined as:

$$ w = \sqrt{\det(J(\theta) J^T(\theta))} $$

For a square Jacobian ($J$ is $n \times n$), this reduces to:

$$ w = \vert \det(J(\theta)) \vert $$

**Step 2: Behaviour near singularity (2 marks)**

- As the manipulator approaches a singular configuration, $\det(J) \to 0$, so $w \to 0$.
- At the exact singular configuration, $w = 0$, indicating **complete loss of manipulability** in at least one Cartesian direction.

**Step 3: Derivation for 2-link planar arm (3 marks)**

From our earlier result, $\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2)$. Therefore:

$$ w = \vert L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2) \vert $$

**Verification at singular configurations:**

- At $\theta_2 = 0$: $w = \vert L_1 L_2 \sin(0) \vert = \vert L_1 L_2 \cdot 0 \vert = 0$ ✓
- At $\theta_2 = \pi$: $w = \vert L_1 L_2 \sin(\pi) \vert = \vert L_1 L_2 \cdot 0 \vert = 0$ ✓

**Step 4: Interpretation (1 mark)**
$w$ is a **scalar dexterity measure** — higher $w$ means the robot can move equally well in all directions; $w = 0$ means the robot has lost at least one DOF.

---

### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Damped Least-Squares (DLS) Inverse — Definition (2 marks)**

The DLS inverse is a regularized inverse of the Jacobian, defined as:

$$ J^{\dagger}_{DLS} = J^T (J J^T + \lambda^2 I)^{-1} $$

where $\lambda > 0$ is a **damping factor** (scalar regularization parameter). The joint velocity is then:

$$ \dot{\theta} = J^{\dagger}_{DLS} \dot{x} = J^T (J J^T + \lambda^2 I)^{-1} \dot{x} $$

**Why this works (2 marks):**

- Even when $J$ is singular (or near-singular), the term $J J^T + \lambda^2 I$ is **always invertible** because $\lambda^2 I$ shifts the eigenvalues upward.
- This prevents $J^{-1}$ from blowing up, giving **bounded, smooth joint velocities** near singularities.
- The trade-off: a small tracking error is introduced, but the motion is executable.

**Comparison with Pseudo-Inverse (3 marks):**

| Feature | Moore-Penrose Pseudo-Inverse $J^{+}$ | Damped Least-Squares $J^{\dagger}_{DLS}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $J^+ = J^T (J J^T)^{-1}$ | $J^T (J J^T + \lambda^2 I)^{-1}$ |
| Behaviour at singularity | $\to \infty$ (unbounded) | Bounded (finite) |
| Tracking accuracy | Exact (when non-singular) | Approximate (small error) |
| Numerical stability | Poor near singularity | **Excellent** |
| Joint velocity smoothness | Jerky near singularity | **Smooth** |
| Tuning parameter | None | $\lambda$ (must be chosen carefully) |
| Industrial use | Less common in modern controllers | **Widely used** (e.g., in KUKA, ABB) |

**Conclusion (0 marks, recommended):** The DLS method is the **industry-standard** workaround for singularities, balancing accuracy and feasibility.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\det(J) = 0$ with $J = 0$:** Many students write "$J$ becomes zero" instead of "the determinant of $J$ becomes zero." The matrix $J$ is not zero; it is **rank-deficient**. **−1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the absolute value in manipulability:** Manipulability is $\vert \det(J) \vert$, not $\det(J)$. The square root requires a non-negative argument. **−1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Wrong trigonometric identity in 2-link Jacobian derivation:** The determinant simplifies to $L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2)$ — many students forget the $L_2^2$ terms cancel. **−2 marks** if missed.
>
> 4. **Not drawing a diagram:** For 14-mark questions, a missing sketch of the 2-link arm or singularity classification **costs 1–2 marks**.
>
> 5. **Saying "singularity is a defect":** Singularities are **mathematical properties**, not manufacturing defects. Writing this shows a **conceptual misunderstanding**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the damping factor $\lambda$:** When asked about DLS, students often write $J^+ = (J J^T)^{-1} J^T$ and call it "DLS" — this is the **pseudo-inverse**, not DLS. The $\lambda^2 I$ term is **essential**. **−1 mark.**

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Memorize These 12 Points:**

- ☐ **Singularity** = configuration where $\det(J(\theta)) = 0$.
- ☐ **Rank condition:** $\text{rank}(J) < n$ (for $n$-DOF manipulator).
- ☐ **Three types:** Boundary, Interior, Wrist.
- ☐ **Boundary:** Fully stretched or folded arm (end-effector at workspace edge).
- ☐ **Interior:** Inside workspace, joint axis alignment (rare for simple arms).
- ☐ **Wrist:** Collinear wrist joints (joints 4 & 6 align in a spherical wrist).
- ☐ **Effect on velocities:** $\dot{\theta} = J^{-1} \dot{x}$ becomes infinite at singularity.
- ☐ **Effect on forces:** Joint torques become indeterminate in some directions.
- ☐ **Manipulability index:** $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^T)}$; $w = 0$ at singularity.
- ☐ **Condition number:** $\kappa(J) = \sigma_{max}/\sigma_{min} \to \infty$ at singularity.
- ☐ **DLS inverse:** $J^{\dagger} = J^T (J J^T + \lambda^2 I)^{-1}$ — bounded near singularity.
- ☐ **2-link planar arm result:** $\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin(\theta_2)$ — singular at $\theta_2 = 0, \pi$.

> **Final Tip:** Always check for singularities **before** executing a trajectory in any industrial robot — this is what separates a *textbook engineer* from a *production-ready engineer*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force-Mass-Acceleration and Torque-Inertia-Angular-Acceleration Relationships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Force-Mass-Acceleration and Torque-Mass-Angular-Acceleration Relationships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Linear Dynamics: The Force-Mass-Acceleration Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton's Second Law of Motion (Linear Form)**
> The acceleration of a rigid body is directly proportional to the net force acting upon it and inversely proportional to its mass, with the direction of acceleration being the same as the direction of the net force.
>
> $$\vec{F} = m \vec{a}$$
>
> where $\vec{F}$ is the net applied force vector in **Newtons (N)**, $m$ is the scalar mass in **kilograms (kg)**, and $\vec{a}$ is the resulting linear acceleration vector in **m/s²**.

## 1.2 Rotational Dynamics: The Torque-Inertia-Angular Acceleration Relationship

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newton-Euler Equation (Rotational Form)**
> The angular acceleration of a rigid body is directly proportional to the net torque applied about its center of mass and inversely proportional to its mass moment of inertia tensor.
>
> $$\vec{\tau} = \mathbf{I} \vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I} \vec{\omega})$$
>
> For the simplified planar (scalar) case:
> $$\tau = I \alpha$$
>
> where $\vec{\tau}$ is the net torque vector in **Newton-meters (N·m)**, $\mathbf{I}$ is the inertia tensor in **kg·m²**, $\vec{\alpha}$ is the angular acceleration vector in **rad/s²**, and $\vec{\omega}$ is the angular velocity vector in **rad/s**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy for Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — Pushing a Shopping Cart:**

Imagine you are at a supermarket pushing two different shopping carts:
- **Cart A (Empty):** You apply a small force $\vec{F}$ with your hand, and the cart accelerates rapidly. This is because $m$ is small, so by $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$, the resulting $\vec{a}$ is large.
- **Cart B (Fully Loaded with Groceries):** You apply the **same** force, but the cart barely moves. Here, $m$ is large, so $\vec{a}$ is small.

**Now extend this analogy to rotation** — imagine spinning a basketball versus spinning a heavy bicycle wheel on the same axle using the same torque:
- The **basketball** (small moment of inertia $I$) spins up to a high angular acceleration $\alpha$.
- The **bicycle wheel** (large moment of inertia $I$) takes much more effort to spin up.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Robotics:** Every joint motor in a robot must apply the correct amount of torque to overcome the inertia of the links it moves. Ignoring inertia leads to sluggish robots, tracking errors, and motor stalling. The Force-Mass-Acceleration and Torque-Inertia-Angular-Acceleration relationships are the **two foundational pillars** of robot dynamics — they tell engineers *exactly* how much actuator effort is required to achieve a desired motion.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Logical Breakdown

### Linear Dynamics (Translation)

**Step 1 — Identify all external forces:** Sum every force vector acting on the body, including gravity, contact forces, friction, and actuator forces.

**Step 2 — Compute the resultant force:** The vector sum of all forces is the net (resultant) force $\sum \vec{F}_i$.

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law:** The net force equals the rate of change of linear momentum:
$$\sum \vec{F} = \frac{d}{dt}(m\vec{v}) = m\vec{a} \quad \text{(when } m \text{ is constant)}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for unknowns:** Rearrange to find the unknown force, mass, or acceleration depending on the problem.

### Rotational Dynamics (Rotation about a Fixed Axis or Center of Mass)

**Step 1 — Identify the inertia property:** The **mass moment of inertia** $I$ (scalar, planar) or **inertia tensor** $\mathbf{I}$ (3×3 matrix, spatial) characterizes how the mass is distributed relative to the axis of rotation.

**Step 2 — Compute the net torque:** Sum the moments of all forces about the chosen reference point (typically the center of mass or joint axis).

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law for Rotation:** The net torque equals the rate of change of angular momentum $\vec{H}$:
$$\sum \vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$$

**Step 4 — Account for gyroscopic terms (spatial case):** The cross-product term $\vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$ appears only when rotation is about a non-principal axis or when $\vec{\omega}$ is changing direction in 3D space.

## 2.2 The Why and How Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters | How It Is Used in Robotics |
|------|----------------|----------------------------|
| Identify forces/torques | Robot joints experience multiple loads | Computed via joint torque sensors or dynamic models |
| Sum forces/torques | Vector nature requires careful addition | Free-body diagrams of each link |
| Apply Newton's laws | Provides governing equation of motion | Base of all robot dynamic models (Lagrangian, Newton-Euler) |
| Solve for actuator demand | Determines motor sizing | Inverse dynamics for trajectory tracking |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Robotics numerical problem.**

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---------------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Newton's Second Law (Linear) | $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ | $\text{N} = \text{kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2$ | Constant mass assumption |
| 2 | Newton's Second Law (Momentum) | $\vec{F} = \dfrac{d(m\vec{v})}{dt}$ | N | General form, valid for variable mass |
| 3 | Rotational Equation (Scalar) | $\tau = I\alpha$ | $\text{N} \cdot \text{m}$ | Planar rotation only |
| 4 | Rotational Equation (Vector) | $\vec{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$ | $\text{N} \cdot \text{m}$ | Full Newton-Euler equation |
| 5 | Angular Momentum | $\vec{H} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2 / \text{s}$ | Foundation of rotational dynamics |
| 6 | Mass Moment of Inertia (Point Mass) | $I = mr^2$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$ | Distance $r$ from axis |
| 7 | Parallel Axis Theorem | $I_P = I_{cm} + md^2$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$ | $d$ = distance between parallel axes |
| 8 | Perpendicular Axis Theorem | $I_z = I_x + I_y$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$ | Planar lamina only |
| 9 | Inertia Tensor (3D) | $\mathbf{I} = \begin{bmatrix} I_{xx} & -I_{xy} & -I_{xz} \\ -I_{yx} & I_{yy} & -I_{yz} \\ -I_{zx} & I_{zy} & I_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$ | Products of inertia: $I_{xy} = \sum m_i x_i y_i$ |
| 10 | Robot Joint Torque (Scalar) | $\tau_i = I_{eff,i} \ddot{q}_i$ | $\text{N} \cdot \text{m}$ | $q_i$ = joint variable, $I_{eff}$ = effective inertia |
| 11 | Effective Inertia at Joint $i$ | $I_{eff,i} = I_{motor,i} + \sum_{j} \left(\dfrac{\partial \vec{v}_{cj}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right)^T m_j \left(\dfrac{\partial \vec{v}_{cj}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right)$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^2$ | Reflected inertia from coupled links |
| 12 | Gravitational Torque | $\tau_g = m g r \cos(\theta)$ | $\text{N} \cdot \text{m}$ | Torque due to gravity on a link |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Field | Use of F=ma and τ=Iα |
|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Industrial Robot Arms (e.g., FANUC, KUKA)** | Motor sizing — must overcome link inertia plus payload |
| **Mobile Robots (AGVs, Drones)** | Traction force calculations for wheel/rotor design |
| **Humanoid Robots (e.g., ASIMO, Atlas)** | Balance control via precise torque distribution |
| **Space Manipulators (Canadarm, Robonaut)** | Zero-gravity inertial coupling is dominant |
| **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci)** | Force-feedback control requires accurate F=ma modeling |
| **Self-Driving Cars** | Vehicle dynamics: braking force vs. deceleration |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Rotational Equation of Motion (Euler's Equation)

### Starting Point — Newton's Second Law for a Particle

For a single particle of mass $m_i$ at position $\vec{r}_i$ from a reference point $O$:

$$\vec{F}_i = m_i \vec{a}_i = m_i \ddot{\vec{r}}_i$$

### Step 1 — Take the Moment About Point O

The moment (torque) of force $\vec{F}_i$ about point $O$ is:
$$\vec{\tau}_i = \vec{r}_i \times \vec{F}_i$$

Substituting Newton's law:
$$\vec{\tau}_i = \vec{r}_i \times (m_i \ddot{\vec{r}}_i)$$

### Step 2 — Recognize Angular Momentum

Recall that the angular momentum of particle $i$ about point $O$ is:
$$\vec{H}_i = \vec{r}_i \times (m_i \dot{\vec{r}}_i)$$

Differentiating with respect to time:
$$\frac{d\vec{H}_i}{dt} = \dot{\vec{r}}_i \times (m_i \dot{\vec{r}}_i) + \vec{r}_i \times (m_i \ddot{\vec{r}}_i)$$

The first term vanishes because $\dot{\vec{r}}_i \times \dot{\vec{r}}_i = \vec{0}$, so:
$$\frac{d\vec{H}_i}{dt} = \vec{r}_i \times (m_i \ddot{\vec{r}}_i) = \vec{\tau}_i$$

### Step 3 — Sum Over All Particles in the Body

For a rigid body composed of many particles:
$$\sum_i \vec{\tau}_i = \sum_i \frac{d\vec{H}_i}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\sum_i \vec{H}_i\right) = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$$

Therefore:
$$\sum \vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$$

This is the rotational analog of Newton's second law, valid for any reference point.

### Step 4 — Express Angular Momentum Using the Inertia Tensor

For a rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ about its center of mass:
$$\vec{H} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$$

where $\mathbf{I}$ is the inertia tensor about the center of mass. Substituting:
$$\sum \vec{\tau} = \frac{d}{dt}(\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$$

### Step 5 — Differentiate (Assume I Is Constant in Body Frame)

If $\mathbf{I}$ is expressed in the body-fixed frame, it is constant in time, so:
$$\sum \vec{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\dot{\vec{\omega}} + \dot{\mathbf{I}}\vec{\omega}$$

When changing from body frame to inertial frame, the rate of change of the inertia tensor introduces the gyroscopic term:
$$\dot{\mathbf{I}}\vec{\omega} = \vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$$

Therefore, the **Euler Equation of Motion** becomes:
$$\sum \vec{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$$

where $\vec{\alpha} = \dot{\vec{\omega}}$ is the angular acceleration.

### Step 6 — Simplified Planar (Scalar) Form

If rotation is about a single principal axis (e.g., $z$-axis) and $\vec{\omega}$ is aligned with that axis, then $\vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}) = \vec{0}$, and the equation reduces to:
$$\tau_z = I_{zz} \alpha_z$$

or simply:
$$\tau = I\alpha$$

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — A Robotic Link

**Problem Statement:**
A robotic link of mass $m = 5 \text{ kg}$ is treated as a uniform slender rod of length $L = 0.8 \text{ m}$ rotating about one end. The motor applies a torque $\tau = 12 \text{ N·m}$. Find the angular acceleration $\alpha$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the inertia about the rotation axis:**

For a uniform slender rod rotating about one end, the moment of inertia is:
$$I = \frac{1}{3}mL^2$$

Substituting values:
$$I = \frac{1}{3} \times 5 \times (0.8)^2$$

$$I = \frac{1}{3} \times 5 \times 0.64$$

$$I = \frac{3.2}{3} = 1.0667 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the scalar rotational equation $\tau = I\alpha$:**

Rearranging for $\alpha$:
$$\alpha = \frac{\tau}{I} = \frac{12}{1.0667}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 11.25 \text{ rad/s}^2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the linear acceleration at the tip:**

The tangential acceleration at the tip (distance $L$ from axis) is:
$$a_{tip} = \alpha L = 11.25 \times 0.8 = 9.0 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Verify with Newton's second law (force form):**

The equivalent tip force producing this acceleration on the link's effective point mass $m_{eff} = \frac{1}{3}m$ (because rotational KE = $\frac{1}{2}I\omega^2 = \frac{1}{2}(\frac{1}{3}mL^2)\omega^2 = \frac{1}{2}(\frac{1}{3}m)(L\omega)^2$) is:
$$F = m_{eff} \cdot a_{tip} = \frac{5}{3} \times 9.0 = 15.0 \text{ N}$$

This corresponds to torque $F \cdot L = 15.0 \times 0.8 = 12.0$ N·m, confirming our result.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Types

| Problem Type | Given | Find | Key Formula |
|--------------|-------|------|-------------|
| Linear acceleration from force | $F, m$ | $a$ | $a = F/m$ |
| Force to stop a moving body | $m, v_i, v_f, t$ | $F$ | $F = m(v_f - v_i)/t$ |
| Angular accel. from torque | $\tau, I$ | $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \tau/I$ |
| Torque to stop spinning disc | $I, \omega_i, t$ | $\tau$ | $\tau = -I\omega_i/t$ |
| Effective inertia at robot joint | $I_{motor}$, link inertia, gear ratio $n$ | $I_{eff}$ | $I_{eff} = I_{motor} + n^2 I_{link}$ |
| Combined translational + rotational | Sliding + spinning | Net force/torque | $F_{net} = ma$, $\tau_{net} = I\alpha$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Robot Dynamic Computation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired Trajectory] --> B[Trajectory Generator]
    B --> C[Desired Joint Position q, Velocity q-dot, Acceleration q-double-dot]
    C --> D[Inverse Dynamics Solver]
    D --> E[Mass Matrix M]
    D --> F[Coriolis Centrifugal Vector C]
    D --> G[Gravity Vector G]
    D --> H[Friction Vector F]
    E --> I[Generalized Force Torque Computation]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Required Joint Torque Tau]
    J --> K[Robot Joint Actuator]
    K --> L[Robot Link Motion]
    L --> M[Sensor Feedback q, q-dot]
    M --> D
    L --> N[Force Mass Acceleration F equals m times a]
    L --> O[Torque Inertia Angular Acceleration Tau equals I times alpha]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style J fill:#90EE90
    style N fill:#ADD8E6
    style O fill:#ADD8E6
```

## 4.2 Force-Mass-Acceleration Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Applied Force F] --> B1[Net Force Sum]
    A2[Gravity mg] --> B1
    A3[Contact Normal N] --> B1
    A4[Friction mu times N] --> B1
    B1 --> C1{Mass m is constant?}
    C1 -- Yes --> D1[Acceleration a equals F net divided by m]
    C1 -- No --> D2[Acceleration a equals d m v divided by dt divided by m]
    D1 --> E1[Linear Motion of Link]
    D2 --> E1
    style B1 fill:#FFD700
    style E1 fill:#98FB98
```

## 4.3 Torque-Inertia-Angular Acceleration Interaction Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Motor Torque Tau m] --> B1[Net Torque Sum]
    A2[Gravity Torque Tau g] --> B1
    A3[Reaction Torque from Coupled Links] --> B1
    A4[Friction Torque Tau f] --> B1
    B1 --> C1{Is rotation 3D about non principal axis?}
    C1 -- No, planar --> D1[Angular Acceleration alpha equals Tau divided by I]
    C1 -- Yes, spatial --> D2[Euler Equation Tau equals I alpha plus omega cross I omega]
    D1 --> E1[Rotational Motion of Link]
    D2 --> E1
    D2 --> F1[Gyroscopic Effect omega cross I omega]
    F1 --> E1
    style B1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style E1 fill:#98FB98
    style F1 fill:#FFA07A
```

## 4.4 Robot Joint Actuator Force Torque Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Robot Joint Command] --> Q1{What motion is required?}
    Q1 -- Pure Translation --> L1[Compute F equals m times a]
    Q1 -- Pure Rotation --> L2[Compute Tau equals I times alpha]
    Q1 -- Combined --> L3[Compute both F and Tau]
    L1 --> M1[Sum all external forces]
    M1 --> M2[Apply Newtons Second Law]
    M2 --> M3[Determine required actuator force]
    L2 --> N1[Sum all external torques about joint axis]
    N1 --> N2[Apply Euler Equation]
    N2 --> N3[Determine required actuator torque]
    L3 --> O1[Use full Newton Euler Recursive Algorithm]
    O1 --> O2[Output joint force and torque vectors]
    style Start fill:#FFDEAD
    style M3 fill:#90EE90
    style N3 fill:#90EE90
    style O2 fill:#90EE90
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*  
> **State and explain Newton's Second Law of Motion. How is it applied in computing the joint torque requirement of a robotic manipulator?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Newton's Second Law states that the rate of change of linear momentum of a body is directly proportional to the net external force applied, and the change takes place in the direction of the applied force. Mathematically:
$$\vec{F} = \frac{d}{dt}(m\vec{v}) = m\vec{a} \quad \text{(for constant mass)}$$

In robotics, this law is applied to every link of a manipulator to determine the joint forces and torques required to produce a desired motion. By computing the net force on each link (including gravity, payload, and inertial forces from $m\vec{a}$), engineers can size actuators and design controllers for accurate trajectory tracking.

---

> **Question 2.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*  
> **Differentiate between the scalar form $\tau = I\alpha$ and the vector Newton-Euler equation. When is each form used in robotics?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **scalar form** $\tau = I\alpha$ applies to **planar rotation** about a single fixed principal axis, where angular velocity is aligned with the axis and gyroscopic effects are absent. It is simple and used for educational analysis or single-DOF robot joints.

The **vector Newton-Euler equation** $\vec{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$ applies to **3D spatial rotation** about the center of mass. The additional term $\vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$ captures the **gyroscopic torque** that arises when the angular velocity vector is not aligned with a principal axis or is changing direction. It is essential for modeling multi-DOF robot arms, space manipulators, and aerial robots where complex 3D rotations occur.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the Newton-Euler rotational equation of motion for a rigid body, starting from the definition of angular momentum. Clearly explain each step and state the physical significance of the gyroscopic term.

**(b) [7 marks]** A robot link is modeled as a uniform rectangular plate of mass $m = 4$ kg and dimensions $0.6$ m $\times$ 0.4 m. The plate rotates about an axis perpendicular to its plane and passing through its center. If a torque of $\tau = 6$ N·m is applied, calculate:
   (i) the mass moment of inertia of the plate,
   (ii) the resulting angular acceleration,
   (iii) the angular velocity after $t = 2$ seconds (starting from rest).

### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of the Newton-Euler Equation:**

**Step 1:** For a particle of mass $m_i$ at position $\vec{r}_i$:
$$\vec{\tau}_i = \vec{r}_i \times \vec{F}_i = \vec{r}_i \times (m_i \ddot{\vec{r}}_i)$$

**Step 2:** Define angular momentum $\vec{H}_i = \vec{r}_i \times (m_i \dot{\vec{r}}_i)$ and differentiate:
$$\frac{d\vec{H}_i}{dt} = \dot{\vec{r}}_i \times (m_i \dot{\vec{r}}_i) + \vec{r}_i \times (m_i \ddot{\vec{r}}_i) = \vec{r}_i \times (m_i \ddot{\vec{r}}_i) = \vec{\tau}_i$$

**Step 3:** Sum over all particles:
$$\sum \vec{\tau} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$$

**Step 4:** Express $\vec{H} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\omega}$ and differentiate in the body frame:
$$\sum \vec{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$$

**Physical significance of the gyroscopic term:** The term $\vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$ represents the torque required to change the **direction** of the angular velocity vector even if its magnitude is constant. It appears in spinning objects (gyroscopes, flywheels, satellite rotors) and is responsible for precession. In robotics, it is critical for high-speed manipulators and aerial vehicles.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Mass moment of inertia** of a rectangular plate about an axis perpendicular to the plate through its center:
$$I = \frac{1}{12}m(a^2 + b^2)$$

where $a = 0.6$ m, $b = 0.4$ m.

$$I = \frac{1}{12} \times 4 \times ((0.6)^2 + (0.4)^2)$$

$$I = \frac{1}{3} \times (0.36 + 0.16) = \frac{1}{3} \times 0.52$$

$$\boxed{I = 0.1733 \text{ kg·m}^2}$$

**(ii) Angular acceleration:**
$$\alpha = \frac{\tau}{I} = \frac{6}{0.1733}$$

$$\boxed{\alpha = 34.62 \text{ rad/s}^2}$$

**(iii) Angular velocity after 2 seconds** (starting from rest, $\omega_0 = 0$):
$$\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t = 0 + 34.62 \times 2$$

$$\boxed{\omega = 69.23 \text{ rad/s}}$$

---

> **Question B.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of effective inertia at a robot joint. Derive the relationship between motor inertia and link inertia when a gear reducer of ratio $n$ is used. Why is effective inertia important in actuator selection?

**(b) [7 marks]** A 2 kg robotic arm link (modeled as a slender rod) of length 0.5 m is hinged at one end. A horizontal force $F = 20$ N is applied at the free end perpendicular to the link, and gravity acts downward.
   (i) Determine the linear acceleration of the free end using $F = ma$.
   (ii) Determine the angular acceleration of the link using $\tau = I\alpha$.
   (iii) Show that both methods give the same tangential acceleration at the free end.

### Model Solution

**(a) Effective Inertia at a Robot Joint:**

**Definition:** Effective inertia $I_{eff}$ at a robot joint is the total inertia that the joint motor must overcome or accelerate, including its own rotor inertia plus the reflected inertias of all connected links and the payload.

**Derivation for geared joint:** Consider a motor with rotor inertia $I_m$ driving a link of inertia $I_L$ through a gear reducer with ratio $n = \omega_m / \omega_L$ (motor spins $n$ times faster than the link).

The kinetic energy of the system is:
$$KE = \frac{1}{2}I_m \omega_m^2 + \frac{1}{2}I_L \omega_L^2 = \frac{1}{2}I_m (n\omega_L)^2 + \frac{1}{2}I_L \omega_L^2 = \frac{1}{2}(n^2 I_m + I_L)\omega_L^2$$

Therefore, the effective inertia at the link side is:
$$\boxed{I_{eff} = n^2 I_m + I_L}$$

Reflected to the motor side (dividing by $n^2$):
$$I_{eff,motor} = I_m + \frac{I_L}{n^2}$$

**Importance in actuator selection:** The actuator must supply enough torque to accelerate $I_{eff}$ to the desired angular acceleration. A well-designed gear ratio keeps $I_{eff}$ manageable, ensuring the motor is not overloaded and the system can respond quickly to control commands.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Linear acceleration of free end (force method):**

For a uniform rod hinged at one end, the effective mass for translational motion of the center of mass is $m$ but the center of mass is at $L/2$. However, the rod rotates, so we use the moment equation.

Treating the rod as a point mass at distance $L/2$ (center of mass) for the purpose of $\tau = I\alpha$:

The torque about the hinge due to $F$:
$$\tau_F = F \times L = 20 \times 0.5 = 10 \text{ N·m}$$

The moment of inertia of the rod about the hinge:
$$I = \frac{1}{3}mL^2 = \frac{1}{3} \times 2 \times (0.5)^2 = \frac{1}{3} \times 2 \times 0.25 = 0.1667 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

Angular acceleration:
$$\alpha = \frac{\tau_F}{I} = \frac{10}{0.1667} = 60 \text{ rad/s}^2$$

Linear acceleration of free end:
$$a_{tip} = \alpha L = 60 \times 0.5 = 30 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**(ii) Angular acceleration (torque method):**

From above: $\alpha = 60$ rad/s² (same calculation as part i, derived from torque method).

**(iii) Verification:**

From force method: $a_{tip} = 30$ m/s².  
From torque method: $a_{tip} = \alpha L = 60 \times 0.5 = 30$ m/s².  
Both methods agree. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks if units of $I$ (kg·m²) or $\tau$ (N·m) are missing.
> 2. **Confusing scalar and vector forms** — Using $\tau = I\alpha$ for a 3D problem will lose 2–3 marks; always state assumptions.
> 3. **Wrong moment of inertia formula** — Memorize $I = \frac{1}{3}mL^2$ (rod about end), $\frac{1}{12}mL^2$ (rod about center), $\frac{1}{2}mr^2$ (disc), $\frac{2}{5}mr^2$ (solid sphere). Mixing these up is the #1 reason students lose marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the gyroscopic term** — In any 3D rotation problem, include $\vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$ or the answer is incomplete.
> 5. **Not showing the parallel axis theorem step** — When asked to find $I$ about a non-centroidal axis, KTU expects explicit use of $I_P = I_{cm} + md^2$.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Newton's Second Law (Linear):** $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ — foundation of all translational motion analysis.
- **Newton-Euler (Rotational):** $\vec{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\vec{\alpha} + \vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$ — general 3D form.
- **Scalar Rotational Form:** $\tau = I\alpha$ — used only for planar/single-axis rotation with no gyroscopic effects.
- **Mass Moment of Inertia** is the rotational analog of mass — it depends on both mass **and** its geometric distribution about the rotation axis.
- **Parallel Axis Theorem:** $I_P = I_{cm} + md^2$ — required when computing $I$ about a non-centroidal axis.
- **Perpendicular Axis Theorem:** $I_z = I_x + I_y$ — applies **only** to planar laminas.
- **Effective Inertia at a geared joint:** $I_{eff} = n^2 I_m + I_L$ (link side).
- **Gyroscopic term** $\vec{\omega} \times (\mathbf{I}\vec{\omega})$ vanishes for principal-axis rotation and is essential for spatial robotics.
