- **Linear analogy:** $F \leftrightarrow \tau$, $m \leftrightarrow I$, $a \leftrightarrow \alpha$, $v \leftrightarrow \omega$, $p \leftrightarrow H$.
- **Units to remember:** Force in N, mass in kg, acceleration in m/s², torque in N·m, inertia in kg·m², angular acceleration in rad/s².
- **Real-world impact:** Every robot motor specification is determined by these two equations — they are the gateway to actuator sizing, control design, and dynamic simulation.
- **KTU Favorite Topics:** Parallel axis theorem problems, effective inertia with gears, and derivation of Euler's equation are the most frequently tested.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lagrangian Mechanics - Overview
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lagrangian Mechanics - Overview

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lagrangian Mechanics (in Robotics Context):** A systematic, energy-based formulation of classical mechanics used to derive the equations of motion of multi-body robotic systems. The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ is defined as the difference between the total **kinetic energy** $\mathcal{K}$ and the total **potential energy** $\mathcal{P}$ of the system, expressed as a function of the *generalized coordinates* $q_i$ and their time derivatives (generalized velocities) $\dot{q}_i$.

Mathematically, the Lagrangian function is given by:

$$
\mathcal{L}(q_i, \dot{q}_i, t) \;=\; \mathcal{K}(q_i, \dot{q}_i, t) \;-\; \mathcal{P}(q_i, t)
$$

The **Euler-Lagrange Equation**, which is the heart of this formulation, is written as:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} \;=\; \tau_i
$$

where:
- $q_i$ is the **generalized coordinate** of the $i^{th}$ link (e.g., joint angle $\theta_i$ or prismatic displacement $d_i$).
- $\dot{q}_i$ is the **generalized velocity** of the $i^{th}$ joint.
- $\tau_i$ is the **generalized force** (joint torque for revolute joints, joint force for prismatic joints) acting at the $i^{th}$ joint.
- $n$ is the total number of generalized coordinates, equal to the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of the manipulator.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Energy Bank" Analogy:** Imagine you have a robotic arm as a system of "energy accounts." Every moving link deposits money into a **Kinetic Energy (KE) savings account** based on how fast it moves, and every raised link is a debt in the **Potential Energy (PE) account** because gravity "owes" it the ability to fall. The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$ is essentially the **net balance** of this energy account at any instant. The Euler-Lagrange equation acts like a financial auditor that says: *"The rate of change of this energy balance, with respect to how fast the joint is moving versus how much it has been displaced, must equal the external torque you are applying."*

In plain English for a first-time learner:
- **Newton-Euler method** asks: *"What forces are acting on each link?"* (force/torque balance).
- **Lagrangian method** asks: *"What is the total energy picture of the whole robot?"* (energy balance) and then derives the motion.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use Lagrangian in Robotics?** It scales beautifully. For a 2-link arm vs a 6-link PUMA robot, the procedure is the **same**—just add more $q_i$'s. There is no need to draw free-body diagrams for every link and solve a tangled web of constraint forces.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Units (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Generalized coordinate | $q_i$ | rad (revolute) or m (prismatic) |
| Generalized velocity | $\dot{q}_i$ | rad/s or m/s |
| Joint torque / force | $\tau_i$ | N·m or N |
| Link mass | $m_i$ | **kg** |
| Link inertia | $I_i$ | **kg·m²** |
| Gravity acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Lagrangian | $\mathcal{L}$ | **Joules (J)** |

---

## 1.4 Why Lagrangian Mechanics is Mandatory in KTU Robotics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **KTU 2024 Scheme** explicitly lists Lagrangian formulation in Module 3 as a key tool for deriving **manipulator dynamics equations**. Questions are frequently asked to:
> 1. Derive the dynamic equation of a 2-DOF planar manipulator.
> 2. Write the manipulator equation in the standard form $\boldsymbol{\tau} = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$.
> 3. Compute elements of the mass matrix $M(q)$ and gravity vector $G(q)$ for a simple R-R or R-P robot.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step by Step

The Lagrangian procedure for a robotic manipulator follows a strict, repeatable sequence:

### Step 1: Identify the Generalized Coordinates
Choose a minimum set of independent variables $q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n$ that completely describe the configuration of the robot.
- **For revolute joints:** $q_i = \theta_i$ (joint angle in radians).
- **For prismatic joints:** $q_i = d_i$ (joint displacement in meters).

> [!NOTE]
> The number of generalized coordinates **n** must equal the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of the manipulator.

### Step 2: Express Position of Every Point of Interest
Using forward kinematics, write the position vector of the **center of mass** of each link, $\vec{r}_{c_i}$, as a function of the generalized coordinates.

$$
\vec{r}_{c_i} = \vec{r}_{c_i}(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n)
$$

### Step 3: Differentiate to Get Velocities
Take the time derivative of the position vectors to obtain velocity vectors $v_{c_i}$:

$$
\vec{v}_{c_i} = \frac{d\vec{r}_{c_i}}{dt} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_i}}{\partial q_j}\,\dot{q}_j
$$

### Step 4: Compute the Total Kinetic Energy $\mathcal{K}$
Sum the kinetic energies of all links. Each link has **translational KE** (of its center of mass) plus **rotational KE** (about its own center of mass):

$$
\mathcal{K} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\frac{1}{2}\,m_i\,\vec{v}_{c_i}\cdot\vec{v}_{c_i} \;+\; \frac{1}{2}\,\vec{\omega}_i^{\,T}\,I_{c_i}\,\vec{\omega}_i\right]
$$

This is reorganized into the compact **quadratic-in-velocity form**:

$$
\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}\,\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\,\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\,\dot{q}^{T}\,M(q)\,\dot{q}
$$

Here, $M(q)$ is the symmetric, positive-definite **Mass (Inertia) Matrix** of the manipulator.

### Step 5: Compute the Total Potential Energy $\mathcal{P}$
Sum the gravitational potential energy of every link (the only external potential field in standard KTU problems):

$$
\mathcal{P} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,g\,h_{c_i}(q)
$$

where $h_{c_i}(q)$ is the **vertical height** of the center of mass of link $i$ in the world frame.

### Step 6: Form the Lagrangian
$$
\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}) = \mathcal{K}(q,\dot{q}) - \mathcal{P}(q)
$$

### Step 7: Apply the Euler-Lagrange Equation for Each Coordinate
For every $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i
$$

This yields **$n$ coupled second-order ODEs**, which are the equations of motion.

### Step 8: Reorganize into Standard Manipulator Form

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} \;=\; M(q)\,\ddot{q} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \;+\; G(q)
$$

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $M(q)\ddot{q}$ | **Inertial torque** — torque to accelerate joints |
| $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ | **Coriolis & centrifugal torque** — coupling velocity terms |
| $G(q)$ | **Gravity torque** — torque to hold arm against gravity |

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Equations Required for KTU Module 3**

| \# | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$ | Lagrangian definition |
| 2 | $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | Euler-Lagrange equation |
| 3 | $\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^{T}M(q)\dot{q}$ | KE in mass-matrix form |
| 4 | $\mathcal{P} = \sum m_i g h_{c_i}$ | PE due to gravity |
| 5 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | Standard manipulator equation |
| 6 | $M_{ij} = \sum_{k} \left[m_k \frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_i}\cdot\frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_j} + \frac{\partial \vec{\omega}_k}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\cdot I_{c_k}\frac{\partial \vec{\omega}_k}{\partial \dot{q}_j}\right]$ | Mass matrix element formula |
| 7 | $G_i = \frac{\partial \mathcal{P}}{\partial q_i}$ | Gravity vector element |
| 8 | $v_{c_i}^2 = \sum_{j}\sum_{k} \frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_i}}{\partial q_j}\cdot\frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_i}}{\partial q_k}\,\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k$ | Squared velocity of COM |
| 9 | $\vec{v}_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q)\,\dot{q}$ | Linear velocity via Jacobian |
| 10 | $\vec{\omega}_i = J_{\omega_i}(q)\,\dot{q}$ | Angular velocity via Jacobian |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Equation 6 uses a Jacobian-based formulation that the examiner loves. When computing $M_{ij}$ from scratch, the **partial derivatives must be with respect to generalized coordinates**, not joint variables in a different parameterization.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Area | Why Lagrangian is Used |
|---|---|
| **Industrial robot arms** (KUKA, ABB, FANUC) | Real-time torque computation for control |
| **Humanoid robots** (ASIMO, Atlas) | Managing 20+ coupled DOFs efficiently |
| **Space manipulators** (Canadarm) | Micro-gravity energy bookkeeping |
| **Vehicle dynamics** | Suspension and chassis modelling |
| **Animation / CGI** | Physics engines for character motion |

> [!TIP]
> The Lagrangian form provides a **systematic path** that does not require handling internal constraint forces between links—those are absorbed implicitly. This is why it is preferred over Newton-Euler for deriving the symbolic form of the manipulator equation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation: 2-DOF Planar R-R Manipulator (The KTU Classic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 3. Approximately 70% of Part B questions on Lagrangian mechanics use this exact configuration.

### Problem Setup

Consider a 2-link planar manipulator with:
- Link 1: mass $m_1$, length $l_1$, center of mass at $l_{c1}$ from joint 1.
- Link 2: mass $m_2$, length $l_2$, center of mass at $l_{c2}$ from joint 2.
- Generalized coordinates: $q_1 = \theta_1$, $q_2 = \theta_2$.
- Gravity $g$ acts in the $-Y$ direction.

We assume the link inertias about their COMs are $I_1$ and $I_2$. (For thin uniform rods, $I_i = \frac{1}{12}m_i l_i^2$.)

### Step 1 — Position of Center of Masses

Using standard forward kinematics in the base frame:

$$
x_{c1} = l_{c1}\cos\theta_1
$$

$$
y_{c1} = l_{c1}\sin\theta_1
$$

$$
x_{c2} = l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
y_{c2} = l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

### Step 2 — Velocities (Differentiate with respect to time)

For link 1:

$$
\dot{x}_{c1} = -l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1
$$

$$
\dot{y}_{c1} = l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1
$$

For link 2:

$$
\dot{x}_{c2} = -l_1\sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 - l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)
$$

$$
\dot{y}_{c2} = l_1\cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)
$$

### Step 3 — Squared Velocities

Link 1:

$$
v_{c1}^2 = \dot{x}_{c1}^2 + \dot{y}_{c1}^2 = l_{c1}^2\,\dot{\theta}_1^{\,2}
$$

Link 2 (expand and group by $\dot{\theta}_1, \dot{\theta}_2$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{c2}^2 &= \left[-l_1\sin\theta_1 - l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]^2 \dot{\theta}_1^{\,2} \\
&\quad + 2\left[l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\,\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 \\
&\quad + l_{c2}^2\sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)\,\dot{\theta}_2^{\,2} \\
&\quad + \left[l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]^2 \dot{\theta}_1^{\,2} \\
&\quad + 2\left[l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\,\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 \\
&\quad + l_{c2}^2\cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)\,\dot{\theta}_2^{\,2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Grouping the squared terms (using $\sin^2+\cos^2=1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{c2}^2 &= \left[l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2\right]\dot{\theta}_1^{\,2} \\
&\quad + 2\left[l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2\right]\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 \\
&\quad + l_{c2}^2\,\dot{\theta}_2^{\,2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Kinetic Energy

Translational KE: $\frac{1}{2}m_1 v_{c1}^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2 v_{c2}^2$.
Rotational KE: $\frac{1}{2}I_1 \dot{\theta}_1^{\,2} + \frac{1}{2}I_2 (\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2$.

Combining and collecting in the quadratic form $\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}\sum M_{ij}\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{11} &= m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2\!\left(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2\right) + I_1 + I_2 \\
M_{12} = M_{21} &= m_2\!\left(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos\theta_2\right) + I_2 \\
M_{22} &= m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Potential Energy

Heights of the COMs:

$$
h_{c1} = l_{c1}\sin\theta_1
$$

$$
h_{c2} = l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

$$
\mathcal{P} = m_1 g\,l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 + m_2 g\!\left[l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]
$$

### Step 6 — Gravity Vector

$$
G_1 = \frac{\partial \mathcal{P}}{\partial \theta_1} = m_1 g l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 + m_2 g\!\left[l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)\right]
$$

$$
G_2 = \frac{\partial \mathcal{P}}{\partial \theta_2} = m_2 g\,l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

### Step 7 — Coriolis/Centrifugal Term

For planar R-R arms, the Christoffel terms give:

$$
C(q,\dot{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_2 & -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) \\ m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2\,\dot{\theta}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Manipulator Equation

$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau} \;=\; M(q)\ddot{q} \;+\; C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} \;+\; G(q)\;}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Sanity Check:** Set $m_2 = 0$. The matrix $M$ should collapse to a diagonal with $M_{11} = m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1$ and $M_{22} = I_1$... wait, this needs $\theta_2$ to be fixed. Instead, verify that when $\theta_2 = 90°$, the coupling terms $\cos\theta_2 = 0$ and link 2 becomes independent for inertia purposes—physically this matches the "elbow at right angle" decoupling intuition.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the General Mass Matrix Element Formula

Starting from the velocity of COM of link $k$:

$$
\vec{v}_{c_k} = \left(\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_j}\dot{q}_j\right)
$$

Squared:

$$
v_{c_k}^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} \left(\frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_i}\cdot\frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_j}\right)\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j
$$

Plug into translational KE sum, and add the rotational KE using the angular velocity Jacobian $J_{\omega_k}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{K} &= \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left[m_k \sum_{i,j}\frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_i}\cdot\frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_j}\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j + \vec{\omega}_k^{T} I_{c_k}\vec{\omega}_k\right] \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i,j}\left[\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left(m_k \frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_i}\cdot\frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_j} + \frac{\partial \vec{\omega}_k}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\cdot I_{c_k}\frac{\partial \vec{\omega}_k}{\partial \dot{q}_j}\right)\right]\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j
\end{aligned}
$$

Comparing with $\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}\sum M_{ij}\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j$:

$$
\boxed{\;M_{ij}(q) = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\left[m_k \frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_i}\cdot\frac{\partial \vec{r}_{c_k}}{\partial q_j} + J_{\omega_k,i}^{T} I_{c_k} J_{\omega_k,j}\right]\;}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Lagrangian Derivation Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete workflow** for deriving manipulator dynamics using Lagrangian mechanics—the exact sequence a KTU student should follow during an exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Define Robot Geometry"] --> nodeB["Step 1: Choose Generalized Coordinates q1 q2 ... qn"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Step 2: Write Position of Each COM using Forward Kinematics"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Step 3: Differentiate to Get Velocities v_ci and w_i"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Step 4: Compute Total Kinetic Energy K"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["Step 5: Compute Total Potential Energy P"]
    nodeE --> nodeG["Step 6: Form Lagrangian L = K minus P"]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH["Step 7: Apply Euler Lagrange Equation d/dt dL dqi_dot minus dL dqi equals tau_i"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Step 8: Reorganize into tau = M q ddot plus C q ddot plus G q"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["OUTPUT: Equations of Motion"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["KTU Exam Validation: Check M is Symmetric and Positive Definite"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["END"]
```

---

## 4.2 Energy Flow in a 2-DOF Manipulator (Block Diagram)

This diagram shows how the energy components flow into the Lagrangian and ultimately produce the joint torques.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    blockA["Link 1 Mass m1"] --> blockE["Translational KE of Link 1: 1 over 2 m1 v_c1 squared"]
    blockB["Link 2 Mass m2"] --> blockF["Translational KE of Link 2: 1 over 2 m2 v_c2 squared"]
    blockC["Inertia I1"] --> blockG["Rotational KE of Link 1: 1 over 2 I1 theta1 dot squared"]
    blockD["Inertia I2"] --> blockH["Rotational KE of Link 2: 1 over 2 I2 theta1 plus theta2 dot squared"]
    blockA2["Gravity g"] --> blockI["Potential Energy P equals sum m_i g h_ci"]
    blockE --> blockJ["Total Kinetic Energy K"]
    blockF --> blockJ
    blockG --> blockJ
    blockH --> blockJ
    blockI --> blockK["Lagrangian L equals K minus P"]
    blockJ --> blockK
    blockK --> blockL["Euler Lagrange Equation yields"]
    blockL --> blockM["Tau 1 = M11 theta1 ddot plus M12 theta2 ddot plus C terms plus G1"]
    blockL --> blockN["Tau 2 = M21 theta1 ddot plus M22 theta2 ddot plus C terms plus G2"]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Mapping — Lagrangian vs Newton-Euler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeX["Robot Manipulator"] --> nodeY["Choose Method for Equation of Motion"]
    nodeY --> nodeZ["Lagrangian Method"]
    nodeY --> nodeW["Newton Euler Method"]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ1["Energy Based: Uses K and P"]
    nodeZ1 --> nodeZ2["Symbolic Closed Form for Control"]
    nodeZ2 --> nodeZ3["Best for Low DOF and Analysis"]
    nodeW --> nodeW1["Force Based: F equals ma and Tau equals I alpha"]
    nodeW1 --> nodeW2["Recursive Forward Backward Algorithm"]
    nodeW2 --> nodeW3["Best for Real Time High DOF Computation"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Practical Insight:** The Lagrangian method gives you a **closed-form symbolic expression** for the mass matrix $M(q)$ and gravity vector $G(q)$—perfect for analytical exam problems with 2 or 3 DOF. For real-time control of a 6-DOF arm, the recursive Newton-Euler is faster, but KTU Module 3 focuses on the Lagrangian path.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention for Gravity:** Forgetting to set the gravity vector direction ($-Y$ vs $+Y$) is the #1 cause of wrong signs in $G(q)$. Always draw the world frame explicitly.
> 2. **Squaring Velocities Carefully:** Many students write $v^2 = \dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2$ but forget the $\dot{z}^2$ in 3D problems. For planar 2-DOF, only two terms exist.
> 3. **Symmetry of $M(q)$:** Examiners check $M_{12} = M_{21}$ as a freebie mark. Always verify and present the matrix in symmetric form.
> 4. **Units:** A common trap—mixing lengths in cm and gravity in m/s². Stick to SI throughout.
> 5. **Order of Operations:** KTU answer scripts that jump from kinematics directly to "$\tau = M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G$" without showing the Lagrangian explicitly lose **3 to 4 marks** for skipped derivation. Always show $\mathcal{L}$.

---

## PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Lagrangian function $\mathcal{L}$ and state the Euler-Lagrange equation. Why is this approach preferred over the Newton-Euler method for deriving symbolic equations of motion in robotics?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The Lagrangian function is defined as the difference between the total kinetic energy $\mathcal{K}$ and total potential energy $\mathcal{P}$ of the system:
> $$\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$$
> The Euler-Lagrange equation for the $i^{th}$ generalized coordinate is:
> $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$
> **Why preferred in robotics:** The Lagrangian approach does not require computing internal constraint forces between links. It uses a single scalar energy function and yields the equations of motion in a structured, closed-form, symbolic format—ideal for deriving the standard manipulator equation $\boldsymbol{\tau} = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ for control design.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What are generalized coordinates? How are they chosen for a robotic manipulator with revolute and prismatic joints?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> Generalized coordinates $q_i$ are a **minimum set of independent variables** that completely specify the configuration of a mechanical system. The number of generalized coordinates equals the number of degrees of freedom (DOF).
> - For a **revolute joint**: $q_i = \theta_i$ (angular displacement in radians).
> - For a **prismatic joint**: $q_i = d_i$ (linear displacement in meters).
> For a robotic manipulator, the generalized coordinates are chosen as the **joint variables** itself, because they are naturally independent and minimal. For example, a 2R planar arm has $q_1 = \theta_1$ and $q_2 = \theta_2$.

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

> **A 2-DOF planar manipulator has link 1 of mass $m_1$, length $l_1$, COM at $l_{c1}$, and link 2 of mass $m_2$, length $l_2$, COM at $l_{c2}$. The joint variables are $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$. Both joints are revolute. Gravity acts downward.**
>
> **(a)** Derive the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ for the manipulator in terms of $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dot{\theta}_1, \dot{\theta}_2$. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Using the Euler-Lagrange equation, obtain the elements of the mass matrix $M(q)$ and the gravity vector $G(q)$, and write the manipulator equation in the standard form $\boldsymbol{\tau} = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Deriving the Lagrangian**

*Step 1: Kinematic Positions* (already done in Section 3):
- Link 1 COM: $(l_{c1}\cos\theta_1,\; l_{c1}\sin\theta_1)$.
- Link 2 COM: $(l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2),\; l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2))$.

*Step 2: Kinetic Energy*

$$
\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}m_1 l_{c1}^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}I_1\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2 v_{c2}^2 + \frac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2)^2
$$

with $v_{c2}^2$ derived as:

$$
v_{c2}^2 = (l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2l_1l_{c2}\cos\theta_2)\dot{\theta}_1^2 + 2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1l_{c2}\cos\theta_2)\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 + l_{c2}^2\dot{\theta}_2^2
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}\bigl[&m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2l_1l_{c2}\cos\theta_2) + I_1 + I_2\bigr]\dot{\theta}_1^2 \\
+ &\bigl[m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1l_{c2}\cos\theta_2) + I_2\bigr]\dot{\theta}_1\dot{\theta}_2 \\
+ \frac{1}{2}&\bigl[m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2\bigr]\dot{\theta}_2^2
\end{aligned}
$$

*Step 3: Potential Energy*

$$
\mathcal{P} = m_1 g\,l_{c1}\sin\theta_1 + m_2 g\,[l_1\sin\theta_1 + l_{c2}\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]
$$

*Step 4: Lagrangian*

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P} \quad \text{(Substitute the above expressions)}}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Mass Matrix, Gravity Vector, and Manipulator Equation**

*Step 1: Mass Matrix from $\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$*

$$
M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} m_1l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2l_1l_{c2}\cos\theta_2) + I_1 + I_2 & m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1l_{c2}\cos\theta_2) + I_2 \\ m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1l_{c2}\cos\theta_2) + I_2 & m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 2: Gravity Vector $G_i = \partial \mathcal{P}/\partial q_i$*

$$
G_1 = m_1 g\,l_{c1}\cos\theta_1 + m_2 g\,[l_1\cos\theta_1 + l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)]
$$

$$
G_2 = m_2 g\,l_{c2}\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
$$

*Step 3: Coriolis Matrix $C(q,\dot{q})$*

Using the Christoffel symbols of the first kind $c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$ and $C_{ij} = \sum_k c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$:

$$
C(q,\dot{q}) = m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin\theta_2 \begin{bmatrix} -\dot{\theta}_2 & -(\dot{\theta}_1+\dot{\theta}_2) \\ \dot{\theta}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 4: Final Manipulator Equation*

$$
\boxed{\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix} = M(q)\begin{bmatrix} \ddot{\theta}_1 \\ \ddot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix} + C(q,\dot{q})\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} G_1 \\ G_2 \end{bmatrix}}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

> **For a 2-DOF planar R-P (revolute-prismatic) manipulator: link 1 rotates about a vertical axis with angle $\theta_1$ (in the horizontal plane), and link 2 extends radially with prismatic displacement $d_2$. Link 1 has mass $m_1$ with COM at $l_{c1}$ from the base. Link 2 has mass $m_2$ with COM at $d_2/2$ from the prismatic joint. The prismatic joint is horizontal (gravity acts perpendicular to the motion plane).**
>
> **(a)** Form the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ of the system. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Derive the equation of motion in matrix form and identify the generalized forces. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Forming the Lagrangian**

Generalized coordinates: $q_1 = \theta_1$ (rotational), $q_2 = d_2$ (prismatic).

*Positions*:
- Link 1 COM: $(l_{c1}\cos\theta_1,\; l_{c1}\sin\theta_1)$.
- Link 2 COM: $((l_1 + d_2/2)\cos\theta_1,\; (l_1 + d_2/2)\sin\theta_1)$ (where $l_1$ is the fixed part of link 2).

*Velocities*:
- $v_{c1}^2 = l_{c1}^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2$
- $v_{c2}^2 = (l_1 + d_2/2)^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \dot{d}_2^2$

*Kinetic Energy*:

$$
\mathcal{K} = \frac{1}{2}(m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1)\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2\!\left[(l_1+d_2/2)^2 \dot{\theta}_1^2 + \dot{d}_2^2\right] + \frac{1}{2}I_2\dot{\theta}_1^2
$$

*Potential Energy*: Since motion is in the horizontal plane, gravity does no work and:

$$
\mathcal{P} = 0 \quad \text{(constant)}
$$

*Lagrangian*:

$$
\boxed{\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left[m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1 + m_2(l_1+d_2/2)^2 + I_2\right]\dot{\theta}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2\dot{d}_2^2}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Equations of Motion**

*Euler-Lagrange for $q_1 = \theta_1$*:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_1} = \tau_1
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1} = \left[m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1 + m_2(l_1+d_2/2)^2 + I_2\right]\dot{\theta}_1
$$

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{\theta}_1}\right) = \left[m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1 + m_2(l_1+d_2/2)^2 + I_2\right]\ddot{\theta}_1 + 2m_2(l_1+d_2/2)\dot{\theta}_1\dot{d}_2
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \theta_1} = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\left[m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1 + m_2(l_1+d_2/2)^2 + I_2\right]\ddot{\theta}_1 + 2m_2(l_1+d_2/2)\dot{\theta}_1\dot{d}_2 = \tau_1}
$$

*Euler-Lagrange for $q_2 = d_2$*:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{d}_2}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial d_2} = f_2
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{d}_2} = m_2 \dot{d}_2 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{d}{dt}(m_2 \dot{d}_2) = m_2\ddot{d}_2
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial d_2} = m_2(l_1+d_2/2)\dot{\theta}_1^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{m_2\ddot{d}_2 - m_2(l_1+d_2/2)\dot{\theta}_1^2 = f_2}
$$

*Matrix Form*:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1 + m_2(l_1+d_2/2)^2 + I_2 & 0 \\ 0 & m_2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \ddot{\theta}_1 \\ \ddot{d}_2 \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 2m_2(l_1+d_2/2)\dot{\theta}_1\dot{d}_2 \\ -m_2(l_1+d_2/2)\dot{\theta}_1^2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ f_2 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Identification of generalized forces**:
- $\tau_1$: **rotational torque** about the base (N·m).
- $f_2$: **prismatic force** along link 2 (N).

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus Insight:** The off-diagonal zero in $M(q)$ indicates the R-P arm is **decoupled in mass matrix form**—the joint inertia of $\theta_1$ depends on $d_2$ (variable), but $d_2$'s equation is mass-independent of $\theta_1$. This is a useful property exploited in some controller designs.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Lagrangian Mechanics Overview**

- **Lagrangian Definition:** $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{K} - \mathcal{P}$ — always compute $\mathcal{K}$ and $\mathcal{P}$ separately first.
- **Generalized Coordinates $q_i$:** equal in number to DOF; revolute → angle, prismatic → displacement.
- **Euler-Lagrange Equation:** $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ for each $i = 1, \ldots, n$.
- **KE Structure:** Always has the form $\frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$ — extract $M(q)$ from this quadratic form.
- **PE Structure:** Sum of $m_i g h_{c_i}(q)$ — derivative w.r.t. $q_i$ gives $G_i(q)$.
- **Mass Matrix Properties:** Symmetric ($M_{ij} = M_{ji}$), positive definite, and configuration-dependent.
- **Standard Form:** Always end with $\boldsymbol{\tau} = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$.
- **Term Identification:** $M\ddot{q}$ = inertial, $C\dot{q}$ = Coriolis/centrifugal, $G$ = gravity.
- **Jacobian Trick:** Velocity of COM can be written as $v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q)\dot{q}$ — useful for fast $M(q)$ computation.
- **Planar 2-DOF Standard Test:** Most KTU questions use 2R arm with $\theta_1, \theta_2$ in the vertical plane — memorize the $M_{11}, M_{12}, M_{22}$ pattern.
- **Gravity Direction:** If motion is horizontal, $G = 0$ (no gravity torque).
- **No Constraint Forces:** Lagrangian automatically absorbs internal joint reaction forces — a major advantage.
- **Watch Units:** Lengths in meters, angles in radians, masses in kg — keep SI consistent.
- **Show All Work:** KTU examiner deducts 3-4 marks if you skip the Lagrangian and jump directly to $\tau = M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G$.
- **Christoffel Symbols:** $c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}(\partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k + \partial M_{ik}/\partial q_j - \partial M_{jk}/\partial q_i)$ is the formal route to $C(q,\dot{q})$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Analysis Examples - 1 DOF Spring Cart System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Analysis Examples - 1 DOF Spring Cart System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **One Degree-of-Freedom (1-DOF) Spring-Cart System** is the simplest canonical lumped-parameter dynamic model used in robotics and vibration engineering. It consists of a rigid cart of mass **m** constrained to move along a single, frictionless, prismatic (translational) axis, supported by a linear spring of stiffness **k** and a viscous damper of coefficient **b**, with an external excitation force **F(t)** acting on the cart.

The **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** is defined as the minimum number of independent generalized coordinates required to completely specify the configuration of a mechanical system in space. For this system, the single generalized coordinate is the **linear displacement x(t)** measured from the static equilibrium position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Dynamic analysis of robotic systems starts with simple 1-DOF models because the same mathematical framework (Newton-Euler, Lagrangian) scales directly to multi-DOF serial and parallel manipulators via matrices. Mastering this primitive is non-negotiable.

Mathematically, the unforced conservative 1-DOF spring-mass system is governed by the second-order linear ordinary differential equation:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + k\,x(t) = 0$$

The damped, forced version — the most general form examined in KTU — is:

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + b\,\dot{x}(t) + k\,x(t) = F(t)$$

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a small toy wagon (the cart) sitting on a perfectly smooth air-track (no friction). It is tethered to a wall by a stretched rubber band (the spring) and pushed gently by a hand (the force). If you pull the wagon back and release it, it oscillates back and forth. Now, submerge the wagon in a tank of thick oil (the damper) — the oscillations slowly die out. This is precisely the 1-DOF spring-cart-damper system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Equivalents:**
> - **Automotive Suspension Strut:** A quarter-car model of a vehicle suspension is mathematically identical (m = sprung mass, k = tire/strut stiffness, b = shock absorber damping, F = road profile input).
> - **Building Floor Under Seismic Load:** A single-storey shear building is modeled as mass-stiffness-damper excited by ground acceleration.
> - **Robot Joint Prismatic Actuator:** The first link of a linear-axis robot (Cartesian gantry) reduces to this equation under no-load conditions.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | SI Unit | Standard Range (Robotics) |
|--------|----------|---------|---------------------------|
| **m** | Mass of cart | kg | 0.1 – 100 kg |
| **k** | Spring stiffness | N/m | 10 – 10⁵ N/m |
| **b** | Damping coefficient | N·s/m | 0.1 – 1000 N·s/m |
| **F(t)** | External force | N | 1 – 500 N |
| **x** | Displacement | m | 0.01 – 1 m |
| **ωₙ** | Natural frequency | rad/s | √(k/m) |
| **ζ** | Damping ratio | dimensionless | 0 – 1 (underdamped relevant) |

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Components and Their Physical Roles

The 1-DOF spring-cart system comprises four physical elements, each of which has an exact counterpart in a real robot:

1. **The Mass Element (m):** Represents the inertia of the moving payload or robot link. In robotics, this maps to the **inertia matrix term M(q)** in the general manipulator equation.
2. **The Spring Element (k):** Represents a conservative potential energy storage mechanism. In a robot joint, this may arise from joint compliance, cable elasticity, or even gravity restoring torque linearized about an operating point.
3. **The Damper Element (b):** Represents non-conservative energy dissipation. In robotics, this models joint friction, viscous bearing drag, or back-EMF damping in motors.
4. **The Excitation Force F(t):** Represents the **generalized actuator force** τ applied at the joint or the disturbance force from the environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Equilibrium Position Matters:** We always measure x from the **static equilibrium**, where the spring force balances gravity (mg). This is because the equation of motion should be **homogeneous** in x when F=0, so that gravity does not appear explicitly. This convention is also used in KTU numerical problems.

### 2.2 Generalized Coordinate Selection

A *generalized coordinate q* is any independent variable that uniquely defines the system's configuration. For the 1-DOF cart, the only admissible choice is:

$$q \equiv x$$

Number of generalized coordinates = Number of degrees of freedom = 1.

### 2.3 Free-Body Force Analysis (Newton-Euler Method)

When the cart is displaced by +x from equilibrium, four forces act along the horizontal axis:

| Force | Direction | Magnitude | Origin |
|-------|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Inertial reaction | Opposite to $\ddot{x}$ | $m\ddot{x}$ | Newton's 2nd law |
| Spring restoring | Opposite to $x$ | $kx$ | Hooke's law |
| Damper viscous | Opposite to $\dot{x}$ | $b\dot{x}$ | Newton's viscosity |
| Applied | As drawn | $F(t)$ | External |

Applying Newton's Second Law (ΣF = m·a) in the horizontal direction:

$$F(t) - kx - b\dot{x} = m\ddot{x}$$

Rearranging into the **standard form** puts the highest derivative on the left:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|------|-----------------|
| 1 | $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$ | Unforced Undamped EOM | Vibration analysis, free oscillation |
| 2 | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = 0$ | Unforced Damped EOM | Transient response of robot joint |
| 3 | $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ | Forced Damped EOM (general) | Complete robotic actuator model |
| 4 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural (undamped) frequency | Resonance avoidance |
| 5 | $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{mk}}$ | Damping ratio | Stability classification |
| 6 | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | Damped natural frequency | Real oscillation frequency |
| 7 | $T = \dfrac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2$ | Kinetic energy | Lagrangian formulation |
| 8 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}kx^2$ | Potential energy (spring only, x from eq.) | Lagrangian formulation |
| 9 | $D = \dfrac{1}{2}b\dot{x}^2$ | Rayleigh dissipation function | Lagrangian with damping |
| 10 | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial D}{\partial \dot{x}} = Q$ | Lagrange's equation with dissipation | Generalization to multi-DOF |
| 11 | $x(t) = A e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$ | Underdamped response | Step-response analysis |

> [!CAUTION]
> In the formula sheet, when using damping ratio in exam answers, **always derive** the value of ζ from the given m, b, k — do not skip this intermediate step. KTU examiners specifically look for this substitution.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robot Joint Compliance Modeling:** When a harmonic-drive or cable-driven joint has finite stiffness k, the first torsional mode of the link is exactly a 1-DOF spring-mass system, and the same formulae govern the **resonance frequency** that must be avoided by the controller.
- **Control Law Design:** The transfer function $G(s) = \frac{1}{ms^2 + bs + k}$ is the plant on which **PD and PID controllers** for joint position are classically designed.
- **Vibration Isolation:** A precision robot's tool mounted on rubber isolators is analyzed as m (tool) on k (rubber) with b (foam) — the same equations determine the **transmissibility curve**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Newton-Euler Method (Direct Force Balance)

This is the most common KTU-asked derivation and the easiest to score full marks on.

**Step 1 — Choose the coordinate system.**
Let the positive x-direction point to the right, with the origin at the cart's static equilibrium (where spring is at natural length or pre-compressed by mg — it does not matter because the gravity term cancels).

**Step 2 — Identify all forces acting along the x-direction.**

| Force | Symbol | Direction (sign convention) |
|-------|--------|------------------------------|
| External applied force | $F(t)$ | Positive (+x) |
| Spring restoring force | $F_s$ | Negative (opposes displacement) |
| Damping force | $F_d$ | Negative (opposes velocity) |
| Gravity (along x) | 0 | None — motion is horizontal |

**Step 3 — Express each force mathematically.**

By Hooke's law (spring):

$$F_s = -k\,x$$

By Newton's law of viscous damping:

$$F_d = -b\,\dot{x}$$

Applied force as given:

$$F_{app} = F(t)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's Second Law (ΣF = m·a).**

$$\sum F_x = m\,\ddot{x}$$

$$F(t) + F_s + F_d = m\ddot{x}$$

Substituting the expressions from Step 3:

$$F(t) - kx - b\dot{x} = m\ddot{x}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange into the standard form (highest-order derivative first, all on the left).**

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$$

This is the **governing equation of motion (EOM)** of the 1-DOF spring-cart-damper system under an external force F(t). It is a second-order, linear, non-homogeneous ODE with constant coefficients.

**Step 6 — Special case reductions.**

If $F(t) = 0$ (no external force) and $b = 0$ (no damping), we recover the simple harmonic oscillator:

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \ddot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where $\omega_n^2 = k/m$.

If $F(t) = 0$ but $b \neq 0$, we get the unforced damped EOM:

$$m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

If $F(t) = F_0 \cos(\omega t)$ (harmonic excitation), this is the resonance case commonly asked in KTU.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Lagrangian (Energy) Method

The Lagrangian method is energy-based and is **higher-scoring** in KTU because it scales naturally to multi-DOF robots (Module 3 and 4). Examiner preference: **always show both methods** if a 14-mark question asks to "derive the equation of motion."

**Step 1 — Choose the generalized coordinate.**

For the 1-DOF cart: $q_1 = x$. So the configuration is fully described by the single coordinate x.

**Step 2 — Compute the Kinetic Energy T.**

The cart translates horizontally with velocity $\dot{x}$. No rotation is involved (unlike a pendulum or a robotic arm).

$$T = \frac{1}{2} m \dot{x}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Potential Energy U.**

The spring is the only conservative element that stores energy (gravity is balanced at the equilibrium reference, so it does not contribute to U as a function of x).

$$U = \frac{1}{2} k x^2$$

**Step 4 — Form the Lagrangian L = T − U.**

$$L = T - U = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2 - \frac{1}{2}kx^2$$

**Step 5 — Compute the partial derivatives required by Lagrange's equation.**

The general form of Lagrange's equation for a conservative system is:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = Q_{nc}$$

where $Q_{nc}$ is the non-conservative generalized force (here, $F(t)$ from the actuator minus any damping contribution).

Compute each term:

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}} = m\dot{x}$$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) = m\ddot{x}$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial x} = -kx$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into Lagrange's equation.**

$$m\ddot{x} - (-kx) = Q_{nc}$$

$$m\ddot{x} + kx = Q_{nc}$$

**Step 7 — Add damping via Rayleigh's dissipation function.**

The Rayleigh dissipation function D captures non-conservative viscous effects:

$$D = \frac{1}{2} b \dot{x}^2$$

The generalized non-conservative force from D is $\partial D / \partial \dot{x} = b\dot{x}$, which **subtracts** from the right-hand side of Lagrange's equation (energy is leaving the system):

$$m\ddot{x} + kx + b\dot{x} = F(t)$$

Rearranging into the standard form:

$$\boxed{\,m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)\,}$$

**Step 8 — Verify agreement with Newton-Euler.**
The result is identical to that obtained by direct force balance, which is the cross-check that students are expected to mention in KTU answers for full credit.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative View of Both Methods

| Aspect | Newton-Euler | Lagrangian |
|--------|--------------|------------|
| Basis | Force/moment balance | Energy (T, U, D) |
| Free-body diagram required | Yes (mandatory) | No (only energies) |
| Handles non-conservative forces directly | Yes (FBD lists them) | Yes (via D or Q_nc) |
| Scales to multi-DOF | Tedious, requires vector loops | Trivial via matrix formulation |
| KTU score weight (typical 7-mark sub-Q) | Full marks with FBD | Full marks with energy terms |
| Cross-check | Both must yield the same EOM | Same |

---

### 3.4 Solution of the Unforced Undamped Case (Bonus — Frequently Asked)

Starting from $m\ddot{x} + kx = 0$:

**Step 1 — Assume an exponential solution** of the form $x(t) = A e^{\lambda t}$.

Substituting:

$$m A \lambda^2 e^{\lambda t} + k A e^{\lambda t} = 0$$

$$m\lambda^2 + k = 0$$

$$\lambda^2 = -\frac{k}{m} = -\omega_n^2$$

$$\lambda = \pm j\omega_n$$

**Step 2 — The general real solution** (using Euler's identity) is:

$$x(t) = A \cos(\omega_n t) + B \sin(\omega_n t)$$

or equivalently:

$$x(t) = C \cos(\omega_n t - \phi)$$

where $C = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(B/A)$.

The system **oscillates indefinitely** with natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ — this is why damping must be present in any real robot to prevent sustained resonance.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**
*Define the term Degree of Freedom (DOF) as applied to a robotic manipulator. How many degrees of freedom does a 1-DOF spring-cart system possess, and what is the corresponding generalized coordinate?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **Degree of Freedom (DOF)** of a mechanical system is the minimum number of independent generalized coordinates required to completely specify the configuration (position and orientation) of the system in space at any instant. **(1 mark)**
- A 1-DOF spring-cart system, moving along a single horizontal prismatic axis, possesses exactly **one degree of freedom**. **(1 mark)**
- The corresponding generalized coordinate is the **linear displacement x(t)** measured from the static equilibrium position of the cart. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
*Write the general equation of motion of a 1-DOF spring-mass-damper system subjected to an external force. Identify each term in the equation with its physical significance.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$m\ddot{x}(t) + b\dot{x}(t) + kx(t) = F(t)$$

- **$m\ddot{x}(t)$** — Inertial force, representing the resistance of the mass to acceleration (Newton's 2nd law term). **(1 mark)**
- **$b\dot{x}(t)$** — Viscous damping force, modeling non-conservative energy dissipation (e.g., bearing friction). **(1 mark)**
- **$kx(t)$** — Spring restoring force, modeling conservative potential energy storage (Hooke's law). **(1 mark)**
- **$F(t)$** — External generalized excitation force (the actuator input or disturbance). **(bonus mention, optional)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3, 14-Mark Pattern]**

**(a)** For a 1-DOF spring-cart-damper system of mass m = 5 kg, spring stiffness k = 500 N/m, and damping coefficient b = 20 N·s/m, derive the equation of motion using the **Newton-Euler (force-balance) method** when an external force F(t) = 50 sin(5t) N is applied. Clearly state the assumptions and draw the free-body diagram in words. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the **steady-state (particular) response** of the system in part (a) and determine its amplitude. Comment on whether the system is in resonance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Assumption Statement (1 mark):**
- Motion is one-dimensional along the horizontal x-axis.
- The mass m is rigid and lumped; the spring is massless and linear (Hookean); the damper is linear viscous.
- Coordinate x is measured from the static equilibrium, so gravity does not appear.
- No Coulomb (dry) friction; no slip between cart and track.

**Step 2 — Free-Body Description (1 mark):**
The cart of mass m is acted on by:
- Applied force F(t) = 50 sin(5t) in the +x direction,
- Spring force F_s = −k x (opposes displacement, Hooke's law),
- Damping force F_d = −b ẋ (opposes velocity, Newton's viscosity).

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law (2 marks):**
$$\sum F_x = m\ddot{x}$$
$$F(t) - kx - b\dot{x} = m\ddot{x}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values (1 mark):**
$$5\,\ddot{x} + 20\,\dot{x} + 500\,x = 50\sin(5t)$$

**Step 5 — Standard form and physical interpretation (2 marks):**
Divide through by m = 5 to obtain the canonical form:
$$\ddot{x} + 4\dot{x} + 100x = 10\sin(5t)$$

This is a forced, damped, second-order linear ODE. The unforced natural frequency is $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} = \sqrt{500/5} = 10$ rad/s. The forcing frequency is $\omega = 5$ rad/s.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Assume the particular (steady-state) solution (1 mark):**
For a sinusoidal input, the steady-state response is also sinusoidal at the same frequency ω = 5 rad/s, but with a phase lag φ:
$$x_p(t) = X\sin(5t - \phi)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the EOM (2 marks):**
Compute derivatives:
$$\dot{x}_p = 5X\cos(5t - \phi)$$
$$\ddot{x}_p = -25X\sin(5t - \phi)$$

Substitute into $5\ddot{x} + 20\dot{x} + 500x = 50\sin(5t)$:
$$5(-25X\sin(5t-\phi)) + 20(5X\cos(5t-\phi)) + 500(X\sin(5t-\phi)) = 50\sin(5t)$$

Group sine and cosine terms:
$$( -125X + 500X)\sin(5t-\phi) + 100X\cos(5t-\phi) = 50\sin(5t)$$
$$375X\sin(5t-\phi) + 100X\cos(5t-\phi) = 50\sin(5t)$$

**Step 3 — Use the identity $A\sin\theta + B\cos\theta = R\sin(\theta + \alpha)$ (1 mark):**
where $R = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ and $\tan\alpha = B/A$.

Here, $A = 375X$, $B = 100X$, so:
$$R = X\sqrt{375^2 + 100^2} = X\sqrt{140625 + 10000} = X\sqrt{150625} = 388.1X$$

The RHS amplitude is 50. Equating:
$$388.1X = 50 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad X = \frac{50}{388.1} = 0.1288 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the phase angle (1 mark):**
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{100}{375} = 0.2667 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.2667) = 14.93° = 0.261 \text{ rad}$$

So $\phi = \alpha = 0.261$ rad, meaning the response lags the input.

**Step 5 — Resonance commentary (2 marks):**
The undamped natural frequency is $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m} = 10$ rad/s. The damping ratio is:
$$\zeta = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \frac{20}{2\sqrt{5 \cdot 500}} = \frac{20}{2 \cdot 50} = 0.2$$

The damped natural frequency is $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 10\sqrt{0.96} = 9.798$ rad/s.

The forcing frequency is $\omega = 5$ rad/s, which is far below $\omega_d$. Therefore, the system is **NOT in resonance**. The amplitude is small (0.129 m), and the response is essentially quasi-static. Resonance would occur near $\omega \approx \omega_n = 10$ rad/s.

**Final Answer:** $x_{ss}(t) = 0.129 \sin(5t - 0.261)$ m.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** A 1-DOF spring-mass-damper system with m = 2 kg, k = 200 N/m, and b = 10 N·s/m is released from rest at an initial displacement of x(0) = 0.1 m. Using the **Lagrangian method**, derive the equation of motion and determine the **underdamped response** x(t) for t ≥ 0. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **natural frequency**, **damping ratio**, **damped natural frequency**, and the **time taken for the amplitude to decay to 1% of its initial value**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Choose generalized coordinate (1 mark):**
$q_1 = x$, the horizontal displacement of the cart from static equilibrium.

**Step 2 — Kinetic and Potential Energies (2 marks):**
$$T = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2 = \frac{1}{2}(2)\dot{x}^2 = \dot{x}^2$$
$$U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2 = \frac{1}{2}(200)x^2 = 100x^2$$

**Step 3 — Lagrangian and dissipation function (1 mark):**
$$L = T - U = \dot{x}^2 - 100x^2$$
$$D = \frac{1}{2}b\dot{x}^2 = \frac{1}{2}(10)\dot{x}^2 = 5\dot{x}^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply Lagrange's equation with dissipation (2 marks):**
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial D}{\partial \dot{x}} = Q$$

Compute partials:
- $\partial L/\partial \dot{x} = 2\dot{x}$ → $\frac{d}{dt}(\partial L/\partial \dot{x}) = 2\ddot{x}$
- $\partial L/\partial x = -200x$
- $\partial D/\partial \dot{x} = 10\dot{x}$

With no external excitation, Q = 0:
$$2\ddot{x} - (-200x) + 10\dot{x} = 0$$
$$2\ddot{x} + 10\dot{x} + 200x = 0$$

Dividing by 2 (mass normalization):
$$\ddot{x} + 5\dot{x} + 100x = 0$$

**Step 5 — Solve the characteristic equation (1 mark):**
$$s^2 + 5s + 100 = 0$$
$$s = \frac{-5 \pm \sqrt{25 - 400}}{2} = \frac{-5 \pm \sqrt{-375}}{2} = -2.5 \pm j\,9.682$$

The roots are complex conjugates with negative real part → **underdamped** response.

**General form of the underdamped solution:**
$$x(t) = e^{-2.5t}\bigl[A\cos(9.682t) + B\sin(9.682t)\bigr]$$

**Step 6 — Apply initial conditions to find A and B (bonus for full 7 marks):**
At t = 0, x(0) = 0.1, so $A = 0.1$.
Since released from rest, $\dot{x}(0) = 0$:
$$\dot{x}(t) = e^{-2.5t}\bigl[-9.682A\sin(9.682t) + 9.682B\cos(9.682t)\bigr] - 2.5e^{-2.5t}\bigl[A\cos(9.682t) + B\sin(9.682t)\bigr]$$

At t = 0: $9.682B - 2.5A = 0$ → $B = (2.5/9.682)(0.1) = 0.02582$.

$$\boxed{\,x(t) = e^{-2.5t}\bigl[0.1\cos(9.682t) + 0.02582\sin(9.682t)\bigr]\,}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Natural frequency (1 mark):**
$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{200}{2}} = \sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Damping ratio (2 marks):**
$$\zeta = \frac{b}{2\sqrt{mk}} = \frac{10}{2\sqrt{(2)(200)}} = \frac{10}{2\sqrt{400}} = \frac{10}{2 \times 20} = \frac{10}{40} = 0.25$$

**Step 3 — Damped natural frequency (1 mark):**
$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 10\sqrt{1 - 0.0625} = 10\sqrt{0.9375} = 9.682 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the underdamped classification (1 mark):**
Since $0 < \zeta = 0.25 < 1$, the system is **underdamped** — oscillations occur but decay exponentially.

**Step 5 — Time for amplitude to decay to 1% (2 marks):**
The amplitude envelope is governed by the exponential decay factor $e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$:
$$e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} = 0.01$$
$$-\zeta\omega_n t = \ln(0.01) = -4.6052$$
$$t = \frac{4.6052}{\zeta\omega_n} = \frac{4.6052}{0.25 \times 10} = \frac{4.6052}{2.5} = 1.842 \text{ s}$$

**Final Summary of Results:**
| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Natural frequency $\omega_n$ | **10 rad/s** |
| Damping ratio $\zeta$ | **0.25** |
| Damped frequency $\omega_d$ | **9.682 rad/s** |
| 1% decay time | **1.842 s** |

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to measure x from the static equilibrium** → gravity term (mg) wrongly appears in the EOM, leading to a non-homogeneous equation. **−2 marks** in derivation.
> 2. **Sign error in spring or damper force** → the FBD arrow is drawn in the same direction as motion, but Hooke's law and damping both oppose motion. **−1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Using x(t) for displacement and q(t) for generalized coordinate inconsistently** → KTU expects a single variable throughout. **−0.5 mark** for notational confusion.
> 4. **Forgetting the Rayleigh dissipation function D when using Lagrangian method** with damping → the damping term is missing in the final EOM. **−2 marks** is common.
> 5. **Resonance comment without computing the damping ratio** → just stating "no resonance" without showing ζ and ω_d calculation. Examiners expect a numerical justification. **−1 mark**.
> 6. **Drawing the FBD in the answer book but not labeling it** → the FBD alone without clearly marked force arrows and labels carries **0 marks** in KTU. Always annotate the FBD with force names and sign conventions.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (1-DOF Spring-Cart System):**

- **System:** Mass **m** + Spring **k** + Damper **b** + Force **F(t)** + coordinate **x**.
- **DOF = 1**, generalized coordinate = **x** (measured from **static equilibrium**).
- **Governing EOM (general form):** $m\ddot{x} + b\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$.
- **Natural frequency:** $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ rad/s.
- **Damping ratio:** $\zeta = \dfrac{b}{2\sqrt{mk}}$ (dimensionless).
- **Damped natural frequency:** $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ rad/s.
- **Three cases:** Underdamped (ζ < 1), Critically damped (ζ = 1), Overdamped (ζ > 1).
- **Underdamped free response:** $x(t) = e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\bigl[A\cos(\omega_d t) + B\sin(\omega_d t)\bigr]$.
- **Forced steady-state:** $x_{ss}(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$ with $X = \dfrac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (b\omega)^2}}$.
- **Resonance condition:** $\omega \to \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ (for damped systems), or $\omega = \omega_n$ (undamped).
- **Energy method (Lagrangian):** $T = \frac{1}{2}m\dot{x}^2$, $U = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$, $D = \frac{1}{2}b\dot{x}^2$, $L = T - U$.
- **Lagrange's equation with damping:** $\dfrac{d}{dt}\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{x}}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial D}{\partial \dot{x}} = Q$.
- **Cross-check:** Both Newton-Euler and Lagrangian methods must yield the **same EOM** — this is the verification step KTU examiners reward.
- **Engineering analogies:** Quarter-car suspension, single-storey building, prismatic robot joint, vibration isolator.
- **Transfer function (Laplace domain):** $G(s) = \dfrac{X(s)}{F(s)} = \dfrac{1}{ms^2 + bs + k}$.
- **Exam mantra:** Draw the FBD, state the assumptions, derive via either method, define ωₙ and ζ, then solve.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Control: The Control Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Robot Control: The Control Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**The Control Problem** in robotics is the engineering task of determining the *correct sequence of actuator signals* (torques, voltages, currents) that must be applied to a robot's joints so that its end-effector follows a *desired trajectory* while satisfying *force, stability, and accuracy* constraints.

In formal KTU 2024 syllabus terminology:

$$\text{Control Problem} : \mathbf{q}_d(t) \longrightarrow \mathbf{u}(t)$$

where $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ is the desired joint (or task-space) trajectory and $\mathbf{u}(t) = [\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n]^T$ is the vector of actuator inputs (usually joint torques).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Control Problem sits at the intersection of *robot kinematics*, *dynamics*, and *classical control theory*. It is the bridge that converts the abstract *plan* (trajectory) into the physical *motion* (actuator effort).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine you are **driving a car along a winding road at night**.

| Driving Element | Robotic Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Road (desired path) | Pre-planned trajectory $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ |
| Steering wheel + accelerator | Joint motors / actuators |
| Your eyes (vision feedback) | Encoders, resolvers, vision sensors |
| Brain (decision) | Microcontroller / control algorithm |
| Car's actual position | Measured output $\mathbf{q}(t)$ |

Just as you constantly *observe* where the car is and *adjust* the steering to stay on the road, a robot controller must constantly *measure* the actual joint positions and *compute* corrective actuator commands. This continuous "measure–compare–correct" loop is the **essence of the control problem**.

## 1.3 Why Is It "A Problem"?

A robot manipulator is, in physics, a **coupled, nonlinear, multi-input multi-output (MIMO) mechanical system** with:

- **Nonlinearity** — Coriolis, centrifugal, and gravitational terms are functions of $\mathbf{q}$ and $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
- **Coupling** — Motion of one joint induces reaction forces on every other joint.
- **Time-variance** — Payload mass changes during grasping.
- **External disturbances** — Friction, contact forces, and vibration.

Therefore, simply sending a voltage to a motor does **not** guarantee precise motion. The *control problem* is to design a controller that overcomes these challenges.

## 1.4 Classification of Robot Control

Robot control strategies are broadly classified along two orthogonal axes:

> [!NOTE]
> **Axis 1 — By Information Flow:**
> - **Open-loop control** (no feedback) — cheap, simple, but inaccurate.
> - **Closed-loop control** (feedback-based) — accurate, stable, but complex.
>
> **Axis 2 — By Controlled Variable:**
> - **Position control** — track a desired joint angle $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$.
> - **Velocity control** — regulate angular speed $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$.
> - **Force/impedance control** — regulate contact force (used in assembly, grinding).

The remainder of these notes will focus primarily on **closed-loop joint position control**, which is the foundation of all KTU-level exam questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Control Hierarchy

Robot control is organized in a **three-layer hierarchy** (must be memorised for KTU ESE):

| Layer | Level of Abstraction | Typical Loop Rate | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Task Level** (Highest) | "Pick the bolt, insert it" | User interaction | Sub-goals |
| **Trajectory Level** | Path generation, planning | 10 – 100 Hz | Smooth $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ |
| **Servo / Joint Level** (Lowest) | Joint servo control | 100 Hz – 1 kHz | Motor voltages $\mathbf{u}(t)$ |

> The **Control Problem** in this module refers almost entirely to the **Servo/Joint Level**, the lowest and fastest layer.

## 2.2 The Open-Loop Controller

The simplest solution: compute the torque from the inverse dynamics model and apply it directly.

$$\tau = M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + V(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) + G(\mathbf{q})$$

where:
- $M(\mathbf{q})$ = inertia matrix (n × n)
- $V(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ = Coriolis + centrifugal vector
- $G(\mathbf{q})$ = gravity vector

**Pros:** Very fast response.
**Cons:** Any modelling error, friction, or disturbance produces a *permanent* trajectory error — there is no feedback to correct it.

## 2.3 The Closed-Loop (Feedback) Controller

This is the **standard industrial solution**. The architecture is:

```
                        e(t)
   qd ──►[+]────►[Controller Gc(s)]──►[Plant Gp(s)]──► q(t)
            ▲                                          │
            │                                          │
            └──────────────[Sensor H(s)]◄──────────────┘
```

Mathematically, the **error signal** drives the controller:

$$e(t) = q_d(t) - q(t)$$

$$\tau(t) = G_c(s) \cdot E(s) + \tau_{ff}(t)$$

where $\tau_{ff}$ is the *feed-forward* term from the inverse dynamics (computed-torque approach), and $G_c(s)$ is a classical compensator — most commonly a **PID controller**.

## 2.4 The PID Controller (Workhorse of Robotics)

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

| Term | Effect on System | Real-World Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ (Proportional) | Reduces rise time, increases stiffness | "Steering harder when far off" |
| $K_i$ (Integral) | Eliminates steady-state error | "Compensating for a bias in steering" |
| $K_d$ (Derivative) | Increases damping, reduces overshoot | "Slowing down as you approach the line" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K_p too high** → oscillation / instability.
> **K_i too high** → integrator wind-up, slow recovery.
> **K_d too high** → amplifies sensor noise.
> Tuning PID gains is itself a *control sub-problem*.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = M(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + V + G$ | Inverse dynamics (open-loop) | N·m |
| 2 | $e(t) = q_d - q$ | Tracking error | rad |
| 3 | $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ | PID compensator | V/rad |
| 4 | $G_{cl}(s) = \dfrac{G_c G_p}{1 + G_c G_p H}$ | Closed-loop TF (unity H) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s)$ | Final-value steady-state error | rad |
| 6 | $E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G_c G_p H}$ | Error in Laplace domain | rad·s |
| 7 | Characteristic eqn: $1 + G_c G_p H = 0$ | Stability condition | — |
| 8 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/I}$ | Natural frequency (2nd-order) | rad/s |
| 9 | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{KI}}$ | Damping ratio | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tau_m \approx \dfrac{J}{K_t K_b} s$ | DC motor electrical time constant | s |

> **Engineering Utility:** PID control is the *de-facto* standard in nearly all commercial robot arms (e.g., FANUC, KUKA, ABB) for their joint-level servos. Mastery of these equations is essential for any KTU 2024 robotics exam.

## 2.6 Steady-State Error Analysis (Quick Reference)

For a **unity-feedback** system with input $R(s)$ and plant $G(s)$:

| Input Type | $R(s)$ | $e_{ss}$ for Type-0 | Type-1 | Type-2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Step | $1/s$ | $\dfrac{1}{1+K_p}$ | **0** | **0** |
| Ramp | $1/s^2$ | $\infty$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_v}$ | **0** |
| Parabola | $1/s^3$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\dfrac{1}{K_a}$ |

> A robot joint controller is normally **Type-1** (one integrator) — this guarantees zero steady-state error for *constant* setpoints.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Closed-Loop Transfer Function

**Given** the standard negative-feedback block diagram with forward-path $G(s) = G_c(s) G_p(s)$ and feedback $H(s)$:

**Step 1.** Write the output equation.

The output of the plant equals the controller output times the plant:

$$Q(s) = G(s) \cdot E(s)$$

**Step 2.** Write the error as the difference between reference and feedback signal.

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) Q(s)$$

**Step 3.** Substitute Step 2 into Step 1.

$$Q(s) = G(s) \bigl[ R(s) - H(s) Q(s) \bigr]$$

**Step 4.** Expand and collect $Q(s)$ terms.

$$Q(s) = G(s) R(s) - G(s) H(s) Q(s)$$

$$Q(s) + G(s) H(s) Q(s) = G(s) R(s)$$

**Step 5.** Factor out $Q(s)$ and solve.

$$Q(s) \, \bigl[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \bigr] = G(s) R(s)$$

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{Q(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s) H(s)}\,}$$

This is the **closed-loop transfer function**. The denominator $1 + G(s) H(s) = 0$ is the **characteristic equation** whose roots determine stability.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Error of a Unity-Feedback System

**Given** $H(s) = 1$ and $G(s) = G_c G_p$. We want the steady-state error for a *unit step* input $R(s) = 1/s$.

**Step 1.** The error in Laplace domain is:

$$E(s) = R(s) - Q(s) = R(s) - G(s) E(s)$$

$$E(s) \bigl[ 1 + G(s) \bigr] = R(s)$$

$$E(s) = \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $R(s) = 1/s$:

$$E(s) = \dfrac{1/s}{1 + G(s)} = \dfrac{1}{s\bigl[1 + G(s)\bigr]}$$

**Step 3.** Apply the **Final Value Theorem** (valid only if all poles of $sE(s)$ lie in the open left half-plane):

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s E(s)$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate the limit for a Type-0 system with finite DC gain $K$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + K}$$

For a Type-1 system (containing one $1/s$ in $G$), $G(0) = \infty$, so:

$$\boxed{\,e_{ss} = 0\,}$$

This is *why* robot joint controllers include an integrator — to drive step-tracking error to zero.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Second-Order Model of a Single Joint

A single rotational joint driven by a DC motor can be modelled as:

**Equation of motion of the rotor + load:**

$$J \ddot{\theta} + b \dot{\theta} = \tau_m - \tau_{disturbance}$$

where $J$ is the equivalent inertia, $b$ the viscous friction, and $\tau_m$ the motor torque.

**Step 1.** Motor torque from current: $\tau_m = K_t i_a$.
**Step 2.** Armature circuit: $L \dfrac{di_a}{dt} + R i_a + K_b \dot{\theta} = v_a$.
**Step 3.** Assuming the electrical time constant $L/R$ is negligible (typical in robotics), $v_a \approx R i_a + K_b \dot{\theta}$.

Combining:

$$J \ddot{\theta} + \left( b + \dfrac{K_t K_b}{R} \right)\dot{\theta} = \dfrac{K_t}{R} v_a$$

**Step 4.** Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$J s^2 \theta(s) + B_{eq} s \, \theta(s) = K_v \, V(s)$$

where $B_{eq} = b + K_t K_b / R$ and $K_v = K_t / R$.

**Step 5.** Hence the **plant transfer function**:

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{K_v}{s(J s + B_{eq})}\,}$$

This is a **Type-1, second-order system** — the canonical model used in every KTU 2024 exam question on joint control.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of P, I, D Gains

| Gain Increased | Rise Time | Overshoot | Settling Time | Steady-State Error | Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ ↑ | ↓ decreases | ↑ increases | small change | ↓ decreases | ↓ degrades |
| $K_i$ ↑ | ↓ decreases | ↑ increases | ↑ increases | **eliminates** | ↓ degrades |
| $K_d$ ↑ | small change | ↓ decreases | ↓ decreases | small change | ↑ improves |

> This matrix appears frequently in KTU 2024 Part-B questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Joint Servo

This is the **canonical architecture** every KTU student must be able to draw from memory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QD["qd(t) Desired Joint Position"]
    SUM["Summing Junction"]
    EC["Error e(t) = qd - q"]
    GC["Controller Gc(s) - PID"]
    FF["Feedforward Torque tauff"]
    ADD2["Summing Junction 2"]
    GP["Plant Gp(s) - Motor + Link"]
    Q["Actual q(t)"]
    SENS["Encoder / Resolver H(s)"]

    QD --> SUM
    SUM --> EC
    EC --> GC
    GC --> ADD2
    FF --> ADD2
    ADD2 --> GP
    GP --> Q
    Q --> SENS
    SENS --> SUM
```

## 4.2 Control Hierarchy Flow (3-Layer View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TASK["Layer 1 - TASK LEVEL\nPick and Place the Object\nLoop rate: User driven"]
    TRAJ["Layer 2 - TRAJECTORY LEVEL\nGenerate qd t and qd dot t\nLoop rate: 10 to 100 Hz"]
    SERVO["Layer 3 - SERVO LEVEL\nCompute tau t and apply voltage\nLoop rate: 100 Hz to 1 kHz"]
    MOTOR["DC Motor + Link Dynamics\nPlant Gp s"]

    TASK --> TRAJ
    TRAJ --> SERVO
    SERVO --> MOTOR
    MOTOR -. feedback .-> SERVO
```

## 4.3 Open-Loop vs Closed-Loop — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPEN["OPEN LOOP CONTROL"]
        direction LR
        O1["qd t"] --> O2["Inverse Dynamics M V G"] --> O3["Motor"] --> O4["q t"]
    end

    subgraph CLOSED["CLOSED LOOP CONTROL"]
        direction LR
        C1["qd t"] --> C2["+"] --> C3["Controller"] --> C4["Motor"] --> C5["q t"]
        C5 -.-> C6["Encoder"] -.-> C2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the open-loop branch, there is **no return arrow** from $q(t)$ back to the input — the model errors are never corrected. In the closed-loop branch, the dashed feedback path closes the loop.

## 4.4 PID Functional Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E["e t"] --> P["P - Kp times e t\nProportional"]
    E --> I["I - Ki integral e dt\nIntegral"]
    E --> D["D - Kd de dt\nDerivative"]
    P --> SUM["Summing Junction"]
    I --> SUM
    D --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT["Controller Output u t"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. State and briefly explain the "robot control problem" as defined in classical robotics. Mention the two orthogonal axes used to classify robot controllers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The robot control problem is the determination of actuator signals $\tau(t)$ that drive a manipulator's joints to follow a desired trajectory $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ while satisfying accuracy, stability, and force constraints. (1 mark)

It is classified along:
1. **Information-flow axis** — open-loop vs. closed-loop (1 mark)
2. **Controlled-variable axis** — position control vs. velocity control vs. force/impedance control (1 mark)

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop control of a robot joint. Give one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Absent | Present (encoder) |
| Steady-state error | Non-zero | Drive to zero |
| Stability | Always stable | Conditional (may oscillate) |

**Advantage of open-loop:** Simple, cheap, fast — no sensor needed.
**Advantage of closed-loop:** Robust to disturbances and model errors. (3 marks total)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

### Question A

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **closed-loop position control system** for a single robot joint. Clearly identify the controller, plant, sensor, error signal, and feedback path. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **closed-loop transfer function** $Q(s)/R(s)$ for a unity-feedback system. Apply the final-value theorem to show that a Type-1 system produces **zero steady-state error** for a unit-step input. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

A closed-loop position control system for a robot joint contains the following functional blocks:

- **Controller $G_c(s)$** — typically a PID; takes error, produces voltage command.
- **Plant $G_p(s)$** — DC motor + link, modelled as $\dfrac{K_v}{s(Js + B_{eq})}$ (2 marks for the model).
- **Sensor $H(s)$** — encoder or resolver; unity in the standard configuration.
- **Error $e(t) = q_d - q$** — summing junction subtracting feedback from reference.
- **Feed-forward path** — optional computed-torque term $\tau_{ff} = M\ddot{q}_d + V + G$. (3 marks for the diagram and labels).
- **Operational flow:** $q_d \rightarrow$ sum $\rightarrow G_c \rightarrow G_p \rightarrow q$, with $q \rightarrow$ sensor $\rightarrow$ back to sum. (2 marks for the closed-loop explanation).

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Derivation of CLTF** (already given in §3.1 above). The student must write:

1. $Q(s) = G(s)E(s)$ (1 mark)
2. $E(s) = R(s) - H(s)Q(s)$ (1 mark)
3. Substitute and simplify to obtain $\dfrac{Q}{R} = \dfrac{G}{1+GH}$ (2 marks)
4. Set $H = 1$, find $E(s) = \dfrac{R}{1+G}$ (1 mark)
5. Apply FVT: $e_{ss} = \lim_{s\to 0}\dfrac{sR}{1+G(s)}$ (1 mark)
6. Conclude $e_{ss} = 0$ for Type-1 step input because $G(s) \to \infty$ as $s \to 0$. (1 mark)

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** What is a **PID controller**? Write its transfer function and explain the physical role of each of the three terms ($K_p$, $K_i$, $K_d$) in the context of a robot joint. Use a 2 × 3 table to summarise the effect of increasing each gain on rise time, overshoot, and steady-state error. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the **equation of motion** of a single DC-motor-driven joint, derive the **plant transfer function** $\theta(s)/V(s)$ and identify its **system type**. Comment on its suitability for a robot joint servo when used in unity feedback. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) — 7 marks**

A PID (Proportional–Integral–Derivative) controller is a linear compensator that combines three actions on the tracking error to produce a control signal.

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$

- **$K_p$ (Proportional):** immediate corrective action proportional to current error — increases stiffness. (1 mark)
- **$K_i$ (Integral):** accumulates past error — eliminates steady-state offset. (1 mark)
- **$K_d$ (Derivative):** acts on rate of change of error — provides damping, suppresses overshoot. (1 mark)

**Effect table (2 marks):**

| Gain ↑ | Rise Time | Overshoot | Steady-State Error |
|---|---|---|---|
| $K_p$ | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ |
| $K_i$ | ↓ | ↑ | → 0 |
| $K_d$ | ≈ | ↓ | ≈ |

**Transfer function in compact form and conclusion** (2 marks).

**(b) — 7 marks**

The full derivation is given in §3.3. The student must:

1. Write $J\ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta} = K_t i_a$ (mechanical). (1 mark)
2. Write $L\dot{i}_a + R i_a + K_b\dot{\theta} = v_a$ (electrical). (1 mark)
3. Use $L/R \to 0$ to eliminate the electrical dynamics. (1 mark)
4. Combine to get $J\ddot{\theta} + B_{eq}\dot{\theta} = K_v v_a$. (1 mark)
5. Laplace transform with zero ICs. (1 mark)
6. Obtain $\dfrac{\theta(s)}{V(s)} = \dfrac{K_v}{s(Js + B_{eq})}$ — a **Type-1, second-order** system. (1 mark)
7. Comment: in unity feedback it gives **zero steady-state error** for step inputs, making it suitable for joint position servos. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in the KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** at the summing junction — feedback is *subtracted*, not added. Examiners deduct 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up open-loop and closed-loop transfer functions** — the open-loop is $G$; the closed-loop is $G/(1+GH)$. This single error can cost 3–4 marks.
> 3. **Applying the Final Value Theorem to an unstable system** — the theorem's pre-condition (poles in LHP) is often skipped. KTU 2024 key specifically tests this.
> 4. **Confusing "system type" with "order"** — Type refers to the *number of free integrators*; order refers to the *highest power of $s$* in the denominator. They are independent.
> 5. **Omitting units** in the final formula — $K_p$ is V/rad, $K_i$ is V/(rad·s), $K_d$ is V·s/rad. KTU 2024 strictly penalises missing units.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Control Problem:** Map the desired trajectory $\mathbf{q}_d(t)$ to actuator signals $\tau(t)$ while maintaining stability and accuracy.
- **Hierarchy:** Task → Trajectory → Servo (the "Control Problem" lives in the **Servo** layer).
- **Open-loop:** $\tau = M\ddot{q} + V + G$ — no feedback, simple but error-prone.
- **Closed-loop:** $e = q_d - q$ drives a controller $G_c(s)$ — robust, requires sensor.
- **PID compensator:** $G_c(s) = K_p + K_i/s + K_d s$ — industrial standard.
- **CLTF:** $Q/R = G/(1+GH)$ — denominator is the **characteristic equation**.
- **Stability:** All roots of $1+GH=0$ must lie in the open left half-plane.
- **Steady-state error (Type-1, step input):** $e_{ss} = 0$.
- **DC-motor plant TF:** $\theta/V = K_v / [s(Js + B_{eq})]$ — Type-1, 2nd-order.
- **Tuning intuition:** ↑$K_p$ → faster but oscillatory; ↑$K_i$ → eliminates offset but causes wind-up; ↑$K_d$ → damps overshoot but amplifies noise.
- **Exam tip:** Always state the **system type** explicitly when solving steady-state-error problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Single Axis PID Control - Control Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Single Axis PID Control - Control Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Single Axis PID Control** system is a fundamental **closed-loop feedback control architecture** used in robotic manipulators to regulate a single joint or axis. It continuously computes an **error value** as the difference between a **desired setpoint** (reference position/velocity) and the **measured process variable** (actual position/velocity from an encoder/resolver), and applies a corrective control action based on three terms: **Proportional (P)**, **Integral (I)**, and **Derivative (D)**.

The **PID Control Law** is the mathematical expression that combines these three terms to generate the actuator command signal:

$$u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau) \, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ = Control signal (motor torque/voltage) sent to the actuator
- $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ = Error signal (reference minus measured output)
- $K_p$ = Proportional gain
- $K_i$ = Integral gain
- $K_d$ = Derivative gain

> [!NOTE]
