## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define **Path Planning** and **Trajectory Planning** in robotics. Highlight any **two key differences** between them.

**Model Answer:**

**Path Planning:** It is the process of determining a geometric, collision-free sequence of configurations (or waypoints) that the robot must traverse from a start position to a goal position in its workspace or configuration space, **without consideration of time or velocity**.

**Trajectory Planning:** It is the process of specifying the **time history** of the robot's position, velocity, and acceleration along the geometric path, ensuring smooth and dynamically feasible motion.

**Two key differences:**

1. Path planning addresses **geometry (where)**; trajectory planning addresses **time and dynamics (when and how fast)**.
2. Path planning does not consider velocity or acceleration bounds; trajectory planning strictly enforces joint velocity, acceleration, and jerk limits.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

What is a **configuration space (C-space)**? Why is it preferred over Cartesian workspace for path planning?

**Model Answer:**

The **Configuration Space (C-space)** of a robot with $n$ joints is the set $\mathcal{C} \subset \mathbb{R}^n$ of all possible joint configurations $q = (q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n)$ that the robot can attain.

**Why preferred over Cartesian workspace:**
1. The robot itself reduces to a **single point** in C-space, which dramatically simplifies collision-avoidance computation.
2. Obstacles in the workspace can be mapped into **C-obstacles**, turning a complex geometric problem into a simpler point-avoidance problem.
3. Joint limits are naturally represented as a hypercube $\mathcal{C}_{valid} \subseteq \mathcal{C}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Path Planning Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **components of a path planning problem**. Describe with neat sketches how the path is represented as a mapping $\pi : [0, 1] \to \mathcal{C}_{free}$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compare **joint space** and **Cartesian space** path planning techniques. Mention one major advantage and one limitation of each.

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

The path planning problem is formally defined by a 5-tuple:

1. **Robot Model:** A kinematic chain with $n$ joints, configuration $q = (q_1, \dots, q_n) \in \mathcal{C}$.
2. **Workspace $\mathcal{W}$:** The 3-D physical environment in which the robot operates.
3. **Obstacle Set $\mathcal{O}$:** Static geometric regions the robot must not enter.
4. **Start Configuration $q_{start}$:** The initial pose, e.g., $\theta_1 = 0, \theta_2 = 30°$.
5. **Goal Configuration $q_{goal}$:** The desired final pose, e.g., $\theta_1 = 45°, \theta_2 = 60°$.

**Path Representation:** A path $\pi$ is a continuous mapping

$$
\pi : [0, 1] \to \mathcal{C}_{free}
$$

such that $\pi(0) = q_{start}$ and $\pi(1) = q_{goal}$, and for every $s \in [0, 1]$, $\pi(s)$ lies in $\mathcal{C}_{free}$ (i.e., no collision). The parameter $s$ is dimensionless — it only indicates "progress along the path," not physical time.

The path may be represented as:
- A sequence of discrete waypoints: $\pi = \{q_0, q_1, q_2, \dots, q_N\}$.
- A continuous parametric curve: $\pi(s) = (q_1(s), q_2(s), \dots, q_n(s))^T$.

The **free configuration space** $\mathcal{C}_{free} = \mathcal{C} \setminus \mathcal{C}_{obs}$ is the set of all joint configurations that avoid the C-obstacles (the pre-images of workspace obstacles under forward kinematics).

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

| Aspect | Joint Space Path Planning | Cartesian Space Path Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Domain of path** | Joint angles $\theta_i$ | End-effector pose $(x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi)$ |
| **Path appearance** | Non-intuitive in Cartesian view | Geometrically intuitive (e.g., straight line) |
| **Computation** | Direct — no IK required | Requires repeated Inverse Kinematics |
| **Singularity handling** | Naturally avoids workspace singularities | May pass through kinematic singularities |
| **Advantage** | Computationally cheap and singularity-free | Straight-line motions in 3-D space are easy to visualize |
| **Limitation** | End-effector path is unpredictable | IK may have no solution or multiple solutions mid-path |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Trajectory Planning Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Derive the coefficients of a **cubic polynomial trajectory** for a single joint moving from $q_0$ to $q_f$ in time $t_f$, with initial velocity $v_0$ and final velocity $v_f$.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A robot joint must move from $q_0 = 20°$ to $q_f = 80°$ in $t_f = 2$ s, with $v_0 = 10°/s$ and $v_f = 0°/s$. Compute the **cubic trajectory** $q(t)$ and verify the velocity at $t = 0$ and $t = t_f$.

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

Assume cubic polynomial form: $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$.

Velocity: $\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$.

Applying the four boundary conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q(0) = q_0 &\Rightarrow a_0 = q_0 \\
\dot{q}(0) = v_0 &\Rightarrow a_1 = v_0 \\
q(t_f) = q_f &\Rightarrow a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = q_f \\
\dot{q}(t_f) = v_f &\Rightarrow a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = v_f
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving the linear system (see Section 3.2 of these notes for full derivation):

$$
\boxed{a_0 = q_0, \quad a_1 = v_0, \quad a_2 = \frac{3(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2v_0 + v_f}{t_f}, \quad a_3 = \frac{2(q_0 - q_f)}{t_f^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{t_f^2}}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:** $q_0 = 20°$, $q_f = 80°$, $t_f = 2$ s, $v_0 = 10°/s$, $v_f = 0°/s$.

**Step 1: Compute the four coefficients.**

$$
a_0 = 20
$$

$$
a_1 = 10
$$

$$
a_2 = \frac{3(80 - 20)}{2^2} - \frac{2(10) + 0}{2} = \frac{180}{4} - \frac{20}{2} = 45 - 10 = 35
$$

$$
a_3 = \frac{2(20 - 80)}{2^3} + \frac{10 + 0}{2^2} = \frac{-120}{8} + \frac{10}{4} = -15 + 2.5 = -12.5
$$

**Step 2: Write the trajectory.**

$$
\boxed{q(t) = 20 + 10 t + 35 t^2 - 12.5 t^3 \quad \text{(degrees)}}
$$

**Step 3: Verify boundary velocities.**

Velocity profile:

$$
\dot{q}(t) = 10 + 70 t - 37.5 t^2
$$

- At $t = 0$: $\dot{q}(0) = 10 + 0 - 0 = 10°/s$ ✓ (matches $v_0$)
- At $t = 2$: $\dot{q}(2) = 10 + 140 - 37.5 \times 4 = 10 + 140 - 150 = 0°/s$ ✓ (matches $v_f$)

**Step 4: Verify final position.**

$q(2) = 20 + 10(2) + 35(4) - 12.5(8) = 20 + 20 + 140 - 100 = 80°$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common mistake #1:** Students often write the cubic formula with **wrong sign** for $a_3$. Double-check: $a_3 = \frac{2(q_0 - q_f)}{t_f^3} + \frac{v_0 + v_f}{t_f^2}$. Note the **first term has $(q_0 - q_f)$**, NOT $(q_f - q_0)$.
> 2. **Common mistake #2:** Students frequently forget to set $t_0 = 0$ before deriving. KTU assumes $t_0 = 0$ unless stated otherwise.
> 3. **Common mistake #3:** Units! If $q$ is in degrees and time in seconds, the units of $a_2$ are **deg/s²**, not rad/s². Do not convert unless the problem asks.
> 4. **Common mistake #4:** In Part B (b), many students skip the verification step and lose 1 mark. Always verify boundary conditions to claim full marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (must memorize before KTU ESE):**

- **Path** = geometry only; **Trajectory** = geometry + time.
- Path planning uses **configuration space**; trajectory planning uses **time-parameterized** $q(t)$.
- Cubic polynomial trajectory requires **4 boundary conditions** (2 position + 2 velocity).
- Quintic polynomial trajectory requires **6 boundary conditions** (2 position + 2 velocity + 2 acceleration) — used when jerk must be bounded.
- **Joint space planning**: simple, no IK, but Cartesian path is unpredictable.
- **Cartesian space planning**: intuitive straight-line paths, but requires **Inverse Kinematics** at every waypoint and may hit **kinematic singularities**.
- The path mapping $\pi : [0,1] \to \mathcal{C}_{free}$ uses parameter $s$, **not** time.
- C-space obstacles $\mathcal{C}_{obs}$ are the **pre-images** of workspace obstacles under forward kinematics $FK(q)$.
- For KTU derivations, always begin by writing the assumed polynomial form, differentiating it, then applying boundary conditions systematically.
- Industrial practice: pick-and-place typically uses **cubic** with $v_0 = v_f = 0$; surgical/precision robotics typically use **quintic** for jerk-limited smooth motion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joint Space versus Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joint Space versus Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Trajectory Planning** in robotics is the temporal and spatial specification of a robot's motion, defining *where* the end-effector (or joints) should be at *every instant of time* during the execution of a task. The two dominant paradigms in KTU 2024 robotics syllabus are:

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Joint Space Trajectory Planning**: A method where the path of the manipulator is specified and controlled entirely in the **configuration space** (i.e., the joint variable space, $q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n$). Each joint is driven from its initial angle to a final angle as a function of time, using smooth polynomial functions. The resulting end-effector path in Cartesian space is an emergent property and is **not explicitly controlled**.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning**: A method where the path of the end-effector is explicitly specified as a function of time in the **task space** (i.e., Cartesian coordinates $X, Y, Z$ and orientation Euler angles / Roll-Pitch-Yaw). The joint angles at each time step are then computed by **inverse kinematics** at a high sampling rate.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The GPS vs. The Steering Wheel

Imagine you are a passenger traveling from your home (Point A) to your office (Point B):

- **Joint Space Planning** is like telling the **engine** of the car: "Start the engine at 1000 RPM, smoothly increase to 3000 RPM over 10 minutes, then smoothly decrease back to 1000 RPM when you arrive." You don't care about the exact road taken, only the engine's internal state profile. The car might take a winding road, a highway, or a back-alley — the path is unpredictable in Cartesian terms, but the actuator (joint) motion is smooth.

- **Cartesian Space Planning** is like setting a **GPS route** that explicitly forces the car to follow specific streets (e.g., "Take MG Road for 200 m, turn left onto Highway 47, drive 3 km"). The car must compute its steering angles (joint angles) continuously to stay on the planned Cartesian path. This requires constant **inverse kinematic recalculations** at every GPS refresh interval.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam**: In *joint space*, smoothness is **in the actuator commands** (easy to control). In *Cartesian space*, smoothness is **in the visible world path** (intuitive for humans), but the joint commands may be jerky.

### 1.3 The Two Reference Frames

| Property | Joint Space ($\mathcal{C}$-space) | Cartesian Space ($\mathcal{W}$-space) |
|----------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Variable | $\theta_i(t)$ or $q_i(t)$ | $x(t), y(t), z(t), \phi(t), \theta(t), \psi(t)$ |
| Also Called | Configuration Space | Task Space / Operational Space |
| Kinematics Used | Direct Kinematics $T(q)$ | Inverse Kinematics $q = f^{-1}(X)$ |
| Dimensionality | $n$ (number of joints) | $6$ (3 position + 3 orientation) |
| Singularities | Immune at planning stage | May pass through them |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Joint Space Trajectory Planning — Step-by-Step Logic

The motion is decomposed into **independent scalar problems**, one per joint. The most common KTU-exam-relevant schemes are:

1. **Cubic Polynomial Trajectory** — For point-to-point motion with zero start and end velocities.
2. **Quintic Polynomial Trajectory** — Adds zero start and end accelerations for smoother motion.
3. **Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSPB)** — Constant velocity core with smooth acceleration/deceleration ramps.
4. **Spline Trajectories** (Cubic B-splines) — For multi-point waypoint tracking.

**Why use Joint Space?**
- The **actuator commands** $q_i(t)$ are bounded by physical limits, making trajectory feasibility easy to verify.
- **No inverse kinematics** is required at runtime → extremely fast, no singularity issues.
- Joint dynamics (inertia, coriolis terms) vary continuously because $\theta_i(t)$ is smooth, simplifying **feedforward torque computation**.

**Limitations of Joint Space Planning**
- The end-effector follows an **uncontrolled, possibly weird** path. It might collide with obstacles.
- The straight-line motion desired by the user is **not guaranteed**.

### 2.2 Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning — Step-by-Step Logic

The end-effector pose $X(t)$ is generated as a function of time in a **straight line** (or any user-defined geometric curve). Common techniques include:

1. **Straight-Line (Geometric) Path** — Linear interpolation in position + linear/SLERP interpolation in orientation.
2. **Circular / Arc Paths** — Used for arc-welding, painting, and cutting tasks.
3. **Polynomial Blends in Cartesian Space** — Trapezoidal or S-curve velocity profiles applied to the Cartesian path.

**Why use Cartesian Space?**
- The **visible path is intuitive** and matches human expectations (e.g., a straight line for pick-and-place).
- Essential for **obstacle avoidance** and **path-constrained tasks** (e.g., a welding torch must follow the seam exactly).

**Limitations of Cartesian Space Planning**
- Requires **inverse kinematics** at every sampling instant (computationally expensive).
- If the path passes through or near a **kinematic singularity**, joint velocities may blow up to infinity.
- Near workspace boundaries, the path may become **unreachable** mid-trajectory.

### 2.3 The Core Trade-Off (KTU High-Yield)

> [!NOTE]  
> **Joint Space = Smooth Actuator, Wild End-Effector**  
> **Cartesian Space = Smooth End-Effector, Wild Actuator**

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | **Cubic polynomial** trajectory for a single joint | $\theta(0)=\theta_0,\; \theta(T)=\theta_f,\; \dot{\theta}(0)=0,\; \dot{\theta}(T)=0$ |
| 2 | $a_0 = \theta_0$ | Coefficient 1 (initial position) | — |
| 3 | $a_1 = 0$ | Coefficient 2 (zero initial velocity) | — |
| 4 | $a_2 = \dfrac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^2}$ | Coefficient 3 (acceleration) | — |
| 5 | $a_3 = \dfrac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^3}$ | Coefficient 4 (jerk) | — |
| 6 | $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | **Quintic polynomial** trajectory (zero start/end accel) | Adds $\ddot{\theta}(0)=0,\; \ddot{\theta}(T)=0$ |
| 7 | $X(t) = X_0 + \lambda(t)(X_f - X_0)$ | **Linear Cartesian interpolation** for position | $\lambda(0)=0,\; \lambda(T)=1$ |
| 8 | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{t}{T}$ (trapezoidal) or $\lambda(t) = 10(\tfrac{t}{T})^3 - 15(\tfrac{t}{T})^4 + 6(\tfrac{t}{T})^5$ (S-curve) | Path parameterization functions | $0 \le \lambda \le 1$ |
| 9 | $q(t) = f^{-1}(X(t))$ | **Inverse kinematics** mapping (computed at every sample) | Valid only outside singularities |
| 10 | $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q) \cdot \dot{X}$ | **Resolved-rate** Jacobian relation | $\det(J) \ne 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Units Reminder**: All joint angles are in **radians (rad)**, all Cartesian positions in **meters (m)**, time $T$ in **seconds (s)**, angular velocity in **rad/s**, linear velocity in **m/s**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Joint Space** dominates in **high-speed pick-and-place**, **SCARA arms**, and **CNC machines** where actuator wear and torque limits dominate.
- **Cartesian Space** dominates in **welding robots (seam tracking)**, **laser cutting**, **painting**, and **surgical robots** where the tool's world-frame path is mission-critical.
- Modern industrial controllers (e.g., **ABB, KUKA, Fanuc**) offer **both** as selectable modes, and the choice is application-driven.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cubic Polynomial Joint Trajectory (Point-to-Point)

**Problem Setup**: A single joint must move from $\theta_0$ at $t=0$ to $\theta_f$ at $t=T_f$. The joint must start and finish at rest (zero velocity). Find the cubic polynomial $\theta(t)$.

**Step 1 — State the assumed form of the trajectory**

We assume a general cubic polynomial in time:
$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Initial Position Constraint**

At $t=0$, the joint must be at $\theta_0$:
$$
\theta(0) = a_0 + a_1(0) + a_2(0)^2 + a_3(0)^3 = a_0 = \theta_0
$$
Therefore $a_0 = \theta_0$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Final Position Constraint**

At $t=T_f$, the joint must be at $\theta_f$:
$$
\theta(T_f) = \theta_0 + a_1 T_f + a_2 T_f^2 + a_3 T_f^3 = \theta_f
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate to get the velocity equation**

$$
\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Initial Velocity Constraint**

At $t=0$, velocity is zero:
$$
\dot{\theta}(0) = a_1 + 0 + 0 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_1 = 0
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Final Velocity Constraint**

At $t=T_f$, velocity is zero:
$$
\dot{\theta}(T_f) = 0 + 2 a_2 T_f + 3 a_3 T_f^2 = 0
$$
$$
2 a_2 T_f = -3 a_3 T_f^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad a_2 = -\dfrac{3}{2} a_3 T_f
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $a_3$ using the final position constraint**

Substituting $a_1=0$ and $a_2 = -\dfrac{3}{2} a_3 T_f$ into Step 3:
$$
\theta_0 + 0 + \left(-\dfrac{3}{2} a_3 T_f\right) T_f^2 + a_3 T_f^3 = \theta_f
$$
$$
\theta_0 - \dfrac{3}{2} a_3 T_f^3 + a_3 T_f^3 = \theta_f
$$
$$
\theta_0 - \dfrac{1}{2} a_3 T_f^3 = \theta_f
$$
$$
a_3 = \dfrac{2(\theta_0 - \theta_f)}{T_f^3} = -\dfrac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T_f^3}
$$

**Step 8 — Back-substitute to find $a_2$**

$$
a_2 = -\dfrac{3}{2} \cdot \left(-\dfrac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T_f^3}\right) \cdot T_f = \dfrac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T_f^2}
$$

**Step 9 — Final Closed-Form Cubic Trajectory**

$$
\theta(t) = \theta_0 + \dfrac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T_f^2} t^2 - \dfrac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T_f^3} t^3
$$

**Step 10 — Compute the mid-point ($t = T_f/2$) for verification**

$$
\theta\left(\dfrac{T_f}{2}\right) = \theta_0 + \dfrac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T_f^2} \cdot \dfrac{T_f^2}{4} - \dfrac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T_f^3} \cdot \dfrac{T_f^3}{8}
$$
$$
= \theta_0 + \dfrac{3}{4}(\theta_f - \theta_0) - \dfrac{1}{4}(\theta_f - \theta_0)
$$
$$
= \theta_0 + \dfrac{1}{2}(\theta_f - \theta_0) = \dfrac{\theta_0 + \theta_f}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]  
> The cubic trajectory **does pass through the midpoint**, but with a non-zero acceleration there. This is a key difference from the quintic polynomial, which would have zero acceleration at both ends AND at the midpoint.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cartesian Linear Interpolation with Path Parameter $\lambda(t)$

**Problem Setup**: The end-effector must travel in a **straight line** in Cartesian space from $X_0$ to $X_f$ in time $T$.

**Step 1 — Define the position path**

For any point on the line, the position is a convex combination of start and end:
$$
X(t) = X_0 + \lambda(t) \cdot (X_f - X_0)
$$
where $\lambda$ is the dimensionless path parameter, $0 \le \lambda \le 1$.

**Step 2 — Choose a path parameterization (Linear)**

$$
\lambda(t) = \dfrac{t}{T}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Cartesian velocity**

$$
\dot{X}(t) = (X_f - X_0) \cdot \dot{\lambda}(t) = \dfrac{X_f - X_0}{T}
$$

This is a **constant** magnitude velocity equal to the straight-line distance divided by time. The total path length is $\Vert X_f - X_0 \Vert$.

**Step 4 — Compute the required joint velocities via the Jacobian**

At each sampling instant $t_k$, the joint velocities needed to maintain the Cartesian velocity are:
$$
\dot{q}(t_k) = J^{-1}(q(t_k)) \cdot \dot{X}(t_k)
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate to get joint positions**

$$
q(t_{k+1}) = q(t_k) + \dot{q}(t_k) \cdot \Delta t
$$

This is the **resolved-rate control** formulation, the most common industrial implementation of Cartesian planning.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Comparison Axis | Joint Space | Cartesian Space |
|-----------------|-------------|------------------|
| Path Predictability | Unpredictable in world frame | **Fully predictable** (straight line by default) |
| Computational Cost | **Low** (closed-form polynomial) | **High** (IK + Jacobian at each sample) |
| Singularity Handling | **No issue** at planning stage | **Critical** — $J^{-1}$ blows up |
| Obstacle Avoidance | Hard to enforce | **Natural** (path is defined explicitly) |
| Actuator Smoothness | **Excellent** (polynomial is smooth) | May have **jerks** from Jacobian variation |
| Orientation Control | Implicit, no control | **Explicit** SLERP/Quaternion interpolation |
| Real-Time Feasibility | **Excellent** (off-line computation) | Requires fast processor for on-line IK |
| Workspace Violations | Possible if path isn't checked | **Cannot violate** if $X(t) \in W$ is checked |
| Industrial Use Case | Palletizing, CNC | Welding, painting, surgery |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level Functional Architecture of a Trajectory Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Specification: Start Pose and Goal Pose] --> B{Choose Planning Space}
    B -->|Joint Space| C[Define Joint Waypoints q0 and qf]
    B -->|Cartesian Space| D[Define Cartesian Waypoints X0 and Xf]
    C --> E[Select Polynomial: Cubic or Quintic]
    D --> F[Define Path Parameter lambda t]
    E --> G[Compute Coefficients a0 to a3 or a0 to a5]
    F --> H[Compute X of t and dX by dt]
    G --> I[Generate Joint Trajectory q of t]
    H --> J[Apply Inverse Kinematics q of t = IK of X of t]
    J --> K[Apply Jacobian Transpose J inverse times dX by dt]
    I --> L[Send Smooth Joint Commands to Servo Drivers]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Robot Actuators Execute Motion]
    M --> N[End Effector Reaches Goal]
    N --> O[Task Complete]
```

### 4.2 Side-by-Side Conceptual Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph JS[Joint Space Planning]
        JS1[Start Joint Angles q0] --> JS2[Polynomial in Joint Space q of t]
        JS2 --> JS3[Servo Drive Direct Command]
        JS3 --> JS4[End Effector Path Emergent]
    end
    subgraph CS[Cartesian Space Planning]
        CS1[Start Pose X0] --> CS2[Path X of t in World Frame]
        CS2 --> CS3[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
        CS3 --> CS4[Joint Commands q of t]
        CS4 --> CS5[End Effector Follows Planned Path]
    end
```

### 4.3 Trajectory Planning Pipeline (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[User Input: Pose Goal and Time T] --> P2[Path Generation Module]
    P2 --> P3[Trajectory Generation Module: Cubic or Quintic or Trapezoidal]
    P3 --> P4[Real Time Interpolator: Sample every 1 to 10 ms]
    P4 --> P5[Inverse Kinematics Solver if Cartesian]
    P4 --> P6[Direct Output if Joint]
    P5 --> P7[Joint Setpoint Stream]
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Servo Loop and Motion Completed]
```

### 4.4 Decision Flowchart — When to Use Which

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the End Effector Path Geometry Critical?}
    Q1 -->|Yes: welding, cutting, painting| R1[Use Cartesian Space Planning]
    Q1 -->|No: pick and place, sorting| Q2{Is Smooth Actuator Torque Critical?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| R2[Use Joint Space Planning]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Does Path Pass Near Singularities?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| R2
    Q3 -->|No| R1
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Differentiate between Joint Space and Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Joint Space | Cartesian Space |
|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| Planning Variable | Joint angles $q_i(t)$ | End-effector pose $X(t)$ |
| Path Geometry | Uncontrolled, emergent | Explicitly defined (e.g., straight line) |
| Inverse Kinematics | Not required | Required at every sample |
| Singularities | No effect at planning | Critical — Jacobian may become singular |
| Computational Cost | Low (closed-form) | High (iterative IK) |
| Best For | Pick-and-place, palletizing | Welding, painting, surgery |

---

**Q2. State the four boundary conditions used to derive a cubic polynomial joint trajectory. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

For a joint moving from $\theta_0$ to $\theta_f$ in time $T$ with start and end at rest, the four constraints are:

1. Initial position: $\theta(0) = \theta_0$
2. Final position: $\theta(T) = \theta_f$
3. Initial velocity: $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$
4. Final velocity: $\dot{\theta}(T) = 0$

These four conditions yield a system of four linear equations in four unknowns $(a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3)$, giving the unique cubic trajectory.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Past Paper Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the coefficients of a **cubic polynomial trajectory** for a single robot joint moving from initial angle $\theta_0 = 20^\circ$ to final angle $\theta_f = 80^\circ$ in time $T = 4$ seconds, with zero initial and final velocities. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast **Joint Space** and **Cartesian Space** trajectory planning under the following heads: (i) path predictability, (ii) computational complexity, (iii) singularity handling, and (iv) typical applications. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Given: $\theta_0 = 20^\circ$, $\theta_f = 80^\circ$, $T = 4$ s. The cubic polynomial form is:
$$
\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3
$$

The four constraints yield (as derived in Section 3.1):
- $a_0 = \theta_0 = 20^\circ$
- $a_1 = 0$
- $a_2 = \dfrac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^2} = \dfrac{3(80 - 20)}{16} = \dfrac{180}{16} = 11.25^\circ/\text{s}^2$
- $a_3 = -\dfrac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^3} = -\dfrac{2(60)}{64} = -1.875^\circ/\text{s}^3$

The final trajectory is:
$$
\theta(t) = 20 + 11.25 t^2 - 1.875 t^3
$$

Verification at $t=4$ s: $\theta(4) = 20 + 11.25(16) - 1.875(64) = 20 + 180 - 120 = 80^\circ$ ✓

**Model Solution for (b):**

(i) **Path Predictability**: In joint space, the end-effector path is **uncontrolled** and may be a complex curve in world coordinates. In Cartesian space, the end-effector follows the **explicitly defined** path (usually a straight line), making it fully predictable.

(ii) **Computational Complexity**: Joint space uses **closed-form polynomial** evaluation — extremely fast. Cartesian space requires **inverse kinematics at every sample** (typically 1–10 ms apart), which is computationally heavy and may need iterative solvers.

(iii) **Singularity Handling**: Joint space is **immune to singularities** at the planning stage. Cartesian space may pass through configurations where the Jacobian $J$ becomes singular, causing **infinite joint velocities** in the resolved-rate equation $\dot{q} = J^{-1} \dot{X}$.

(iv) **Typical Applications**: Joint space is used in **pick-and-place, palletizing, and CNC** machines. Cartesian space is used in **arc welding, laser cutting, painting, and surgical robotics** where the visible path geometry is mission-critical.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Explain the **Cartesian Space Trajectory Planning** method using the **resolved-rate** approach. Derive the relation between joint velocities and Cartesian velocities via the Jacobian matrix. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF planar robot with link lengths $L_1 = L_2 = 1$ m must move its end-effector in a **straight line in Cartesian space** from point $P_0 = (1.5, 0.5)$ m to point $P_f = (0.5, 1.5)$ m in 2 seconds, with a trapezoidal velocity profile. Compute the required joint angles at $t = 0$, $t = 1$ s, and $t = 2$ s. The inverse kinematics solution is given by:
$$
\theta_2 = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{x^2 + y^2 - L_1^2 - L_2^2}{2 L_1 L_2}\right), \quad \theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{y}{x}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{L_2 \sin\theta_2}{L_1 + L_2 \cos\theta_2}\right)
$$
**(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

In Cartesian space trajectory planning, the end-effector path $X(t)$ is specified as a function of time. To control the robot, the joint velocities $\dot{q}(t)$ must be computed continuously.

Differentiating the forward kinematics equation $X = f(q)$ with respect to time:
$$
\dot{X} = \dfrac{\partial f(q)}{\partial q} \dot{q} = J(q) \dot{q}
$$
where $J(q)$ is the $6 \times n$ Jacobian matrix. For a non-redundant robot ($n = 6$) in a non-singular configuration ($\det J \ne 0$), the joint velocity is:
$$
\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q) \cdot \dot{X}
$$

This is the **resolved-rate control** law. At each sampling instant $t_k$:
1. Read the desired Cartesian velocity $\dot{X}(t_k) = (X_f - X_0) / T$ (for linear motion).
2. Compute $J^{-1}(q(t_k))$ using the current joint angles.
3. Compute $\dot{q}(t_k) = J^{-1} \cdot \dot{X}$.
4. Integrate: $q(t_{k+1}) = q(t_k) + \dot{q}(t_k) \cdot \Delta t$.
5. Send $q(t_{k+1})$ to the joint servo drivers.

**Model Solution for (b):**

The straight-line interpolation in Cartesian space gives the intermediate point at $t = 1$ s (midpoint, since the profile is symmetric trapezoidal):
$$
P_{mid} = \dfrac{P_0 + P_f}{2} = \left(\dfrac{1.5 + 0.5}{2}, \dfrac{0.5 + 1.5}{2}\right) = (1.0, 1.0) \text{ m}
$$

**At $t = 0$ s:** $P_0 = (1.5, 0.5)$, $r^2 = 1.5^2 + 0.5^2 = 2.5$
$$
\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{2.5 - 1 - 1}{2(1)(1)} = 0.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 75.52^\circ
$$
$$
\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.5}{1.5}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sin 75.52^\circ}{1 + \cos 75.52^\circ}\right) = 18.43^\circ - 41.41^\circ = -22.98^\circ
$$

**At $t = 1$ s:** $P_{mid} = (1.0, 1.0)$, $r^2 = 2.0$
$$
\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{2.0 - 1 - 1}{2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 90^\circ
$$
$$
\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}(1) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{1 + 0}\right) = 45^\circ - 45^\circ = 0^\circ
$$

**At $t = 2$ s:** $P_f = (0.5, 1.5)$, $r^2 = 2.5$
$$
\cos\theta_2 = \dfrac{2.5 - 1 - 1}{2} = 0.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_2 = 75.52^\circ
$$
$$
\theta_1 = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{1.5}{0.5}\right) - \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\sin 75.52^\circ}{1 + \cos 75.52^\circ}\right) = 71.57^\circ - 41.41^\circ = 30.16^\circ
$$

| Time (s) | $x$ (m) | $y$ (m) | $\theta_1$ (deg) | $\theta_2$ (deg) |
|----------|---------|---------|------------------|------------------|
| 0 | 1.5 | 0.5 | $-22.98$ | $75.52$ |
| 1 | 1.0 | 1.0 | $0.00$ | $90.00$ |
| 2 | 0.5 | 1.5 | $30.16$ | $75.52$ |

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing the units**: A common mistake is to give the cubic polynomial coefficients in degrees without converting to radians in derivations. Always **declare your units explicitly** and stay consistent.
> 2. **Forgetting boundary conditions**: The cubic trajectory has **exactly 4 boundary conditions** (2 positions + 2 velocities). The quintic has **6** (adds 2 accelerations). Mixing them up leads to incorrect coefficient formulas.
> 3. **Jacobian singular inversion**: In Cartesian planning, students often write $J^{-1}$ without checking $\det(J) \ne 0$. If the trajectory passes near a singularity, the joint velocities **diverge** — the examiner will deduct marks if this is not discussed.
> 4. **SLERP vs. LERP for orientation**: Linear interpolation of Euler angles does **not** give a linear rotation in 3D. Use **SLERP (Spherical Linear Interpolation)** for quaternions or **axis-angle** representation.
> 5. **Path vs. Trajectory confusion**: A **path** is a geometric curve in space. A **trajectory** is a path with a time law. Examiners will deduct marks if you interchange these terms.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Trajectory Planning** = specifying position as a function of time.
- ✅ **Joint Space** = smooth actuator commands; path is implicit and uncontrolled.
- ✅ **Cartesian Space** = smooth end-effector path; requires inverse kinematics and Jacobian.
- ✅ **Cubic polynomial** uses 4 boundary conditions; coefficients are $a_0=\theta_0$, $a_1=0$, $a_2=\frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^2}$, $a_3=-\frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{T^3}$.
- ✅ **Quintic polynomial** adds 2 acceleration constraints for smoother motion at the cost of higher-order derivatives.
- ✅ **Cartesian linear interpolation** uses $X(t) = X_0 + \lambda(t)(X_f - X_0)$ with $\lambda(t) = t/T$ for linear, or $10\tau^3 - 15\tau^4 + 6\tau^5$ for S-curve smoothness.
- ✅ **Resolved-rate** Cartesian control: $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q) \cdot \dot{X}$ — only valid when $\det(J) \ne 0$.
- ✅ **Trade-off**: Joint space = actuator-friendly, Cartesian space = world-friendly.
- ✅ **Industrial choice**: Joint space for pick-and-place / CNC, Cartesian space for welding / painting / surgery.
- ✅ **Singularities** are the Achilles' heel of Cartesian planning — always check the Jacobian determinant.
- ✅ **Orientation** in Cartesian space requires SLERP (quaternion) or axis-angle; LERP of Euler angles is **incorrect**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cubic Polynomial Based Trajectory Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cubic Polynomial Based Trajectory Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Trajectory Planning** in robotics is the generation of time-based reference inputs (position, velocity, and acceleration profiles) for the actuators of a robot manipulator, ensuring that the end-effector or joints move from an initial configuration to a final configuration in a **smooth, continuous, and predictable** manner, while respecting kinematic constraints.

**Cubic Polynomial Based Trajectory Planning** is the technique of representing the motion of each robot joint (in joint-space) as a **third-order polynomial function of time**. The cubic (degree-3) polynomial is the lowest-order polynomial that can independently specify the joint **position at the start and end** of the motion segment, as well as the joint **velocity at both endpoints**, producing a trajectory with continuous position and velocity, but only piecewise-continuous (linear) acceleration.

The general form of a cubic trajectory segment is:

$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

A cubic polynomial possesses **four (4) unknown coefficients** ($a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$), and therefore requires **four (4) independent boundary conditions** to be uniquely solved.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** A cubic polynomial trajectory is the *minimum-order* polynomial that guarantees continuity of **position** AND **velocity** at the trajectory endpoints. It is the workhorse of single-segment point-to-point (PTP) motion in industrial robots.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Drive" Intuition

Imagine a car on a straight highway that must travel from **City A** to **City B**, a distance of 100 km, in exactly **2 hours**.

- The **cubic polynomial** is like designing a *smooth speed profile* for the car.
- The car must **start at rest** at City A (initial velocity = 0) and **end at rest** at City B (final velocity = 0) — these are the velocity boundary conditions.
- It must cover exactly the **distance from A to B** in the **allotted time** — these are the position boundary conditions.
- The cubic curve smoothly accelerates the car, reaches a peak cruising speed, and then smoothly decelerates — just like a robot joint smoothly starts, moves, and stops.

A **linear** ramp would jerk the car instantly (unrealistic for passengers or mechanisms). A **cubic** curve is the simplest mathematical function that gives the car a *graceful* take-off and a *graceful* stop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as you cannot specify *both* the start/end positions *and* start/end velocities with a lower-order polynomial (e.g., a quadratic has only 3 coefficients), the **cubic** is the *minimum mathematical complexity* that satisfies the four real-world requirements of a smooth start-and-stop motion.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Notations

The following standard notation is adopted throughout KTU 2024 assessments:

- $t_0$ = initial time of the trajectory segment (typically set to **0 s** for simplicity)
- $t_f$ = final time of the trajectory segment (the motion duration, measured in **seconds**)
- $\theta_0$ = initial joint position (in **radians** or **degrees**)
- $\theta_f$ = final joint position (in **radians** or **degrees**)
- $\dot{\theta}_0$ = initial joint velocity (in **rad/s**)
- $\dot{\theta}_f$ = final joint velocity (in **rad/s**)
- $\ddot{\theta}(t)$ = joint acceleration, obtained by second differentiation of $\theta(t)$ (in **rad/s²**)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Cubic Polynomial — Structure and Coefficients

A single cubic segment of a joint trajectory is mathematically expressed as:

$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

The **first and second time-derivatives** of this polynomial — the velocity and acceleration profiles — are obtained analytically by differentiation:

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

$$\ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the **acceleration is a linear function of time** for a cubic trajectory. This means acceleration changes abruptly at the segment boundaries, producing a *jerk discontinuity*. For jerk-continuous trajectories, a **quintic (5th-order) polynomial** is used — but that is outside the scope of cubic planning.

## 2.2 The Four Boundary Conditions (Set-Up Logic)

The four boundary conditions that determine the four coefficients are:

1. **Position at start:** $\theta(t_0) = \theta_0$ (the joint starts at the known initial angle)
2. **Position at end:** $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$ (the joint must arrive at the known final angle)
3. **Velocity at start:** $\dot{\theta}(t_0) = \dot{\theta}_0$ (usually **0** if the robot is starting from rest)
4. **Velocity at end:** $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = \dot{\theta}_f$ (usually **0** if the robot must come to rest at the goal)

## 2.3 The General Solution for the Coefficients (Most General Form)

By substituting the four boundary conditions into the polynomial and its derivative, and simplifying, the **closed-form general solution** for the four coefficients is:

$$a_0 = \theta_0$$

$$a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3}{t_f^2}(\theta_f - \theta_0) - \frac{2}{t_f}\dot{\theta}_0 - \frac{1}{t_f}\dot{\theta}_f$$

$$a_3 = -\frac{2}{t_f^3}(\theta_f - \theta_0) + \frac{1}{t_f^2}(\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f)$$

## 2.4 The Standard Special Case — Rest-to-Rest Motion

In the overwhelming majority of KTU examination problems, the robot joint starts and ends at **rest** ($\dot{\theta}_0 = 0$ and $\dot{\theta}_f = 0$). In this canonical case, the formulas simplify dramatically:

$$a_0 = \theta_0$$

$$a_1 = 0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2}$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Anchor (KTU Board Exam Priority):** The four coefficients of a *rest-to-rest* cubic trajectory are a very frequent Part-A or sub-part question. Memorize $a_0 = \theta_0$, $a_1 = 0$, and the expressions for $a_2$ and $a_3$ above.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Cubic Position | $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | rad (or deg) |
| 2 | Cubic Velocity | $\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Cubic Acceleration | $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$ | rad/s² (linear in t) |
| 4 | $a_0$ coefficient | $a_0 = \theta_0$ | Initial position |
| 5 | $a_1$ coefficient | $a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$ | Initial velocity |
| 6 | $a_2$ coefficient (general) | $\frac{3}{t_f^2}(\theta_f - \theta_0) - \frac{2 \dot{\theta}_0}{t_f} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_f}{t_f}$ | See derivation |
| 7 | $a_3$ coefficient (general) | $-\frac{2}{t_f^3}(\theta_f - \theta_0) + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{t_f^2}$ | See derivation |
| 8 | $a_2$ coefficient (rest-to-rest) | $\frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2}$ | When $\dot{\theta}_0 = \dot{\theta}_f = 0$ |
| 9 | $a_3$ coefficient (rest-to-rest) | $\frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3}$ | When $\dot{\theta}_0 = \dot{\theta}_f = 0$ |
| 10 | Time of peak velocity | $t_{peak} = -\frac{a_2}{3 a_3}$ | Found by setting $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0$ |
| 11 | Peak velocity magnitude | $\dot{\theta}_{max} = a_1 - \frac{a_2^2}{3 a_3}$ | Substitute $t_{peak}$ into $\dot{\theta}(t)$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cubic polynomial trajectory planning is the **industrial standard** for:

- **CNC machine tools** that move a tool from one coordinate to another with smooth start/stop.
- **SCARA and 6-DOF articulated robots** performing **pick-and-place** operations on assembly lines.
- **Pick-and-place packaging robots** in food, pharmaceutical, and electronics manufacturing.
- **3D printers and additive manufacturing** machines that move the print head smoothly between waypoints.
- **Camera gimbals and stabilization platforms** that require gentle acceleration to avoid vibration.

The cubic profile's simplicity, low computational cost, and guaranteed smoothness at endpoints make it ideal for **real-time embedded controllers** in industrial automation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the General Cubic Coefficients

We are given a cubic polynomial:

$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

Its first derivative (velocity) is:

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

We are given the four boundary conditions. To make the derivation clean, we shift the time origin by setting $t_0 = 0$, so the trajectory is defined for $t \in [0, t_f]$. The four conditions become:

$$\theta(0) = \theta_0 \quad (1)$$

$$\theta(t_f) = \theta_f \quad (2)$$

$$\dot{\theta}(0) = \dot{\theta}_0 \quad (3)$$

$$\dot{\theta}(t_f) = \dot{\theta}_f \quad (4)$$

### Step 1 — Apply Condition (1) at $t = 0$:

Substitute $t = 0$ into the position equation:

$$\theta(0) = a_0 + a_1 (0) + a_2 (0)^2 + a_3 (0)^3 = a_0$$

Equating to $\theta_0$:

$$\boxed{a_0 = \theta_0}$$

This is the **initial position** of the joint.

### Step 2 — Apply Condition (3) at $t = 0$:

Substitute $t = 0$ into the velocity equation:

$$\dot{\theta}(0) = a_1 + 2 a_2 (0) + 3 a_3 (0)^2 = a_1$$

Equating to $\dot{\theta}_0$:

$$\boxed{a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0}$$

This is the **initial velocity** of the joint.

### Step 3 — Apply Condition (2) at $t = t_f$:

Substitute $t = t_f$ into the position equation:

$$\theta(t_f) = a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f$$

Substituting the known values of $a_0$ and $a_1$:

$$\theta_0 + \dot{\theta}_0 \, t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f$$

Rearranging to isolate $a_2$ and $a_3$:

$$a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f - \theta_0 - \dot{\theta}_0 \, t_f \quad (5)$$

### Step 4 — Apply Condition (4) at $t = t_f$:

Substitute $t = t_f$ into the velocity equation:

$$\dot{\theta}(t_f) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{\theta}_f$$

Substituting the known value of $a_1$:

$$\dot{\theta}_0 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{\theta}_f$$

Rearranging:

$$2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0 \quad (6)$$

### Step 5 — Solve the 2×2 Linear System in $a_2$ and $a_3$:

Equation (5) divided by $t_f^2$:

$$a_2 + a_3 t_f = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0 - \dot{\theta}_0 \, t_f}{t_f^2} \quad (5')$$

Equation (6) divided by $t_f^2$:

$$\frac{2 a_2}{t_f} + 3 a_3 = \frac{\dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0}{t_f^2} \quad (6')$$

Multiplying equation (5') by $2/t_f$ to eliminate $a_2$:

$$\frac{2 a_2}{t_f} + 2 a_3 = \frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0 - \dot{\theta}_0 \, t_f)}{t_f^3} \quad (7)$$

Subtracting (6') from (7):

$$(2 a_3 - 3 a_3) = \frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0 - \dot{\theta}_0 \, t_f)}{t_f^3} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0}{t_f^2}$$

$$-a_3 = \frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} - \frac{2 \dot{\theta}_0}{t_f^2} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0}{t_f^2}$$

$$-a_3 = \frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} - \frac{2 \dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0}{t_f^2}$$

$$-a_3 = \frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{t_f^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{t_f^2}}$$

Substituting $a_3$ back into (5') and solving for $a_2$:

$$a_2 = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0 - \dot{\theta}_0 \, t_f}{t_f^2} - a_3 t_f$$

$$a_2 = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_f^2} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_0}{t_f} - \left[\frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{t_f^2}\right] t_f$$

$$a_2 = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_f^2} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_0}{t_f} + \frac{2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{t_f}$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2 \dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{t_f}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2 \dot{\theta}_0}{t_f} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_f}{t_f}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Rest-to-Rest Cubic Trajectory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Pattern:** A 7-mark sub-question typically provides $\theta_0$, $\theta_f$, and $t_f$, and asks for the cubic trajectory equation, peak velocity, and a tabulated sample of values.

**Problem Statement (Model):**
A single robot joint must move from an initial angle of $\theta_0 = 10°$ to a final angle of $\theta_f = 60°$ in a total time of $t_f = 4$ s. The joint starts and ends at rest. Determine:
- (a) The cubic trajectory equation $\theta(t)$
- (b) The peak velocity and the time at which it occurs
- (c) The acceleration at $t = 2$ s

**Given Data:**

$$\theta_0 = 10°, \quad \theta_f = 60°, \quad t_f = 4 \, \text{s}, \quad \dot{\theta}_0 = 0, \quad \dot{\theta}_f = 0$$

**Step 1 — Compute the four coefficients (Rest-to-Rest Case):**

$$a_0 = \theta_0 = 10$$

$$a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0 = 0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2} = \frac{3(60 - 10)}{4^2} = \frac{3 \times 50}{16} = \frac{150}{16} = 9.375$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} = \frac{-2(60 - 10)}{4^3} = \frac{-100}{64} = -1.5625$$

**Step 2 — Write the cubic trajectory equation:**

$$\theta(t) = 10 + 0 \cdot t + 9.375 \, t^2 - 1.5625 \, t^3$$

$$\boxed{\theta(t) = 10 + 9.375 \, t^2 - 1.5625 \, t^3 \quad \text{(degrees)}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the velocity and acceleration profiles:**

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = 18.75 \, t - 4.6875 \, t^2 \quad \text{(deg/s)}$$

$$\ddot{\theta}(t) = 18.75 - 9.375 \, t \quad \text{(deg/s}^2\text{)}$$

**Step 4 — Find the time of peak velocity:**

Peak velocity occurs when the acceleration is zero:

$$\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0 \implies 18.75 - 9.375 \, t = 0$$

$$t_{peak} = \frac{18.75}{9.375} = 2.0 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the peak velocity magnitude:**

$$\dot{\theta}(2.0) = 18.75(2.0) - 4.6875(2.0)^2 = 37.5 - 18.75 = 18.75 \, \text{deg/s}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{\theta}_{max} = 18.75 \, \text{deg/s at } t = 2.0 \, \text{s}}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the acceleration at $t = 2$ s:**

$$\ddot{\theta}(2) = 18.75 - 9.375(2) = 18.75 - 18.75 = 0 \, \text{deg/s}^2$$

This confirms the result of Step 4 — the peak velocity occurs precisely at the instant when the acceleration crosses zero (sign change from positive to negative).

**Step 7 — Verification of boundary conditions:**

- At $t = 0$: $\theta(0) = 10 + 0 - 0 = 10°$ ✓
- At $t = 4$: $\theta(4) = 10 + 9.375(16) - 1.5625(64) = 10 + 150 - 100 = 60°$ ✓
- Velocity at $t = 0$: $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$ ✓
- Velocity at $t = 4$: $\dot{\theta}(4) = 18.75(4) - 4.6875(16) = 75 - 75 = 0$ ✓

All four boundary conditions are satisfied.

## 3.3 Cubic Trajectories With Non-Zero Boundary Velocities — Brief Extension

If the joint is *not* at rest (for example, when smoothly chaining two trajectory segments), the **general** coefficient formulas (Section 2.3) must be used. Let us illustrate with a quick numerical check.

**Modified Problem:** Suppose the same motion ($\theta_0 = 10°$, $\theta_f = 60°$, $t_f = 4$ s) is now required to have an initial velocity of $\dot{\theta}_0 = 5 \, \text{deg/s}$ and a final velocity of $\dot{\theta}_f = 8 \, \text{deg/s}$.

$$a_0 = 10$$

$$a_1 = 5$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(50)}{16} - \frac{2(5)}{4} - \frac{8}{4} = 9.375 - 2.5 - 2.0 = 4.875$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(50)}{64} + \frac{5 + 8}{16} = -1.5625 + 0.8125 = -0.75$$

The trajectory is $\theta(t) = 10 + 5 t + 4.875 t^2 - 0.75 t^3$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Cubic Trajectory Planning Process

The following block-level functional architecture flow depicts the **logical decision and computation flow** for generating a cubic polynomial trajectory. This is the algorithmic view of the procedure, mapping out the boundary-condition selection and the sequential computation of each coefficient.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([User Specification: Problem Statement]) --> specNode1[Identify Initial Position theta0]
    startA --> specNode2[Identify Final Position thetaf]
    startA --> specNode3[Identify Time Duration tf]
    startA --> specNode4[Identify Boundary Velocities dtheta0 and dthetaf]
    specNode1 --> assembleNode[Assemble Four Boundary Conditions]
    specNode2 --> assembleNode
    specNode3 --> assembleNode
    specNode4 --> assembleNode
    assembleNode --> coefA0[Compute a0 equals theta0]
    assembleNode --> coefA1[Compute a1 equals dtheta0]
    assembleNode --> coefA2[Compute a2 from 3 By tfSquared Formula]
    assembleNode --> coefA3[Compute a3 from minus 2 By tfCubed Formula]
    coefA0 --> polyNode[Form Cubic Polynomial theta of t]
    coefA1 --> polyNode
    coefA2 --> polyNode
    coefA3 --> polyNode
    polyNode --> derivV[First Derivative gives Velocity Profile]
    polyNode --> derivA[Second Derivative gives Acceleration Profile]
    derivV --> peakFind[Find Peak Velocity by Setting Acceleration to Zero]
    derivA --> peakFind
    peakFind --> verifyNode[Verify All Four Boundary Conditions]
    verifyNode --> outputNode([Output Final Trajectory Equation and Plots])
    verifyNode --> failCheck{Boundary Mismatch Detected}
    failCheck -->|Yes| alertNode[Alert: Recompute Coefficients]
    alertNode --> coefA2
    failCheck -->|No| outputNode
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Cubic Trajectory Generation in a Robot Controller

The following diagram shows how the cubic trajectory planner is typically embedded within the higher-level motion control architecture of a robot manipulator. The planner acts as a *reference generator* that feeds the low-level joint servo controllers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    userInput[Task Level Command: Move Joint from theta0 to thetaf] --> plannerBlock[Cubic Trajectory Planner]
    plannerBlock --> polyOut[Reference theta of t in Joint Space]
    polyOut --> servo1[Joint Servo Controller 1]
    polyOut --> servo2[Joint Servo Controller 2]
    polyOut --> servoN[Joint Servo Controller N]
    servo1 --> motor1[Actuator and Gearbox]
    servo2 --> motor2[Actuator and Gearbox]
    servoN --> motorN[Actuator and Gearbox]
    motor1 --> joint1[Robot Joint 1]
    motor2 --> joint2[Robot Joint 2]
    motorN --> jointN[Robot Joint N]
    joint1 --> feedback1[Encoder Feedback]
    joint2 --> feedback2[Encoder Feedback]
    jointN --> feedbackN[Encoder Feedback]
    feedback1 --> servo1
    feedback2 --> servo2
    feedbackN --> servoN
    plannerBlock --> boundaryCheck[Boundary Condition Validator: 4 Conditions]
    boundaryCheck --> report[Trajectory Quality Report: Smoothness and Overshoot Metrics]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Time-Domain Evolution of Position, Velocity, and Acceleration

The following diagram illustrates the *time-domain shape* of the cubic trajectory profiles. Each profile exhibits a distinct mathematical behavior that is crucial for understanding robot motion quality.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    tAxis[Time Axis: t equals 0 to tf] --> posCurve[Position theta of t: Cubic S Curve]
    tAxis --> velCurve[Velocity dtheta of t: Parabolic Inverted Profile]
    tAxis --> accCurve[Acceleration ddtheta of t: Linear Ramp with Sign Change]
    posCurve --> posNote[Starts Flat at theta0, Rises Smoothly, Ends Flat at thetaf]
    velCurve --> velNote[Starts at Zero, Peaks at t equals tf divided by 2, Returns to Zero]
    accCurve --> accNote[Starts Positive, Crosses Zero at Midpoint, Ends Negative: JERK DISCONTINUITY at Boundaries]
    posNote --> qualityNote[Position is Continuous and Smooth: C1 Continuity]
    velNote --> qualityNote
    accNote --> qualityNote
    qualityNote --> resultBlock[Result: Smooth Single Segment Point to Point Motion]
```
