# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question 1: Define trajectory planning in the context of a robot manipulator. Why is a cubic polynomial the minimum-degree polynomial needed for single-segment motion?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Trajectory Planning** is the process of generating a time-based reference path (specifying position, velocity, and acceleration as functions of time) for a robot's joints to move from an initial configuration to a final configuration in a smooth and predictable manner.

A cubic polynomial $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ has **four (4) unknown coefficients**. To uniquely determine them, we need **four independent boundary conditions**:

1. Initial position $\theta(0) = \theta_0$
2. Final position $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$
3. Initial velocity $\dot{\theta}(0) = \dot{\theta}_0$
4. Final velocity $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = \dot{\theta}_f$

A lower-order polynomial (constant = 0 equations, linear = 2 equations, quadratic = 3 equations) has **insufficient degrees of freedom** to satisfy all four constraints simultaneously. Therefore, the cubic is the **minimum-degree polynomial** that guarantees continuity of both position and velocity at the trajectory endpoints.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question 2: For a cubic polynomial trajectory segment with $\theta_0 = 20°$, $\theta_f = 80°$, $t_f = 5$ s, and rest-to-rest motion, write down the explicit expressions for the four coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$ with numerical values.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

For rest-to-rest motion ($\dot{\theta}_0 = \dot{\theta}_f = 0$), the coefficients are:

$$a_0 = \theta_0 = 20$$

$$a_1 = 0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2} = \frac{3(80 - 20)}{5^2} = \frac{180}{25} = 7.2$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} = \frac{-2(80 - 20)}{5^3} = \frac{-120}{125} = -0.96$$

The cubic trajectory equation is:

$$\theta(t) = 20 + 7.2 \, t^2 - 0.96 \, t^3 \quad \text{degrees}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]**

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**A robotic manipulator joint must move from an initial angle of $\theta_0 = 15°$ to a final angle of $\theta_f = 75°$ in a duration of $t_f = 3$ s, with the joint starting and ending at rest.**

**(a)** Derive the general formulas for the four coefficients of a cubic polynomial trajectory. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Using the given data, determine the cubic trajectory equation, find the time at which the peak velocity occurs, and compute the peak velocity magnitude. Also, calculate the acceleration at $t = 1$ s. (7 Marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

The cubic trajectory is:

$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

Velocity: $\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$

**Boundary Conditions (with $t_0 = 0$):**

- (1) $\theta(0) = \theta_0$ $\implies$ $a_0 = \theta_0$
- (3) $\dot{\theta}(0) = \dot{\theta}_0$ $\implies$ $a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0$
- (2) $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$ $\implies$ $a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f$
- (4) $\dot{\theta}(t_f) = \dot{\theta}_f$ $\implies$ $a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{\theta}_f$

**Substituting (1) and (3) into (2) and (4):**

$$a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f - \theta_0 - \dot{\theta}_0 t_f$$

$$2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0$$

**Solving this 2×2 linear system** (by eliminating $a_2$) yields the closed-form general solution:

$$a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2 \dot{\theta}_0}{t_f} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_f}{t_f}$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{t_f^2}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\theta_0 = 15°$, $\theta_f = 75°$, $t_f = 3$ s, $\dot{\theta}_0 = 0$, $\dot{\theta}_f = 0$.

**Coefficient Computation (Rest-to-Rest):**

$$a_0 = 15$$

$$a_1 = 0$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(75 - 15)}{3^2} = \frac{180}{9} = 20$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(75 - 15)}{3^3} = \frac{-120}{27} = -4.444$$

**Cubic Trajectory Equation:**

$$\theta(t) = 15 + 20 t^2 - 4.444 t^3 \quad \text{degrees}$$

**Velocity and Acceleration:**

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = 40 t - 13.333 t^2$$

$$\ddot{\theta}(t) = 40 - 26.667 t$$

**Time of Peak Velocity (where $\ddot{\theta} = 0$):**

$$40 - 26.667 \, t_{peak} = 0 \implies t_{peak} = \frac{40}{26.667} = 1.5 \, \text{s}$$

**Peak Velocity Magnitude:**

$$\dot{\theta}(1.5) = 40(1.5) - 13.333(1.5)^2 = 60 - 30 = 30 \, \text{deg/s}$$

**Acceleration at $t = 1$ s:**

$$\ddot{\theta}(1) = 40 - 26.667(1) = 13.333 \, \text{deg/s}^2$$

**Verification:** $\theta(3) = 15 + 20(9) - 4.444(27) = 15 + 180 - 120 = 75°$ ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (1-mark deduction):** Forgetting that $a_0 = \theta_0$ is *not* an assumption but a direct consequence of the boundary condition at $t=0$. Students sometimes write $a_0$ as a generic constant.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (2-mark deduction):** Sign error in $a_3$ — it is **negative** when the robot is moving in the *positive* direction. Always cross-verify with $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (1-mark deduction):** Writing peak velocity time as $t_f / 2$ without showing the calculation from $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0$. The result $t_f/2$ holds *only* for symmetric rest-to-rest motion; you must show the working.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (1-mark deduction):** Failing to include units (degrees vs radians) in the final answer. Always state the units explicitly.

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**A two-joint robot manipulator is being controlled. Joint 1 must execute a cubic polynomial trajectory from an initial position of $\theta_0 = 30°$ to a final position of $\theta_f = 90°$ in $t_f = 2$ s. The joint has an initial velocity of $\dot{\theta}_0 = 10°/\text{s}$ and a final velocity of $\dot{\theta}_f = 5°/\text{s}$ (i.e., it is not at rest).**

**(a)** Determine all four coefficients of the cubic trajectory using the general (non-zero-velocity) formulas. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write down the position, velocity, and acceleration equations. Verify that the boundary conditions are satisfied and compute the time at which the velocity reaches its maximum value. (7 Marks)

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $\theta_0 = 30°$, $\theta_f = 90°$, $t_f = 2$ s, $\dot{\theta}_0 = 10°/\text{s}$, $\dot{\theta}_f = 5°/\text{s}$.

**Coefficient Computation (General Case):**

$$a_0 = \theta_0 = 30$$

$$a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0 = 10$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2 \dot{\theta}_0}{t_f} - \frac{\dot{\theta}_f}{t_f}$$

$$a_2 = \frac{3(90 - 30)}{2^2} - \frac{2(10)}{2} - \frac{5}{2} = \frac{180}{4} - 10 - 2.5 = 45 - 12.5 = 32.5$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{t_f^2}$$

$$a_3 = \frac{-2(60)}{8} + \frac{10 + 5}{4} = -15 + 3.75 = -11.25$$

**Resulting Cubic Polynomial:**

$$\theta(t) = 30 + 10 t + 32.5 t^2 - 11.25 t^3 \quad \text{degrees}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Velocity Profile:**

$$\dot{\theta}(t) = 10 + 65 t - 33.75 t^2 \quad \text{deg/s}$$

**Acceleration Profile:**

$$\ddot{\theta}(t) = 65 - 67.5 t \quad \text{deg/s}^2$$

**Verification of Boundary Conditions:**

- Position at $t = 0$: $\theta(0) = 30 + 0 + 0 - 0 = 30°$ ✓
- Velocity at $t = 0$: $\dot{\theta}(0) = 10 + 0 - 0 = 10°/\text{s}$ ✓
- Position at $t = 2$: $\theta(2) = 30 + 10(2) + 32.5(4) - 11.25(8) = 30 + 20 + 130 - 90 = 90°$ ✓
- Velocity at $t = 2$: $\dot{\theta}(2) = 10 + 65(2) - 33.75(4) = 10 + 130 - 135 = 5°/\text{s}$ ✓

All four boundary conditions are satisfied.

**Time of Peak Velocity (where $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0$):**

$$65 - 67.5 \, t_{peak} = 0 \implies t_{peak} = \frac{65}{67.5} \approx 0.963 \, \text{s}$$

**Peak Velocity Magnitude:**

$$\dot{\theta}(0.963) = 10 + 65(0.963) - 33.75(0.963)^2 \approx 10 + 62.6 - 31.3 \approx 41.3 \, \text{deg/s}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Unlike the rest-to-rest case (where the peak velocity occurs at exactly $t = t_f/2$), the non-zero-velocity trajectory peaks *earlier* in the motion — at approximately 48% of the total time. This is because the joint is already moving at $10°/\text{s}$ at the start, so the additional acceleration contributes to a higher peak sooner.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout (For Question B):**
> - **Critical Error (2-mark deduction):** Applying the *rest-to-rest* simplified formulas when the boundary velocities are **non-zero**. The general formula must be used.
> - **Sign Convention (1-mark deduction):** The coefficients $a_2$ and $a_3$ are *positive* and *negative* respectively in this case, but signs flip if the joint moves from a higher to a lower angle. Always substitute carefully.
> - **Units Consistency (1-mark deduction):** If the problem mixes degrees and radians, convert at the *start* (1 rad = 57.2958°). Mixing units is a guaranteed mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Cubic Polynomial Trajectory Planning (KTU 2024)**

- **General form:** $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ — a **third-degree** polynomial in time.
- **Four unknown coefficients** require **four boundary conditions** for a unique solution.
- The **standard four boundary conditions** are: initial position $\theta_0$, final position $\theta_f$, initial velocity $\dot{\theta}_0$, and final velocity $\dot{\theta}_f$.
- For the **canonical rest-to-rest** case ($\dot{\theta}_0 = \dot{\theta}_f = 0$): $a_0 = \theta_0$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = 3(\theta_f - \theta_0)/t_f^2$, $a_3 = -2(\theta_f - \theta_0)/t_f^3$.
- **Velocity is a quadratic** in time (parabolic inverted-U shape for rest-to-rest).
- **Acceleration is a linear** function of time (ramps down from positive to negative for rest-to-rest).
- **Peak velocity** for rest-to-rest motion occurs at exactly $t = t_f/2$, found by setting $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 0$.
- **Peak velocity magnitude** for rest-to-rest motion is $\dot{\theta}_{max} = \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{2 t_f}$ (derived by substituting $t = t_f/2$ into the velocity expression).
- The cubic trajectory has **continuous position and velocity** but has a **jerk discontinuity** at the start and end of the segment (acceleration changes abruptly).
- For smoother jerk-continuous trajectories, a **quintic (5th-order) polynomial** is required (six coefficients, six boundary conditions including accelerations).
- For trajectories with **intermediate via points**, multiple cubic segments are stitched together with **continuity constraints on position and velocity** at the via points — this is the topic of multi-segment cubic spline planning.
- **Assumption used universally in KTU problems:** $t_0 = 0$. If the problem states $t_0 \neq 0$, perform a time shift $t' = t - t_0$ before applying the formulas.
- **Units:** Always work in **consistent units** — typically SI (radians, seconds). Convert degrees to radians ($1° = \pi/180$ rad) if the problem is in SI mode, or work in degrees if the problem is consistently in degrees.
- **Physical interpretation of $a_0$:** This is the joint's *initial angular position* at $t = 0$.
- **Physical interpretation of $a_1$:** This is the joint's *initial angular velocity* at $t = 0$.
- **Physical interpretation of $a_2$:** Related to the *initial acceleration* — specifically, $\ddot{\theta}(0) = 2 a_2$.
- **Physical interpretation of $a_3$:** Controls the *curvature* (rate of change of acceleration) of the trajectory.
- **Verification step:** Always plug $t = 0$ and $t = t_f$ back into your final trajectory equation to confirm $\theta(0) = \theta_0$ and $\theta(t_f) = \theta_f$. This is a 1-mark "free check" in KTU valuation.
- **Common industrial application:** Pick-and-place robots in manufacturing assembly lines, where smooth start/stop is critical to avoid product damage and mechanical wear.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Point to Point and Continuous Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Point to Point and Continuous Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Path Planning in Robotics** is the computational process of generating a feasible, collision-free, and time-optimized sequence of joint configurations (or end-effector poses) that drives a robotic manipulator from an initial state to a desired goal state while satisfying kinematic, dynamic, and task-specific constraints.

Within the umbrella of robotic path planning, the KTU 2024 Scheme categorizes manipulator motion into two principal operational paradigms:

#### A. Point-to-Point (PTP) Motion Planning

> [!NOTE]
> **Point-to-Point (PTP) Planning** is a trajectory generation strategy in which the robot is commanded to move from an initial point (start configuration) to a final point (goal configuration) **without any specification of the intermediate geometric path**. Only the start and end configurations are defined; the controller independently drives each joint axis to its target, often through the shortest joint-space displacement.

- The path taken by the end-effector between the two points is **unspecified and typically not straight**.
- Each joint axis (1 through *n*) executes its own independent motion profile.
- Motion is described purely in **joint space** ($\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n$).
- Total cycle time is determined by the **slowest joint** to reach its destination.

#### B. Continuous Path (CP) Motion Planning

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous Path (CP) Planning**, also called **Continuous Trajectory Planning** or **Path-Controlled Motion**, is a strategy in which the robot end-effector is required to follow a precisely defined geometric path (e.g., a straight line, circular arc, or spline) between the start and end points. The position, velocity, and often orientation of the end-effector are controlled as continuous functions of time along the entire trajectory.

- Motion is described in **Cartesian space** ($x, y, z, \phi, \theta, \psi$) and mapped back to joint space via **inverse kinematics**.
- Requires high-density interpolation (often 50–2000 Hz) between closely spaced points.
- Path accuracy is the primary design objective, not minimum-time.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy for Easy Recall:**

| Analogy | Maps To | Why It Works |
|---|---|---|
| 🚗 **Driving from Home to Office using a GPS** (GPS chooses any road, you don't care about exact streets, only that you arrive) | **PTP Planning** | Only the start and goal matter; the route is chosen by the planner. In a robot, the *controller* picks the simplest joint motion. |
| 🏎️ **Drifting along a perfectly drawn race track** — you MUST follow the painted line through every curve and chicane | **CP Planning** | The geometry of the path is a strict constraint; deviation is unacceptable. In a robot, the end-effector must trace a precise curve (e.g., a welding seam). |
| ✈️ **Aircraft flying straight from city A to B** (no specified waypoints, autopilot chooses heading changes) | **PTP in Joint Space** | Each control surface (aileron, elevator, rudder) moves independently to reach a target attitude. |
| 🖊️ **A CNC plotter drawing the letter "S"** — every curve must be reproduced exactly | **CP in Cartesian Space** | The end-effector (pen tip) must trace a continuous geometric figure with prescribed velocity. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Use-Case Mapping (KTU Board Favorite):**
> - **PTP** → Spot welding, pick-and-place, palletizing, drilling, riveting, hole punching.
> - **CP** → Arc welding, spray painting, adhesive dispensing, laser cutting, waterjet cutting, seam following, polishing.

---

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics and Constants

- **Interpolation Rate (Path Sampling Frequency):** $f_{int} = \mathbf{50 \text{ Hz} \text{ to } 2000 \text{ Hz}}$ for modern industrial CP controllers.
- **Path Accuracy:** typically $\pm \mathbf{0.02 \text{ mm}}$ to $\pm \mathbf{0.1 \text{ mm}}$ for precision CP robots.
- **PTP Repeatability:** typically $\pm \mathbf{0.025 \text{ mm}}$ to $\pm \mathbf{0.1 \text{ mm}}$ (e.g., FANUC, ABB, KUKA).
- **Trajectory Time Index:** A scalar $t \in [0, T]$ where $T$ is total motion duration (**seconds**).
- **Normalized Path Parameter:** $s(t) = t/T$, so $s \in [0, 1]$, dimensionless.

---

### 1.4 Comparison Snapshot (For Quick Recall)

| Parameter | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Continuous Path (CP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Path Specification** | Only start & end points | Entire geometric path |
| **Working Space** | Joint space | Cartesian (task) space |
| **Inverse Kinematics Required** | Only at start & end | At every interpolated point |
| **Controller Complexity** | Low (open-loop or simple servo) | High (real-time IK solver) |
| **Cycle Time** | Minimized (time-optimal) | Fixed by path length / velocity |
| **Memory Storage** | Low (a few points) | High (hundreds to thousands of points) |
| **Typical Application** | Pick-and-place, spot welding | Arc welding, painting, cutting |
| **Cost of Robot** | Lower | Higher |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of PTP Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PTP planning is essentially a problem of *time-parameterized joint motion*, independent across each joint axis.**

#### Step 1: Specify Initial and Final Joint Configurations
The user (or higher-level planner) provides:
$$\theta_{start} = [\theta_{1}^{s}, \theta_{2}^{s}, \ldots, \theta_{n}^{s}]^{T}, \quad \theta_{end} = [\theta_{1}^{e}, \theta_{2}^{e}, \ldots, \theta_{n}^{e}]^{T}$$

#### Step 2: Compute Per-Joint Displacements
Each joint $i$ must travel:
$$\Delta\theta_{i} = \theta_{i}^{e} - \theta_{i}^{s}, \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

#### Step 3: Choose a Motion Profile (Trajectory Function)
Common PTP profiles:
- **Single-Degree Polynomial (Bang-Bang):** Constant velocity, abrupt accel/decel. $\Rightarrow$ High vibration, rarely used.
- **Trapezoidal Velocity Profile:** Constant acceleration, cruise, constant deceleration.
- **Triangular Velocity Profile:** Constant acceleration until midpoint, then deceleration.
- **Cubic Polynomial:** Smooth position, continuous velocity, finite acceleration.
- **Quintic Polynomial:** Smooth position, velocity, AND acceleration.

#### Step 4: Synchronize Joint Motions
The **slowest joint** dictates the total motion time $T$. Faster joints are typically:
- Scaled to match the slowest joint's duration, OR
- Executed in *coarse-fine* fashion (start earlier, finish earlier), OR
- Held at the goal until others arrive (less common).

#### Step 5: Servo the Joints
Each joint's servo loop tracks its individual time-indexed reference, ensuring no overshoot or steady-state error.

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of CP Planning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CP planning transforms a geometric path into a time-indexed trajectory in Cartesian space, then converts to joint commands via inverse kinematics.**

#### Step 1: Specify the Geometric Path
The user provides a sequence of waypoints defining a curve:
$$P = \{(x_1, y_1, z_1), (x_2, y_2, z_2), \ldots, (x_m, y_m, z_m)\}$$

#### Step 2: Fit a Smooth Curve Through the Waypoints
Possible interpolants:
- **Linear segments** (polyline)
- **Circular arcs** (circular interpolation)
- **Cubic splines**
- **Bézier curves**
- **NURBS** (Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline, used in advanced CNC)

#### Step 3: Define a Velocity Profile Along the Path
A scalar function $s(t)$ parameterizes progress along the path:
$$\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p}(s(t)), \quad s \in [0, 1]$$

Velocity and acceleration of the end-effector are then:
$$\mathbf{v}(t) = \frac{d\mathbf{p}}{ds} \cdot \dot{s}, \quad \mathbf{a}(t) = \frac{d^{2}\mathbf{p}}{ds^{2}} \cdot \dot{s}^{2} + \frac{d\mathbf{p}}{ds} \cdot \ddot{s}$$

#### Step 4: Inverse Kinematics at Each Sample
At each interpolated Cartesian pose, the controller solves:
$$\boldsymbol{\theta}(t) = IK(\mathbf{p}(t), \boldsymbol{\phi}(t))$$

This is computationally intensive — often the bottleneck in CP systems.

#### Step 5: Joint Servo
The computed joint trajectories $\theta_i(t)$ are sent to the individual joint controllers.

---

### 2.3 The Two Canonical CP Path Primitives (KTU High-Yield)

#### 2.3.1 Straight-Line (Linear) Interpolation Between Two Poses

For two Cartesian poses $A$ and $B$ with position vectors $\mathbf{p}_A$ and $\mathbf{p}_B$:

$$\mathbf{p}(s) = \mathbf{p}_A + s \cdot (\mathbf{p}_B - \mathbf{p}_A), \quad s \in [0,1]$$

If orientation is also interpolated (via SLERP — Spherical Linear Interpolation on unit quaternions):
$$\mathbf{q}(s) = \text{Slerp}(\mathbf{q}_A, \mathbf{q}_B; s) = \frac{\sin((1-s)\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\mathbf{q}_A + \frac{\sin(s\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\mathbf{q}_B$$

where $\Omega = \cos^{-1}(\mathbf{q}_A \cdot \mathbf{q}_B)$ is the angle between the two quaternion orientations.

#### 2.3.2 Circular Interpolation (Most Common CP Primitive in Welding)

For three non-collinear points $P_1, P_2, P_3$, the unique circle in 3D space has:
- **Center** $\mathbf{C}$ (intersection of perpendicular bisectors of chords $P_1P_2$ and $P_2P_3$).
- **Radius** $r = \vert \mathbf{P}_1 - \mathbf{C} \vert$.
- **Normal** $\mathbf{n} = \frac{(\mathbf{P}_2 - \mathbf{P}_1) \times (\mathbf{P}_3 - \mathbf{P}_2)}{\vert (\mathbf{P}_2 - \mathbf{P}_1) \times (\mathbf{P}_3 - \mathbf{P}_2) \vert}$.

A point on the circle at angle $\phi$ (measured from $P_1$ relative to $\mathbf{C}$):
$$\mathbf{p}(\phi) = \mathbf{C} + r \cdot (\cos\phi \cdot \mathbf{u} + \sin\phi \cdot \mathbf{v})$$

where $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are orthonormal vectors in the circle's plane.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Print this table and keep it for last-minute revision.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cubic polynomial trajectory (joint $i$) | $\theta_{i}(t) = a_{0} + a_{1}t + a_{2}t^{2} + a_{3}t^{3}$ | $a_0, \ldots, a_3$ are constants from BCs | rad |
| 2 | Cubic — initial position | $\theta_{i}(0) = \theta_{i}^{s} \Rightarrow a_0 = \theta_{i}^{s}$ | Start angle | rad |
| 3 | Cubic — final position | $\theta_{i}(T) = \theta_{i}^{e} \Rightarrow a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^{2} + a_3 T^{3} = \theta_{i}^{e}$ | End angle | rad |
| 4 | Cubic — zero initial velocity | $\dot{\theta}_{i}(0) = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 = 0$ | Start at rest | rad/s |
| 5 | Cubic — zero final velocity | $\dot{\theta}_{i}(T) = 0 \Rightarrow a_1 + 2a_2 T + 3a_3 T^{2} = 0$ | End at rest | rad/s |
| 6 | Cubic coefficient $a_2$ | $a_2 = \dfrac{3(\theta_{i}^{e} - \theta_{i}^{s})}{T^{2}}$ | — | rad/s² |
| 7 | Cubic coefficient $a_3$ | $a_3 = \dfrac{-2(\theta_{i}^{e} - \theta_{i}^{s})}{T^{3}}$ | — | rad/s³ |
| 8 | Quintic polynomial | $\theta(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{5} a_k t^{k}$ | 6 BCs (pos, vel, acc at both ends) | rad |
| 9 | LSPB blend time | $t_{b} = \dfrac{\dot{\theta}_{\max}}{a_{\max}}$ | Blend duration | s |
| 10 | LSPB cruise duration | $t_{c} = T - 2t_{b}$ (if reachable) | Constant-velocity phase | s |
| 11 | Cartesian distance | $d = \vert \mathbf{p}_B - \mathbf{p}_A \vert$ | Straight-line distance | m |
| 12 | Circular arc length | $L = r \cdot \theta_{arc}$ | Arc length, $r$ radius, $\theta_{arc}$ swept angle | m |
| 13 | Trapezoidal peak velocity (reached) | $\dot{\theta}_{\max} = \dfrac{\Delta\theta}{T - t_{b}}$ | If cruise exists | rad/s |
| 14 | Trapezoidal peak velocity (unreached) | $\dot{\theta}_{\max}^{2} = a_{\max} \cdot \Delta\theta$ | Triangular limit | rad²/s² |
| 15 | Path-parameter velocity | $\dot{\mathbf{p}} = \dfrac{d\mathbf{p}}{ds}\dot{s}$ | Tangent × path speed | m/s |
| 16 | Path-parameter acceleration | $\ddot{\mathbf{p}} = \dfrac{d^{2}\mathbf{p}}{ds^{2}}\dot{s}^{2} + \dfrac{d\mathbf{p}}{ds}\ddot{s}$ | Curvature + tangential | m/s² |
| 17 | SLERP interpolation | $\mathbf{q}(s) = \dfrac{\sin((1-s)\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\mathbf{q}_A + \dfrac{\sin(s\Omega)}{\sin\Omega}\mathbf{q}_B$ | Quaternion orientation | unitless |
| 18 | Curvature of a 3D path | $\kappa = \dfrac{\vert \mathbf{p}' \times \mathbf{p}'' \vert}{\vert \mathbf{p}' \vert^{3}}$ | $\mathbf{p}' = d\mathbf{p}/ds$, $\mathbf{p}'' = d^2\mathbf{p}/ds^2$ | 1/m |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **PTP planning** dominates in **high-throughput manufacturing**: a single robot can perform 60–180 pick-and-place cycles per minute, with motion times of 0.3–1.0 seconds per cycle. Speed and repeatability are paramount.
> - **CP planning** dominates in **quality-critical surface processing**: arc-welding seams demand path accuracy of $\pm 0.5 \text{ mm}$ at travel speeds of 5–50 mm/s; spray painting requires smooth velocity transitions to avoid orange-peel defects (sudden accel spikes cause uneven paint deposition).
> - In **surgical robotics (e.g., Da Vinci systems)**, CP planning with quintic splines ensures jerk-free tool motion, reducing tissue trauma.
> - In **space manipulator rendezvous and docking**, CP planning is mission-critical: the end-effector must follow a precisely defined approach corridor to safely capture a tumbling satellite.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — Cubic Polynomial Joint Trajectory for PTP Motion

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup (Standard KTU Question Type):**
> A single joint must move from $\theta_s = 20^{\circ}$ to $\theta_e = 80^{\circ}$ in $T = 4$ seconds. The joint starts and ends at rest. Derive the cubic polynomial $\theta(t)$ that achieves this motion.

#### Step 1: Write the General Cubic Polynomial
A cubic polynomial in time $t$ has four coefficients to determine:
$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

**Logic:** Four unknowns require four independent equations (boundary conditions).

#### Step 2: Compute the Velocity Profile
The first time derivative gives joint velocity:
$$\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

**Logic:** Differentiate $\theta(t)$ once with respect to $t$ using the power rule: $\frac{d}{dt}(a_k t^k) = k \cdot a_k t^{k-1}$.

#### Step 3: Apply Boundary Condition 1 — Initial Position
At $t = 0$, the joint is at $\theta_s$:
$$\theta(0) = a_0 + a_1(0) + a_2(0)^2 + a_3(0)^3 = a_0 = \theta_s = 20^{\circ}$$

**Logic:** Substituting $t = 0$ annihilates all terms except the constant $a_0$.

$$\boxed{a_0 = 20^{\circ}}$$

#### Step 4: Apply Boundary Condition 2 — Initial Velocity is Zero
At $t = 0$, the joint starts from rest (zero velocity):
$$\dot{\theta}(0) = a_1 + 2 a_2(0) + 3 a_3(0)^2 = a_1 = 0$$

**Logic:** Substituting $t = 0$ in the velocity expression leaves only $a_1$.

$$\boxed{a_1 = 0}$$

#### Step 5: Apply Boundary Condition 3 — Final Position
At $t = T = 4$ s, the joint is at $\theta_e = 80^{\circ}$:
$$\theta(4) = a_0 + a_1(4) + a_2(4)^2 + a_3(4)^3 = 80^{\circ}$$

Substituting $a_0 = 20$ and $a_1 = 0$:
$$20 + 0 + 16 a_2 + 64 a_3 = 80$$
$$16 a_2 + 64 a_3 = 60 \quad \text{...(Equation I)}$$

**Logic:** $(4)^2 = 16$, $(4)^3 = 64$, and $80 - 20 = 60$.

#### Step 6: Apply Boundary Condition 4 — Final Velocity is Zero
At $t = 4$ s, the joint comes to rest:
$$\dot{\theta}(4) = a_1 + 2 a_2(4) + 3 a_3(4)^2 = 0$$

Substituting $a_1 = 0$:
$$0 + 8 a_2 + 48 a_3 = 0$$
$$8 a_2 + 48 a_3 = 0 \quad \text{...(Equation II)}$$

**Logic:** $2 \cdot 4 = 8$, $3 \cdot 4^2 = 48$.

#### Step 7: Solve the 2×2 Linear System
We have:
$$16 a_2 + 64 a_3 = 60 \quad \text{(I)}$$
$$8 a_2 + 48 a_3 = 0 \quad \text{(II)}$$

**Logic:** Two equations, two unknowns. We can solve by elimination or substitution.

From Equation (II):
$$8 a_2 = -48 a_3 \implies a_2 = -6 a_3$$

**Logic:** Divide both sides by 8: $a_2 = -48 a_3 / 8 = -6 a_3$.

Substitute into Equation (I):
$$16(-6 a_3) + 64 a_3 = 60$$
$$-96 a_3 + 64 a_3 = 60$$
$$-32 a_3 = 60$$
$$\boxed{a_3 = -\frac{60}{32} = -1.875 \text{ deg/s}^3}$$

**Logic:** Combine like terms: $-96 + 64 = -32$. Solve: $a_3 = 60/(-32) = -1.875$.

Then:
$$\boxed{a_2 = -6 \times (-1.875) = 11.25 \text{ deg/s}^2}$$

**Logic:** Multiply $a_3$ by $-6$ to recover $a_2$.

#### Step 8: Assemble the Final Trajectory
$$\theta(t) = 20 + 0 \cdot t + 11.25 t^2 - 1.875 t^3$$
$$\boxed{\theta(t) = 20 + 11.25 t^2 - 1.875 t^3 \quad \text{(degrees, } t \text{ in seconds)}}$$

**Logic:** Substitute all four coefficients back into the general form.

#### Step 9: Verification
At $t = 0$: $\theta(0) = 20 + 0 - 0 = 20^{\circ}$ ✓
At $t = 4$: $\theta(4) = 20 + 11.25(16) - 1.875(64) = 20 + 180 - 120 = 80^{\circ}$ ✓
Velocity: $\dot{\theta}(t) = 22.5 t - 5.625 t^2$
At $t = 0$: $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$ ✓
At $t = 4$: $\dot{\theta}(4) = 90 - 90 = 0$ ✓

**Logic:** This is the KTU examiner's "must-do" verification step — never skip it.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Trapezoidal Velocity Profile for PTP Motion

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:**
> A joint must move $\Delta\theta = 60^{\circ}$ in $T = 3$ s with maximum acceleration $a_{\max} = 40^{\circ}/\text{s}^2$. The velocity profile is trapezoidal (accel → cruise → decel). Determine: (a) the blend time $t_b$, (b) the peak velocity $v_{\max}$, and (c) the cruise duration $t_c$.

#### Step 1: Compute the Blend Time
The blend (acceleration) phase duration is:
$$t_b = \frac{v_{\max}}{a_{\max}}$$

**Logic:** By definition of constant acceleration: $v = a \cdot t \implies t = v/a$. The blend phase is symmetric (accel = decel) for a balanced trapezoidal profile.

#### Step 2: Express Total Displacement
Total displacement is the sum of three phases:
$$\Delta\theta = \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} a_{\max} t_b^{2}}_{\text{accel}} + \underbrace{v_{\max} t_c}_{\text{cruise}} + \underbrace{\tfrac{1}{2} a_{\max} t_b^{2}}_{\text{decel}}$$

$$\Delta\theta = a_{\max} t_b^{2} + v_{\max} t_c$$

**Logic:** Area under a trapezoidal velocity profile = area of two triangles (accel + decel) + rectangle (cruise). Each triangle has base $t_b$ and height $v_{\max}$, so area = $\tfrac{1}{2} t_b v_{\max}$. But $v_{\max} = a_{\max} t_b$, so each triangle area = $\tfrac{1}{2} a_{\max} t_b^2$.

#### Step 3: Express Total Time
$$T = 2 t_b + t_c$$

**Logic:** Two blend phases (accel and decel) plus one cruise phase.

#### Step 4: Substitute and Eliminate $t_c$
From Step 3: $t_c = T - 2 t_b$

Substituting into Step 2's equation:
$$\Delta\theta = a_{\max} t_b^{2} + v_{\max} (T - 2 t_b)$$

Using $v_{\max} = a_{\max} t_b$:
$$\Delta\theta = a_{\max} t_b^{2} + a_{\max} t_b (T - 2 t_b) = a_{\max} t_b T - a_{\max} t_b^{2}$$

**Logic:** Expand: $a_{\max} t_b^2 + a_{\max} t_b T - 2 a_{\max} t_b^2 = -a_{\max} t_b^2 + a_{\max} t_b T$.

#### Step 5: Solve for $t_b$
Rearranging:
$$a_{\max} t_b^2 - a_{\max} t_b T + \Delta\theta = 0$$

**Logic:** Standard quadratic in $t_b$, with $A = a_{\max}$, $B = -a_{\max} T$, $C = \Delta\theta$.

Using the quadratic formula:
$$t_b = \frac{a_{\max} T \pm \sqrt{a_{\max}^{2} T^{2} - 4 a_{\max} \Delta\theta}}{2 a_{\max}}$$

$$t_b = \frac{T}{2} \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{T^{2} - \frac{4 \Delta\theta}{a_{\max}}}$$

**Logic:** Factor out $a_{\max}$ from the discriminant and divide numerator and denominator by $2 a_{\max}$.

Substituting values ($T = 3$, $a_{\max} = 40$, $\Delta\theta = 60$):
$$t_b = \frac{3}{2} \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{9 - \frac{240}{40}} = 1.5 \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{9 - 6} = 1.5 \pm \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{3}$$

$$t_b = 1.5 \pm 0.866 \text{ s}$$

**Logic:** $\frac{4 \times 60}{40} = \frac{240}{40} = 6$. $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$, half = $0.866$.

Take the **smaller root** (so $t_c \geq 0$):
$$\boxed{t_b = 1.5 - 0.866 = 0.634 \text{ s}}$$

**Logic:** The larger root ($t_b = 2.366$ s) would imply $2 t_b > T$, giving negative cruise time — physically meaningless. Always take the smaller root for trapezoidal profiles.

#### Step 6: Compute Peak Velocity
$$v_{\max} = a_{\max} t_b = 40 \times 0.634 = \boxed{25.36 \text{ deg/s}}$$

**Logic:** Direct multiplication: peak velocity equals acceleration times blend time.

#### Step 7: Compute Cruise Duration
$$t_c = T - 2 t_b = 3 - 2(0.634) = \boxed{1.732 \text{ s}}$$

**Logic:** Total time minus both blend phases. Positive $t_c$ confirms a valid trapezoidal (not triangular) profile.

#### Step 8: Verification
Total displacement: $40 \times (0.634)^2 + 25.36 \times 1.732 = 16.07 + 43.92 \approx 60^{\circ}$ ✓

**Logic:** This matches the required $\Delta\theta$ within rounding error.

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Straight-Line CP Interpolation in 3D

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Setup:**
> A robot end-effector must move linearly from $P_A = (0.2, 0.1, 0.3)$ m to $P_B = (0.5, 0.4, 0.3)$ m at a constant speed of $v = 0.1$ m/s. Compute: (a) the path length, (b) the total travel time, (c) the parametric position at $t = 1.5$ s.

#### Step 1: Compute the Path Vector
$$\Delta\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{p}_B - \mathbf{p}_A = (0.3, 0.3, 0.0) \text{ m}$$

**Logic:** Component-wise subtraction: $(0.5 - 0.2, 0.4 - 0.1, 0.3 - 0.3) = (0.3, 0.3, 0.0)$.

#### Step 2: Compute the Path Length (Euclidean Norm)
$$L = \vert \Delta\mathbf{p} \vert = \sqrt{0.3^2 + 0.3^2 + 0.0^2} = \sqrt{0.18}$$
$$\boxed{L = 0.4243 \text{ m}}$$

**Logic:** Magnitude of a 3D vector: $\vert \mathbf{v} \vert = \sqrt{v_x^2 + v_y^2 + v_z^2}$. Here $\sqrt{0.18} = \sqrt{9/50} \approx 0.4243$.

#### Step 3: Compute the Total Travel Time
$$T = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{0.4243}{0.1} = \boxed{4.243 \text{ s}}$$

**Logic:** Time = distance / speed (constant speed assumption).

#### Step 4: Define the Parametric Path Equation
$$\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p}_A + \left(\frac{t}{T}\right)(\mathbf{p}_B - \mathbf{p}_A), \quad t \in [0, T]$$

**Logic:** Linear interpolation with normalized parameter $s = t/T \in [0,1]$.

#### Step 5: Evaluate at $t = 1.5$ s
Normalized parameter: $s = 1.5 / 4.243 = 0.3535$

**Logic:** Divide current time by total time.

Position:
$$\mathbf{p}(1.5) = (0.2, 0.1, 0.3) + 0.3535 \times (0.3, 0.3, 0.0)$$
$$\mathbf{p}(1.5) = (0.2 + 0.1061, 0.1 + 0.1061, 0.3 + 0.0)$$
$$\boxed{\mathbf{p}(1.5) = (0.3061, 0.2061, 0.3) \text{ m}}$$

**Logic:** Scalar multiplication of the displacement vector by $s$, then vector addition to the start.

#### Step 6: Verify Speed Consistency
Instantaneous velocity: $\mathbf{v}(t) = (\mathbf{p}_B - \mathbf{p}_A)/T = (0.3, 0.3, 0.0)/4.243 = (0.0707, 0.0707, 0.0)$ m/s

Speed: $\vert \mathbf{v} \vert = \sqrt{0.0707^2 + 0.0707^2} = 0.1$ m/s ✓

**Logic:** For constant-speed linear interpolation, velocity is constant — confirming our setup.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Planning Method

| Scenario | Recommended Method | Justification (KTU-Examiner Friendly) |
|---|---|---|
| Pick-and-place, high speed, no path concern | PTP with trapezoidal velocity | Minimum cycle time, low computational cost |
| Pick-and-place with smooth stop required | PTP with cubic polynomial | Eliminates jerk at endpoints |
| Spot welding on car body | PTP with shortest-path joint motion | Path between spots irrelevant |
| Arc welding a straight seam | CP with linear interpolation | Path must be straight for weld quality |
| Arc welding a curved seam (e.g., pipe) | CP with circular interpolation | Path must follow pipe geometry |
| Spray painting a curved surface | CP with spline interpolation | Smooth velocity prevents paint defects |
| High-speed CP through sharp corners | CP with parabolic blends or S-curves | Reduces jerk, prevents overshoot |
| Surgical tool motion in soft tissue | CP with quintic polynomial | Zero jerk is medically necessary |
| Heavy-payload robot, dynamics matter | Time-optimal PTP with torque limits | Dynamics-aware planning |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CP Trajectory Planner

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Specification<br/>Waypoints and Constraints] --> B[Path Geometry Generator<br/>Linear Circular Spline Bezier]
    B --> C[Smooth Interpolant<br/>Position vs s 0 to 1]
    C --> D[Velocity Profile Designer<br/>Trapezoidal S-curve Cubic Blend]
    D --> E[Time Parameterization<br/>s of t  Normalized]
    E --> F[Inverse Kinematics Solver<br/>Closed Form or Iterative]
    F --> G[Joint Trajectories<br/>theta1 t to thetan t]
    G --> H[Joint Servo Controllers<br/>PID Loops]
    H --> I[Robot Actuators<br/>Servo Motors Encoders]
    I --> J[End Effector Motion<br/>Cartesian Pose]
    J --> K[Feedback Sensors<br/>Position Velocity Current]
    K --> H
    F --> L[Kinematic Constraint Check<br/>Velocity Acceleration Torque Limits]
    L --> D
```

**Logic of the flow:**
1. The **task** enters as Cartesian waypoints.
2. A **path geometry** (straight line, arc, spline) is fit through these points.
3. The **smooth interpolant** converts the geometry into a position-vs-parameter curve.
4. A **velocity profile** assigns time derivatives, ensuring smoothness (S-curve preferred for high-end CP).
5. The **time parameterization** explicitly converts $s \in [0,1]$ to $t \in [0,T]$.
6. The **inverse kinematics solver** converts each sampled Cartesian pose into joint angles.
7. The **kinematic constraint check** ensures joint velocity, acceleration, and torque limits are not violated — if violated, the profile is rescaled (slower motion).
8. The **joint servos** track the planned joint trajectories.
9. The **feedback loop** corrects tracking errors in real time.

---

### 4.2 Comparative Architecture of PTP vs CP Planning Pipelines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PTP_Planning
        A1[Start Joint Angles] --> B1[End Joint Angles]
        B1 --> C1[Per Joint Displacement]
        C1 --> D1[Cubic or Trapezoidal<br/>Profile per Joint]
        D1 --> E1[Joint Servo]
    end
    subgraph CP_Planning
        A2[Start Cartesian Pose] --> B2[Goal Cartesian Pose]
        B2 --> C2[Geometric Path Fit<br/>Linear Arc Spline]
        C2 --> D2[Sample Path at High Rate<br/>50 to 2000 Hz]
        D2 --> E2[Inverse Kinematics<br/>at each sample]
        E2 --> F2[Joint Angle Trajectories]
        F2 --> G2[Joint Servo]
    end
```

**Logic of the comparison:**
- **PTP planning** operates purely in joint space, requiring only two endpoint configurations.
- **CP planning** operates in Cartesian space, requiring an entire geometric path, dense sampling, and **real-time inverse kinematics** — a dramatically heavier computational load.

---

### 4.3 State Machine of a Trajectory Execution Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Planning: User sends target pose
    Planning --> Validation: Trajectory generated
    Validation --> Idle: Limits violated reject
    Validation --> Ready: Limits satisfied
    Ready --> Executing: Start command issued
    Executing --> Tracking: Joint servos active
    Tracking --> Executing: Feedback OK
    Tracking --> Aborted: Tracking error exceeds limit
    Aborted --> Idle: Operator reset
    Executing --> Completed: Time T reached
    Completed --> Idle: Cycle complete
```

**Logic of the states:**
- A robot task begins in **Idle**.
- **Planning** generates the trajectory (PTP or CP).
- **Validation** checks kinematic and dynamic limits.
- On approval, the system enters **Ready**.
- Upon start, **Executing → Tracking** forms the active control loop.
- **Aborted** is a safety state for large tracking errors.
- **Completed** returns the system to Idle for the next cycle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Each Part A question carries 3 marks, expected answer length: 3–5 sentences + a diagram if needed.

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]`
**Differentiate between Point-to-Point (PTP) and Continuous Path (CP) motion control in industrial robots. Give one industrial application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PTP | CP |
|---|---|---|
| **Path definition** | Only start and end points specified | Entire geometric path specified |
| **Working domain** | Joint space | Cartesian space |
| **Computation** | Light (no IK during motion) | Heavy (continuous IK required) |
| **Cost** | Lower | Higher |

**Industrial Applications:**
- **PTP →** Spot welding in automotive body assembly, pick-and-place in packaging lines.
- **CP →** Arc welding of seams, spray painting of car bodies, laser cutting of sheet metal.

**Examiner Tip:** Tabular comparison is the highest-scoring format for 3-mark questions.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the four boundary conditions required to determine the coefficients of a cubic polynomial trajectory used for a single-joint PTP motion from rest to rest.**

**Model Answer:**

A cubic polynomial $\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ has 4 unknown coefficients, requiring exactly 4 boundary conditions:

1. **Initial position:** $\theta(0) = \theta_s$
2. **Final position:** $\theta(T) = \theta_e$
3. **Initial velocity (zero, starts from rest):** $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0$
4. **Final velocity (zero, ends at rest):** $\dot{\theta}(T) = 0$

**Examiner Tip:** Students often forget to state the *physical meaning* of each BC — the 4th mark is usually awarded for explaining "why" (rest-to-rest motion requires zero velocity at both ends).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Answer **one full question** of 14 marks OR **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** from a single question. We provide TWO alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) so the student has the standard internal choice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Slot]`

**A single robotic joint must move from $\theta_s = 10^{\circ}$ to $\theta_e = 70^{\circ}$ in $T = 4$ seconds, starting and ending at rest. Use a cubic polynomial trajectory.**

**(a)** Derive the cubic polynomial $\theta(t)$ and its velocity profile $\dot{\theta}(t)$.
**(b)** Compute the joint position, velocity, and acceleration at $t = 2$ s, and comment on the smoothness of the motion.

---

##### **Model Solution to Q-A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)**

**Step 1: General cubic polynomial**
$$\theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$$

**Step 2: Velocity**
$$\dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2$$

**Step 3: BC1** $\theta(0) = 10^{\circ} \implies a_0 = 10^{\circ}$

**Step 4: BC2** $\dot{\theta}(0) = 0 \implies a_1 = 0$

**Step 5: BC3** $\theta(4) = 70^{\circ}$
$$10 + 0 + 16 a_2 + 64 a_3 = 70 \implies 16 a_2 + 64 a_3 = 60 \quad \text{...(I)}$$

**Step 6: BC4** $\dot{\theta}(4) = 0$
$$0 + 8 a_2 + 48 a_3 = 0 \implies a_2 = -6 a_3 \quad \text{...(II)}$$

**Step 7: Solve** — Substitute (II) into (I):
$$16(-6 a_3) + 64 a_3 = 60 \implies -32 a_3 = 60 \implies a_3 = -1.875$$
$$a_2 = -6(-1.875) = 11.25$$

**Final Trajectory:**
$$\boxed{\theta(t) = 10 + 11.25 t^2 - 1.875 t^3 \quad \text{(degrees)}}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{\theta}(t) = 22.5 t - 5.625 t^2 \quad \text{(deg/s)}}$$

**Part (b) — Evaluation at $t = 2$ s (7 marks)**

**Position:**
$$\theta(2) = 10 + 11.25(4) - 1.875(8) = 10 + 45 - 15 = \boxed{40^{\circ}}$$

**Velocity:**
$$\dot{\theta}(2) = 22.5(2) - 5.625(4) = 45 - 22.5 = \boxed{22.5 \text{ deg/s}}$$

**Acceleration:**
$$\ddot{\theta}(t) = 22.5 - 11.25 t$$
$$\ddot{\theta}(2) = 22.5 - 22.5 = \boxed{0 \text{ deg/s}^2}$$

**Smoothness Comment:**
- Velocity is **continuous** throughout the motion (zero at start and end, peaks in the middle).
- Acceleration is **linear** in $t$ (i.e., $\ddot{\theta}(t) = 22.5 - 11.25 t$), changing sign at the velocity peak.
- However, the **jerk** (third derivative) is **constant** at $-11.25 \text{ deg/s}^3$ — a non-zero constant jerk can excite vibrations in flexible structures.
- For **smoother motion** (continuous acceleration), a **quintic polynomial** would be preferred, but it requires 6 boundary conditions (position, velocity, acceleration at both ends).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Don't** forget to compute the **acceleration** — it carries **2 of the 7 marks** in part (b). Many students stop at velocity.
> - **Don't** state "the motion is smooth" without computing jerk or commenting on it. A 1-mark deduction is typical for unsupported smoothness claims.
> - **Always** verify your final polynomial satisfies all 4 BCs — partial marks are awarded for each correct verification.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Explain the **Continuous Path (CP) planning** method for a robotic manipulator. With a neat block diagram, describe the major functional blocks of a CP trajectory planner. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot end-effector must follow a straight line in 3D Cartesian space from point $P_A = (0.3, 0.0, 0.4)$ m to $P_B = (0.6, 0.2, 0.4)$ m. The motion is at constant speed $v = 0.15$ m/s. Determine: (i) the total path length, (ii) the total travel time, and (iii) the position of the end-effector at $t = 1.0$ s. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution to Q-B**

**Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation + Block Diagram (7 marks)**

**Continuous Path (CP) Planning — Definition:**
CP planning is a trajectory generation method in which the end-effector of the robot is required to follow a precisely defined geometric path between the start and goal configurations. Unlike PTP, the path geometry is a hard constraint and must be maintained at every instant of motion. The path is defined in Cartesian (task) space and converted to joint-space commands via repeated inverse kinematics.

**Typical Industrial Applications:**
- Arc welding (seam tracking)
- Spray painting
- Laser/waterjet cutting
- Adhesive dispensing
- Edge deburring

**Block Diagram of CP Planner:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cartesian Waypoints Input] --> B[Geometric Path Fit]
    B --> C[Position vs Normalized Parameter s]
    C --> D[Velocity Profile Assignment]
    D --> E[Time Parameterization s of t]
    E --> F[Inverse Kinematics at Each Sample]
    F --> G{Joint Limits Satisfied?}
    G -- No --> D
    G -- Yes --> H[Joint Servo Commands]
    H --> I[Actuators Drive Robot]
    I --> J[Encoder Feedback]
    J --> H
```

**Marking Breakdown:**
- 2 marks: Correct definition + applications
- 3 marks: Block diagram (functional blocks, arrows, feedback)
- 2 marks: Explanation of each block's role

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Compute the path vector**
$$\Delta\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{p}_B - \mathbf{p}_A = (0.3, 0.2, 0.0) \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute the path length (Euclidean norm)**
$$L = \vert \Delta\mathbf{p} \vert = \sqrt{0.3^2 + 0.2^2 + 0.0^2} = \sqrt{0.09 + 0.04} = \sqrt{0.13}$$
$$\boxed{L \approx 0.3606 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 3: Compute the total travel time**
$$T = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{0.3606}{0.15} = \boxed{2.404 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 4: Compute normalized parameter at $t = 1.0$ s**
$$s = \frac{t}{T} = \frac{1.0}{2.404} = 0.4160$$

**Step 5: Compute the interpolated position**
$$\mathbf{p}(1.0) = \mathbf{p}_A + s \cdot (\mathbf{p}_B - \mathbf{p}_A)$$
$$\mathbf{p}(1.0) = (0.3, 0.0, 0.4) + 0.4160 \times (0.3, 0.2, 0.0)$$
$$\mathbf{p}(1.0) = (0.3 + 0.1248, 0.0 + 0.0832, 0.4 + 0.0)$$
$$\boxed{\mathbf{p}(1.0) = (0.4248, 0.0832, 0.4) \text{ m}}$$

**Step 6: Verification (recommended for full marks)**
Distance traveled in 1 s: $v \times t = 0.15 \times 1.0 = 0.15$ m
Check: $\vert (0.1248, 0.0832, 0) \vert = \sqrt{0.01557 + 0.00692} = \sqrt{0.02249} \approx 0.150$ m ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for CP Numerical Questions:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $s = t \cdot v / L$ incorrectly — the correct form is $s = t/T = vt/L$. Both are equivalent, but students mix up the dimensions.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert units. Path components are in **meters**, time in **seconds**, velocity in **m/s**. Mixing cm with m loses 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not verifying the final answer. A 1-mark bonus is given for explicit verification.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In the block diagram, students often forget the **feedback loop** from actuators/encoders back to the joint servo — this costs 1 full mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (High-Density Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this as your final-30-minutes revision pass before the exam.**

- ✅ **PTP = Joint-space motion.** Only start and end joint angles are specified. Path is unspecified.
- ✅ **CP = Cartesian-space motion.** The entire geometric path is specified and tracked.
- ✅ **PTP applications** → spot welding, pick-and-place, drilling, palletizing.
- ✅ **CP applications** → arc welding, painting, laser cutting, gluing, polishing.
- ✅ **Cubic polynomial trajectory** has **4 coefficients** → requires **4 boundary conditions** (position + velocity at both ends for a rest-to-rest motion).
- ✅ **Cubic coefficients for rest-to-rest motion** are: $a_0 = \theta_s$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = 3\Delta\theta/T^2$, $a_3 = -2\Delta\theta/T^3$ — **memorize this standard result**.
- ✅ **Quintic polynomial trajectory** has **6 coefficients** → 6 BCs (position, velocity, **acceleration** at both ends). Used for zero-jerk motion.
- ✅ **Trapezoidal velocity profile** = constant accel → constant velocity → constant decel. Total displacement = $a_{max} t_b^2 + v_{max} t_c$ (accel triangle + decel triangle + cruise rectangle).
- ✅ **Triangular velocity profile** = constant accel → constant decel (no cruise phase). Triggered when $\Delta\theta$ is small relative to $a_{max}$ and $T$.
- ✅ **Trapezoidal blend time** = $t_b = \dfrac{T}{2} \pm \dfrac{1}{2}\sqrt{T^2 - \dfrac{4\Delta\theta}{a_{max}}}$ — always take the **smaller root** for positive cruise time.
- ✅ **Linear (straight-line) CP interpolation** uses $\mathbf{p}(t) = \mathbf{p}_A + (t/T)(\mathbf{p}_B - \mathbf{p}_A)$.
- ✅ **Circular CP interpolation** requires 3 non-collinear points to define the unique circle in 3D.
- ✅ **Path length** for linear motion = Euclidean norm of $\Delta\mathbf{p}$.
- ✅ **Path length** for circular motion = $r \cdot \theta_{arc}$.
- ✅ **SLERP** is used for **orientation** interpolation in CP; **linear interpolation** is for **position**.
- ✅ **CP planning requires real-time inverse kinematics** — this is the main computational bottleneck.
- ✅ **Cubic polynomial has non-zero constant jerk** — for jerk-free motion, use quintic polynomial.
- ✅ **LSPB** (Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends) is the most common industrial trajectory — simple, smooth, time-tunable.
- ✅ **Jerk** = third derivative of position; its discontinuity causes vibration in flexible structures.
- ✅ **Synchronization in PTP** — the slowest joint determines the total motion time; faster joints must be slowed or held.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Part A is 3 marks (definition/short answer); Part B is 14 marks (numerical + conceptual mix). Always show **all 4 BCs** in cubic trajectory derivations.
- ✅ **Always verify** your trajectory at $t = 0$ and $t = T$ to catch algebraic errors — this is what separates a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Point to Point and Continuous Path Planning**
> *These notes are optimized for KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examinations. Cross-reference with the official KTU syllabus (OERAT611) for any module updates.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Robot Selection Considerations for an Application: Number of Axes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Robot Selection Considerations for an Application: Number of Axes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of industrial robotics, the **Number of Axes** of a robot is defined as the total count of independent, programmable, rotational or translational motions that the robot manipulator can execute to position and orient its end-effector within its workspace. Each axis represents one independent degree of controlled motion, typically actuated by a dedicated servo motor or pneumatic/hydraulic actuator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> "An **axis** (also called a **joint**) of a robot is an independent line of motion about which a rigid body of the manipulator rotates (revolute) or translates (prismatic). The total number of axes determines the robot's Degrees of Freedom (DOF) and consequently its ability to reach a point in space from a desired approach angle."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Arm

Imagine your own **human arm** as a robotic manipulator. Trace its independent motions from shoulder to fingertip:

| Body Part | Axis Type | Motion Description |
|---|---|---|
| Shoulder | Axis 1 | Swing the entire arm forward/backward (pitch) |
| Shoulder | Axis 2 | Lift the arm sideways away from the body (yaw) |
| Upper Arm | Axis 3 | Twist the arm about its own length (roll) |
| Elbow | Axis 4 | Bend the forearm up and down (pitch) |
| Forearm | Axis 5 | Twist the forearm (roll) |
| Wrist | Axis 6 | Bend the hand up/down (pitch) |
| Wrist | Axis 7 | Bend the hand sideways (yaw) |

A human arm has **7 axes** — slightly redundant. A standard 6-axis industrial robot behaves almost like a "simplified human arm" without one redundancy, which is why 6-axis arms are the industry workhorse for tasks like **welding, painting, and assembly**.

## 1.3 Axes vs. Degrees of Freedom (DOF) — Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Axes** = *Physical, countable* independent motorized joints on the robot.
> **DOF** = *Mathematical* number of independent parameters required to fully describe the position and orientation of the end-effector in 3D space.

For a **rigid body in 3-dimensional space**, the theoretical maximum DOF is:

$$DOF_{max} = 3 \text{ (translation)} + 3 \text{ (rotation)} = 6$$

Therefore:
- A robot with **≤ 6 axes** is called **non-redundant** (DOF = number of axes).
- A robot with **> 6 axes** (typically 7) is called **kinematically redundant** — it has more joints than the minimum needed to reach a point with arbitrary orientation.

## 1.4 KTU Highlight: Three Sub-Categories of Axes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every industrial robot arm can be functionally decomposed into three logical sub-groups:
> 1. **Major Axes (Axes 1, 2, 3)** → Position the **wrist center** in 3D space.
> 2. **Wrist Axes (Axes 4, 5, 6)** → Orient the **end-effector** (tool) at that point.
> 3. **Redundant/Track Axis (Axis 7, if present)** → Extend reach or avoid obstacles.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why the Number of Axes Matters — The "Why" Behind the Choice

When a KTU exam asks you to *select the number of axes for an application*, you must reason from these four engineering criteria:

1. **Workspace Reachability** — Can the robot reach *every* point in the work envelope?
2. **Orientation Dexterity** — Can the tool approach the workpiece from the *required* angle?
3. **Singularity Avoidance** — Does the chosen axis count permit the robot to *escape* its singular configurations?
4. **Application Complexity vs. Cost** — Is a 6-axis arm justified for a simple palletizing job (over-spec = wasted money)?

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic — The Six Functional Tiers

### **Tier 1: 3-Axis Robots (Cartesian / Gantry)**
- **Configuration:** 3 prismatic joints (X, Y, Z).
- **Strengths:** Highest positional accuracy, no singularities in pure translation, large rectangular workspace.
- **Weaknesses:** Zero end-effector orientation control — tool always points the same way (e.g., downward).
- **KTU-Typical Applications:** Pick-and-place from conveyors, CNC machine tending, 3D printing, PCB assembly.

### **Tier 2: 4-Axis Robots (SCARA family)**
- **Configuration:** 3 in-plane motions (R, R, P) + 1 vertical prismatic lift.
- **Strengths:** Very high speed in the horizontal plane; excellent for vertical-insertion tasks.
- **Weaknesses:** Cannot tilt the tool sideways — orientation limited to rotation about the vertical axis.
- **KTU-Typical Applications:** Palletizing, screw-driving, electronic component insertion, packaging.

### **Tier 3: 5-Axis Robots**
- **Configuration:** 3 positioning axes + 2 wrist axes (typically missing the tool roll).
- **Strengths:** Cheaper than 6-axis; sufficient for tasks where a single tool orientation roll is acceptable.
- **Weaknesses:** Cannot rotate the tool continuously about its own axis — limited for tube/pipe welding.
- **KTU-Typical Applications:** Simple arc welding, glue dispensing, water-jet cutting, basic painting.

### **Tier 4: 6-Axis Robots (Industry Standard)**
- **Configuration:** 3 positioning + 3 wrist axes → full spatial dexterity.
- **Strengths:** Can position AND orient the end-effector in any arbitrary pose in 3D space.
- **Weaknesses:** Higher cost, more complex programming, internal singularities exist.
- **KTU-Typical Applications:** Complex arc welding, automotive painting, machining, polishing, complex assembly.

### **Tier 5: 7-Axis Robots (Redundant Manipulators)**
- **Configuration:** 6 standard axes + 1 redundant elbow or shoulder joint.
- **Strengths:** Can "bend around" obstacles, reach into tight cavities (e.g., automotive interior), reconfigure posture for human-robot collaboration.
- **Weaknesses:** Most expensive; requires advanced inverse-kinematics solvers (e.g., Jacobian pseudo-inverse).
- **KTU-Typical Applications:** Aerospace drilling, surgical robotics, polishing of car bodies, complex bin-picking.

### **Tier 6: Specialized High-Axis Robots (8 to 13 Axes)**
- Two robot arms mounted on a single rail or mobile platform (e.g., dual-arm mobile manipulators).
- **KTU-Typical Applications:** Service robotics, space manipulation, advanced manufacturing cells.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **must-know** for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions in the KTU End Semester Examination.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Max DOF for a rigid body in 3D | $DOF_{max} = 3T + 3R$ | T = translation, R = rotation; max value = 6 |
| 2 | Relationship: Axes & DOF (non-redundant) | $DOF = n$ | where $n$ = number of axes (for $n \leq 6$) |
| 3 | Redundancy | $r = n - 6$ | $r > 0$ means kinematically redundant |
| 4 | Workspace Volume (Spherical) | $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$ | $R$ = maximum reach (m); units = m³ |
| 5 | Workspace Surface Area | $A = 4\pi R^{2}$ | Units = m² |
| 6 | Reach Constraint | $R_{min} \leq d \leq R_{max}$ | $d$ = distance from base to wrist center |
| 7 | Orientation Dextrous Workspace | All orientations reachable | Only for 6+ axes (full 3D) |
| 8 | Position-only Workspace | No orientation | Achievable with 3 axes |
| 9 | Singularity Condition (planar 2R) | $\sin\theta_{2} = 0$ | Joints 2 fully extended/folded |
| 10 | Jacobian Singularity (general) | $\det(J) = 0$ | Robot loses DOF locally |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a question says *"the tool must approach the workpiece from any angle"*, your answer must immediately be **6 axes**. If it adds *"reach around an obstacle"*, upgrade to **7 axes**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The "Number of Axes" decision directly impacts:
- **Capital Expenditure (CAPEX):** A 6-axis robot costs 3–4× a Cartesian gantry of similar reach.
- **Cycle Time:** Fewer axes = simpler motion planning = higher throughput for repetitive tasks.
- **Programming Complexity:** Each additional axis multiplies inverse-kinematics solving time.
- **Maintenance Cost:** More servo motors, more encoders, more cables to maintain.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why a Rigid Body Needs Exactly 6 Parameters

A rigid body in 3D space is fully described by a position vector and an orientation matrix. Let us prove the requirement of 6 independent parameters.

**Step 1 — Position Vector:**
The position of a point in 3D Cartesian space is given by three independent coordinates:

$$
\vec{p} = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Position Parameters:**
This vector has $3$ independent scalar components. Therefore, **3 translation parameters** are required to locate the body.

**Step 3 — Orientation Requirement:**
Orientation in 3D cannot be represented by a vector — it requires a rotation matrix $R \in SO(3)$, where $SO(3)$ is the Special Orthogonal Group of dimension 3:

$$
R = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & r_{13} \\ r_{21} & r_{22} & r_{23} \\ r_{31} & r_{32} & r_{33} \end{bmatrix}, \quad R^{T}R = I, \quad \det(R) = +1
$$

**Step 4 — Count Independent Orientation Parameters:**
The rotation matrix has $9$ entries, but it is constrained by 6 equations (3 from orthogonality of rows + 3 from orthogonality of columns — but only 6 are independent). Hence, independent parameters:

$$
n_{orient} = 9 - 6 = 3
$$

**Step 5 — Classical Euler Angle Interpretation:**
The 3 independent orientation parameters are commonly expressed as roll-pitch-yaw $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ or Z-Y-X Euler angles. So orientation requires exactly **3 rotation parameters**.

**Step 6 — Total DOF Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
DOF_{total} &= DOF_{translation} + DOF_{rotation} \\
&= 3 + 3 \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A rigid body in 3D space has exactly **6 DOF**. Therefore, to fully position and arbitrarily orient an end-effector, a robot **must have at least 6 independent controllable axes**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Workspace Volume of a Spherical-Reach Robot

For a robot with a maximum reach $R$ (sum of all link lengths), the boundary of the reachable workspace (assuming an idealized spherical envelope) is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Surface Element in Spherical Coordinates:**
A differential surface area on a sphere of radius $r$ is:

$$
dA = r^{2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi
$$

where $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ is the polar angle and $\phi \in [0, 2\pi]$ is the azimuthal angle.

**Step 2 — Total Surface Area of Reach Boundary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} R^{2} \sin\theta \, d\theta \, d\phi \\
&= R^{2} \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta \\
&= R^{2} \cdot (2\pi) \cdot [-\cos\theta]_{0}^{\pi} \\
&= R^{2} \cdot (2\pi) \cdot (1 - (-1)) \\
&= 4\pi R^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Workspace Volume (if the envelope is a solid sphere of radius $R$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \sin\theta \, dr \, d\theta \, d\phi \\
&= \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\phi \int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\theta \, d\theta \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} dr \\
&= (2\pi)(2)\left[\frac{r^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{R} \\
&= \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A robot with link-length sum $R$ can theoretically access a workspace volume of $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$. In practice, the *dexterous workspace* (where any orientation is also achievable) is much smaller — often only **20–30%** of this total reachable volume.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Minimum Number of Axes for Full Pose

A "pose" = position + orientation. The minimum number of independent robot axes required to achieve any arbitrary pose is computed as:

$$
n_{min} = 3 \;(\text{position}) + 3 \;(\text{orientation}) = 6
$$

**Decision Matrix for KTU Selection Problems:**

| Application | Min. Axes Justification | Recommended Axes |
|---|---|---|
| Conveyor pick-and-place (top-down only) | 3 translation only | 3 |
| Horizontal plane assembly | 3 in-plane + 1 vertical = 4 | 4 (SCARA) |
| Spray painting on flat panels | 3 position + 1 rotation = 4 | 4–5 |
| Arc welding on a pipe | 5 (cannot fully roll tool with 4) | 5–6 |
| Spot welding on a car body | Full pose needed + obstacles | 6 |
| Trimming plastic car bumper | 6 + reach around edges | 6 |
| Sanding an engine block cavity | Reach inside concave geometry | 7 (redundant) |

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Singularity and Dexterity vs. Axis Count

| Axis Count | Redundancy $(r = n-6)$ | Singularity Risk | Dexterity Index $\eta$ (qualitative) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 3 | $-3$ | Very Low | Low (no orientation) |
| 4 | $-2$ | Low | Low–Medium |
| 5 | $-1$ | Medium | Medium |
| 6 | $0$ | Medium–High | High |
| 7 | $+1$ | Low (self-avoidance) | Very High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surprise Insight:** A 7-axis robot has *less* singularity risk than a 6-axis robot because the extra joint lets the controller "route around" singular configurations, similar to how a human arm can shift its elbow to keep the hand steady.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Axis Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Define Application] --> B{Does task need tool orientation change?}
    B -- NO --> C{Tool approach only top-down?}
    C -- YES --> D[3-AXIS Cartesian or Gantry]
    C -- NO --> E{Task in horizontal plane only?}
    E -- YES --> F[4-AXIS SCARA]
    E -- NO --> G[5-AXIS Arm]
    B -- YES --> H{Any tool roll required?}
    H -- NO --> I[5-AXIS Arm]
    H -- YES --> J{Must reach around obstacles?}
    J -- NO --> K[6-AXIS Articulated Arm]
    J -- YES --> L[7-AXIS Redundant Arm]
    D --> M[END: Document Selection]
    F --> M
    G --> M
    I --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Axis Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph P[POSITIONING SUBSYSTEM - Major Axes 1, 2, 3]
        A1[Axis 1: Base Rotation] --> A2[Axis 2: Shoulder Pitch]
        A2 --> A3[Axis 3: Elbow Pitch]
    end
    subgraph W[ORIENTATION SUBSYSTEM - Wrist Axes 4, 5, 6]
        A3 --> A4[Axis 4: Wrist Roll]
        A4 --> A5[Axis 5: Wrist Pitch]
        A5 --> A6[Axis 6: Tool Roll]
    end
    subgraph R[REDUNDANCY SUBSYSTEM - Axis 7 if present]
        A6 --> A7[Axis 7: Redundant Elbow / Track]
    end
    A7 --> EE[End Effector / Tool]
    A6 --> EE
    style P fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4
    style W fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#FF7F0E
```
