```mermaid
    style EE fill:#FFE6E6,stroke:#D62728
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Application-to-Axis Mapping

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> AppAnalysis
    AppAnalysis --> Cartesian3: 3 prismatic
    AppAnalysis --> Scara4: 3 in plane plus 1 vertical
    AppAnalysis --> Arm5: 3 position plus 2 wrist
    AppAnalysis --> Arm6: 3 position plus 3 wrist
    AppAnalysis --> Arm7: 6 standard plus 1 redundant
    Cartesian3 --> [*]: Pick and Place
    Scara4 --> [*]: Palletizing / Insertion
    Arm5 --> [*]: Simple Welding
    Arm6 --> [*]: Complex Welding / Painting
    Arm7 --> [*]: Aerospace / Surgical
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Define the term 'Number of Axes' of a robot. How is it related to the Degrees of Freedom (DOF) of a manipulator?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The *Number of Axes* of a robot is the count of independent, programmable, rotational or translational motions (joints) that the manipulator can perform. Each axis is typically driven by a dedicated actuator and represents one line of controlled motion.
- **Relationship (1 mark):** For a non-redundant robot ($\text{axes} \leq 6$), the number of axes equals the Degrees of Freedom (DOF). For a redundant robot ($\text{axes} > 6$), the redundancy is given by $r = n - 6$, where $n$ is the number of axes.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between a 4-axis SCARA robot and a 6-axis articulated robot with respect to workspace and applications."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | 4-Axis SCARA | 6-Axis Articulated |
|---|---|---|
| Axis Composition | 3 in-plane revolute + 1 vertical prismatic | 3 positioning + 3 wrist axes |
| Workspace | Cylindrical (flat disc shape) | Spherical (irregular 3D lobe) |
| Orientation Capability | Rotation about vertical axis only | Full spatial orientation |
| Typical Application | Pick-and-place, screw insertion | Welding, painting, complex assembly |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU End Semester Examinations provide **internal choice** in Part B. You must answer **either Option A OR Option B in full**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the classification of robot axes into **major axes** and **wrist axes**. Why is it said that the first three axes *position* the wrist while the last three *orient* the end-effector?

**(b) [7 marks]** An automotive manufacturer needs to select a robot for **arc welding** the chassis of a car. Compare 5-axis, 6-axis, and 7-axis robots for this application. Recommend the most suitable configuration with justification.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A 6-axis articulated robot is logically divided into two sub-systems:

1. **Major Axes (Axes 1, 2, 3):** These three joints connect the robot base to the wrist center. They form the *positioning sub-system* because they collectively place the wrist center at any desired point $(x, y, z)$ in the work envelope.
2. **Wrist Axes (Axes 4, 5, 6):** These three joints are concentrated at the wrist and form the *orientation sub-system*. They rotate the end-effector (tool) about the wrist center to achieve the desired roll-pitch-yaw $(\phi, \theta, \psi)$ orientation.

**Why this division (2 marks):**
By decoupling positioning (a translation problem in $\mathbb{R}^3$) from orientation (a rotation problem in $SO(3)$), the inverse kinematics can be solved in two simpler stages:
- **Stage 1:** Solve for the 3 major-axis joint angles using the wrist-center coordinates.
- **Stage 2:** Solve for the 3 wrist-axis joint angles using Euler-angle decomposition.

This is the foundation of the **Pieper's solution** (1968) for 6-DOF manipulators with a spherical wrist.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

| Criterion | 5-Axis | 6-Axis | 7-Axis |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tool roll about its axis | ❌ Limited | ✅ Full | ✅ Full |
| Reach around chassis obstructions | ❌ Poor | ⚠️ Fair | ✅ Excellent |
| Cycle time | ✅ Faster | ⚠️ Moderate | ❌ Slowest |
| Cost | ✅ Lowest | ⚠️ Mid | ❌ Highest |
| Programming complexity | ✅ Low | ⚠️ Moderate | ❌ High |

**Recommendation (3 marks):** A **6-axis articulated robot** is recommended for chassis arc welding because:
1. The welding torch must approach the joints from multiple angles (requires full 6 DOF).
2. The car body has internal cavities; a 6-axis arm can reach most of them.
3. If the chassis has complex tube structures that require the arm to *reach through* the frame, a **7-axis** variant should be considered for obstacle avoidance.
4. 5-axis is rejected because it cannot provide continuous tool roll, which is needed for circumferential pipe welding.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A factory needs to automate the **palletizing of cardboard boxes** onto a wooden pallet. Each box weighs 8 kg, and the pick/place coordinates are fixed. The conveyor is 1.2 m wide, the pallet is 1.5 m away. Discuss:
- (i) The minimum number of axes required.
- (ii) Justify the choice of a **4-axis SCARA** over a **6-axis articulated** robot for this task.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the maximum workspace volume of a robot with link lengths $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m. State the units. What is the *dextrous workspace* and how does it differ from the *reachable workspace*?

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Minimum axes = 4 (2 marks):**
- The boxes are picked from a fixed height on the conveyor (axis 3 vertical lift).
- They are placed onto a fixed grid on the pallet (axes 1 & 2 horizontal motion in the X–Y plane).
- Orientation of the box is irrelevant (a square box has rotational symmetry), so no extra wrist axes are needed.
- Hence, **4 axes suffice**.

**(ii) SCARA vs 6-Axis Articulated (5 marks):**

| Factor | 4-Axis SCARA | 6-Axis Articulated |
|---|---|---|
| Cycle Speed | ✅ Up to 200 picks/min | ❌ Slower (~60 picks/min) |
| Cost | ✅ ₹ 4–6 lakh | ❌ ₹ 15–25 lakh |
| Accuracy in vertical insertion | ✅ Excellent (rigid prismatic Z-axis) | ⚠️ Lower (compliance issues) |
| Programming Complexity | ✅ Simple (2D pick pattern) | ❌ Complex 3D trajectory |
| Suitability for Palletizing | ✅ Ideal | ❌ Over-engineered |

**Verdict:** A SCARA is the engineering-optimal choice — lower cost, higher speed, sufficient DOF.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Total reach $R$ is the sum of link lengths:

$$
R = L_1 + L_2 = 0.5 + 0.4 = 0.9 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2:** Maximum workspace volume (assuming a spherical envelope):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{max} &= \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3} \\
&= \frac{4}{3}\pi (0.9)^{3} \\
&= \frac{4}{3}\pi (0.729) \\
&= 0.972\pi \\
&\approx 3.053 \text{ m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Units: $\text{m}^{3}$ (cubic meters).

**Step 4 (Dextrous vs Reachable Workspace — 2 marks):**
- **Reachable Workspace:** The set of *all points* the wrist center can physically reach. It forms a sphere of radius $R = 0.9$ m, giving $V \approx 3.053$ m³.
- **Dextrous Workspace:** The set of points *where the end-effector can be arbitrarily oriented* (i.e., achieve any roll-pitch-yaw). This is a much smaller region, typically a sphere of radius $\vert R - L_1 \vert = 0.4$ m, giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{dex} &= \frac{4}{3}\pi (0.4)^{3} \\
&= \frac{4}{3}\pi (0.064) \\
&\approx 0.268 \text{ m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

The dextrous workspace is approximately **8.8%** of the reachable workspace — a key KTU comparison point.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> 1. **Conflating "Axes" with "DOF"** (–1 mark): Axes are *physical*; DOF is *mathematical*. A 6-axis robot has 6 DOF; a 7-axis robot has 6 task-space DOF and 1 redundant joint-space DOF.
> 2. **Forgetting the "Why"** (–1 mark): When asked to select axis count, you MUST cite the *application requirement* (orientation / obstacle / cycle time), not just the number.
> 3. **Skipping the 2D vs 3D reasoning** (–1 mark): Always explicitly state whether the task is planar (3–4 axes) or spatial (5–6 axes).
> 4. **Unit Errors in Workspace Volume** (–0.5 marks): Reach is in *meters (m)*, workspace volume is in *cubic meters (m³)*, surface area is in *square meters (m²)*. Mixing these loses marks.
> 5. **Omitting the "redundancy" definition for 7-axis questions** (–1 mark): Always write: "Redundancy $r = n - 6 = 1$, giving one extra internal degree of freedom to avoid obstacles/singularities."

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ **Axis = Joint = One independent motor-driven motion** (rotational or translational).
- ✅ A **rigid body in 3D has 6 DOF** (3 translation + 3 rotation) — this is the *theoretical minimum* for full pose control.
- ✅ **3 axes** → Cartesian / Gantry → pick-and-place, CNC tending.
- ✅ **4 axes** → SCARA → palletizing, screw insertion, packaging.
- ✅ **5 axes** → simple arc welding, glue dispensing (no full tool roll).
- ✅ **6 axes** → industry workhorse → complex welding, painting, assembly.
- ✅ **7 axes** → redundant → aerospace, surgical, obstacle-rich cells.
- ✅ **Major axes (1–3)** *position* the wrist center; **Wrist axes (4–6)** *orient* the end-effector.
- ✅ **Redundancy:** $r = n - 6$ (positive means kinematically redundant).
- ✅ **Workspace Volume:** $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$ where $R$ is the total reach.
- ✅ **Dextrous workspace ⊂ Reachable workspace** (typically 20–30% of total volume).
- ✅ **Singularity:** $\det(J) = 0$ — robot momentarily loses a DOF.
- ✅ **7-axis robots have LOWER singularity risk** than 6-axis (extra joint allows reconfiguration).
- ✅ **Selection rule of thumb (KTU):** Match axis count to *minimum DOF needed* — over-specifying wastes cost; under-specifying fails the application.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Work Volume
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Work Volume

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Work Volume (Workspace):** The **work volume** of a robot manipulator is defined as the **locus (set) of all points in three-dimensional space that the end-effector (or tool tip) of the robot can reach**, given the physical joint limits, link lengths, and mechanical constraints of the manipulator structure.

It is also commonly referred to in literature as the **Reach Envelope**, **Working Envelope**, or simply the **Workspace** of the robot.

## 1.2 Sub-Categories of Work Volume

A robotic work volume is sub-divided into two mathematically distinct regions:

| # | Sub-Type | Rigorous Definition |
|---|----------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Reachable Workspace (W<sub>R</sub>)** | The set of all points in space that the end-effector (TCP - Tool Centre Point) can reach in **at least one orientation** of the wrist/end-effector. |
| 2 | **Dexterous Workspace (W<sub>D</sub>)** | The set of all points in space that the end-effector can reach in **all possible orientations**. Mathematically: **W<sub>D</sub> ⊂ W<sub>R</sub>** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sub-Space Rule:** The *Dexterous Workspace* is always a **proper subset** of the *Reachable Workspace*. For most industrial manipulators (e.g., articulated arms), the dexterous workspace is a much smaller region — often a small "sweet spot" near the geometric centre of the envelope.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Human Arm:**
> Imagine a person standing in a room and stretching out their arm while rotating at the shoulder, elbow, and wrist.
> - The **Reachable Workspace** is like every point in the room that your **fingertip** can ever touch, regardless of how your hand is oriented.
> - The **Dexterous Workspace** is the much smaller region where your fingertip can touch a point with your hand pointing **in every direction** (e.g., reaching into a small box and being able to turn a screw from any angle).
> - The **physical limits of your joints** (shoulder cannot rotate 360°, elbow cannot bend backwards) define the *envelope* of your reach.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics

- **Standard Metric:** Work volume is typically expressed in **cubic metres (m³)** or **cubic millimetres (mm³)** depending on the robot scale.
- **Reach Radius (R):** Maximum straight-line distance from the base joint to the end-effector — measured in **millimetres (mm)** or **metres (m)**.
- **Number of Joints (n):** The number of **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** determines the topology (shape) of the envelope.
- **Payload (W):** Measured in **kilograms (kg)** or **Newtons (N)** — does *not* alter the work volume but affects usable reach.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Focus:** Examiners frequently test the *distinction* between **Reachable** and **Dexterous** workspaces — this is a **2-mark sure-shot** question if framed properly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Influencing Work Volume

The work volume of a robot is determined by **four** primary engineering factors:

1. **Robot Configuration / Kinematic Structure** (PPP, RPP, RRP, RRR …) — the most dominant factor.
2. **Link Lengths** (l<sub>1</sub>, l<sub>2</sub>, l<sub>3</sub> …) — physical dimensions of the rigid links.
3. **Joint Limits** (θ<sub>min</sub>, θ<sub>max</sub> for revolute; d<sub>min</sub>, d<sub>max</sub> for prismatic) — mechanical stops and cabling limits.
4. **End-Effector Dimensions** — the size of the gripper/wrist assembly reduces usable volume.

## 2.2 Classification of Work Volume by Robot Configuration

### A. Cartesian / Rectangular Robot (PPP) — *Gantry Configuration*
- **Structure:** Three mutually perpendicular **prismatic (P)** joints.
- **Envelope Shape:** A perfect **rectangular cuboid (box)**.
- **Engineering Example:** Pick-and-place gantries, CNC bridges, 3D printers.
- **Characteristic:** No internal singularities; uniform reachability throughout the box.

### B. Cylindrical Robot (RPP) — *Post-style*
- **Structure:** One **revolute (R)** base joint + two **prismatic (P)** joints.
- **Envelope Shape:** A portion of a **hollow cylinder** (annular cylindrical shell).
- **Engineering Example:** Early Unimate robots, foundry loading robots.
- **Characteristic:** Inner cylindrical hole (radius r<sub>min</sub>) is unreachable.

### C. Spherical / Polar Robot (RRP)
- **Structure:** Two **revolute (R)** joints + one **prismatic (P)** joint.
- **Envelope Shape:** A portion of a **hollow sphere (spherical sector)**.
- **Engineering Example:** Unimate 2000 series, early painting robots.
- **Characteristic:** Wedge-shaped or hemispherical envelope with an unreachable central cone.

### D. Articulated / Jointed-Angle Robot (RRR)
- **Structure:** Three **revolute (R)** joints in series (shoulder, elbow, wrist equivalent).
- **Envelope Shape:** A **complex, approximately spherical** region with internal voids.
- **Engineering Example:** PUMA, KUKA KR series, ABB IRB, FANUC R-2000.
- **Characteristic:** Largest dexterous-to-reachable ratio for general-purpose work.

### E. SCARA Robot (RRP)
- **Structure:** Two **revolute (R)** joints + one **prismatic (P)** joint — all axes vertical/parallel.
- **Envelope Shape:** A **cylindrical** envelope with a height equal to the prismatic stroke.
- **Engineering Example:** Epson SCARA, Adept Cobra — used in PCB assembly.

### F. Parallel Robot (e.g., Delta, Stewart)
- **Structure:** Multiple kinematic chains connecting base to platform.
- **Envelope Shape:** A small **dome / regular octahedral** region.
- **Engineering Example:** Delta pickers in food packaging; Stewart platform in flight simulators.
- **Characteristic:** Very **small** work volume but extremely **high stiffness and speed**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM CHEAT SHEET — Standard Work Volume Equations**

| Robot Type | Joint Notation | Envelope Geometry | Volume Formula | Boundary Conditions |
|------------|----------------|-------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Cartesian (PPP)** | P–P–P | Rectangular Box | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | $L_x, L_y, L_z$ = stroke lengths along X, Y, Z |
| **Cylindrical (RPP)** | R–P–P | Hollow Cylinder (annulus extruded) | $V = \pi \left(R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2}\right) \cdot H$ | $R_{max}$ = outer reach, $R_{min}$ = inner reach, $H$ = vertical stroke |
| **Spherical (RRP)** | R–R–P | Spherical Sector | $V = \dfrac{2}{3}\,\pi\,R^{3}\left(1 - \cos\alpha\right)$ | $\alpha$ = half-angle of polar sweep, $R$ = max reach |
| **Full Sphere** | — | Complete Sphere | $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\,\pi\,R^{3}$ | Idealised upper bound |
| **Articulated (RRR)** | R–R–R | Irregular (≈ sphere) | Numerical / Monte-Carlo integration | Complex closed-form unavailable |
| **SCARA (RRP)** | R–R–P | Cylinder | $V = \pi R^{2} \cdot H$ | $R = l_1 + l_2$, $H$ = prismatic stroke |
| **Parallel (Delta)** | R–R–R–P | Dome | $V \approx \dfrac{2}{3}\,\pi\,r^{2}\,h$ | $r$ = dome radius, $h$ = dome height |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** Throughout this sheet, $R$ denotes maximum reach, $H$ denotes vertical (or axial) stroke, and the units throughout are in **metres (m)** yielding volume in **cubic metres (m³)**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The work volume dictates **robot selection** for any industrial task. Engineers must verify that the *entire task space* (e.g., welding path on a car chassis, painting trajectory on an aircraft fuselage) lies **inside** the reachable workspace, and ideally within the **dexterous** workspace to avoid kinematic singularities and suboptimal wrist configurations.

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Practice:** A factory layout engineer will overlay the robot's 2D work envelope (top-view) onto the workstation CAD drawing to verify reachability **before** installing the manipulator.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Work Volume of a Cartesian (PPP) Robot

**Problem Setup:** A Cartesian robot has:
- X-axis stroke: $L_x$ (from $x_{min}$ to $x_{max}$)
- Y-axis stroke: $L_y$ (from $y_{min}$ to $y_{max}$)
- Z-axis stroke: $L_z$ (from $z_{min}$ to $z_{max}$)

**Derivation Logic:**

The work volume is the set of all $(x, y, z)$ triples such that each coordinate lies within its joint limits. The boundary forms a closed rectangular box with independent sides.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cartesian} &= \int_{x_{min}}^{x_{max}} \int_{y_{min}}^{y_{max}} \int_{z_{min}}^{z_{max}} dx\, dy\, dz \\[4pt]
&= \left[\,x\,\right]_{x_{min}}^{x_{max}} \cdot \left[\,y\,\right]_{y_{min}}^{y_{max}} \cdot \left[\,z\,\right]_{z_{min}}^{z_{max}} \\[4pt]
&= (x_{max} - x_{min}) \cdot (y_{max} - y_{min}) \cdot (z_{max} - z_{min}) \\[4pt]
&= L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Since all three axes are independent prismatic joints, the volume factorises into a simple product of stroke lengths.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Work Volume of a Cylindrical (RPP) Robot

**Problem Setup:** A cylindrical robot has:
- Radial reach: $r \in [R_{min},\, R_{max}]$
- Vertical reach: $z \in [0,\, H]$
- Angular reach: $\theta \in [0,\, 2\pi]$ (full revolution)

**Derivation Logic:**

In cylindrical coordinates, the differential volume element is $dV = r \, dr \, d\theta \, dz$. We integrate over the three independent coordinate ranges.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{cyl} &= \int_{0}^{H} \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{R_{min}}^{R_{max}} r\, dr\, d\theta\, dz \\[4pt]
&= \int_{0}^{H} dz \;\cdot\; \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta \;\cdot\; \int_{R_{min}}^{R_{max}} r\, dr \\[4pt]
&= \left[\,z\,\right]_{0}^{H} \;\cdot\; \left[\,\theta\,\right]_{0}^{2\pi} \;\cdot\; \left[\,\dfrac{r^{2}}{2}\,\right]_{R_{min}}^{R_{max}} \\[4pt]
&= H \cdot 2\pi \cdot \dfrac{1}{2}\left(R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \pi \left(R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2}\right) H
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Boundary Case:** If the robot can swing fully to the centre axis (i.e., $R_{min} = 0$), the volume reduces to the **solid cylinder**: $V = \pi R^{2} H$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Work Volume of a Spherical/Polar (RRP) Robot

**Problem Setup:** A polar robot has:
- Maximum radial reach: $R$
- Polar (vertical tilt) angle: $\phi \in [\phi_{min},\, \phi_{max}]$
- Azimuthal angle: $\theta \in [0,\, 2\pi]$

**Derivation Logic:**

The differential volume element in spherical coordinates is $dV = r^{2} \sin\phi \, dr \, d\phi \, d\theta$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sph} &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{\phi_{min}}^{\phi_{max}} \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \sin\phi \; dr\, d\phi\, d\theta \\[4pt]
&= \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta \;\cdot\; \int_{\phi_{min}}^{\phi_{max}} \sin\phi \, d\phi \;\cdot\; \int_{0}^{R} r^{2}\, dr \\[4pt]
&= 2\pi \;\cdot\; \left[\,-\cos\phi\,\right]_{\phi_{min}}^{\phi_{max}} \;\cdot\; \left[\,\dfrac{r^{3}}{3}\,\right]_{0}^{R} \\[4pt]
&= 2\pi \cdot \left(\cos\phi_{min} - \cos\phi_{max}\right) \cdot \dfrac{R^{3}}{3} \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{2\pi R^{3}}{3}\left(\cos\phi_{min} - \cos\phi_{max}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Special Case 1 — Full Sphere:** If $\phi_{min} = 0$ and $\phi_{max} = \pi$, we obtain $V = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$ ✓
>
> **Special Case 2 — Hemispherical Envelope:** If $\phi \in [0, \pi/2]$, we obtain $V = \dfrac{2}{3}\pi R^{3}$

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Robot Type | Joints | Envelope Shape | Inner Dead Zone? | Dexterous Subset? | Best Application |
|------------|--------|----------------|------------------|-------------------|------------------|
| Cartesian (PPP) | 3P | Rectangular box | No | Almost all of it (orientation is fixed) | Pick-place, CNC |
| Cylindrical (RPP) | 1R+2P | Hollow cylinder | Yes — central core | Outer ring only | Foundry, machine tending |
| Spherical (RRP) | 2R+1P | Spherical sector | Yes — central cone | Outer shell | Die casting, painting |
| Articulated (RRR) | 3R | Irregular spherical | Yes — near base | Small central region | Welding, assembly |
| SCARA (RRP) | 2R+1P | Cylinder (flat) | Yes — base pillar | Outer cylindrical band | PCB assembly |
| Parallel (RRR) | 3R + struts | Small dome | No central hole (obstructed top) | Very small | High-speed pick-place |

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The presence (or absence) of a *dead zone* in the work envelope is determined by the number of prismatic joints. **More prismatic joints ⇒ more straightforward (Cartesian-like) envelopes; more revolute joints ⇒ larger dexterous workspaces but with internal singularities.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture: Work Volume Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["ROBOT WORK VOLUME W"] --> B["Subdivision 1 Reachability"]
    A --> C["Subdivision 2 Kinematic Type"]

    B --> B1["Reachable Workspace WR"]
    B --> B2["Dexterous Workspace WD"]
    B1 -.->|"WR is a superset of WD"| B2

    C --> C1["Prismatic Dominant"]
    C --> C2["Revolute Dominant"]
    C --> C3["Mixed Hybrid"]

    C1 --> C1A["Cartesian PPP - Box"]
    C1 --> C1B["Cylindrical RPP - Cylinder"]
    C3 --> C3A["Spherical RRP - Sphere"]
    C3 --> C3B["SCARA RRP - Flat Cylinder"]
    C2 --> C2A["Articulated RRR - Irregular"]
    C2 --> C2B["Parallel Delta - Dome"]
```

## 4.2 Work Envelope Comparison (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CART["CARTESIAN PPP"]
        CART_A["Prismatic X"] --> CART_B["Prismatic Y"] --> CART_C["Prismatic Z"] --> CART_D["Tool Centre Point"]
    end
    subgraph CYL["CYLINDRICAL RPP"]
        CYL_A["Revolute Base Theta"] --> CYL_B["Prismatic Radial R"] --> CYL_C["Prismatic Vertical Z"] --> CYL_D["Tool Centre Point"]
    end
    subgraph POL["SPHERICAL RRP"]
        POL_A["Revolute Base Theta"] --> POL_B["Revolute Shoulder Phi"] --> POL_C["Prismatic Radial R"] --> POL_D["Tool Centre Point"]
    end
    subgraph ART["ARTICULATED RRR"]
        ART_A["Revolute Waist"] --> ART_B["Revolute Shoulder"] --> ART_C["Revolute Elbow"] --> ART_D["Tool Centre Point"]
    end
    subgraph SCA["SCARA RRP"]
        SCA_A["Revolute Joint 1"] --> SCA_B["Revolute Joint 2"] --> SCA_C["Prismatic Z Vertical"] --> SCA_D["Tool Centre Point"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Reachability Decision Flow (Application Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Select Robot for Task"] --> Q1{"Task path is rectangular?"}
    Q1 -->|YES| A["Use Cartesian PPP Robot"]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{"Task is around vertical axis with axial reach?"}
    Q2 -->|YES| B["Use Cylindrical RPP Robot"]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{"Task requires hemispherical reach?"}
    Q3 -->|YES| C["Use Spherical RRP Robot"]
    Q3 -->|NO| Q4{"Task needs high dexterity in 3D space?"}
    Q4 -->|YES| D["Use Articulated RRR Robot"]
    Q4 -->|NO| Q5{"Task is planar assembly with vertical insertion?"}
    Q5 -->|YES| E["Use SCARA RRP Robot"]
    Q5 -->|NO| F["Use Parallel Delta Robot for high-speed pick-place"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These schematics represent the **functional joint topology** of each robot architecture. Physical drawings (showing 3D envelope shapes) are best memorised using the **geometric primitive** associated with each configuration: **box → cylinder → sphere → irregular sphere → flat cylinder → dome**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q: Define work volume of a robot. Differentiate between reachable workspace and dexterous workspace.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> *Definition (1 Mark):* The work volume (or workspace) of a robot manipulator is the **set of all points in 3D space that the end-effector or tool centre point can reach** within the physical limits imposed by the joint constraints and link dimensions of the robot.
>
> *Reachable Workspace (1 Mark):* The **reachable workspace (W<sub>R</sub>)** is the region in space where the end-effector can be positioned in **at least one orientation**. It represents the **largest** envelope of the robot.
>
> *Dexterous Workspace (1 Mark):* The **dexterous workspace (W<sub>D</sub>)** is the sub-region where the end-effector can be positioned in **all possible orientations** about that point. It is always a **subset** of the reachable workspace, i.e., $W_D \subseteq W_R$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q: List any four types of robot configurations with their corresponding work envelope shapes.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
>
> | # | Robot Configuration | Joint Notation | Work Envelope Shape |
> |---|---------------------|----------------|---------------------|
> | 1 | Cartesian (Gantry) | P–P–P | Rectangular Box |
> | 2 | Cylindrical | R–P–P | Hollow Cylinder |
> | 3 | Spherical / Polar | R–R–P | Spherical Sector |
> | 4 | Articulated (Jointed) | R–R–R | Irregular Spherical |
> | 5 *(bonus)* | SCARA | R–R–P | Flat Cylinder |
>
> *(Any four correctly stated = 3 marks. Each row = 0.75 mark.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
>
> **Common mistakes students make that cost marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting joint limit boundaries** — e.g., writing $V = \pi R^2 H$ for a cylindrical robot without specifying that the inner radius $R_{min}$ is non-zero (the central dead-zone must be subtracted).
> 2. **Confusing reachable and dexterous workspaces** — these are **not synonyms**; a mark is deducted for using them interchangeably.
> 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers** — always end numerical solutions with the **unit** (m³ or mm³). KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark for missing units.
> 4. **Omitting diagrams** — for a 7-mark sub-question, a neat labelled diagram is **mandatory**. Allocate ~2 marks' worth of effort to the figure.
> 5. **Joint notation errors** — confusing R (Revolute) with P (Prismatic) in configuration naming.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choose this OR Question B**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question A]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, derive the mathematical expression for the work volume of a **cylindrical robot configuration**. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cylindrical robot has an outer radial reach of **1.2 m**, an inner radial dead-zone of **0.4 m**, and a vertical stroke of **1.5 m**. Compute its **(i)** work volume and **(ii)** the percentage of volume that is unreachable due to the inner dead-zone. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation — Cylindrical Robot Work Volume (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
- The base revolute joint sweeps through a full $2\pi$ radians ($360°$).
- The end-effector is treated as a point mass at the TCP.
- The inner radius $R_{min}$ exists because the elbow/forearm cannot fold into the central axis.
- Vertical stroke is constant over the full rotation (no gravitational sag).

**Diagram Description (2 Marks):**
- Draw the cylindrical envelope: outer radius $R_{max}$, inner radius $R_{min}$, height $H$, and the base revolute axis.
- Label the three joints: $\theta$ (base), $r$ (radial), $z$ (vertical).

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

In cylindrical coordinates, the differential volume element is $dV = r\, dr\, d\theta\, dz$. The work volume is the triple integral over the reachable range:

$$
V = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{H} \int_{R_{min}}^{R_{max}} r\, dr\, dz\, d\theta
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta \;\cdot\; \int_{0}^{H} dz \;\cdot\; \int_{R_{min}}^{R_{max}} r\, dr \\[4pt]
&= \left[\,\theta\,\right]_{0}^{2\pi} \cdot \left[\,z\,\right]_{0}^{H} \cdot \left[\,\frac{r^{2}}{2}\,\right]_{R_{min}}^{R_{max}} \\[4pt]
&= 2\pi \cdot H \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left(R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \pi \left(R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2}\right) H \quad \left[\text{in } m^{3}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

**Given (0.5 Mark):**
- $R_{max} = 1.2\ \text{m}$, $R_{min} = 0.4\ \text{m}$, $H = 1.5\ \text{m}$

**(i) Work Volume Calculation (4 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{net} &= \pi \left(R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2}\right) H \\[4pt]
&= \pi \left(1.2^{2} - 0.4^{2}\right) \times 1.5 \\[4pt]
&= \pi \left(1.44 - 0.16\right) \times 1.5 \\[4pt]
&= \pi \times 1.28 \times 1.5 \\[4pt]
&= \pi \times 1.92 \\[4pt]
&= 6.0319\ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step:**
- $R_{max}^{2} = 1.44\ \text{m}^{2}$
- $R_{min}^{2} = 0.16\ \text{m}^{2}$
- Difference $= 1.28\ \text{m}^{2}$
- Multiplied by $H = 1.5\ \text{m}$ → $1.92\ \text{m}^{2} \cdot \text{m} = 1.92\ \text{m}^{3}$ (pre-π)
- Final: $V \approx 6.032\ \text{m}^{3}$

**(ii) Unreachable Volume Percentage (2.5 Marks):**

The unreachable volume corresponds to the **inner cylinder** of radius $R_{min}$ and height $H$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{unreachable} &= \pi R_{min}^{2} H = \pi \times 0.16 \times 1.5 = 0.7540\ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total possible volume (if $R_{min} = 0$):

$$
V_{total} = \pi R_{max}^{2} H = \pi \times 1.44 \times 1.5 = 6.7860\ \text{m}^{3}
$$

$$
\text{Unreachable \%} = \frac{V_{unreachable}}{V_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{0.7540}{6.7860} \times 100 \approx 11.11\ \%
$$

**Final Answer (0.5 Mark for stating):** $V_{net} = 6.032\ \text{m}^{3}$; Unreachable region $\approx 11.11\%$ of the total cylindrical envelope.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question B]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the work volume of a **spherical (polar) robot configuration**. Derive the formula for its volume when the polar angle sweeps from $\phi = 0$ to $\phi = \phi_{max}$. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the work volume characteristics of **Cartesian, Cylindrical, Spherical, and Articulated** robot configurations in a **tabular form** with at least **five** comparison parameters. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Spherical Robot — Work Volume (7 Marks):**

**Configuration (1 Mark):** A spherical (polar) robot has the **RRP** joint notation — two revolute joints (base rotation $\theta$, shoulder elevation $\phi$) and one prismatic joint (radial extension $r$).

**Diagram Description (2 Marks):** Draw a hemispherical/spherical sector with the base at the origin, the radial extension along the polar axis, and the elevation angle $\phi$ from the horizontal. Label the unreachable central cone and the outer spherical boundary.

**Derivation (4 Marks):**

Differential volume in spherical coordinates: $dV = r^{2} \sin\phi \, dr \, d\phi \, d\theta$.

$$
V_{sph} = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\phi_{max}} \int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \sin\phi \; dr\, d\phi\, d\theta
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{sph} &= \int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta \cdot \int_{0}^{\phi_{max}} \sin\phi \, d\phi \cdot \int_{0}^{R} r^{2}\, dr \\[4pt]
&= 2\pi \cdot \left[\,-\cos\phi\,\right]_{0}^{\phi_{max}} \cdot \left[\,\frac{r^{3}}{3}\,\right]_{0}^{R} \\[4pt]
&= 2\pi \cdot \left(1 - \cos\phi_{max}\right) \cdot \frac{R^{3}}{3} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2\pi R^{3}}{3}\left(1 - \cos\phi_{max}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Special Case Check (built into 4-mark derivation):** If $\phi_{max} = \pi/2$ (hemisphere), then $V = \frac{2\pi R^{3}}{3}\left(1 - 0\right) = \frac{2\pi R^{3}}{3}$. If $\phi_{max} = \pi$ (full sphere), $V = \frac{4\pi R^{3}}{3}$ ✓

**(b) Comparative Table (7 Marks):**

| Parameter | Cartesian (PPP) | Cylindrical (RPP) | Spherical (RRP) | Articulated (RRR) |
|-----------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| **Joint Notation** | P–P–P | R–P–P | R–R–P | R–R–R |
| **Envelope Shape** | Rectangular box | Hollow cylinder | Spherical sector | Irregular sphere |
| **Volume Formula** | $V = L_x L_y L_z$ | $V = \pi(R_o^2 - R_i^2) H$ | $V = \tfrac{2}{3}\pi R^3(1 - \cos\phi)$ | Numerical / complex |
| **Inner Dead Zone** | None | Yes (central core) | Yes (central cone) | Yes (near base) |
| **Dexterity** | Low (fixed orientation) | Moderate | Moderate | **Highest** |
| **Best Application** | CNC, pick-place | Foundry loading | Die casting, painting | Welding, assembly |
| **Mechanical Rigidity** | **Highest** | High | Moderate | Lowest (gravity load) |

*(Each row of well-explained content = ~1 mark. The table alone is 4 marks; add 3 marks for the explanatory paragraphs justifying the comparison.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Work Volume**

- **Definition (Core):** Work volume = locus of all reachable end-effector positions in 3D space, bounded by physical joint limits and link lengths.
- **Two Sub-Types:** Reachable Workspace ($W_R$, ≥1 orientation) ⊃ Dexterous Workspace ($W_D$, all orientations). Always remember: **$W_D \subseteq W_R$**.
- **Joint Notation Rule:** **R = Revolute (rotational), P = Prismatic (linear)**. Memorise the order from base upward.
- **6 Major Configurations:** Cartesian (PPP-box), Cylindrical (RPP-cylinder), Spherical (RRP-sphere), Articulated (RRR-irregular), SCARA (RRP-flat cylinder), Parallel (Delta-dome).
- **Key Volume Formulas to Memorise:**
  - Cartesian: $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$
  - Cylindrical: $V = \pi(R_{max}^{2} - R_{min}^{2}) H$
  - Spherical (sector): $V = \frac{2\pi R^{3}}{3}(1 - \cos\phi_{max})$
  - Full Sphere: $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi R^{3}$
- **Dead Zone Insight:** The **inner unreachable region** is the geometric signature of having prismatic joints in series — it grows as $R_{min}$ increases.
- **Dexterity Insight:** **More revolute joints ⇒ larger dexterous workspace.** Articulated arms have the highest dexterity of all serial configurations.
- **Always Include:** Neat diagram, joint notation, assumptions, units, and final numerical answer with **m³** or **mm³** in every problem.
- **Common Trap:** Do **not** confuse $R$ (radial reach) with $r$ (integration variable) in spherical/cylindrical derivations.
- **Industrial Heuristic:** The robot base is usually **not** placed at the centre of the work cell — it is offset so that the **task workspace** overlaps with the **dexterous** portion of the robot's envelope, not just the reachable boundary.
- **KTU Pattern Recognition:** If the question gives a numerical value of $L_x, L_y, L_z$ → Cartesian. If it gives $R$ and $H$ → Cylindrical. If it gives $R$ and $\phi$ → Spherical. If it gives link lengths $l_1, l_2$ → Articulated (use geometry, not formula).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacity and Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Capacity and Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Defining Capacity in Robotics

In the context of industrial robotics, **Capacity** is a composite performance specification that describes *how much a robot can handle* and *over what volume it can handle it*. It is formally decomposed into three sub-specifications as per the KTU 2024 syllabus:

1. **Payload Capacity (W):** The maximum mass (including the end-effector and the workpiece) that the robot manipulator can carry while still maintaining its rated accuracy, repeatability, and speed throughout its entire workspace. Units: **kg** or **N** (force).
2. **Reach / Arm Length (R):** The maximum radial distance from the base axis to the centre of the end-effector (Tool Centre Point, TCP). Units: **mm** or **m**.
3. **Workspace Volume (V):** The three-dimensional volume swept by the TCP when all joints move through their full mechanical limits. Units: **m³** or **mm³**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Capacity of an industrial robot is the maximum load it can carry, the maximum reach, and the volume of the workspace it can access without sacrificing rated performance.*

### 1.2 Defining Speed in Robotics

**Speed** quantifies *how fast the manipulator executes motion*. It is classified into two hierarchical levels:

1. **Joint Speed (ωᵢ):** The maximum angular velocity attainable at each individual joint *i*. Units: **rad/s** (rotational) or **mm/s** (linear/prismatic).
2. **End-Effector Speed / TCP Velocity (vₜ):** The resultant linear velocity of the Tool Centre Point in Cartesian space. Units: **m/s** or **mm/s**.
3. **Speed of Response:** The time required for the robot to accelerate from rest to its rated operating speed, or to decelerate to rest, governed by actuator torque limits and payload inertia.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cycle Time (T_c):** The total time taken by the robot to complete one full operational sequence (e.g., pick-place-pick). It is the *inverse of productivity* and is the most commercially tracked speed metric.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Human Arm:**
> Think of your own arm. Its **capacity** is the heaviest grocery bag you can hold steadily without your hand shaking (payload), the farthest point on the top shelf you can touch (reach), and the spherical bubble of space your fingers can reach (workspace). Its **speed** is how fast you can snatch a fly from the air (joint speed and TCP speed combined).
>
> Now notice the trade-off: when your arm carries a heavy bag, you cannot swing it as fast as when it is empty. The same physics governs every robot manipulator — **higher payload always reduces the maximum safe operating speed.**

A robot with a high **capacity** but low **speed** is a *warehouse crane*; a robot with high **speed** but low **capacity** is a *pick-and-place Delta bot on a conveyor line*. KTU examiners love this dichotomy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Breakdown of Capacity

The payload capacity is **not a single static number** — it is a function of:

- **Configuration (θ):** A robot can carry more weight when its arm is folded close to the base (short moment arm) and less when fully extended.
- **Direction of load:** Vertical lifting vs horizontal extension places different stresses on actuators.
- **Speed of motion:** At higher speeds, inertial forces reduce effective payload.
- **Duty cycle:** Continuous rated payload is lower than peak short-duration payload.

The mathematical relationship between these effects is captured through the **torque equations** at each joint:

$$\tau_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(\theta)\,\ddot{\theta}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} C_{ijk}(\theta)\,\dot{\theta}_j\dot{\theta}_k + g_i(\theta, W)$$

where the gravity term $g_i$ scales linearly with payload $W$. Hence, any increase in $W$ must be compensated by an equal increase in actuator torque $\tau_i$, or the joint must slow down.

### 2.2 Theoretical Breakdown of Speed

The end-effector velocity vector is mapped from joint velocities through the **Jacobian matrix $J(\theta)$**:

$$\mathbf{v}_t = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{v} \\ \boldsymbol{\omega} \end{bmatrix} = J(\theta)\,\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$$

For a single revolute joint rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ at a link of length $r$, the linear speed of the link tip is:

$$v = \omega \, r \quad \text{[m/s]}$$

**Why this matters:** Even if every joint spins at its rated maximum, the TCP speed *varies inside the workspace* because $r$ (effective lever arm to TCP) changes with configuration. KTU problems frequently test this non-uniform speed distribution.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Mathematical Form | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear TCP speed (single revolute joint) | $v = \omega \, r$ | m/s | $r$ = distance from joint axis to TCP |
| 2 | General TCP velocity | $\mathbf{v}_t = J(\theta)\,\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$ | m/s | $J$ is the Jacobian, $n \times n$ matrix |
| 3 | Joint torque requirement | $\tau_i = M_{ij}\ddot{\theta}_j + g_i(\theta, W)$ | N·m | Gravity term dominates at low speeds |
| 4 | Cycle time (two-point motion) | $T_c = t_a + t_{const} + t_d + t_{pick} + t_{place}$ | s | Sum of all phase durations |
| 5 | Average speed | $v_{avg} = \dfrac{\text{distance travelled}}{T_c}$ | m/s | Used for productivity calculation |
| 6 | Workspace volume (spherical approx.) | $V \approx \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R_{max}^3$ | m³ | Coarse envelope estimate |
| 7 | Payload-induced torque | $\tau_{payload} = W \cdot g \cdot r$ | N·m | $g = 9.81$ m/s² |
| 8 | Power requirement (idealised) | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | W | At joint level |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Speed-capacity tables directly drive *robot selection* in automotive assembly lines. A welding robot needs moderate payload (5–10 kg) but extreme repeatability; a palletizing robot needs high payload (50–500 kg) but tolerates lower speed.

### 2.4 The Speed–Capacity Trade-Off (Physical Insight)

The actuators of a robot have a **fixed power envelope** $P_{max}$. Mechanical power at any joint is $P = \tau \cdot \omega$. Therefore:

$$P_{max} \geq \tau_{payload} \cdot \omega_{max} \;\Rightarrow\; \omega_{max} \leq \dfrac{P_{max}}{\tau_{payload}}$$

Increasing payload increases the necessary torque $\tau_{payload}$, which mathematically *forces* the maximum permissible angular speed $\omega_{max}$ to drop. This is the **fundamental inverse coupling** between capacity and speed that every KTU answer on this topic must explicitly acknowledge.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — End-Effector Speed for a 2R Planar Manipulator

Consider a simple **2-revolute planar manipulator** with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$.

**Step 1:** Write the forward kinematics (position of TCP):

$$\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both equations with respect to time *t* to obtain the velocity components:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= -L_1 \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 - L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2) \\
\dot{y} &= \;\;L_1 \cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot{\theta}_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot{\theta}_1 + \dot{\theta}_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Arrange in matrix form. This *is* the Jacobian $J(\theta)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} &= 
\begin{bmatrix}
-L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
\;\;L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & \;\;L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} \dot{\theta}_1 \\ \dot{\theta}_2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** The magnitude of the TCP speed is:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\dot{x}^2 + \dot{y}^2}$$

**Step 5 (KTU Exam Substitution):** For a numerical KTU problem, substitute $L_1 = 0.5$ m, $L_2 = 0.4$ m, $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 45°$, $\dot{\theta}_1 = 1.2$ rad/s, $\dot{\theta}_2 = 0.8$ rad/s.

Compute trigonometric values first:

$$\sin 30° = 0.500, \quad \cos 30° = 0.866$$
$$\sin 75° = 0.966, \quad \cos 75° = 0.259$$
$$\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 75°$$

Compute Jacobian entries:

$$\begin{aligned}
J_{11} &= -(0.5)(0.500) - (0.4)(0.966) = -0.250 - 0.386 = -0.636 \\
J_{12} &= -(0.4)(0.966) = -0.386 \\
J_{21} &= (0.5)(0.866) + (0.4)(0.259) = 0.433 + 0.104 = 0.537 \\
J_{22} &= (0.4)(0.259) = 0.104
\end{aligned}$$

Now compute velocity components:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x} &= (-0.636)(1.2) + (-0.386)(0.8) = -0.763 - 0.309 = -1.072 \;\text{m/s} \\
\dot{y} &= (0.537)(1.2) + (0.104)(0.8) = 0.644 + 0.083 = 0.727 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Final TCP speed magnitude:

$$\begin{aligned}
v_t &= \sqrt{(-1.072)^2 + (0.727)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{1.149 + 0.529} \\
&= \sqrt{1.678} \\
&\approx 1.295 \;\text{m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Payload Torque on a Single Joint

A robot arm of length $r$ holds a payload of mass $W$ horizontally. The torque about the shoulder joint due to gravity alone is:

$$\tau_{payload} = W \cdot g \cdot r$$

where $g = 9.81 \;\text{m/s}^2$.

**Step 1:** Substitute $W = 10$ kg, $r = 0.8$ m:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{payload} &= 10 \times 9.81 \times 0.8 \\
&= 78.48 \;\text{N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** If the joint motor can deliver maximum torque $\tau_{max} = 100$ N·m, the *remaining* torque budget for acceleration is:

$$\tau_{accel} = \tau_{max} - \tau_{payload} = 100 - 78.48 = 21.52 \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore the *maximum safe angular acceleration* is reduced, and consequently the maximum achievable joint speed during the move is lower than the no-payload rated speed. This is the mathematical proof of the speed–capacity trade-off.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Speed vs Capacity Decision Table

| Application Domain | Required Payload | Required Speed | Robot Family Selected |
|---|---|---|---|
| Electronic PCB assembly | Low (1–3 kg) | Very High (Delta, 5 m/s) | **Delta / SCARA** |
| Arc welding (automotive) | Medium (5–10 kg) | Moderate (1 m/s) | **6-DOF Articulated** |
| Palletizing | High (50–500 kg) | Low (0.5 m/s) | **Heavy Articulated / Gantry** |
| Pick-and-place (food) | Low (1–5 kg) | Very High (10 m/s) | **Delta / High-speed SCARA** |
| CNC machine tending | Medium (20 kg) | Moderate (0.8 m/s) | **6-DOF Articulated** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Speed and Capacity Coupling Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Robot Specifications] --> nodeB[Capacity Block]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Speed Block]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Payload W kg]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Reach R m]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Workspace Volume V m3]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Joint Speed omega rad per s]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[TCP Speed v m per s]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Cycle Time Tc s]
    nodeB1 --> nodeD[Coupling Constraint]
    nodeB2 --> nodeD
    nodeC1 --> nodeD
    nodeC2 --> nodeD
    nodeC3 --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Actuator Power Envelope Pmax]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Inverse Tradeoff P equals tau times omega]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Final Robot Selection]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeB3 --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH[Industrial Application]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Hierarchical Decomposition of Speed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeP[Robot Speed] --> nodeQ[Joint Level Speed]
    nodeP --> nodeR[End Effector Level Speed]
    nodeP --> nodeS[Task Level Speed]
    nodeQ --> nodeQ1[Angular omega rad per s]
    nodeQ --> nodeQ2[Linear mm per s]
    nodeR --> nodeR1[Cartesian TCP velocity v]
    nodeR --> nodeR2[Mapped via Jacobian J theta]
    nodeS --> nodeS1[Cycle Time Tc]
    nodeS --> nodeS2[Throughput parts per hour]
    nodeR2 --> nodeR1
    nodeS1 --> nodeS2
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Capacity Decomposition

```mermaid

    nodeX[Robot Capacity] --> nodeY[Static Capacity]
    nodeX --> nodeZ[Dynamic Capacity]
    nodeY --> nodeY1[Rated Payload]
    nodeY --> nodeY2[Maximum Reach]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ1[Dynamic Payload at Speed v]
    nodeZ --> nodeZ2[Inertia Limited Capacity]
```
