```mermaid
    nodeZ1 --> nodeW
    nodeZ2 --> nodeW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams instead of physical drawings?** The interactions between speed, capacity, and the actuator power envelope are functional relationships that are best represented as data-flow architecture. Mermaid block diagrams render reliably across all browsers and KTU's PDF conversion pipelines.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the following terms with respect to industrial robots: (i) Payload capacity, (ii) Reach, (iii) Workspace volume.*

**Model Answer:**

> **(i) Payload Capacity:** The maximum mass, including the mass of the end-effector and the workpiece, that the robot manipulator can carry throughout its entire workspace while maintaining its rated accuracy, repeatability, and speed. It is expressed in **kg** or **N**.
>
> **(ii) Reach:** The maximum radial distance from the central axis of the robot's base to the farthest point of the Tool Centre Point (TCP) when the arm is fully extended. It defines the *radius of the working envelope* and is measured in **mm** or **m**.
>
> **(iii) Workspace Volume:** The total three-dimensional space within which the TCP of the robot can be positioned by moving all joints through their full mechanical limits. It is measured in **m³** and is geometrically dependent on the robot's kinematic configuration (Cartesian, cylindrical, spherical, articulated, or SCARA).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Differentiate between joint speed and end-effector speed. Why is end-effector speed not uniform throughout the workspace?*

**Model Answer:**

> **Joint Speed** is the angular velocity (rad/s) or linear velocity (mm/s) of an individual joint actuator. It is a *local* specification of each rotational or prismatic axis.
>
> **End-Effector Speed (TCP speed)** is the resultant linear velocity of the Tool Centre Point in Cartesian space. It is a *global* specification measured at the robot's tip.
>
> The end-effector speed is **not uniform** throughout the workspace because the linear velocity at the TCP depends on both the joint velocities and the **effective lever arm** of each joint to the TCP. This lever arm varies with the robot's configuration (i.e., the values of $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n$). The mapping is given by $\mathbf{v}_t = J(\theta)\,\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$, where the Jacobian $J(\theta)$ is a strong function of configuration $\theta$. Hence, the same joint speeds produce different TCP speeds at different points in the workspace, especially near **singularities** where $J$ becomes ill-conditioned and TCP speed may become theoretically infinite or zero.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat block diagram the relationship between the **speed and capacity** of an industrial robot. Discuss why increasing the payload reduces the maximum safe operating speed. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 2-revolute planar manipulator has link lengths $L_1 = 0.6$ m and $L_2 = 0.5$ m. At a given instant, the joint angles are $\theta_1 = 45°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$, and the joint angular velocities are $\dot{\theta}_1 = 1.5$ rad/s and $\dot{\theta}_2 = 1.0$ rad/s. Compute the linear velocity of the end-effector (TCP). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

> **Step 1 — Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the architectural block diagram).
>
> **Step 2 — Verbal Explanation:** The speed and capacity of a robot are coupled through the *actuator power envelope*. The mechanical power delivered by any joint motor is $P = \tau \cdot \omega$, where $\tau$ is the torque and $\omega$ is the angular velocity.
>
> **Step 3 — Payload Torque:** When a payload $W$ is attached at a distance $r$ from a joint, the torque required to *just hold* it against gravity is $\tau_{payload} = W \cdot g \cdot r$.
>
> **Step 4 — Why Speed Drops:** For a given motor with maximum torque $\tau_{max}$ and maximum power $P_{max}$, the maximum safe angular speed is:
> $$\omega_{max} = \frac{P_{max}}{\tau_{max}}$$
> But $\tau_{max}$ must first *hold the payload*, so the available torque for acceleration becomes $\tau_{accel} = \tau_{max} - \tau_{payload}$. As $W$ rises, $\tau_{payload}$ rises, and either:
> (i) The motor slows down to stay within thermal/power limits, OR
> (ii) The available acceleration drops, lengthening the time to reach top speed, increasing cycle time.
>
> **Step 5 — Conclusion:** Hence, increasing payload *forces* a reduction in the maximum safe operating speed. This is the **fundamental speed–capacity trade-off** in industrial robotics.

**Model Solution for (b):**

> **Step 1 — Forward Kinematics:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> x &= L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
> y &= L_1\sin\theta_1 + L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 2 — Jacobian:**
> $$J = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin\theta_1 - L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -L_2\sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ L_1\cos\theta_1 + L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & L_2\cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values:** $\theta_1 = 45°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$, so $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 75°$.
> $$\sin 45° = 0.7071, \quad \cos 45° = 0.7071$$
> $$\sin 75° = 0.9659, \quad \cos 75° = 0.2588$$
>
> $$\begin{aligned}
> J_{11} &= -(0.6)(0.7071) - (0.5)(0.9659) = -0.4243 - 0.4830 = -0.9073 \\
> J_{12} &= -(0.5)(0.9659) = -0.4830 \\
> J_{21} &= (0.6)(0.7071) + (0.5)(0.2588) = 0.4243 + 0.1294 = 0.5537 \\
> J_{22} &= (0.5)(0.2588) = 0.1294
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Compute Velocity Components:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> \dot{x} &= J_{11}\dot{\theta}_1 + J_{12}\dot{\theta}_2 = (-0.9073)(1.5) + (-0.4830)(1.0) \\
> &= -1.3610 - 0.4830 = -1.8440 \;\text{m/s} \\
> \dot{y} &= J_{21}\dot{\theta}_1 + J_{22}\dot{\theta}_2 = (0.5537)(1.5) + (0.1294)(1.0) \\
> &= 0.8306 + 0.1294 = 0.9600 \;\text{m/s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Magnitude of TCP Velocity:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> v_t &= \sqrt{(-1.8440)^2 + (0.9600)^2} \\
> &= \sqrt{3.400 + 0.922} \\
> &= \sqrt{4.322} \\
> &\approx 2.079 \;\text{m/s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Final Answer:** $v_t \approx 2.08$ m/s.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Define **cycle time** of an industrial robot. A pick-and-place robot moves a component over a horizontal distance of 1.2 m with a peak speed of 1.5 m/s. The trapezoidal velocity profile uses equal acceleration and deceleration times of 0.2 s each, with a constant-velocity phase in between. The pick and place operations each take 0.3 s. Calculate the total cycle time. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the factors that affect the **payload capacity** of an industrial robot. How does the configuration of the arm influence the effective payload? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

> **Definition:** Cycle time ($T_c$) is the total time taken by a robot to complete one full operational sequence, including motion time and end-effector actuation time (gripping, releasing, welding, etc.).
>
> **Step 1 — Trapezoidal Profile Phase Times:**
> - Acceleration phase: $t_a = 0.2$ s
> - Deceleration phase: $t_d = 0.2$ s
> - Constant velocity phase: $t_{const}$ (to be determined)
> - Pick operation: $t_{pick} = 0.3$ s
> - Place operation: $t_{place} = 0.3$ s
>
> **Step 2 — Distance Covered During Acceleration:**
> $$d_a = \frac{1}{2}\, v_{peak} \cdot t_a = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.5 \times 0.2 = 0.15 \;\text{m}$$
>
> **Step 3 — Distance Covered During Deceleration:** (By symmetry of trapezoidal profile)
> $$d_d = 0.15 \;\text{m}$$
>
> **Step 4 — Distance Covered During Constant Velocity:**
> $$d_{const} = D_{total} - d_a - d_d = 1.2 - 0.15 - 0.15 = 0.90 \;\text{m}$$
>
> **Step 5 — Time of Constant Velocity Phase:**
> $$t_{const} = \frac{d_{const}}{v_{peak}} = \frac{0.90}{1.5} = 0.60 \;\text{s}$$
>
> **Step 6 — Total Cycle Time:**
> $$\begin{aligned}
> T_c &= t_a + t_{const} + t_d + t_{pick} + t_{place} \\
> &= 0.2 + 0.6 + 0.2 + 0.3 + 0.3 \\
> &= 1.6 \;\text{s}
> \end{aligned}$$
>
> **Final Answer:** $T_c = 1.6$ s.

**Model Solution for (b):**

> **Definition Recap:** Payload capacity is the maximum load a robot can manipulate within rated performance specifications.
>
> **Factors Affecting Payload Capacity:**
>
> 1. **Robot Configuration (Geometry):** When the arm is extended, the moment arm about the base joint is large, increasing the torque demand. When folded close to the base, the moment arm is small, and the same motor can hold a much larger payload. Hence, payload capacity is *configuration-dependent*.
>
> 2. **Joint Actuator Torque Limits:** Each joint motor has a maximum continuous torque rating. The smallest-rated joint in the kinematic chain dictates the *effective* payload.
>
> 3. **Speed of Operation:** Higher speeds introduce inertial forces that add to static gravity loads, reducing dynamic payload capacity. Robots have separate *static* and *dynamic* payload ratings.
>
> 4. **Duty Cycle and Thermal Limits:** Continuous heavy loading heats the motors; thermal cutbacks reduce effective payload over time.
>
> 5. **End-Effector Mass:** The mass of the gripper is included in the payload rating, so a heavier gripper reduces the remaining workpiece capacity.
>
> 6. **Mechanical Stiffness and Deflection:** A compliant arm deflects more under heavy loads, degrading accuracy. Manufacturers therefore de-rate payload to preserve accuracy.
>
> 7. **Joint Limits and Reach:** As the arm extends to maximum reach, structural stress rises; manufacturers specify payload at *full extension* for conservative ratings.
>
> **Mathematical Link:**
> $$\tau_{payload} = W \cdot g \cdot r$$
> Doubling the moment arm $r$ doubles the torque demand, halving the safe payload $W$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse** *rated payload* with *peak payload*. Rated is continuous; peak is short-burst. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Always show Jacobian derivation steps** in velocity problems. Skipping from forward kinematics directly to the answer is a guaranteed 3-mark loss.
> 3. **Units trap:** Forgetting to convert degrees to radians in Jacobian entries is the most common arithmetic error. KTU examiners explicitly hunt for this.
> 4. **Cycle time formula:** Students often forget to include *both* pick and place operations. Always list every phase.
> 5. **Trade-off explanation:** If a question asks about speed–capacity trade-off, you *must* explicitly write the equation $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ and show how increased $\tau$ forces reduced $\omega$. Vague qualitative answers score zero.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Capacity** = Payload + Reach + Workspace Volume (the "what can it carry and where").
- **Speed** = Joint Speed + TCP Speed + Cycle Time (the "how fast can it do it").
- The **Jacobian matrix** $J(\theta)$ is the bridge: $\mathbf{v}_t = J(\theta)\,\dot{\boldsymbol{\theta}}$.
- TCP speed is **not uniform** — it depends on configuration via $J(\theta)$.
- **Payload torque:** $\tau = W \cdot g \cdot r$ — moment arm $r$ is the key multiplier.
- **Trade-off equation:** $P_{max} = \tau \cdot \omega$ — increasing $\tau$ (for higher payload) reduces $\omega$ (max speed).
- **Cycle time** = sum of all motion phases + all actuation phases (pick, place, weld, etc.).
- **Workspace volume** depends on the kinematic structure (Cartesian, cylindrical, spherical, articulated, SCARA, Delta).
- **Dynamic payload** < **Static payload** — always account for inertial loads at speed.
- For KTU numerical problems: **show every Jacobian entry substitution**; do not skip steps.
- Use $g = 9.81$ m/s² unless the problem explicitly states otherwise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stroke and Reach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stroke and Reach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Reach — The "Outstretched Arm" of a Robot

In the context of industrial robotics, **Reach** is formally defined as the **maximum perpendicular distance** measured from the **center of the robot's base** (or shoulder joint, for articulated arms) to the **farthest point of the end-effector (TCP - Tool Center Point)** when the manipulator is fully extended along a principal axis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Reach is the maximum distance the robot's end-effector can travel from the base of the robot when the arm is fully extended.* It defines the **outer boundary** of the robot's working envelope.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Human Arm" Intuition
Imagine you are standing at the center of a room and you want to touch a wall. If you fully stretch your arm outward horizontally, the tip of your middle finger marks your **reach**. The reach is fixed by the **sum of the lengths of your upper arm, forearm, and hand**. Now imagine sweeping your fully-stretched arm all around — the tip traces a hemisphere in space. The radius of that hemisphere is your **reach**.

> [!NOTE]
> Reach is a **static geometric limit** — it is the radius of the largest sphere that fits inside the robot's work envelope.

### 1.2 Stroke — The "Sweep Distance" of a Joint

**Stroke** is defined as the **total distance traveled by a robot joint (or the end-effector) between its two extreme mechanical limits** (minimum and maximum positions). It is essentially a **range of motion** along a given axis or direction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *Stroke is the distance travelled by a particular joint or by the end-effector between its two extreme positions.* It is the **linear (or angular) range** the actuator is designed to cover.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Window Pane" Intuition
Think of a sliding window. The windowpane can move only between two stops — the fully-closed position and the fully-open position. The **distance between these two stops is the stroke** of the window. Similarly, a robot's prismatic (linear) joint has a fixed start point and a fixed end point; the distance between them is its stroke.

> [!NOTE]
> Stroke is a **dynamic kinematic range** — it is the *path length* the end-effector or joint can sweep through.

### 1.3 Standardized Industry Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reach** | **R** | **millimeter (mm)** or **meter (m)** | Maximum tip-to-base distance |
| **Linear Stroke** | **S_L** | **mm** or **m** | Linear range of a prismatic joint |
| **Angular Stroke** | **S_θ** | **radian (rad)** or **degree (°)** | Angular range of a revolute joint |
| **Work Envelope** | **V_w** | **m³** or **mm³** | Total 3-D workspace volume |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Decomposition of Reach and Stroke by Robot Configuration

The way **reach** and **stroke** are quantified depends fundamentally on the **kinematic configuration** of the robot. KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes four canonical configurations:

### A. Cartesian (Rectangular / Gantry) Robot
- **Three prismatic joints** moving along X, Y, Z axes.
- **Reach** = diagonal of the rectangular box:
  
$$R_{cartesian} = \sqrt{X^2_{max} + Y^2_{max} + Z^2_{max}}$$

- **Stroke** along each axis = (Max position − Min position):

$$S_X = X_{max} - X_{min}, \quad S_Y = Y_{max} - Y_{min}, \quad S_Z = Z_{max} - Z_{min}$$

### B. Cylindrical Robot
- One revolute joint (base rotation θ) + two prismatic joints (radial r, vertical z).
- **Reach** occurs when the arm is fully extended horizontally and elevated to maximum z:

$$R_{cylindrical} = \sqrt{r^2_{max} + z^2_{max}}$$

- **Radial stroke**: $S_r = r_{max} - r_{min}$
- **Vertical stroke**: $S_z = z_{max} - z_{min}$

### C. Polar (Spherical) Robot
- Two revolute joints + one prismatic joint (radial extension).
- **Reach** is the maximum radial extension:

$$R_{polar} = r_{max}$$

(when also elevated to the maximum polar angle limit)

### D. Articulated (Revolute / Anthropomorphic) Robot
- All three primary joints are revolute.
- **Reach** is the **sum of the link lengths** (with the arm fully straightened):

$$R_{articulated} = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$$

- **Stroke** is the *difference* between maximum and minimum tip distances from the base:

$$S_{tip} = R_{max} - R_{min} = (L_1 + L_2 + L_3) - (\text{shortest folded distance})$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Parameters

- **Reach** tells the manufacturing engineer *whether the robot can physically access* a given point on a workpiece or conveyor.
- **Stroke** tells the engineer *how much linear or angular travel* is available for executing tasks like welding seams, painting strokes, or pick-and-place motions.
- Together, they define the **Work Envelope (Work Volume)** — the set of all reachable points.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial Use Case:** When designing a robotic welding cell for an automobile chassis, the engineer first measures the largest dimension of the chassis → uses it to specify the **reach**. Then the engineer measures the longest weld seam → uses it to specify the **stroke** required.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Stroke and Reach problems. Memorize it.

| # | Robot Type | Reach (R) Formula | Stroke (S) Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cartesian** | $R = \sqrt{X^2_{max} + Y^2_{max} + Z^2_{max}}$ | $S_i = i_{max} - i_{min}$ for $i \in \{X,Y,Z\}$ | mm |
| 2 | **Cylindrical** | $R = \sqrt{r^2_{max} + z^2_{max}}$ | $S_r = r_{max} - r_{min}$; $S_z = z_{max} - z_{min}$ | mm |
| 3 | **Polar** | $R = r_{max}$ (at max elevation) | $S_r = r_{max} - r_{min}$ | mm |
| 4 | **Articulated** | $R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L_i$ | $S = R_{max} - R_{min}$ | mm |
| 5 | **SCARA** | $R = L_1 + L_2$ | $S = (L_1+L_2) - \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ | mm |
| 6 | **Work Envelope** | $V_w$ depends on configuration | $V_w = \int\!\!\int\!\!\int_{envelope} dV$ | m³ |

**Key Geometric Notes:**

- Articulated robot **minimum reach** (folded): $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$ (for 2-link), or $R_{min} = L_1 - L_2 - L_3$ when fully folded inward.
- **Work Envelope volume for an articulated robot** is a complex 3-D shape — typically a sphere with "holes" carved out by joint limits. For KTU, an approximate spherical volume is used:

$$V_{articulated} \approx \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3_{max} - \frac{4}{3}\pi R^3_{min}$$

- For a **cylindrical robot** the envelope is an annular cylinder (a hollow cylinder):

$$V_{cylindrical} = \pi (r^2_{max} - r^2_{min}) \cdot S_z$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reach of a 2-Link Articulated Robot (Planar Arm)

**Problem Setup:** Consider a 2-link planar manipulator rotating in the X-Y plane, with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, and joint angles $\theta_1$ and $\theta_2$ (both measured from the previous link).

### Step 1: Write the Forward Kinematics

The position of the end-effector in the X-Y plane is given by:

$$x = L_1 \cos(\theta_1) + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

$$y = L_1 \sin(\theta_1) + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

### Step 2: Compute the Squared Distance from Base

The reach $R$ is the radial distance of the end-effector from the base:

$$R^2 = x^2 + y^2$$

### Step 3: Expand Using Trigonometric Identity

Substitute the expressions for $x$ and $y$, expand, and group terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 &= (L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2))^2 + (L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2))^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Expanding each squared term:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 = L_1^2 \cos^2\theta_1 &+ 2L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^2 \cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\
&+ L_1^2 \sin^2\theta_1 + 2L_1 L_2 \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) + L_2^2 \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Apply Pythagorean Identity

Use $\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi = 1$ on the squared link terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 = L_1^2 (\cos^2\theta_1 + \sin^2\theta_1) + L_2^2 (\cos^2(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin^2(\theta_1+\theta_2)) \\
+ 2L_1 L_2 [\cos\theta_1 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) + \sin\theta_1 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2)]
\end{aligned}
$$

This simplifies to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2L_1 L_2 \cos(\theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

(using the cosine addition formula on the bracketed term).

### Step 5: Identify the Maximum and Minimum Reach

- **Maximum reach** ($R_{max}$) occurs when $\cos(\theta_2) = +1$, i.e., $\theta_2 = 0$ (arm fully straightened):

$$R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$$

- **Minimum reach** ($R_{min}$) occurs when $\cos(\theta_2) = -1$, i.e., $\theta_2 = 180°$ (arm fully folded back on itself):

$$R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$$

### Step 6: Compute the Stroke

The stroke of the end-effector is the **range** of reachable radial distances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S = R_{max} - R_{min} = (L_1 + L_2) - \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If $L_1 > L_2$, then $R_{min} = L_1 - L_2$, and so $S = 2 L_2$.
> If $L_2 > L_1$, then $R_{min} = L_2 - L_1$, and so $S = 2 L_1$.
> In all cases, **stroke = twice the length of the shorter link** (for a 2-link arm).

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Question:** A 2-link planar robot arm has $L_1 = 400 \text{ mm}$ and $L_2 = 300 \text{ mm}$. Find the **reach**, the **minimum reach**, and the **stroke** of the end-effector.

### Solution

**Step 1: Maximum reach (fully extended).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= L_1 + L_2 \\
&= 400 + 300 \\
&= 700 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Minimum reach (fully folded).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert \\
&= \vert 400 - 300 \vert \\
&= 100 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Stroke.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= R_{max} - R_{min} \\
&= 700 - 100 \\
&= 600 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Or equivalently, $S = 2 \times \min(L_1, L_2) = 2 \times 300 = 600 \text{ mm}$.)

**Step 4: Verification using the reach formula at a sample angle.**

Let $\theta_1 = 30°$, $\theta_2 = 90°$. Then $\theta_1 + \theta_2 = 120°$ and $\cos(\theta_2) = \cos(90°) = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2} \\
&= \sqrt{400^2 + 300^2 + 0} \\
&= \sqrt{160000 + 90000} \\
&= \sqrt{250000} \\
&= 500 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This lies between $R_{min} = 100$ and $R_{max} = 700$ ✓ — confirming the formula is correct.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Robot Type | Reach Boundary | Stroke Boundary | Envelope Shape | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian** | $R = \sqrt{X^2+Y^2+Z^2}$ | Per-axis linear range | Rectangular cuboid | CNC, 3-D printing |
| **Cylindrical** | $R = \sqrt{r^2+z^2}$ | $S_r$ (radial), $S_z$ (vertical) | Hollow cylinder | Loading CNC lathes |
| **Polar (Spherical)** | $R = r_{max}$ | $S_r = r_{max} - r_{min}$ | Spherical sector | Die casting, forging |
| **Articulated** | $R = L_1+L_2+L_3$ | $S = R_{max} - R_{min}$ | Sphere with cuts | Welding, painting |
| **SCARA** | $R = L_1+L_2$ | $S = 2 \min(L_1, L_2)$ | Annular disc | PCB assembly |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Reach and Stroke Determination Flow

The following **Mermaid flow diagram** illustrates the logical procedure used to compute reach and stroke from a robot's kinematic specification — a typical KTU board question expects this analytical approach.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Robot Kinematic Specs] --> B{Identify Robot Configuration}
    B --> C1[Cartesian: 3 prismatic joints]
    B --> C2[Cylindrical: 1 revolute + 2 prismatic]
    B --> C3[Polar: 2 revolute + 1 prismatic]
    B --> C4[Articulated: 3 revolute joints]
    C1 --> D1[Extract Xmax Ymax Zmax limits]
    C2 --> D2[Extract rmax rmin and zmax zmin limits]
    C3 --> D3[Extract rmax rmin and elevation limits]
    C4 --> D4[Extract link lengths L1 L2 L3]
    D1 --> E1[Compute R = sqrt X2 Y2 Z2]
    D2 --> E2[Compute R = sqrt rmax2 zmax2]
    D3 --> E3[Compute R = rmax]
    D4 --> E4[Compute R = L1 L2 L3]
    E1 --> F[Compute Stroke S per axis]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Compute Work Envelope Volume Vw]
    G --> H[Validate against workspace requirements]
    H --> I[End: Reach and Stroke Finalized]
```

## 4.2 Workspace Geometry Comparison Diagram

This Mermaid block diagram compares the **shape of the work envelope** for the four principal configurations — a high-frequency KTU diagram-type question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Cartesian
        CA[Reach R: Cuboid diagonal] --> CB[Stroke S: 3 linear ranges]
    end
    subgraph Cylindrical
        CC[Reach R: sqrt r2 z2] --> CD[Stroke S: radial and vertical]
    end
    subgraph Polar
        CE[Reach R: rmax] --> CF[Stroke S: radial sweep]
    end
    subgraph Articulated
        CG[Reach R: sum of links] --> CH[Stroke S: Rmax minus Rmin]
    end
```

## 4.3 2-Link Planar Arm: Reach and Stroke Geometry (Schematic Block)

A block representation of the 2-link planar derivation in Section 3, showing the input joint angles and the resulting reach output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Link Length L1] --> M[Forward Kinematics Block]
    B[Link Length L2] --> M
    C[Joint Angle theta1] --> M
    D[Joint Angle theta2] --> M
    M --> E[Output: R2 equals L1 squared plus L2 squared plus 2 L1 L2 cos theta2]
    E --> F{Range Check}
    F --> G1[If cos theta2 equals plus 1: Rmax equals L1 plus L2]
    F --> G2[If cos theta2 equals minus 1: Rmin equals L1 minus L2]
    G1 --> H[Stroke S equals Rmax minus Rmin]
    G2 --> H
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above are simplified block-functional representations. In KTU board examinations, students are expected to **hand-draw** the physical work-envelope shapes (cuboid, hollow cylinder, sphere, sphere with cuts) using labeled dimensions. Use these Mermaid diagrams as a logical *flow* aid only.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the terms "Reach" and "Stroke" as applied to industrial robots. How do they differ?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Reach** is the *maximum perpendicular distance* from the center of the robot's base to the end-effector when the arm is fully extended. It is a **static geometric limit** that defines the outer boundary of the work envelope.
- **Stroke** is the *total distance traveled* by a robot joint (or the end-effector) between its two extreme mechanical positions. It is a **dynamic kinematic range** that defines how much travel is available for task execution.
- **Difference:** Reach is a single maximum distance (a scalar limit), whereas stroke is a range of motion (a difference between two extreme positions). Reach tells *how far* the robot can go; stroke tells *how much it can move* once it gets there.

*(3 marks: 1 mark each for the two definitions + 1 mark for the difference.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**For a 2-link planar robot arm with link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, derive the expression for the maximum and minimum reach.**

**Model Answer:**

The squared distance of the end-effector from the base is:

$$R^2 = L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos(\theta_2)$$

- **Maximum reach** occurs at $\theta_2 = 0°$ (arm fully extended): $\quad R_{max} = L_1 + L_2$
- **Minimum reach** occurs at $\theta_2 = 180°$ (arm fully folded): $\quad R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$

*(3 marks: 1 mark for setup, 1 mark for $R_{max}$, 1 mark for $R_{min}$.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the concepts of **reach** and **stroke** for an industrial robot. Discuss how reach and stroke are determined for each of the following configurations:
   (i) Cartesian robot
   (ii) Cylindrical robot
   (iii) Polar robot
   (iv) Articulated robot
*Include a labeled sketch of the work envelope for at least one configuration.*  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical robot has a **radial reach** of 1.2 m and a **vertical stroke** of 0.8 m. The base rotation is unlimited. Calculate:
   (i) the maximum reach of the robot,
   (ii) the work-envelope volume, and
   (iii) the stroke along the radial direction if the inner clearance is 0.3 m.  **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

### Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 marks)

**Definition of Reach (2 marks):**
Reach is the maximum distance the robot's end-effector (TCP) can extend from its base. It is a static geometric parameter that defines the **outer boundary** of the robot's work envelope. It is typically specified in **mm** or **m**.

**Definition of Stroke (2 marks):**
Stroke is the range of motion of a particular joint or the end-effector between its two extreme positions. For prismatic joints, stroke is linear (mm); for revolute joints, stroke is angular (degrees or radians). Stroke defines the *useful working range* inside the envelope.

**Configuration-Specific Reach & Stroke (3 marks — distribute 0.75 each):**

| Config | Reach Formula | Stroke Formula |
|---|---|---|
| **Cartesian** | $R = \sqrt{X^2_{max} + Y^2_{max} + Z^2_{max}}$ | $S_X = X_{max} - X_{min}$, $S_Y$, $S_Z$ |
| **Cylindrical** | $R = \sqrt{r^2_{max} + z^2_{max}}$ | $S_r = r_{max} - r_{min}$, $S_z = z_{max} - z_{min}$ |
| **Polar** | $R = r_{max}$ (at max elevation angle) | $S_r = r_{max} - r_{min}$ |
| **Articulated** | $R = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$ | $S = R_{max} - R_{min}$ |

*(Sketch — 1 extra mark for a labeled work-envelope drawing, e.g., the hollow-cylinder envelope of a cylindrical robot with $r_{max}$, $r_{min}$, and $S_z$ labeled.)*

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $r_{max} = 1.2$ m
- $z_{max} = 0.8$ m (vertical stroke = $S_z$)
- Inner clearance: $r_{min} = 0.3$ m

**(i) Maximum reach (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= \sqrt{r^2_{max} + z^2_{max}} \\
&= \sqrt{(1.2)^2 + (0.8)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{1.44 + 0.64} \\
&= \sqrt{2.08} \\
&\approx 1.442 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Work-envelope volume (3 marks):**

For a cylindrical robot, the envelope is an **annular cylinder** (hollow cylinder) of height $S_z$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_w &= \pi (r^2_{max} - r^2_{min}) \cdot S_z \\
&= \pi \left[(1.2)^2 - (0.3)^2\right] \cdot 0.8 \\
&= \pi (1.44 - 0.09) \cdot 0.8 \\
&= \pi (1.35) \cdot 0.8 \\
&= 1.08\pi \\
&\approx 3.393 \text{ m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Radial stroke (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_r &= r_{max} - r_{min} \\
&= 1.2 - 0.3 \\
&= 0.9 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the procedure to determine the **work envelope** of an articulated robot. Derive the expression for the reach of a 2-link planar arm.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-link articulated robot has link lengths $L_1 = 500$ mm, $L_2 = 400$ mm, $L_3 = 300$ mm. Determine:
   (i) the maximum reach,
   (ii) the minimum reach (fully folded), and
   (iii) the stroke of the end-effector.  **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

### Part (a) — Work Envelope Procedure (7 marks)

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

The procedure is:
1. Identify the link lengths $L_1, L_2, ..., L_n$.
2. Identify joint limits ($\theta_1^{min}, \theta_1^{max}, ...$).
3. Compute $R_{max} = \sum L_i$ (fully extended).
4. Compute $R_{min}$ (fully folded configuration).
5. Map the 3-D envelope by sampling joint angles within limits.
6. Render the envelope as a sphere with "bite" regions removed by joint-limit constraints.

**Derivation of 2-link reach (4 marks):**

Starting from forward kinematics in the X-Y plane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_1 \cos\theta_1 + L_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) \\
y &= L_1 \sin\theta_1 + L_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Squaring and adding, applying $\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R^2 &= x^2 + y^2 \\
&= L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$R = \sqrt{L_1^2 + L_2^2 + 2 L_1 L_2 \cos\theta_2}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $L_1 = 500$ mm, $L_2 = 400$ mm, $L_3 = 300$ mm.

**(i) Maximum reach (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{max} &= L_1 + L_2 + L_3 \\
&= 500 + 400 + 300 \\
&= 1200 \text{ mm} \\
&= 1.2 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Minimum reach (3 marks):**

For a 3-link arm fully folded back on itself, the minimum reach is the **absolute difference between the sum of the two longer links and the shortest link**. With $L_1 = 500 > L_2 = 400 > L_3 = 300$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= L_1 - (L_2 + L_3) \\
&= 500 - (400 + 300) \\
&= 500 - 700 \\
&= -200 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since reach cannot be negative, and physically the arm folds with $L_3$ lying on top of $L_2$, the **correct physical minimum reach** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= (L_1 + L_3) - L_2 \\
&= (500 + 300) - 400 \\
&= 400 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Equivalently, the shortest physically reachable distance when the arm is folded such that $L_2$ and $L_3$ are collinear and pointing back toward the base is **$L_1 - L_2 + L_3$** = 400 mm; or in the alternative fold, **$L_1 + L_3 - L_2$** = 400 mm.)

**(iii) Stroke (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= R_{max} - R_{min} \\
&= 1200 - 400 \\
&= 800 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing reach with stroke** — students often interchange the two. *Reach = single maximum distance; Stroke = range (difference of two extremes).*
> 2. **Forgetting units** — KTU strictly penalizes missing mm/m units in the final answer (deduct 0.5 mark).
> 3. **Sign errors in minimum reach** — for a 2-link arm, $R_{min} = \vert L_1 - L_2 \vert$, not $L_1 - L_2$. Always use the absolute value.
> 4. **Wrong envelope formula** — students frequently use $V = \pi r^2 h$ (full cylinder) for a cylindrical robot instead of $V = \pi (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2) S_z$ (annular cylinder). The inner clearance is *not* a free space — it is a hollow region the arm cannot reach.
> 5. **Skipping the sketch** — KTU awards 1–2 marks for a labeled work-envelope diagram. Omitting it costs easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Stroke and Reach**

- ✅ **Reach (R)** = *maximum* tip-to-base distance; defines the *outer* boundary of the workspace.
- ✅ **Stroke (S)** = *range* of motion between two extreme positions; defines the *useful working span* inside the envelope.
- ✅ **Articulated robot reach** = sum of all link lengths: $R = L_1 + L_2 + L_3$.
- ✅ **Articulated robot minimum reach** = absolute difference (2-link) or $L_1 + L_3 - L_2$ pattern (3-link, folded).
- ✅ **2-link stroke** = $2 \times$ the shorter link length: $S = 2 \min(L_1, L_2)$.
- ✅ **Cylindrical reach** = $R = \sqrt{r_{max}^2 + z_{max}^2}$.
- ✅ **Cylindrical envelope volume** = $\pi (r_{max}^2 - r_{min}^2) S_z$ — *annular*, not full cylinder.
- ✅ **Polar reach** = $r_{max}$; envelope = spherical sector.
- ✅ **Cartesian reach** = $R = \sqrt{X_{max}^2 + Y_{max}^2 + Z_{max}^2}$; envelope = cuboid.
- ✅ **Reach is a scalar maximum**; **Stroke is a range (scalar difference)** — never mix them up.
- ✅ **Units are critical** in KTU valuation — always write mm or m in the final numerical answer.
- ✅ **Always include a labeled sketch** of the work envelope in 14-mark questions — 1 to 2 marks are reserved for the diagram.
- ✅ **Reach is NOT the same as work-envelope volume** — reach is a linear dimension; volume requires integration or the appropriate geometric formula per configuration.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Repeatability, Precision and Accuracy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Repeatability, Precision and Accuracy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of industrial robotics, the performance of a manipulator is judged not merely by its load capacity or speed, but by three closely related — yet fundamentally distinct — spatial performance characteristics. The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OERAT611, Module 4) explicitly requires students to differentiate between **Repeatability, Precision, and Accuracy**, as these terms are routinely misapplied in answer scripts and result in mark deductions.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **DEFINITION — Accuracy:**  
> Accuracy is defined as the ability of a robot to position its end-effector at a *target point* (the commanded/ideal point in the workspace) when a specific coordinate is input. Mathematically, it is the measure of closeness between the **actual achieved position** and the **desired (true) position** in absolute Cartesian space.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **DEFINITION — Precision (or Resolution):**  
> Precision is the smallest incremental change in position or orientation that the robot's control system can command, sense, or execute. It is the *fineness of measurement* or the *granularity* of the manipulator's positioning system. High precision means the robot can distinguish and execute very small displacements.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **DEFINITION — Repeatability:**  
> Repeatability is the ability of the robot to return to a position that has been *previously taught* (i.e., a position already stored in its memory), when the same command is issued repeatedly under identical conditions of load, speed, and environmental state. It is a measure of *consistency*, not of *correctness*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a young archer (let us call him **Archer A**) who is asked to hit the bullseye of a target 20 times. After the 20 arrows are released, three distinct outcomes can occur:

- **Scenario 1 — High Accuracy + High Precision + High Repeatability:**  
  All 20 arrows land in a tight cluster *exactly at the bullseye*. The archer hits where he aims, and every arrow lands virtually on top of the previous one. This is the *ideal robot*.

- **Scenario 2 — High Repeatability + High Precision + Low Accuracy:**  
  All 20 arrows land in a very tight cluster, but the cluster is *slightly offset* from the bullseye (say, 2 cm to the right). The archer is extremely *consistent*, but he is consistently *wrong*. This is a *calibrated* robot with a systematic error.

- **Scenario 3 — Low Repeatability + High Accuracy + Variable:**  
  The arrows land scattered randomly across the entire target. The archer is neither consistent nor on target. This is a worn or poorly maintained manipulator.

- **Scenario 4 — Low Repeatability + High Precision + High Accuracy (average):**  
  The archer's average arrow position hits the bullseye, but the spread is wide. Some arrows miss entirely. This represents a robot with random error dominating.

In the analogy, **Accuracy** = "Are the arrows at the bullseye?", **Precision** = "How fine is the archer's sight adjustment?" (resolution), and **Repeatability** = "Do the arrows bunch together tightly?".

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Takeaway for Examiners:** Most industrial robots are designed to be *highly repeatable but only moderately accurate*. This is because the cost of achieving sub-millimetre absolute accuracy across the entire workspace is enormous; instead, robots are *taught* a few reference points and rely on repeatability to revisit them faithfully.

---

## 1.3 Resolution vs Precision — A Subtle Distinction

The KTU syllabus uses the term *Precision* interchangeably with *Resolution* in some contexts, but a strict reading differentiates them:

- **Resolution** = the smallest discrete step the control system can theoretically output (limited by encoder bit-depth, DAC resolution, gear backlash, etc.).
- **Precision** = the practical ability to *resolve* differences in the physical world (often limited by mechanical compliance, thermal drift, etc.).

For a typical 6-axis industrial robot, the **resolution** is on the order of **0.001 mm to 0.01 mm** at the joint, while the **repeatability** in the workspace is usually specified as **±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Breakdown of the Three Characteristics

### A. Accuracy — The "Correctness" Metric

Accuracy is an *absolute* property. It is judged against an *external reference frame* — the desired position vector in world coordinates. If the robot is commanded to move to point $P_d = (x_d, y_d, z_d)$ but the actual position achieved is $P_a = (x_a, y_a, z_a)$, then the **positional accuracy error** is:

$$\vec{e}_{acc} = P_d - P_a$$

The **magnitude of the accuracy error** is given by the Euclidean norm:

$$e_{acc} = \sqrt{(x_d - x_a)^2 + (y_d - y_a)^2 + (z_d - z_a)^2}$$

Sources of inaccuracy:
- **Geometric errors:** Manufacturing tolerances in link lengths, joint offsets, and axis misalignment.
- **Kinematic errors:** Deviation between the nominal Denavit–Hartenberg (DH) parameters and actual link parameters.
- **Non-geometric errors:** Joint compliance, gear backlash, thermal expansion of links, payload-induced deflection.
- **Calibration errors:** Imperfect identification of the true kinematic model.

### B. Precision (Resolution) — The "Fineness" Metric

Precision is an *intrinsic* property of the robot's hardware. It is bounded by:

1. **Encoder resolution** at each joint: $R_{enc} = \dfrac{2\pi}{2^n}$ radians per count (for an $n$-bit encoder).
2. **DAC resolution** of the servo loop driving each actuator.
3. **Mechanical resolution** of the gear train (limited by backlash and hysteresis).

For a revolute joint, the **angular resolution** is:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{2\pi}{2^n} \text{ (radians/count)}$$

This angular resolution, when transformed by the **forward kinematics Jacobian** $J(\theta)$, translates into a Cartesian resolution at the end-effector:

$$\Delta X = J(\theta) \cdot \Delta\theta$$

This means that the effective Cartesian resolution is *configuration-dependent* — it changes with the pose of the robot. Near singularities, the determinant of $J$ approaches zero, causing $\Delta X$ to become very large in certain directions, thereby *degrading* the effective precision.

### C. Repeatability — The "Consistency" Metric

Repeatability is a *statistical* property, evaluated by commanding the robot to move to the same target position $N$ times (typically $N = 30$ to $N = 100$) and recording the actual positions $P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_N$ achieved.

The **mean (centroid) position** is:

$$\bar{P} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$$

The **repeatability** is then specified as the radius of a sphere (or 3-sigma bounds) within which a high percentage (typically 99.7%, corresponding to the **±3σ** Gaussian convention) of the actual positions fall:

$$R_{rep} = \pm 3\sigma = \pm 3\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(P_i - \bar{P})^2}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why ±3σ?** In a normal (Gaussian) distribution, 99.73% of all samples fall within three standard deviations of the mean. KTU examiners expect students to justify this choice using statistical reasoning.

Repeatability is influenced by:
- Backlash in gear trains
- Stiction and friction in joints
- Servo loop quantization
- Thermal fluctuations
- Joint and link elasticity

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Symbol / Formula** | **Unit** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy Error (scalar) | $e_{acc} = \vert P_d - P_a \vert$ | mm | Deviation from desired position |
| Accuracy Error (vector) | $\vec{e}_{acc} = P_d - P_a$ | mm | Vector form of deviation |
| Angular Resolution | $\Delta\theta = \dfrac{2\pi}{2^n}$ | rad/count | Smallest joint increment |
| Cartesian Resolution | $\Delta X = J(\theta)\cdot\Delta\theta$ | mm | Smallest workspace increment |
| Mean Centroid Position | $\bar{P} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} P_i$ | mm | Average achieved target |
| Repeatability (3σ) | $R_{rep} = \pm 3\sigma$ | mm | 99.7% confidence bound |
| Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(P_i - \bar{P})^2}$ | mm | Spread of achieved points |
| ISO 9283 Repeatability | $RP_l = \bar{l} + 3\sigma_l$ | mm | ISO-standardised metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **ISO 9283** standard defines *pose repeatability* ($RP$) and *pose accuracy* ($AP$) as the KTU-recommended performance metrics. The symbol $\bar{l}$ denotes the mean distance from the centroid to the commanded position, and $\sigma_l$ is the standard deviation of those distances.

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

In real-world deployment:

- **Welding robots** require high **repeatability** (the seam is taught point-by-point; they only need to revisit these taught points).
- **PCB drilling machines** require both **high accuracy** and **high repeatability** (holes must be at exact coordinates, not just consistent coordinates).
- **Pick-and-place machines** for electronics often prioritize **resolution** over accuracy (very small pick positions, but absolute location is calibrated externally).
- **Surgical robots (e.g., Da Vinci)** demand **sub-millimetric accuracy** (otherwise patient harm occurs), necessitating sophisticated kinematic calibration.

> [!NOTE]  
> In nearly all industrial applications, **repeatability is the headline specification** quoted by robot manufacturers (e.g., "±0.05 mm repeatability"), while **accuracy is left unspecified or quoted separately** as it is workspace-dependent.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ±3σ Repeatability Bound

### Statement
Given $N$ repeat attempts to reach a target, the **3-sigma (3σ) repeatability** is the radius of a sphere centred at the mean position $\bar{P}$ that contains 99.7% of all observed positions, assuming a Gaussian (normal) distribution of the errors along each Cartesian axis.

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the measured positions.**  
Let the $i$-th commanded-and-achieved position in 3D space be:

$$P_i = (x_i, y_i, z_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$$

**Step 2 — Compute the centroid (mean position).**  
The mean of the $N$ samples along each axis is given by the arithmetic average:

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i, \quad \bar{y} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} y_i, \quad \bar{z} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i$$

Therefore the centroid vector is:

$$\bar{P} = (\bar{x}, \bar{y}, \bar{z})$$

**Step 3 — Compute the deviations from the centroid.**  
For each sample, the deviation (error) along each axis is:

$$\delta x_i = x_i - \bar{x}, \quad \delta y_i = y_i - \bar{y}, \quad \delta z_i = z_i - \bar{z}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the variance along each axis.**  
Using the *unbiased* sample variance (Bessel's correction, $N-1$ in the denominator):

$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\delta x_i)^2, \quad \sigma_y^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\delta y_i)^2, \quad \sigma_z^2 = \frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N} (\delta z_i)^2$$

**Step 5 — Combine into a scalar repeatability metric.**  
For a 3D isotropic distribution, the *combined* positional standard deviation is:

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the 3-sigma confidence bound.**  
The industry standard (and KTU-expected answer) is to quote repeatability as:

$$\boxed{R_{rep} = \pm 3\sigma = \pm 3\sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2}}$$

This bound corresponds to a 99.73% confidence interval, as derived from the Gaussian cumulative distribution function.

### Numerical Worked Example

**Problem (KTU-style):**  
A 6-axis robot is commanded to move to the same target 10 times. The measured positions (in mm) along the X-axis are:

$$X = \{100.02,\ 100.05,\ 99.98,\ 100.03,\ 100.01,\ 100.04,\ 99.99,\ 100.02,\ 100.05,\ 100.00\}$$

Compute the 3σ repeatability along the X-axis.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mean value of X:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{10}\sum_{i=1}^{10} x_i = \frac{100.02 + 100.05 + 99.98 + 100.03 + 100.01 + 100.04 + 99.99 + 100.02 + 100.05 + 100.00}{10}$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{1000.19}{10} = 100.019 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Deviations from the mean:**

$$\delta x_i = \{0.001,\ 0.031,\ -0.039,\ 0.011,\ -0.009,\ 0.021,\ -0.029,\ 0.001,\ 0.031,\ -0.019\}$$

**Step 3 — Squared deviations:**

$$\delta x_i^2 = \{1\text{e-}6,\ 9.61\text{e-}4,\ 1.521\text{e-}3,\ 1.21\text{e-}4,\ 8.1\text{e-}5,\ 4.41\text{e-}4,\ 8.41\text{e-}4,\ 1\text{e-}6,\ 9.61\text{e-}4,\ 3.61\text{e-}4\}$$

**Step 4 — Sum of squared deviations:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{10} \delta x_i^2 = 0.005721 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 5 — Variance (Bessel-corrected):**

$$\sigma_x^2 = \frac{0.005721}{10 - 1} = \frac{0.005721}{9} = 6.357 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 6 — Standard deviation:**

$$\sigma_x = \sqrt{6.357 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.0252 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7 — 3σ repeatability:**

$$\boxed{R_{rep,x} = \pm 3 \times 0.0252 = \pm 0.0756 \text{ mm} \approx \pm 0.076 \text{ mm}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Resolution from Encoder Bit Depth

A revolute joint driven by a DC servo motor uses an incremental optical encoder. For an encoder with $n$ output bits (or $n$ lines per revolution), the *theoretical* angular resolution is:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{2\pi}{2^n} \text{ radians/count}$$

**Example:** For a 16-bit encoder, $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ counts per revolution.

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{2\pi}{65536} = 9.588 \times 10^{-5} \text{ rad/count} \approx 0.0055^\circ \text{ /count}$$

If the harmonic-drive gear ratio is $k = 100:1$, then the resolution at the *output* (link) shaft is:

$$\Delta\theta_{link} = \frac{\Delta\theta}{k} = \frac{9.588 \times 10^{-5}}{100} = 9.588 \times 10^{-7} \text{ rad/count}$$

In degrees: $\Delta\theta_{link} \approx 5.5 \times 10^{-5}$ ° per count, which is the *theoretical* resolution. In practice, mechanical compliance and backlash degrade this to the *practical* resolution.

---

## 3.3 Comparative Analysis Matrix (KTU Favourite)

| **Property** | **Accuracy** | **Precision / Resolution** | **Repeatability** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | Closeness to *true value* | Smallest distinguishable increment | Closeness of *repeated* attempts to each other |
| Reference Frame | Absolute (world coordinates) | Intrinsic (hardware-bound) | Statistical (centroid-based) |
| Measured Against | Commanded / desired position | Encoder bit depth, DAC granularity | Mean of repeated samples |
| Statistical? | No (single-trial metric) | No (hardware specification) | Yes (±3σ convention) |
| Affected by | Kinematic errors, calibration, deflection | Sensor/DAC resolution, gear ratio | Backlash, friction, thermal noise |
| Cost to Improve | High (requires full calibration) | Moderate (better encoders) | Moderate (better mechanics) |
| Typical Magnitude | 1 – 10 mm (industrial robots) | 0.001 – 0.01 mm | 0.02 – 0.1 mm |
| Example Scenario | Hole drilled at *wrong* location | Cannot move in steps < 0.01 mm | Returns to ±0.05 mm of taught point |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Relationship Between Accuracy, Precision, and Repeatability

The following flow chart depicts the logical relationship between the three concepts as conceptualised in KTU Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Commanded to Reach Target] --> B{Is the centroid of\nactual positions\nnear the true target?}
    B -- YES --> C[High ACCURACY]
    B -- NO --> D[Low ACCURACY\nSystematic Bias Present]
    
    A --> E{Is the spread of\nactual positions\ntightly clustered?}
    E -- YES --> F[High REPEATABILITY\nand PRECISION]
    E -- NO --> G[Low REPEATABILITY\nRandom Noise Dominant]
    
    A --> H{Can the system\nresolve sub-mm\nincrements?}
    H -- YES --> I[High RESOLUTION]
    H -- NO --> J[Coarse RESOLUTION\nLimited by Encoder]
    
    C --> K[IDEAL ROBOT\nCalibrated and Precise]
    D --> L[BIASED ROBOT\nNeeds Recalibration]
    F --> M[CONSISTENT ROBOT\nIndustrial Standard]
    G --> N[NOISY ROBOT\nNeeds Maintenance]
    
    K --> O[Best Performance]
    L --> P[Calibrate to remove bias]
    M --> Q[Acceptable for taught-point tasks]
    N --> R[Service joints and encoders]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture for Performance Evaluation

This block diagram illustrates the data flow of a typical performance test according to ISO 9283 / KTU Module 4 methodology.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CMD[Commanded Target Position\nPd] --> CTRL[Robot Controller\nInverse Kinematics]
    CTRL --> ACT[Joint Actuators\nServo Motors]
    ACT --> MAN[Robot Manipulator\n6-Axis Mechanism]
    MAN --> EE[End-Effector\nActual Position Pa]
    
    EE --> SENS[Position Sensors\nEncoders at each joint]
    SENS --> FBK[Feedback Loop\nForward Kinematics]
    FBK --> REC[Data Acquisition\nN samples collected]
    
    REC --> STAT[Statistical Processor\nMean and Std Deviation]
    STAT --> CALC[Performance Calculator]
    
    CALC --> R1[Accuracy = norm of Pd - mean Pa]
    CALC --> R2[Repeatability = 3-sigma]
    CALC --> R3[Resolution = encoder limit]
    
    CMD -.-> CALC
    
    R1 --> OUT[Performance Report\nKTU Spec Sheet]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Mermaid Diagram: The Target Analogy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Q1[Scenario 1: High Accuracy + High Repeatability]
        T1A[Target Bullseye] --- T1B[Arrows clustered ON bullseye]
    end
    
    subgraph Q2[Scenario 2: High Repeatability + Low Accuracy]
        T2A[Target Bullseye] --- T2B[Arrows clustered OFFSET from bullseye]
    end
    
    subgraph Q3[Scenario 3: Low Repeatability + Variable]
        T3A[Target Bullseye] --- T3B[Arrows SCATTERED everywhere]
    end
    
    Q1 --> R1[Ideal Robot]
    Q2 --> R2[Biased but consistent robot]
    Q3 --> R3[Noisy robot needing maintenance]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Differentiate between *accuracy* and *repeatability* of an industrial robot. Use a suitable example to support your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Accuracy** is the ability of a robot to position its end-effector at the *exact target/commanded* location in the workspace. It is an *absolute* measure and depends on calibration and the kinematic model.
- **Repeatability** is the ability of the robot to *return to a previously taught* position consistently over multiple trials under identical loading and environmental conditions. It is a *statistical* measure expressed as ±3σ.
- **Example:** A welding robot in an automotive assembly line is taught 50 weld points. The robot does *not* need to know the absolute coordinates of these points; it only needs to *revisit* them faithfully. Thus repeatability (±0.1 mm) is critical, while absolute accuracy may be lower (~2 mm). In contrast, a CNC drilling machine must place holes at *specific* coordinates on a printed circuit board, requiring both high accuracy and high repeatability.

> Key contrast: A robot can be highly repeatable but inaccurate (systematic offset), but cannot be highly accurate and poorly repeatable (errors would scatter the achieved positions).

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Define the term "resolution" of a robot. How is it related to the encoder bit-depth?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Resolution** is the smallest incremental change in position or orientation that the robot's control system can sense, command, or execute.
- It is determined by the *bit-depth* $n$ of the position encoders mounted on each joint. For a revolute joint with an $n$-bit encoder, the angular resolution is:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{2\pi}{2^n} \text{ radians per count}$$

- For example, a 16-bit encoder gives $\Delta\theta = 2\pi / 65536 \approx 9.59 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/count. After a 100:1 gear reduction, the link resolution improves to $\approx 9.59 \times 10^{-7}$ rad/count.
- Resolution is a *theoretical* hardware limit; in practice, mechanical compliance, backlash, and friction degrade the *effective* resolution.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 4]**

### Question A (14 Marks)
**(a)** Explain the terms *accuracy*, *precision*, and *repeatability* as applied to industrial robots. Provide the mathematical formulation for each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 6-DOF robot is commanded to reach the same Cartesian target 30 times. The standard deviations of the achieved positions along the X, Y, and Z axes are $\sigma_x = 0.02$ mm, $\sigma_y = 0.015$ mm, and $\sigma_z = 0.025$ mm. Compute the *3-sigma repeatability* and interpret the result. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Definitions and Formulations

**Accuracy:** Closeness of the *actual achieved position* $P_a$ to the *commanded (true) position* $P_d$:

$$\vec{e}_{acc} = P_d - P_a, \quad e_{acc} = \vert \vec{e}_{acc} \vert$$

**Precision (Resolution):** The smallest measurable increment in the workspace, governed by the encoder bit-depth:

$$\Delta\theta = \frac{2\pi}{2^n} \text{ at each joint}, \quad \Delta X = J(\theta) \cdot \Delta\theta \text{ in Cartesian space}$$

**Repeatability:** Statistical consistency of multiple attempts. Defined as the ±3σ bound around the mean:

$$R_{rep} = \pm 3\sigma = \pm 3\sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(P_i - \bar{P})^2}$$

**Tabular Comparison:**

| Property | Depends on | Type | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Accuracy | Calibration, kinematic model | Absolute | 1 – 10 mm |
| Precision | Encoder bit depth, gear ratio | Intrinsic | 0.001 – 0.01 mm |
| Repeatability | Backlash, friction, stiffness | Statistical | 0.02 – 0.1 mm |

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $\sigma_x = 0.02$ mm, $\sigma_y = 0.015$ mm, $\sigma_z = 0.025$ mm, $N = 30$.

**Step 1 — Combined standard deviation:**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2} = \sqrt{(0.02)^2 + (0.015)^2 + (0.025)^2}$$

$$\sigma = \sqrt{0.0004 + 0.000225 + 0.000625} = \sqrt{0.00125}$$

$$\sigma = 0.0354 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — 3-sigma repeatability:**

$$R_{rep} = \pm 3 \times 0.0354 = \pm 0.106 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**  
With 99.73% confidence, the robot's end-effector will return to within a sphere of radius **0.106 mm** centred on the mean achieved position. This is typical of a high-precision industrial robot suitable for electronics assembly or precision welding.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]
**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the difference between accuracy and repeatability using a *target-and-arrow* analogy. Mention how a robot can be *highly repeatable but inaccurate*. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between the *encoder resolution* of a revolute joint and the *Cartesian resolution* of the end-effector. Explain why the resolution is configuration-dependent. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Target Analogy

[Draw or describe three concentric targets with arrows as elaborated in Section 1.2 above]

- **Accurate and Repeatable:** Arrows are tightly clustered ON the bullseye.
- **Repeatable but Inaccurate:** Arrows are tightly clustered but OFFSET from the bullseye (a *systematic error*).
- **Inaccurate and Non-Repeatable:** Arrows are scattered randomly.

A robot is highly repeatable but inaccurate when its taught points are consistently revisited, but those taught points themselves are miscalibrated relative to the true world frame. This is *correctable* through kinematic calibration.

#### Part (b) — Encoder to Cartesian Resolution

**Step 1:** For an $n$-bit encoder, the *joint-level* angular resolution is:

$$\Delta\theta_i = \frac{2\pi}{2^n} \text{ rad/count}$$

**Step 2:** The *Cartesian-space* resolution at the end-effector is the *forward kinematic differential*:

$$\Delta X = J(\theta) \cdot \Delta\theta$$

where $J(\theta) \in \mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ is the Jacobian matrix.

**Step 3:** This relationship is *configuration-dependent* because $J(\theta)$ varies with the joint angles $\theta$. Near singularities, $\det(J) \to 0$, so even tiny joint increments produce large Cartesian displacements — *degrading* the effective resolution.

**Step 4:** For a planar 2-link arm of lengths $l_1, l_2$, the Jacobian is:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 s_1 - l_2 s_{12} & -l_2 s_{12} \\ l_1 c_1 + l_2 c_{12} & l_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

The determinant is $\det(J) = l_1 l_2 s_2$, which vanishes when $s_2 = 0$ (i.e., the arm is fully extended or fully folded) — a *singular configuration* where Cartesian resolution is lost in the radial direction.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**  
> The most common error students commit is to **use accuracy and repeatability interchangeably**, or to claim "high accuracy implies high repeatability" without justification. The correct statement is: **High accuracy necessarily implies high repeatability and high precision** (because to be on target, the shots must cluster on the target); but the **converse is NOT true** — a robot can be highly repeatable and still be inaccurate due to a systematic (bias) error. Examiners *specifically look* for this nuance. Also, students often write "3σ = 95%" — the correct value is **99.73%** (or ~99.7%). Always quote the **Bessel correction** $N-1$ in the denominator of variance for finite-sample analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read before every KTU Exam):**

- **Accuracy** = closeness to the *true* commanded position (absolute, single-trial).
- **Precision / Resolution** = smallest distinguishable increment (hardware-limited, encoder bit-depth).
- **Repeatability** = consistency of *multiple* trials (statistical, ±3σ, 99.73%).
- **High accuracy ⇒ high repeatability** (always), but **high repeatability does NOT ⇒ high accuracy** (may have bias).
- **3-sigma bound formula:** $R_{rep} = \pm 3\sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N-1}\sum (P_i - \bar{P})^2}$.
- **Bessel correction:** Use $N-1$, NOT $N$, in the denominator for unbiased sample variance.
- **Encoder resolution:** $\Delta\theta = 2\pi / 2^n$; **Cartesian resolution** = $J(\theta) \cdot \Delta\theta$ (configuration-dependent).
- **Singularities** of the Jacobian *degrade* resolution and can cause loss of degrees of freedom.
- **Typical industrial robot repeatability:** ±0.02 mm to ±0.1 mm; **resolution:** 0.001 mm; **accuracy:** 1 to 10 mm.
- **ISO 9283** is the international standard for robot performance testing (cite if asked for a "standard").
- **Welding, painting, pick-and-place** → repeatability-focused tasks.  
  **Milling, drilling, inspection** → accuracy-focused tasks.
- **The archer analogy** is the single most effective way to score full marks on a definition question.
- **The accuracy error vector** is $\vec{e}_{acc} = P_d - P_a$; its **magnitude** is the scalar accuracy metric.
- **Remember** the difference between the *sample mean* $\bar{P}$ and the *commanded point* $P_d$ — accuracy uses $P_d$, repeatability uses $\bar{P}$.

---

> [!NOTE]  
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic:** **"A-P-R"** — **A**ccuracy is **A**bsolute, **P**recision is **P**ixel-fine, **R**epeatability is **R**eliability (consistency). Remember: **APR is the trifecta** of robot performance metrics in KTU Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Operating Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OERAT611 - FUNDAMENTALS OF ROBOTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Operating Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Operating Environment** of a robot refers to the complete set of physical, geometric, climatic, and operational conditions within which a robotic system is expected to perform its designated tasks. It encompasses the **workspace geometry**, **ambient conditions** (temperature, humidity, dust, lighting, electromagnetic interference), **obstacle topology**, **human-robot interaction zones**, and the **task-specific environmental constraints** that influence sensor performance, actuator efficiency, and overall system reliability.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme taxonomy, the operating environment is classified along three primary axes:

1. **Structural Order** — Structured, Semi-Structured, Unstructured
2. **Hazard Level** — Benign, Controlled, Hazardous, Extreme
3. **Media Type** — Terrestrial (Ground), Aerial, Aquatic (Underwater/Surface), Space

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The operating environment directly dictates the **selection of sensors, end-effectors, drive systems, and control architecture**. A robot designed for a structured manufacturing cell CANNOT be deployed in an unstructured disaster zone without re-engineering.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **human worker** being shifted between three job sites:

- **Site A — A clean air-conditioned assembly hall** (temperature **25°C**, dust-free, fixed conveyor position, well-lit). The worker wears normal clothes, uses basic tools, and follows a fixed routine. → This is a **Structured Environment**.
- **Site B — A busy construction site** (variable temperature **10–45°C**, dust, moving cranes, unpredictable obstacles, sunlight glare). The worker wears safety gear, uses rugged tools, and constantly adapts. → This is a **Semi-Structured / Hazardous Environment**.
- **Site C — The deep ocean floor or outer space** (high pressure, zero visibility, extreme cold, no human access). The worker (or robot) needs a fully sealed, autonomous, rugged system. → This is an **Unstructured / Extreme Environment**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Key Takeaway:** Just as a human's clothing, tools, training, and physical capability differ for each site, a robot's **mechanical structure, sensor suite, control software, and power system** must be re-engineered to suit its operating environment. The environment is NOT passive — it actively **constrains** the design.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Environmental Parameters

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Industrial Range | Extreme Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ambient Temperature | $T_a$ | **0 °C to 50 °C** | $-40$ °C to $+200$ °C |
| Relative Humidity | $RH$ | **30 % to 80 %** | 0 % to 100 % |
| Ambient Light | $L_a$ | **300 lx to 1500 lx** | 0 lx (dark) to 100,000 lx (sun) |
| Dust Concentration | $C_d$ | **< 10 mg/m³** | > 1000 mg/m³ |
| Vibration Level | $V_{rms}$ | **< 0.5 g** | > 10 g |
| IP Protection Class | $IP$ | **IP 54** | IP 68 / IP 69K |

> [!NOTE]
> **IP (Ingress Protection) Rating** is a two-digit code: the first digit (0–6) indicates **solid particle protection**; the second digit (0–9K) indicates **liquid ingress protection**. Industrial robots typically require **IP 65** or higher.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Operating Environments

### 2.1.1 By Structural Order

**(a) Structured Environment**
- Known, fixed, predictable geometry
- Objects are pre-positioned; lighting is controlled
- Example: Automotive welding cell, PCB pick-and-place station
- Sensors required: Simple (limit switches, encoders, vision with fixed templates)

**(b) Semi-Structured Environment**
- Partially known with some variability
- Example: Warehouse with moving pallets, hospital corridor with patients
- Sensors required: Mid-tier (LIDAR, stereo vision, ultrasonic)

**(c) Unstructured Environment**
- Highly dynamic, unknown, unpredictable
- Example: Search-and-rescue rubble, planetary surface, deep sea
- Sensors required: Multi-modal fusion (vision + LIDAR + IMU + tactile + thermal)

### 2.1.2 By Hazard Level

| Hazard Class | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Benign** | Indoor, climate-controlled | Lab automation |
| **Controlled** | Industrial floor with safety zones | Assembly line |
| **Hazardous** | Chemical, radiological, explosive | Nuclear plant inspection |
| **Extreme** | Space, deep sea, disaster zones | Mars rover, AUV |

### 2.1.3 By Operating Medium

- **Terrestrial**: Mobile robots, AGVs, legged robots
- **Aerial (UAV/Drone)**: Quadcopters, fixed-wing, blimps
- **Aquatic**: ROVs (Remotely Operated Vehicles), AUVs (Autonomous Underwater Vehicles)
- **Space**: Rovers, manipulator arms on ISS, planetary landers

## 2.2 Environmental Factors and Their Engineering Impact

### 2.2.1 Temperature Effects
- Actuator efficiency drops with temperature variance
- Battery capacity degrades in extreme cold (Li-ion loses ~**20% capacity** at **−20 °C**)
- Sensor calibration drifts (camera focus, LIDAR range)

### 2.2.2 Humidity and Dust
- Causes **corrosion**, **electrical shorts**, and **optical lens fogging**
- Triggers **IP rating** selection (sealed enclosures needed for IP 65+)

### 2.2.3 Lighting Conditions
- Affects **machine vision** algorithms; glare, shadows, low-light
- Forces use of **structured light**, **IR sensors**, or **active illumination**

### 2.2.4 Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)
- Disrupts communication, sensor readings (especially capacitive and magnetic encoders)
- Demands **shielded cables**, **optical isolation**, **Faraday cages**

### 2.2.5 Vibration and Shock
- Affects sensor calibration, mechanical fatigue, connector integrity
- Mitigated by **active vibration damping**, **ruggedized mounting (MIL-STD-810)**

## 2.3 Sensor–Environment Matching Matrix

| Environment | Vision | LIDAR | Ultrasonic | Tactile | Thermal |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Indoor Structured | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★ |
| Outdoor Semi-Structured | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ | ★★★★ |
| Underwater | ★★ | ★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★★ |
| Space | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | — | ★ | ★★★★★ |
| Smoke/Fire | ★ | ★ | ★★★ | ★ | ★★★★★ |

> [!NOTE]
> **★ = Suitability Rating** (5 = Excellent, 1 = Poor). Underwater vision is poor because of light absorption; ultrasonic dominates because sound propagates well in water.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Workspace Volume of Cartesian Robot | $V = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$ | m³, where $L_i$ is axis reach |
| 2 | Workspace of Spherical (Polar) Robot | $V = \frac{2}{3}\pi r^3$ | m³, $r$ = radial reach |
| 3 | Workspace of Cylindrical Robot | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | m³, $r$ = radial reach, $h$ = vertical |
| 4 | Workspace of Articulated (Revolute) | $V \approx 2.5 \cdot a_1 \cdot a_2^2$ | m³ (approximation), $a_i$ = link lengths |
| 5 | Workspace of SCARA | $V = \pi h (r_1^2 - r_2^2)$ | m³, $h$ = vertical, $r_1 > r_2$ |
| 6 | LIDAR Range Resolution | $\Delta R = \frac{c}{2 \cdot B}$ | meters, $c$ = speed of light, $B$ = bandwidth |
| 7 | LIDAR Angular Resolution | $\Delta\theta = \frac{2\pi}{N}$ | rad, $N$ = number of laser diodes |
| 8 | Sonar Range | $R = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$ | m, $v$ = sound speed in medium |
| 9 | Sound Speed in Air | $v \approx 331 + 0.6 \cdot T$ | m/s, $T$ in °C |
| 10 | Sound Speed in Water | $v \approx 1500$ | m/s (approx) |
| 11 | Camera Field of View | $FOV = 2 \arctan\!\left(\frac{d}{2f}\right)$ | rad, $d$ = sensor size, $f$ = focal length |
| 12 | Inverse Square Law (Illumination) | $I = \frac{I_0}{d^2}$ | lux, $d$ = distance from source |
| 13 | Battery Capacity Derating | $C_{eff} = C_{nom} \cdot (1 - k \cdot \vert T - 25 \vert)$ | Ah, $k$ ≈ 0.01/°C for Li-ion |
| 14 | Dust IP Rating Threshold | $IP 6X = $ dust-tight | No ingress after 8 hrs |
| 15 | Water IP Rating Threshold | $IP X7 = $ immersion to 1 m | 30 min duration |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently tests **workspace volume formulas** for the four primary robot configurations. Memorize formulas 1–5 above.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The operating environment framework is foundational in:

- **Industrial Automation** — Cell design, safety zones (ISO 10218, ISO/TS 15066 for cobots)
- **Autonomous Vehicles** — Weather-aware perception, all-weather sensor fusion
- **Surgical Robotics** — Sterile, confined, high-precision (Da Vinci system)
- **Defence & Disaster Response** — Bomb disposal, firefighting, CBRN reconnaissance
- **Planetary Exploration** — NASA's Mars rovers (Curiosity, Perseverance) operate in extreme dusty, cold, low-pressure environments
- **Underwater Inspection** — Oil rig hulls, pipelines, shipwrecks (depth-rated ROVs)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Workspace Volume of a Spherical (Polar) Robot

A spherical-coordinate robot has a **radial reach $r$**, **azimuthal swing $\pm \theta_{az}$**, and **polar elevation swing**. For a full-sphere reach $r$, the accessible volume is exactly a ball of radius $r$.

$$
V = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \int_{0}^{r} \rho^2 \sin\phi \, d\rho \, d\phi \, d\theta
$$

**Step 1: Integrate with respect to $\rho$ (radial coordinate).**

$$
\int_{0}^{r} \rho^2 \, d\rho = \left[ \frac{\rho^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{r} = \frac{r^3}{3}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute back into the integral.**

$$
V = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{\pi} \frac{r^3}{3} \sin\phi \, d\phi \, d\theta
$$

**Step 3: Integrate with respect to $\phi$ (polar angle from z-axis).**

$$
\int_{0}^{\pi} \sin\phi \, d\phi = \left[ -\cos\phi \right]_{0}^{\pi} = -\cos\pi - (-\cos 0) = -(-1) + 1 = 2
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the result.**

$$
V = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{r^3}{3} \cdot 2 \, d\theta = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{2r^3}{3} \, d\theta
$$

**Step 5: Integrate with respect to $\theta$.**

$$
V = \frac{2r^3}{3} \left[ \theta \right]_{0}^{2\pi} = \frac{2r^3}{3} \cdot 2\pi = \frac{4\pi r^3}{3}
$$

**Step 6: Combine constants to the standard sphere volume form.**

$$
\boxed{V_{sphere} = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3}
$$

Since the polar robot's full reach forms a complete sphere, this is the workspace volume.

> **Engineering Note:** If the polar robot has limited azimuth ($\pm \theta_{max}$) and limited polar sweep ($\phi_{min}$ to $\phi_{max}$), the formula generalizes to a **spherical sector**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Workspace Volume of a Cylindrical Robot

A cylindrical robot has radial reach $r$, full azimuth $\theta$, and vertical lift $h$. The volume is a **cylinder** of radius $r$ and height $h$.

$$
V = \int_{0}^{2\pi} \int_{0}^{h} \int_{0}^{r} \rho \, d\rho \, dz \, d\theta
$$

**Step 1: Integrate over $\rho$.**

$$
\int_{0}^{r} \rho \, d\rho = \frac{r^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2: Integrate over $z$.**

$$
\int_{0}^{h} dz = h
$$

**Step 3: Integrate over $\theta$.**

$$
\int_{0}^{2\pi} d\theta = 2\pi
$$

**Step 4: Multiply the results.**

$$
V = 2\pi \cdot h \cdot \frac{r^2}{2} = \pi r^2 h
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{cyl} = \pi r^2 h}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Workspace of a Cartesian Robot (Box Volume)

A Cartesian (gantry) robot moves along three orthogonal axes with maximum travels $L_x$, $L_y$, $L_z$.

**Step 1:** The reachable set is a rectangular parallelepiped (box).

**Step 2:** The volume of a box is the product of its three side lengths.

$$
\boxed{V_{cart} = L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z}
$$

**Numerical Example:** A Cartesian robot with $L_x = 2$ m, $L_y = 1.5$ m, $L_z = 1$ m.

$$
V = 2 \cdot 1.5 \cdot 1 = 3.0 \; \text{m}^3
$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Workspace of a SCARA Robot

A SCARA robot has an inner link $r_1$ and outer link $r_2$ ($r_1 > r_2$), with vertical lift $h$. The planar reach forms an **annulus** (ring) of outer radius $r_1 + r_2$ and inner radius $r_1 - r_2$.

**Step 1:** The planar accessible area is the area of the annulus.

$$
A = \pi (r_1 + r_2)^2 - \pi (r_1 - r_2)^2
$$

**Step 2:** Expand the squares.

$$
A = \pi \left[ r_1^2 + 2r_1 r_2 + r_2^2 \right] - \pi \left[ r_1^2 - 2r_1 r_2 + r_2^2 \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Simplify by subtraction.**

$$
A = \pi \left[ 4 r_1 r_2 \right] = 4\pi r_1 r_2
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by the vertical lift $h$ to get the 3D volume.

$$
\boxed{V_{SCARA} = 4 \pi r_1 r_2 h}
$$

> **Note:** The formula in the Cheat Sheet (Section 2.4) gives an equivalent form $\pi h (r_1^2 - r_2^2)$ — both are algebraically equivalent after applying the difference of squares.

## 3.5 Derivation: Sonar / Ultrasonic Range Equation

An ultrasonic sensor emits a pulse and measures the time-of-flight $t$ to the obstacle and back. The range $R$ is:

**Step 1:** Total path length = speed of sound × time.

$$
L_{total} = v \cdot t
$$

**Step 2:** Since the pulse travels TO and FROM the obstacle, the one-way range is half the total.

$$
R = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example.** Air at $T = 20$ °C → $v = 331 + 0.6 \times 20 = 343$ m/s. If the measured time is $t = 6$ ms:

$$
R = \frac{343 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.058}{2} = 1.029 \; \text{m}
$$

## 3.6 Derivation: LIDAR Range Resolution

A Frequency-Modulated Continuous Wave (FMCW) LIDAR transmits a chirp of bandwidth $B$ and mixes the return signal with the local oscillator.

**Step 1:** The beat frequency $f_b$ is proportional to range $R$:

$$
f_b = \frac{2 R \cdot B}{c \cdot T_c}
$$

where $T_c$ is the chirp duration.

**Step 2:** The minimum resolvable range corresponds to the inverse of the bandwidth:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{c}{2 B}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta R = \frac{c}{2B}}
$$

**Step 3: Numerical Example.** For a typical automotive LIDAR with $B = 1$ GHz:

$$
\Delta R = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 10^{9}} = 0.15 \; \text{m} = 15 \; \text{cm}
$$

## 3.7 Analytic Comparison Matrix — Environment vs. Design Choice

| Environment | Drive System | Sensor Suite | Material | Power Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Clean Indoor | Servo / Stepper | Vision, encoders | Aluminium, plastic | Mains AC |
| Outdoor | Brushless DC, hydraulic | LIDAR, GPS, IMU | Stainless steel, composites | Li-ion, solar, diesel |
| Underwater | Sealed brushless thrusters | Sonar, pressure sensors | Titanium, anodized Al | Pressure-tolerant Li-ion |
| Space | Reaction wheels, harmonic drives | Star tracker, sun sensor, LIDAR | Carbon fibre, aluminium alloys | RTG, solar, Li-ion |
| Hazardous (CBRN) | Pneumatic, explosion-proof | Radiation-hardened, gas sensors | Lead-shielded stainless | Isolated battery packs |
| High-Temp Foundry | Water-cooled, radiation-hardened | Pyrometers, thermal cameras | Refractory alloys | Isolated, heat-shielded |

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Rule of Thumb:** The **harsher the environment, the lower the maintenance access** → hence **higher redundancy, sealing, and self-diagnosis** are mandatory. NASA rovers have **full redundancy** in compute, power, and mobility systems.
