# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Robot–Environment Perception Loop

This diagram maps the **closed-loop interaction** between the robot, its sensor suite, and the operating environment. It is the high-yield mental model for any KTU question on "how robots perceive their environment."

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    envBlock[OPERATING ENVIRONMENT] --> sensIn[Sensor Input Stage]
    sensIn --> fuseBlock[Sensor Fusion Processor]
    fuseBlock --> mapBlock[World Model Map]
    mapBlock --> planBlock[Path and Task Planner]
    planBlock --> ctrlBlock[Motion Controller]
    ctrlBlock --> actOut[Actuator Output Stage]
    actOut --> robotAct[Robot Physical Action]
    robotAct --> envBlock
    envBlock --> disturb1[Temperature Drift]
    envBlock --> disturb2[Lighting Variation]
    envBlock --> disturb3[Obstacle Dynamics]
    disturb1 --> sensIn
    disturb2 --> sensIn
    disturb3 --> mapBlock
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The robot acts on the environment (right branch), the environment feeds back through sensors (top branch), and three environmental disturbances (bottom branch) corrupt the sensor and map stages — these disturbances define the "operating environment" in the engineering sense.

## 4.2 Flowchart — Classification of Operating Environments

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start Classification] --> q1{Is the geometry known a priori?}
    q1 -->|Yes, fully fixed| structA[STRUCTURED]
    q1 -->|Partially known| q2{Is the variability bounded?}
    q1 -->|No, dynamic| unstrA[UNSTRUCTURED]
    q2 -->|Yes| semiA[SEMI STRUCTURED]
    q2 -->|No| unstrA
    structA --> hazA{Hazard Level?}
    semiA --> hazA
    unstrA --> hazA
    hazA -->|Low| benA[Benign]
    hazA -->|Medium| contA[Controlled]
    hazA -->|High| hazdA[Hazardous]
    hazA -->|Extreme| extA[Extreme]
```

## 4.3 Architecture — Environment-Adaptive Sensor Suite

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PERCEPTION_MODULE
        camA[RGB Camera]
        lidA[2D LIDAR]
        sonA[Ultrasonic Array]
        imuA[IMU 6 Axis]
        thmA[Thermal Camera]
    end
    subgraph FUSION_AND_DECISION
        fusX[Multimodal Fusion Engine]
        dcyX[Environment Classifier]
        selX[Sensor Selector Switch]
    end
    subgraph ACTUATION_MODULE
        drvA[Drive System]
        armA[Manipulator Arm]
        eefA[End Effector]
    end
    camA --> selX
    lidA --> selX
    sonA --> selX
    imuA --> selX
    thmA --> selX
    selX --> fusX
    fusX --> dcyX
    dcyX --> selX
    fusX --> drvA
    fusX --> armA
    fusX --> eefA
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The **Environment Classifier** detects the current operating regime (e.g., "low light + dust") and dynamically re-weights which sensors are trusted. This is the heart of an **adaptive robot** suited to variable operating environments.

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Robot Selection by Environment

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    medQ -->|Air Indoor| airT[Terrestrial Indoor]
    medQ -->|Air Outdoor| outT[Terrestrial Outdoor]
    medQ -->|Water| watT[Aquatic ROV or AUV]
    medQ -->|Vacuum Space| spcT[Space Rover or Manipulator]
    airT --> pickA[Cartesian or SCARA]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Operating Environment" of a robot. List any FOUR environmental factors that affect robot performance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **operating environment** of a robot is the aggregate set of physical, geometric, climatic, and operational conditions under which the robot is designed to function. It includes the **workspace geometry**, **ambient conditions**, **obstacle distribution**, and **human–robot interaction zones**.

**Four environmental factors:**

1. **Temperature** — affects actuator torque, battery capacity, and sensor calibration.
2. **Humidity and Dust** — causes corrosion, optical fogging, electrical shorts; dictates IP rating.
3. **Lighting** — influences machine-vision algorithms; demands structured or active illumination.
4. **Electromagnetic Interference (EMI)** — disrupts sensors and communication; needs shielding.

> *(Any four relevant factors accepted. Full 3 marks require definition + 4 factors with one-line justification.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a structured and an unstructured environment with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Structured | Unstructured |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Known and fixed | Unknown and dynamic |
| Object positions | Pre-determined | Random and moving |
| Lighting | Controlled | Variable (sun, shadow, glare) |
| Sensors needed | Simple (limit switches, encoders) | Multi-modal (LIDAR + vision + IMU) |
| **Example** | Automotive welding cell | Mars surface, search-and-rescue rubble |

> *(Tabular form preferred. Award full marks for clear contrast + one example each side.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — Choice Option A

**(a)** Classify the operating environments of a robot along **three axes** with **two examples** for each class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A spherical robot has a **maximum radial reach of 1.2 m**. Calculate its workspace volume. A cylindrical robot of the **same reach (r = 1.2 m) and height h = 2 m** is now considered. Calculate its volume and comment on the comparison. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three Axes of Classification (7 marks):**

**Axis 1 — Structural Order:**
- **Structured**: Known, fixed geometry (e.g., PCB pick-and-place cell, car welding line)
- **Semi-Structured**: Partially known (e.g., warehouse, hospital corridor)
- **Unstructured**: Dynamic, unknown (e.g., disaster rubble, planetary surface)

**Axis 2 — Hazard Level:**
- **Benign**: Clean rooms, labs
- **Controlled**: Industrial floors with safety zones
- **Hazardous**: Chemical, nuclear, explosive zones
- **Extreme**: Deep sea, space, fire

**Axis 3 — Operating Medium:**
- **Terrestrial**: Indoor/outdoor ground
- **Aerial**: UAVs, drones
- **Aquatic**: ROVs, AUVs
- **Space**: Rovers, orbital manipulators

**(b) Volume Calculations (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Spherical Robot Volume:**

Using the derived formula $V = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ with $r = 1.2$ m:

$$
V_{sphere} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot (1.2)^3
$$

$$
V_{sphere} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \pi \cdot 1.728 = 2.304 \pi
$$

$$
V_{sphere} \approx 7.238 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Cylindrical Robot Volume:**

Using $V = \pi r^2 h$ with $r = 1.2$ m and $h = 2$ m:

$$
V_{cyl} = \pi \cdot (1.2)^2 \cdot 2 = \pi \cdot 1.44 \cdot 2 = 2.88 \pi
$$

$$
V_{cyl} \approx 9.048 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Comparison:**

$$
\frac{V_{cyl}}{V_{sphere}} = \frac{2.88 \pi}{2.304 \pi} = 1.25
$$

The **cylindrical robot has 25% more workspace volume** than the spherical robot of the same reach, because the cylinder extends vertically over $h = 2$ m, whereas the sphere is limited to its radius. However, the **sphere offers omnidirectional reach**, while the cylinder's reach is restricted to the radial–vertical cylinder boundary.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` — Choice Option B

**(a)** Explain the role of **IP (Ingress Protection) rating** in defining a robot's operating environment. Discuss **IP 54, IP 65, and IP 68** with suitable applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SCARA robot has an **inner link $r_1 = 0.4$ m**, **outer link $r_2 = 0.25$ m**, and **vertical lift $h = 0.3$ m**. Calculate the workspace volume. An ultrasonic sensor on this robot measures a **time-of-flight of 4 ms** in air at **$T = 30$ °C**. Calculate the obstacle range. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) IP Rating Explanation (7 marks):**

The **IP rating** is a two-digit international standard (IEC 60529) that classifies the **degree of protection** provided by a robot's enclosure against **intrusion of solids (first digit, 0–6)** and **liquids (second digit, 0–9K)**.

**IP 54:**
- **5** = Dust-protected (not fully sealed, but dust ingress won't interfere with operation)
- **4** = Protected against splashing water from any direction
- **Application**: Indoor industrial robots, light assembly cells with coolant splash risk

**IP 65:**
- **6** = Dust-tight (no ingress after 8 hours of exposure)
- **5** = Protected against low-pressure water jets
- **Application**: Food processing, outdoor AGVs, pharmaceutical cleanrooms

**IP 68:**
- **6** = Dust-tight
- **8** = Protected against continuous immersion beyond 1 m (manufacturer-specified depth)
- **Application**: Underwater ROVs, submersible inspection robots, deep-well borehole robots

> The higher the IP rating, the harsher the operating environment the robot can tolerate.

**(b) SCARA Volume + Sonar Range (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — SCARA Workspace Volume:**

Using the derived formula $V = 4 \pi r_1 r_2 h$:

$$
V = 4 \pi \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.25 \cdot 0.3
$$

$$
V = 4 \pi \cdot 0.03 = 0.12 \pi
$$

$$
V \approx 0.377 \; \text{m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Speed of Sound at T = 30 °C:**

$$
v = 331 + 0.6 \cdot 30 = 331 + 18 = 349 \; \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Obstacle Range from Sonar Time-of-Flight:**

$$
R = \frac{v \cdot t}{2} = \frac{349 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{1.396}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{R = 0.698 \; \text{m}}
$$

> **Final Answer:** Workspace volume ≈ **0.377 m³**; obstacle range ≈ **0.698 m**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the sonar range equation — the pulse travels **to and from** the obstacle. Writing $R = v \cdot t$ instead of $R = v \cdot t / 2$ costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Mixing sphere volume** $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$ with **circle area** $\pi r^2$ in workspace problems.
> 3. **IP rating digits reversed** — first digit is **solids**, second is **liquids** (this is THE most common KTU error).
> 4. **Not specifying units** in final answers — KTU examiners **deduct 0.5 mark** per missing unit on numerical problems.
> 5. **Writing "structured" or "unstructured" without an example** — full marks always require a paired example.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Operating Environment (Module 4):**

- **Operating Environment** = workspace geometry + ambient conditions + obstacles + human zones.
- **Three Classification Axes:** Structural Order (Structured / Semi / Unstructured) × Hazard Level (Benign / Controlled / Hazardous / Extreme) × Medium (Terrestrial / Aerial / Aquatic / Space).
- **IP Rating Format:** Two digits — **1st = Solids, 2nd = Liquids** (memorize this!).
- **Workspace Volume Formulas (must memorize):**
  * Cartesian: $L_x \cdot L_y \cdot L_z$
  * Spherical: $\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3$
  * Cylindrical: $\pi r^2 h$
  * SCARA: $4 \pi r_1 r_2 h$
- **Sonar Range:** $R = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}$ — **never forget the divisor 2**.
- **Speed of Sound in Air:** $v \approx 331 + 0.6 T$ (m/s, $T$ in °C).
- **Speed of Sound in Water:** $\approx 1500$ m/s (≈ 4× faster than air).
- **LIDAR Range Resolution:** $\Delta R = \frac{c}{2B}$ — wider bandwidth → finer resolution.
- **Camera FOV:** $FOV = 2 \arctan(d / 2f)$ — wider sensor or shorter focal length → wider FOV.
- **Sensor Selection Rule:** **No single sensor works in all environments** — multi-modal fusion is mandatory for unstructured / extreme environments.
- **Design Implication:** Harsher environment → higher IP rating, higher redundancy, rugged materials, isolated power.
- **Real-World Examples to Quote in Exams:** Mars Perseverance Rover (space/extreme), Da Vinci Surgical System (clean indoor), Amazon Kiva AGVs (warehouse semi-structured), Fukushima inspection robots (hazardous).
- **KTU High-Yield Keywords:** IP rating, workspace volume, sonar time-of-flight, structured vs unstructured, sensor fusion, environment classifier, ingress protection.
